interpretation: Platform records reconstruct official documentation. They do not contain product telemetry, conversion rates, observed completion times, or proven causes of abandonment.
Friction gates are documented requirements or transitions. They are not confirmed drop-off points.
Blocker taxonomy entries are candidate hypotheses. Individual reasons remain not diagnosis eligible unless separate case evidence supports them.
A documented journey analysis is not a diagnosis of a real developer attempt.

developerGoal: Set up 1Password developer tools locally and complete the first secure developer workflow.

entryPoint.developer_intent: Set up 1Password developer tools locally and complete the first secure developer workflow.
assumed_prior_state: Discovery is complete and the developer has selected 1Password Developer for local development.
The developer has not installed or configured the 1Password desktop app or CLI.
The developer has not chosen secret loading or SSH and Git as the first workflow.

selectedSurface.name: Mount and read a local .env file from a 1Password Environment
selectedSurface.selection_basis: Workshop selection policy (2026-07-19): when official docs present peer routes, commit to the fastest and most commonly used documented route. Prefer the vendor's first-listed or recommended quickstart, dashboard/console over CLI when both are peers, and the route with an explicit vendor time claim or fewest documented steps when tied. Selected route: Mount and read a local .env file from a 1Password Environment. The Developer quickstart presents securely loading developer secrets as one of two peer workflows. Its Mac and Linux mounted .env route has a concrete observable terminal and uses the account, desktop app, CLI integration, Environment, and variables already established by the shared path. Prior surface before policy commit: 1Password Developer quickstart for local development.

alternatives_considered.name: Administrator quickstart
alternatives_considered.reason_not_primary: It targets team administration, Events API and SIEM, policies, and shared resources, requires a Teams, Business, or Enterprise account plus admin or owner role, and is outside the named developer persona.

alternatives_considered.name: Partner quickstart
alternatives_considered.reason_not_primary: It targets registered partners building billing, Users API, Save in 1Password, or browser-autofill integrations and adds partner-program access.

alternatives_considered.name: Automation and production
alternatives_considered.reason_not_primary: The Developer quickstart explicitly focuses on local development with desktop biometric approval and routes CI/CD, containers, and production to service accounts and deployment guides.

firstMeaningfulSuccess.official_milestone: Try your first secure workflow
firstMeaningfulSuccess.normalized_outcome: After local app and CLI setup and creation of a 1Password Environment, the developer either reads mounted project secrets after biometric approval or completes an SSH and Git workflow with a 1Password-held key and device authentication.
firstMeaningfulSuccess.observable_completion_signal: Secret-loading success is the terminal displaying the mounted .env contents after a 1Password authorization prompt. SSH and Git success is a tested SSH or Git command approved through 1Password, followed by a signed commit that triggers device authentication if the guide's full workflow is completed.

boundary_evidence.official_label_or_terminal_state: The expected environment variables are printed with their saved values.

firstMeaningfulSuccess.why_this_is_the_boundary: The guide establishes a common local tool setup, then changes prerequisites, external systems, actions, and verification based on the chosen workflow. Selecting secrets over SSH and Git would be workshop-owner judgment.

prerequisites.requirement: A 1Password account is required before signing into the desktop app. Official setup guidance first asks the developer to choose family or individual use; the individual path signs up on 1Password.com, confirms the account by email, chooses a strong account password, and saves the Emergency Kit.
A supported 1Password desktop app for Mac, Windows, or Linux and the 1Password CLI are required for the local quickstart.

The local flow uses Touch ID, Windows Hello, or Linux system authentication for human-in-the-loop approval; available biometric or system-auth hardware and OS configuration are needed for that experience.
The developer must be allowed to create a 1Password Environment and add or import project variables. Sharing later requires access-management permission.
The mounted local .env destination is documented for Mac or Linux only. SSH and Git requires a compatible SSH client; on Windows, the agent works only with clients that support Microsoft OpenSSH.
The Developer quickstart requires signup but does not document a free plan, trial, payment-card step, or plan gate in this journey.

stages.action: Open the 1Password Developer quickstart and use its local-development path.
details: The guide contrasts desktop app plus biometric approval for local development with service accounts for automation and production.
stages.success_signal: Steps 1 through 4 and both first-workflow choices are visible.
stages.failure_or_wait: Automation and production require a different guide and authentication model.

stages.action: Decide whether to use 1Password with a family or individually.
details: A family user asks the person who created the family account for an invitation, migrates an existing individual account, or starts a new family account.
An individual user signs up on 1Password.com.
stages.success_signal: One official account route is selected.
stages.failure_or_wait: The family path depends on an organizer when joining an existing account.

stages.action: For the individual route, open 1Password.com and sign up for 1Password.
stages.input: Email address and the signup information requested by the current form
stages.success_signal: 1Password sends the account confirmation email.
stages.failure_or_wait: The support guidance establishes individual signup but does not enumerate every current dynamic form field or billing screen.

stages.action: Open the email from 1Password and confirm the account.
stages.input: Access to the signup email inbox
stages.success_signal: The confirmation continues account setup.
stages.failure_or_wait: A missing or inaccessible email blocks confirmation; the official support page does not document delivery timing or resend controls.

stages.action: Save and safely prepare the Emergency Kit that 1Password asks the developer to save during account creation.
details: The PDF contains the sign-in address, signup email address, unique Secret Key, a place to record the account password, and a Setup Code QR code.
Keep a retrievable copy because the Secret Key is not recoverable by 1Password and is needed for the first sign-in on a new device or browser.
stages.input: Generated Emergency Kit PDF and secure storage location
stages.output: Saved account-recovery and new-device sign-in artifact
stages.success_signal: The PDF is saved somewhere the developer can retrieve independently of the current device; optionally a printed copy records the password in a safe location.
stages.failure_or_wait: Losing all linked devices and the Emergency Kit can make the Secret Key unavailable; team administrators can also disable Emergency Kits or replace the flow with identity-provider unlock.

stages.action: Install the 1Password desktop app for Mac, Windows, or Linux and sign in.
details: On a device or browser not previously linked to the account, enter the Secret Key from the Emergency Kit or use its Setup Code, then enter the account password.
stages.input: Confirmed account, supported desktop OS, account password, and Secret Key or Setup Code when this is a new device
stages.success_signal: The app opens with the developer's account or collection in the sidebar.
stages.failure_or_wait: A missing Secret Key blocks the first sign-in on a new device even when the account password is known.

stages.action: In the desktop app select the account or collection, open Settings > Security, and enable device-based unlock.
details: Use Touch ID on Mac, Windows Hello on Windows, or Linux system authentication.
This enables fingerprint or device authentication for most developer tools.
stages.input: Signed-in desktop app and configured system authentication
stages.success_signal: The chosen unlock setting is on.
stages.failure_or_wait: Missing OS authentication or biometric setup prevents the guide's intended approval experience.

stages.action: Install 1Password CLI using Homebrew, winget, or the current manual release.
details: Homebrew command: brew install 1password-cli.
winget command: winget install 1password-cli.
The full install guide supplies additional Windows, Linux, and manual options.
stages.input: Package manager or downloaded CLI release
stages.success_signal: The op command is available in the terminal.
stages.failure_or_wait: Installation and PATH failures are platform-specific and delegated to the full CLI installation guide.

stages.action: Open desktop Settings > Developer and select Integrate with 1Password CLI.
details: The integration reuses accounts already added to the desktop app and device authentication for CLI commands.
stages.success_signal: Integrate with 1Password CLI is selected.
stages.failure_or_wait: Without the integration, the documented biometric CLI authentication path is unavailable.

stages.action: Run op vault list and approve the prompted sign-in.
stages.output: Vault list for the authenticated account
stages.success_signal: The terminal lists vaults the account can access.
stages.failure_or_wait: A locked app, denied prompt, or account-access problem prevents the list.

stages.action: In the desktop app open Developer > Environments and create a project-specific Environment.
details: Select New environment, enter a name such as iOS App Staging, and select Save.
Environments are labeled beta.
stages.success_signal: The new Environment opens in the app.
stages.failure_or_wait: The guide does not document availability gates for the beta feature.

stages.action: Populate the Environment by importing an existing .env file or adding variables manually.
details: Use Import .env file for existing project secrets or New variable for manual entry.
Manage environment > Manage access is optional sharing work after creation.
stages.input: Existing .env values or new variable names and secrets
stages.success_signal: The variables are visible inside the Environment.
stages.failure_or_wait: Missing source secrets or insufficient Environment permission blocks population.

stages.action: Choose Securely load developer secrets or Simplify your SSH and Git workflows as the first workflow.
details: The first route uses the Environment created in steps 12 and 13.
The second route creates or imports an SSH key and configures the 1Password SSH agent and optional commit signing.
stages.input: Project need, operating system, and desired first outcome
stages.success_signal: One of the two official first workflows is selected.
stages.failure_or_wait: The guide gives neither route precedence; mounted .env is limited to Mac and Linux.

stages.action: In the 1Password desktop app open Developer > Environments and select the project-specific Environment created in the shared path.
stages.success_signal: The saved project variables and destination controls are visible.
stages.failure_or_wait: Wrong account, collection, Environment, or locked desktop state must be corrected before configuration.

stages.action: Select Destinations, then select Configure destination for local .env file.
stages.success_signal: The device-path control is available.
stages.failure_or_wait: This mounted-file route is documented for Mac and Linux; Windows or programmatic access requires a different candidate.

stages.action: Choose the device directory and filename where the local .env destination should appear.
details: The mounted file is not stored on disk or tracked by Git and exists only when accessed.
stages.success_signal: The intended directory and .env filename are shown in the destination settings.
stages.failure_or_wait: An inaccessible, incorrect, or unsafe path must be changed before mounting.

stages.action: Select Mount .env file.
stages.success_signal: 1Password reports the destination as mounted or ready for access.
stages.failure_or_wait: A locked app, unavailable Environment, missing variable, unsupported platform, or desktop integration problem prevents mounting.

stages.action: In a terminal change to the configured device directory.
stages.success_signal: The shell is in the directory containing the chosen mounted filename.
stages.failure_or_wait: A path mismatch or missing directory must be corrected in the destination configuration or shell command.

stages.action: Run cat followed by the mounted .env filename.
stages.output: 1Password authorization request and attempted secret read
stages.success_signal: The desktop app prompts to authorize the access when approval is required.
stages.failure_or_wait: If no prompt or file read occurs, verify the mount, desktop app, device unlock, and CLI integration state.

stages.action: Approve the 1Password authorization prompt with the configured device-based unlock.
stages.success_signal: The terminal command resumes and receives the mounted file contents.
stages.failure_or_wait: Denied, timed-out, or failed device authentication leaves the secret unread; unlock 1Password and retry the cat command.

stages.action: Verify the terminal output contains the expected variable names and values from the 1Password Environment.
details: This proves the project can securely load the developer secrets without a plaintext .env file stored on disk.
stages.success_signal: The expected environment variables are printed with their saved values.
stages.failure_or_wait: Missing or incorrect values require returning to the Environment, correcting variables, remounting if necessary, and repeating authorization and read.

branches.condition: The intended use is CI/CD, containers, or production automation rather than local development.
branches.path: Use a service account with least-privilege vault access and follow Secure your deployments, then select the specific CI/CD, container, SDK, Connect, or integration guide. Do not rely on desktop biometric approval in unattended automation.
branches.effect_on_first_success: Replaces the desktop app and human approval path with service-account or Connect credentials and a deployment-specific terminal signal.

branches.condition: Homebrew is available on Mac.
branches.path: Run brew install 1password-cli, then confirm op is on PATH and continue to desktop integration.
branches.effect_on_first_success: Uses the shortest documented Mac installation route.

branches.condition: winget is available on Windows.
branches.path: Run winget install 1password-cli, then confirm op is on PATH and continue to desktop integration.
branches.effect_on_first_success: Uses the shortest documented Windows installation route.

branches.condition: Neither Homebrew nor winget is the appropriate installer.
branches.path: Download the latest release or use the full CLI installation guide's additional Windows, Linux, or manual instructions, satisfy its platform requirements, and confirm op is executable.
branches.effect_on_first_success: Adds platform-specific download, installation, and PATH steps.

branches.condition: An existing plaintext .env file contains the project variables.
branches.path: Open the Environment and select Import .env file, choose the source file, inspect the imported variables, and save the Environment. After successful migration and verification, plaintext-file cleanup is a later security action.
branches.effect_on_first_success: Bulk-imports existing values but starts with plaintext secrets already on disk.

branches.condition: Variables are entered manually.
branches.path: Select New variable for each required name and value, enter the secret, and save it in the Environment until the first test variable exists.
branches.effect_on_first_success: Adds one form operation per variable and avoids importing a plaintext file.

branches.condition: Securely load developer secrets is selected on Mac or Linux.
branches.path: In Developer > Environments open the created Environment; select Destinations; select Configure destination for local .env file; choose a device path; select Mount .env file; in a terminal navigate to that directory; run cat followed by the mounted filename; approve the 1Password authorization prompt; verify the expected environment variables appear. The mounted file is not stored on disk or tracked by Git and exists only when accessed.
branches.effect_on_first_success: Ends at an authorized read of the mounted project secrets without a persistent plaintext .env file.

branches.condition: Securely load developer secrets is selected on Windows or programmatic access is preferred.
branches.path: Use the latest beta CLI or a supported SDK to read variables from the Environment programmatically, select the relevant CLI or SDK guide, authenticate through the desktop integration, reference the Environment and variables in code or command input, execute the read, and verify the expected value is available only to the process.
branches.effect_on_first_success: Replaces the Mac/Linux mounted-file destination with a CLI or SDK integration and requires another route selection.

branches.condition: Simplify SSH and Git workflows is selected.
branches.path: In a Personal, Private, or Employee vault select New Item > SSH Key; select Add Private Key > Generate a New Key or import an existing key; if generating, choose Ed25519 or RSA, generate, and save; add the public key to the preferred Git service such as GitHub or GitLab; in desktop Settings > Developer enable Use the SSH Agent and optionally display key names; follow the OS-specific SSH or Git client configuration; on Windows verify the client supports Microsoft OpenSSH and its Authentication Agent service is installed and running; run an SSH or Git test such as ssh -T git@github.com and approve the 1Password request; for the guide's commit-signing step, open the key, choose Configure Commit Signing, select Edit Automatically to update .gitconfig, register the public key as a signing key at the Git service, commit code, approve the device-authentication prompt, and verify the commit is signed.
branches.effect_on_first_success: Ends at external Git-service SSH authentication and a signed commit using a key that remains encrypted in 1Password.

branches.condition: The actor is an administrator rather than a local developer.
branches.path: Use the Administrator quickstart: require a Teams, Business, or Enterprise account and owner or administrator role; install and configure the desktop app and CLI; choose Events API monitoring or team developer-tool preparation; for Events, go to Integrations > Events Reporting, choose a SIEM or Other, name the integration, issue and save a bearer token scoped to needed events, configure the SIEM to pull events, and verify event delivery; for team preparation, configure policies, shared Environments, vaults, and service accounts, then onboard users.
branches.effect_on_first_success: Changes persona, plan, permissions, resources, and verification from a personal developer workflow to administration.

branches.condition: The actor is a registered partner building a 1Password integration.
branches.path: Use the Partner quickstart and choose billing, user automation, or web experience. Billing requires Partner Program access and a partner bearer token before creating a billing account and activation link. User automation requires an OAuth app and client-credentials token before user API calls. Web experience installs @1password/save-button, builds a typed save request, renders the custom element, and verifies a visitor can save the item.
branches.effect_on_first_success: Adds partner access, a separate API or package, and a customer-facing integration outcome.

branches.condition: The developer joins or creates a family account instead of using the individual route.
branches.path: If joining an existing family, ask the family organizer for an invitation and follow the invitation to create the member account. If creating the family, sign up for 1Password Families and become the organizer. Each invited family member creates an account password and receives an individual Secret Key and Emergency Kit; the organizer confirms invited accounts before shared items become available.
branches.effect_on_first_success: Adds organizer dependency, invitation, member confirmation, and family-account roles before the same desktop developer-tool setup can begin.

frictionGates.description: Account setup first branches between family and individual use; the family route can depend on an organizer and invitation.
The individual route signs up on 1Password.com, then requires email confirmation and a strong account password. Current dynamic form and billing fields are not enumerated by the support guide.
The Emergency Kit must be saved safely because it contains the Secret Key and Setup Code needed for first sign-in on a new device; 1Password cannot recover the Secret Key.
The intended local path requires Touch ID, Windows Hello, or Linux system authentication and repeated human approval.
Both the desktop app and CLI must be installed, with package-manager and PATH differences across operating systems.
Desktop-to-CLI integration must be enabled before biometric CLI authentication works as documented.
Environments are labeled beta, and the guide does not document account, plan, or rollout availability conditions.
A real project secret must already exist or be created before the loading workflow can demonstrate value.
Secret loading and SSH/Git are co-equal first workflows with different platforms, external systems, and terminal signals.
Mounted .env is documented only for Mac and Linux; Windows must use programmatic CLI or SDK access for Environments.
SSH/Git requires a key, external Git-service registration, agent and client configuration, and Windows OpenSSH compatibility where applicable.

uncertainties.question: Should the workshop's 1Password Developer journey end at mounted secret access or SSH and Git approval?
uncertainties.impact: The choice changes required operating systems, external Git service, item creation, commands, and terminal verification.
uncertainties.reason_unresolved: The official Developer quickstart presents both as equal first workflows.

uncertainties.question: What exact dynamic fields, plan-selection controls, and payment screens appear in the current individual 1Password signup form?
uncertainties.impact: They can add manual fields, subscription selection, trial terms, or payment friction before the now-documented email confirmation, password, and Emergency Kit actions.
uncertainties.reason_unresolved: Official support guidance establishes the individual versus family choice and the ordered confirmation, password, and Emergency Kit actions, but does not enumerate every live form field or billing control.

uncertainties.question: Is Environments beta available to every account in the workshop cohort?
uncertainties.impact: Unavailable Environments would invalidate the common Step 3 and require a vault, item, secret-reference, CLI, or SDK alternative.
uncertainties.reason_unresolved: The guide labels the feature beta but does not state rollout, account, platform, or plan eligibility.

developerGoal: Create a first Ably account and app, then use the official JavaScript guide until the page reports its first successful realtime connection.

entryPoint.developer_intent: Create a first Ably account and app, then use the official JavaScript guide until the page reports its first successful realtime connection.
assumed_prior_state: No Ably account, app, API key, local HTML file, CLI installation, connection, channel, or message is assumed.

A browser, text editor, and local ability to open an HTML file are available; no account registration, terms acceptance, key creation, connection, publish, or subscription was performed during research.

selectedSurface.name: Ably Pub/Sub JavaScript getting-started route
selectedSurface.selection_basis: Under the workshop policy, this is the fastest documented route: the official JavaScript getting-started guide uses one local HTML file, loads the SDK from a CDN, and names the first observable connection before later Pub/Sub exercises.

alternatives_considered.name: Node.js getting started
alternatives_considered.reason_not_primary: It is a peer language route. JavaScript avoids a package-install prerequisite by using the documented CDN path.

alternatives_considered.name: Ably CLI
alternatives_considered.reason_not_primary: The JavaScript guide labels the CLI optional and uses it only as an additional client for later Pub/Sub exercises.

alternatives_considered.name: Basic Pub/Sub reference
alternatives_considered.reason_not_primary: It explains the core channel, subscribe, and publish operations, but the language-specific guide provides the vendor's chronological first-user path.

firstMeaningfulSuccess.official_milestone: Make the first realtime connection to Ably.
firstMeaningfulSuccess.normalized_outcome: A local HTML page connects to Ably Realtime with the JavaScript SDK.
firstMeaningfulSuccess.observable_completion_signal: The page and browser console display `Made my first connection!`.

boundary_evidence.official_label_or_terminal_state: `Made my first connection!`

firstMeaningfulSuccess.why_this_is_the_boundary: This is the guide's first named runtime success; channel subscription, publishing, presence, and history follow it.

prerequisites.requirement: Create a free Ably account through GitHub, Google, or the email form; the post-submission registration transition is not publicly enumerated.
For email signup, agree to the Terms of Service, Privacy Policy, Cookies Policy, and account-management and marketing emails, with an opt-out noted for marketing.
Create a new Ably app in the dashboard.
Create an API key in the app's API Keys tab with publish, subscribe, presence, and history capabilities.
The signup page advertises a free-forever account with no credit card required, so payment is not a first-connection prerequisite.
Have a browser and an HTML file in a local project directory; the guide loads the JavaScript SDK by CDN.

stages.action: Open the official JavaScript getting-started guide.
stages.output: The prerequisites and connection sequence are visible.
stages.success_signal: Step 1 is titled Connect to Ably.
stages.failure_or_wait: None documented.

stages.action: Sign up for an Ably account using the official signup page.
details: Treat signup as one opaque prerequisite under the workshop policy; do not infer unlisted form states.
stages.output: An authenticated Ably account is available.
stages.success_signal: The dashboard can be opened.
stages.failure_or_wait: Exact post-submit signup states are not publicly enumerated.

stages.action: Create a new app in the Ably dashboard.
stages.success_signal: The app's dashboard and API Keys tab are available.
stages.failure_or_wait: None documented.

stages.action: Create and copy an API key with publish, subscribe, presence, and history capabilities.
stages.output: A capable app API key is available.
stages.success_signal: The key can be inserted into the guide's client configuration.
stages.failure_or_wait: Keep the key secret; production browser apps should use token authentication.

stages.action: Create `index.html` with the guide's HTML, CDN SDK script, API key, client ID, and connected-event handler.
stages.input: The published Step 1 code with the developer's API key
stages.output: A runnable local HTML page exists.
stages.success_signal: The handler logs `Made my first connection!` after the connection becomes connected.
stages.failure_or_wait: An invalid or under-scoped key prevents connection.

stages.action: Open `index.html` in a browser.
stages.output: The Ably client instantiates and connects.
stages.success_signal: The page and browser console display `Made my first connection!`.
stages.failure_or_wait: Wait for the connected event; inspect the browser console on failure.

branches.condition: Use GitHub or Google instead of email
branches.path: Select the matching Continue control and complete the external identity-provider route.
branches.effect_on_first_success: Replaces the email form with an undocumented provider flow before converging on the Ably dashboard.

branches.condition: Use the demo key in the published example
branches.path: Run the code with the demonstration credential.
branches.effect_on_first_success: Can show a demonstration connection, but does not satisfy the requested first-user path through the developer's own account, app, and key.

branches.condition: Continue beyond the first connection
branches.path: Subscribe to `my-first-channel`, publish a message, enter presence, and retrieve message history.
branches.effect_on_first_success: Adds later tutorial milestones after the first named connection success.

frictionGates.description: Email registration requires accepting the displayed policies and email statement.
The required post-submission account transition is not publicly enumerated.
The developer's route requires an app-scoped API key with four named capabilities.
The guide uses a key for simplicity but says production client-side apps should use token authentication and never expose API keys.

uncertainties.question: What verification, confirmation, or onboarding states follow Sign up before the first dashboard session?
uncertainties.impact: The mandatory account segment cannot be reconstructed or counted from a no-account state.
uncertainties.reason_unresolved: The official signup page stops at the submission control and the documentation assumes an account.

developerGoal: Create or sign in to an Adobe Developer Console project for Experience Platform, obtain API credentials, and complete the documented XDM classes credential test.

entryPoint.developer_intent: Create or sign in to an Adobe Developer Console project for Experience Platform, obtain API credentials, and complete the documented XDM classes credential test.
assumed_prior_state: The developer has no prior Adobe Experience Platform setup for this journey.
The journey starts at account creation and stops at the first documented successful outcome.

selectedSurface.name: Experience Platform API auth tutorial + cURL XDM classes credential test
selectedSurface.selection_basis: The official documentation presents this as the hosted path from account setup to Test access credentials. It reaches the requested first-success boundary without extending into post-success production work.

firstMeaningfulSuccess.official_milestone: Test access credentials against global XDM classes
firstMeaningfulSuccess.normalized_outcome: An authenticated cURL request to the global XDM classes endpoint returns a successful response.
firstMeaningfulSuccess.observable_completion_signal: The XDM classes request returns HTTP 200 and a JSON response instead of an authentication error.

boundary_evidence.official_label_or_terminal_state: The official documentation leads the developer to: Test access credentials.

firstMeaningfulSuccess.why_this_is_the_boundary: This is the first observable happy-path result on the selected Experience Platform API auth tutorial + cURL XDM classes credential test surface; later configuration and production hardening occur after success.

prerequisites.requirement: An Adobe Developer Console account with access to an Experience Platform organization.
Experience Platform API credentials, including API key, organization ID, access token, and sandbox name.
A terminal with cURL.

stages.action: Sign in to Adobe Developer Console and select the Experience Platform organization.
stages.input: A new developer starting at account creation
stages.output: The organization and an API project are accessible.
stages.success_signal: The organization and an API project are accessible.
stages.failure_or_wait: The path cannot continue until this documented requirement succeeds.

stages.action: Create or open an Experience Platform API project and obtain the required credential values.
stages.output: API key, organization ID, access token, and sandbox name are available.
stages.success_signal: API key, organization ID, access token, and sandbox name are available.
stages.failure_or_wait: The path cannot continue until this documented requirement succeeds.

stages.action: Set shell variables for the documented Experience Platform authentication headers.
stages.output: The terminal contains the credential values required by the API request.
stages.success_signal: The terminal contains the credential values required by the API request.
stages.failure_or_wait: The path cannot continue until this documented requirement succeeds.

stages.action: Send the documented cURL credential test to the global XDM classes endpoint.
stages.output: The endpoint returns HTTP 200 with XDM classes JSON.
stages.success_signal: The endpoint returns HTTP 200 with XDM classes JSON.
stages.failure_or_wait: The path cannot continue until this documented requirement succeeds.

stages.action: Inspect the response and confirm that XDM classes were returned.
stages.output: A successful authenticated XDM classes response is visible.
stages.success_signal: A successful authenticated XDM classes response is visible.
stages.failure_or_wait: The path cannot continue until this documented requirement succeeds.

frictionGates.description: The Adobe organization must be entitled to Experience Platform and the user must be able to create or use API credentials.
Access tokens expire and must be current when the cURL request is sent.

uncertainties.question: Does the current fresh-account UI exactly match every navigation label in the documented Test access credentials path?
uncertainties.impact: Navigation wording may differ while the required outcome and boundary remain the same.
uncertainties.reason_unresolved: The official documentation establishes the journey but account-specific UI state can vary.

sources.title: Test access credentials
evidence_supported: An authenticated cURL request to the global XDM classes endpoint returns a successful response.
The XDM classes request returns HTTP 200 and a JSON response instead of an authentication error.

doesNotProve: Platform records reconstruct official documentation. They do not contain product telemetry, conversion rates, observed completion times, or proven causes of abandonment.
Friction gates are documented requirements or transitions. They are not confirmed drop-off points.
Blocker taxonomy entries are candidate hypotheses. Individual reasons remain not diagnosis eligible unless separate case evidence supports them.
A documented journey analysis is not a diagnosis of a real developer attempt.

developerGoal: Build Adyen's recommended default online-payment flow for a website, make one test card payment through Web Drop-in, and confirm the authorized result in both the client and webhook.

entryPoint.developer_intent: Build Adyen's recommended default online-payment flow for a website, make one test card payment through Web Drop-in, and confirm the authorized result in both the client and webhook.
assumed_prior_state: Discovery is complete and the developer has selected Adyen, but has not yet chosen among online, in-person, Platforms, or plugin integration families.
The developer does not yet have an Adyen test account, API key, client key, merchant account structure, or configured webhook.
The developer has an existing website or web application with a server, client, and a publicly reachable webhook endpoint or a way to expose one.
The first success is confined to Adyen's test environment and does not require a live-account application, contract, compliance form, or real payment card.

selectedSurface.name: Adyen online payments, Web Drop-in, Sessions flow, test environment
selectedSurface.selection_basis: Workshop selection policy (2026-07-19): when official docs present peer routes, commit to the fastest and most commonly used documented route. Prefer the vendor's first-listed or recommended quickstart, dashboard/console over CLI when both are peers, and the route with an explicit vendor time claim or fewest documented steps when tied. Committed to already-reconstructed surface `Adyen online payments, Web Drop-in, Sessions flow, test environment`. Prior basis: Adyen's general getting-started guide presents online payments, in-person payments, Adyen for Platforms, and plugins as co-equal integration choices. For online payments, Adyen says Sessions is the default server-side flow recommended for most integrations, and the Web Drop-in guide provides the most complete documented first end-to-end test-payment route. Adyen does not declare this route canonical for the whole platform, so the platform-level selection still needs human judgment.

alternatives_considered.name: In-person payments
alternatives_considered.reason_not_primary: It requires a POS application or standalone terminal and has a different hardware, account, and payment-verification journey.

alternatives_considered.name: Adyen for Platforms
alternatives_considered.reason_not_primary: It begins with Sales contact, implementation scoping, and an Adyen-provisioned test account rather than the self-service online-payment route.

alternatives_considered.name: Plugins
alternatives_considered.reason_not_primary: The first mile depends on the selected commerce system and plugin rather than Adyen's direct Web Drop-in integration.

alternatives_considered.name: Online payments, Advanced flow
alternatives_considered.reason_not_primary: It uses three Checkout API requests and is documented for use cases that Sessions does not cover; Adyen labels Sessions the default for most integrations.

alternatives_considered.name: Online payments, Web Components
alternatives_considered.reason_not_primary: Components provide individual payment-method UI rather than the pre-built Drop-in, but the same guide treats both as valid client choices.

alternatives_considered.name: Online payments for iOS, Android, React Native, or Flutter
alternatives_considered.reason_not_primary: Each client platform has different SDK, application, redirect, and UI setup even when Sessions remains the server-side flow.

firstMeaningfulSuccess.official_milestone: A completed payment session with an Authorised payment result and a successful AUTHORISATION webhook
firstMeaningfulSuccess.normalized_outcome: The developer creates an Adyen test account and credentials, implements Web Drop-in using the Sessions flow, submits a documented test card payment, shows the authorized result to the shopper, and receives the matching AUTHORISATION webhook with success true.
firstMeaningfulSuccess.observable_completion_signal: The client receives resultCode Authorised, or the optional session-result request returns status completed with resultCode Authorised, and the webhook server receives an AUTHORISATION event whose success field is true for the same merchantReference or pspReference.

boundary_evidence.official_label_or_terminal_state: The Sessions guide demonstrates status completed and resultCode Authorised, states that completed means the payment was authorized, and shows a successful AUTHORISATION webhook with success true.

firstMeaningfulSuccess.why_this_is_the_boundary: It is the first documented end-to-end proof that the account, server credential, client credential, session request, payment UI, shopper action, client result handling, and asynchronous webhook all work together. Live-account approval and production configuration are explicitly later steps.

prerequisites.requirement: A free Adyen test account and access to the test Customer Area are required for the self-service online-payment route.
The Customer Area user needs Merchant admin and Manage API credentials roles; the API credential needs the default payment roles.
The server needs an API credential and API key, while Web Drop-in needs a client key linked to that credential and an allowed origin.
A web application with server-side code, client-side code, a supported browser, and one documented Adyen API-library runtime is required for the representative route.
A public webhook endpoint capable of accepting the configured method and returning a successful HTTP status within 10 seconds is required for end-to-end outcome verification.
The first success uses the test environment and documented test card details; the official first-mile guides do not require a credit card or live payment.
The website architecture must observe the documented iframe, WebView, server-side rendering, browser, redirect, and Content Security Policy limitations that apply to the selected payment flow.

stages.action: Open Adyen's general getting-started guide and select Online payments as the integration family.
details: The same page also offers In-person payments, Adyen for Platforms, and Plugins.
The test account can be created before or while starting the live-account application, but the first success remains in test.
stages.input: Decision to accept a first test payment on a website
stages.success_signal: The online-payments checklist and build guide are the active documentation path.
stages.failure_or_wait: Selecting a different integration family changes the account, setup, hardware, or application journey and cannot be represented by this path.

stages.action: Create a free Adyen test account and sign in to the test Customer Area.
details: The signup creates an admin user and grants access to the unified test dashboard.
The test account supports test payments, inviting team members, applying for live, and starting the integration.
A test account does not guarantee approval to process live payments.
stages.input: Adyen signup identity and business information requested by the current form
stages.output: Test Customer Area access and initial admin user
stages.success_signal: The developer can open the test Customer Area and see the company and merchant account context.
stages.failure_or_wait: The official getting-started page does not enumerate the current signup fields, verification screens, anti-abuse checks, or possible account-creation delays.

stages.action: Determine the test company-account and merchant-account structure, confirm the admin user, and add team users or payment methods needed for the test.
details: Adyen uses one company account with one or more merchant-account subaccounts.
The initial test structure should resemble the intended live structure, though it can be finalized later.
The checklist directs the developer to add the payment methods that the integration will accept.
stages.input: Test business structure, team members, and intended first payment method
stages.output: Selected merchant account, authorized users, and enabled test payment method
stages.success_signal: A merchant account name is available for the Sessions request and the chosen test payment method can be offered.
stages.failure_or_wait: Missing user roles, the wrong account level, or an unavailable payment method blocks later credentials or checkout behavior.

stages.action: Generate and securely store the test API key for the payment API credential.
details: In the test Customer Area, open Developers, API credentials, choose the Payments tab and credential username, open Server settings, Authentication, API key, then generate and copy the key.
The key cannot be copied again after leaving the page.
Save changes after generation.
stages.input: Customer Area access with Merchant admin or Manage API credentials role
stages.output: Test API key associated with the selected API credential
stages.success_signal: The key is stored outside client code and the credential has the default payment roles.
stages.failure_or_wait: Insufficient user roles hide credential controls; losing the one-time displayed value requires generating another key.

stages.action: Generate the client key, add the website's allowed origin, store the key, and save the credential changes.
details: Open Developers, API credentials, select the integration credential, then Client settings, Authentication, Client key.
Generate and copy the test-prefixed client key.
Under allowed origins, add the development domain; localhost or 127.0.0.1 can use HTTP as secure contexts in test, while live requires HTTPS.
New allowed origins can take a few seconds to become available.
stages.input: API credential and exact website origin
stages.output: Client key linked to an allowed test origin
stages.success_signal: The test client key is available to the client application and the origin appears in the credential configuration.
stages.failure_or_wait: An absent or mismatched allowed origin prevents Drop-in client authentication; a newly added origin may require a short wait before retrying.

stages.action: Expose a webhook endpoint, configure a Standard webhook with default event codes in the test Customer Area, and test that Adyen receives a successful acknowledgement.
details: The endpoint must accept JSON, HTTP POST, or SOAP at a public URL and use the documented test ports and TLS requirements.

In Developers, Webhooks, create a new webhook, add the Standard type, set General and Server configuration values, configure security including HMAC or basic authentication as applicable, select events, and save the configuration.
Test configuration for the selected merchant account and event; the endpoint must return a successful HTTP status such as 200 or 202 within 10 seconds.
stages.input: Public webhook URL, method, merchant account, event selection, and security configuration
stages.output: Enabled and tested Standard webhook subscription
stages.success_signal: Test configuration succeeds and the endpoint acknowledges the test event.
stages.failure_or_wait: A timeout, non-2xx response, inaccessible URL, wrong port or TLS setup, authentication failure, or signature-validation failure sends events to retry handling and requires correction before another test.

stages.action: Choose the Sessions server-side flow, Web client platform, Drop-in UI, current Web version, and one documented server language.
details: Adyen calls Sessions the default flow for most online-payment integrations and describes it as one Checkout API request.
Advanced flow uses paymentMethods, payments, and payments/details and supports additional cases.
The Web Drop-in guide offers Java, PHP, C#, Node.js, Go, Python, and Ruby API libraries, with curl request examples also shown.
stages.input: Website architecture, supported use cases, and installed server runtime
stages.output: Web Drop-in plus Sessions implementation plan and language-specific branch
stages.success_signal: The matching tabs and version in the dynamic integration guide are selected.
stages.failure_or_wait: Adyen does not name one server language canonical; selecting another client platform, UI, flow, or version changes the subsequent actions.

stages.action: Install the selected official Adyen server API library and initialize its client with the API key and TEST environment.
details: Use the runtime-specific package command and minimum version documented in the selected tab.
Keep the API key on the server.
The guide also links official example integrations for several languages.
stages.input: Supported runtime, package manager, and test API key
stages.output: Server-side Checkout API client configured for test
stages.success_signal: The application can construct the Checkout service without exposing the API key to the browser.
stages.failure_or_wait: A missing runtime, unsupported version, dependency failure, wrong environment, or invalid API key blocks the Sessions request.

stages.action: Create a server route that makes a test Checkout API /sessions request and returns the session id and sessionData to the client.
details: Include merchantAccount, amount with currency and minor-unit value, returnUrl without PII, and a unique reference of at least three characters.
For Web, set channel as Web where required by the selected code path; countryCode and other fields control available payment methods.
Use an idempotency key for the API request as shown by the language setup.
A successful request returns HTTP 201 with sessionData and id.
stages.input: Merchant account, amount, currency, return URL, reference, API key, and optional shopper context
stages.output: Session id and sessionData returned by the server to the browser
stages.success_signal: The Checkout test endpoint returns HTTP 201 and the client receives both session fields.
stages.failure_or_wait: API errors can result from authentication, permissions, invalid fields, unavailable payment methods, or environment mismatch and must be handled before retrying.

stages.action: Install Adyen Web on the front end using the recommended npm route or the documented embedded script and stylesheet route.
details: The npm route can import Drop-in with all payment methods or import individual payment-method components for tree shaking.
Import the Adyen stylesheet together with the JavaScript library.
stages.input: Web application package system and selected Web version
stages.output: AdyenCheckout, Dropin, and required styles available to client code
stages.success_signal: The browser build resolves the library and stylesheet without version or module errors.
stages.failure_or_wait: Incorrect package version, missing CSS, module-format mismatch, or server-side rendering initialization can prevent the checkout UI from loading.

stages.action: Add a DOM container for Drop-in, configure AdyenCheckout, create Drop-in, and mount it in the browser.
details: Pass the server's session id and sessionData, the test client key, environment test, country code or locale, and required event handlers including onPaymentCompleted and onError.
Configure individual payment methods if needed.
Create the Dropin instance and mount it to the selected CSS selector or HTMLElement.
Initialize AdyenCheckout and Components on the client side when using server-side rendering.
stages.input: Session data, client key, environment, locale or country context, handlers, and DOM selector
stages.output: Rendered Drop-in showing available test payment methods
stages.success_signal: The Drop-in UI is mounted and ready for shopper interaction.
stages.failure_or_wait: An invalid session, wrong client key, disallowed origin, missing locale or country context, unsupported browser, CSP issue, iframe restriction, or client-side initialization error prevents rendering or interaction.

stages.action: Implement redirect-result handling and shopper-facing payment-status handling.
details: When a payment method redirects, read sessionId and redirectResult from the return URL, create AdyenCheckout with the session id, and submit redirectResult.
Use onPaymentCompleted or onPaymentFailed and the resultCode to show the current payment status.
If the shopper never returns, do not submit details and rely on the webhook outcome.
stages.input: Return URL parameters and checkout result events
stages.output: Redirect recovery and visible result state
stages.success_signal: A returned shopper sees the status corresponding to the resultCode, and non-returning flows still have webhook outcome handling.
stages.failure_or_wait: Malformed or absent return parameters, an incorrect return URL, or repeated submitDetails calls can prevent client confirmation; asynchronous methods may remain pending until a webhook arrives.

stages.action: Open the checkout, select card, enter an official Adyen test card number, future expiry date, and test security code, then submit the payment.
details: The official test-card list includes Visa and other scheme numbers with expiry 03/2030 and security code 737 for many examples.
Test card numbers work only on Adyen's test platform and must not be used for live payments.
The guide recommends testing each payment method intended for shoppers.
stages.input: Rendered Drop-in and documented test-card details
stages.output: Submitted test payment in the active session
stages.success_signal: Drop-in completes the payment flow and triggers a payment-completed or payment-failed event.
stages.failure_or_wait: Invalid test details, a refused test scenario, missing payment-method configuration, 3D Secure or redirect handling, session expiry, or network errors require correction or a new session before retrying.

stages.action: Verify the client-side result is Authorised and show the successful status to the shopper.
details: The onPaymentCompleted result provides resultCode.
Optionally send sessionResult and the original session id to the server and call GET /sessions/{id}?sessionResult=... once.
The demonstrated successful response has status completed and a payment resultCode of Authorised; the guide says completed means authorized.
stages.input: Checkout completion result and optional sessionResult
stages.success_signal: The client resultCode is Authorised, or the one-time session-result response is completed with Authorised.
stages.failure_or_wait: Refused, pending, canceled, expired, or failed results are not the target; the session-result response is a snapshot and must not be polled for updates.

stages.action: Receive the asynchronous AUTHORISATION webhook, acknowledge it, and match it to the test order.
details: Match merchantReference to the order reference or use pspReference for end-to-end testing.
A successful event has eventCode AUTHORISATION and success true.
Return a 2xx acknowledgement within 10 seconds, and use the webhook rather than the client result as the order-management outcome.
stages.input: Configured Standard webhook and completed test payment
stages.success_signal: The webhook endpoint records AUTHORISATION with success true for the same test payment and replies 200 or 202.
stages.failure_or_wait: Webhook delivery is asynchronous; a non-2xx response or timeout enters retry handling, while success false includes the refusal reason and requires a new corrected payment attempt.

branches.condition: In-person payments is selected.
branches.path: Leave the online checklist and use the point-of-sale getting-started route. Choose an integrated POS application or standalone terminal model, obtain the required test account and terminal or mobile hardware, configure Terminal API credentials and connection, install or pair the device, make a test point-of-sale payment, and verify the terminal and webhook or Customer Area result.
branches.effect_on_first_success: Adds hardware, terminal logistics, device networking, and Terminal API setup and changes the success from browser Drop-in to a physical or mobile terminal payment.

branches.condition: Adyen for Platforms is selected.
branches.path: Contact Adyen Sales to register interest; work with an Adyen contact to scope user onboarding, split payments, transaction risk, payouts, and reporting; wait for Adyen to provision the test account and email admin credentials; configure Customer Area resources, API credentials, and webhooks; follow the Platforms checklist and APIs; test the platform integration in the test environment.
branches.effect_on_first_success: Replaces self-service account creation with Sales qualification, collaborative design, and Adyen-provisioned credentials before API work can begin.

branches.condition: A commerce plugin is selected.
branches.path: Choose the documented commerce system and compatible Adyen-built or partner plugin, meet that platform's version and account prerequisites, install the plugin, enter Adyen test credentials and merchant account settings, configure payment methods and webhooks, place a test order, and verify its payment result in both systems.
branches.effect_on_first_success: Moves much of the SDK work into a platform-specific extension but adds a commerce-system account, plugin compatibility, and two-system configuration.

branches.condition: The Advanced server-side flow is required.
branches.path: Use the Advanced flow guide and implement paymentMethods to fetch methods, payments to submit payment data, and payments/details to complete redirects or additional actions; preserve client status display and AUTHORISATION webhook handling; test until the same authorized state is observed.
branches.effect_on_first_success: Changes one server request into three and moves more payment-state handling to the merchant server.

branches.condition: Web Components is selected instead of Drop-in.
branches.path: Follow the Components section of the Sessions guide: install the same Web library, create the target DOM element, configure AdyenCheckout with the session, import and configure the chosen payment-method Component, mount that Component, handle redirects and results, submit a test payment, and verify the AUTHORISATION webhook.
branches.effect_on_first_success: Replaces the all-in-one checkout UI with one or more individually configured payment-method components.

branches.condition: iOS, Android, React Native, or Flutter is selected.
branches.path: Choose the platform-specific Drop-in or Components guide, install its Adyen client SDK, configure the mobile application and return/deep-link behavior, create Sessions on the server, present the native checkout, submit platform test payment details, handle app return and result, and verify the AUTHORISATION webhook.
branches.effect_on_first_success: Changes client runtime, package manager, UI mounting, and redirect return handling while retaining server credentials, Sessions, and webhook outcome.

branches.condition: A server API-library language must be selected.
branches.path: Choose one official tab and meet its requirements: Java 11 or later and Maven, PHP 7.3 or later with Composer plus cURL and JSON, .NET Standard 2.0 or later with NuGet, Node.js 18 or later with npm, Go 1.13 or later with Go modules, Python 3.6 or later with pip, or Ruby 2.7 or later with Bundler. Install the package and initialize the matching Checkout client for TEST.
branches.effect_on_first_success: Changes runtime, package installation, client construction, and code examples, but not the required session fields or authorized-payment boundary.

branches.condition: The front end cannot or should not use npm.
branches.path: Use the guide's embedded Adyen Web script and stylesheet tags for the selected version, expose the global classes to client code, then configure and mount Drop-in exactly as in the main path.
branches.effect_on_first_success: Avoids npm and bundler setup but changes dependency loading, version pinning, and import syntax.

branches.condition: Bundle size requires individual payment-method imports.
branches.path: Import Drop-in and only the required payment-method components, pass them in paymentMethodComponents, and add new imports whenever another payment method is enabled.
branches.effect_on_first_success: Reduces bundle size but adds an explicit code change for each payment method.

branches.condition: The selected test payment method does not redirect.
branches.path: Do not execute the return-URL submitDetails route; let Drop-in complete locally, handle onPaymentCompleted or onPaymentFailed, then wait for and process the AUTHORISATION webhook.
branches.effect_on_first_success: Skips the conditional redirect round trip without changing the final authorization proof.

branches.condition: The shopper is redirected but never returns to the website.
branches.path: Do not call submitDetails without redirectResult; leave the client unresolved if necessary and wait for the webhook outcome, then update the order from the webhook.
branches.effect_on_first_success: Removes client-side completion as a reliable signal, so the webhook becomes the only terminal proof.

branches.condition: The client-side card integration is not ready but the server API must be tested.
branches.path: Use the official encrypted-card testing convention by prefixing the documented test card number, expiry month, expiry year, and security code with test_ in a test-only API request; then inspect the API response and webhook. Do not use this test shortcut in live.
branches.effect_on_first_success: Bypasses Drop-in and therefore does not prove the complete representative browser journey, though it can isolate the server payment route.

branches.condition: The session is expired.
branches.path: Create a new /sessions resource, send the new id and sessionData to the browser, recreate AdyenCheckout and Drop-in, re-enter test payment details, and submit again.
branches.effect_on_first_success: Repeats the session and client initialization because the default session cannot be completed after expiry.

branches.condition: The result is refused or failed.
branches.path: Use the client result, webhook reason, API logs, and documented response handling to identify the failure; correct credentials, request fields, payment-method setup, card details, origin, or shopper-action handling; create a fresh session when needed; submit another test payment and recheck both client and webhook.
branches.effect_on_first_success: Adds diagnosis and another payment attempt; success false or a non-Authorised result does not satisfy the boundary.

branches.condition: The payment method is asynchronous and returns paymentPending.
branches.path: Show the pending state to the shopper and wait for the later webhook outcome instead of polling the session-result endpoint; only treat the journey as successful after the webhook reports the required successful authorization state.
branches.effect_on_first_success: Adds an external wait and makes webhook delivery the terminal signal.

branches.condition: The webhook test or delivery fails.
branches.path: Inspect the Customer Area failure reason; verify the public URL, allowed port, TLS, method, authentication or HMAC handling, event selection, merchant account, response code, and response latency; correct the endpoint, rerun Test configuration, then repeat the end-to-end test payment and correlate references.
branches.effect_on_first_success: Adds webhook troubleshooting and another configuration test or payment because client authorization alone does not prove the full documented architecture.

frictionGates.description: Four general integration families lead to materially different first miles, and Adyen does not name one platform-wide canonical route.
The online route requires an Adyen test account and Customer Area access; the exact current signup fields and verification sequence are not described in the documentation.
Company and merchant account structure, users, roles, and test payment methods must be set before the integration can create the intended checkout.
Merchant admin and Manage API credentials roles govern access to keys and credentials.
The API key is displayed for copying during generation and must be stored securely on the server.
The client key and exact allowed origin must match; newly added origins can take a few seconds to propagate.
End-to-end proof requires a public webhook endpoint with allowed ports, supported TLS, authentication or HMAC handling, and a 2xx acknowledgement within 10 seconds.
Server flow, client platform, Drop-in versus Components, Web version, and one of seven server-language routes must be chosen before implementation.
Each server API library has its own runtime floor, package manager, install command, and client construction.
The Sessions request must align merchant account, currency, minor-unit amount, return URL, unique reference, environment, and payment-method context.
Browser support, client-side initialization, iframe and WebView guidance, allowed origin, CSP, library CSS, and redirect constraints can prevent the payment UI or shopper actions from working.
The developer must simulate a shopper with official test credentials and may encounter redirects, 3D Secure, refused scenarios, or session expiry before authorization.
The authoritative payment outcome arrives asynchronously by webhook; pending methods or delayed delivery extend the journey.

uncertainties.question: Which Adyen integration family should represent the workshop's canonical platform first mile?
uncertainties.impact: Online, in-person, Platforms, and plugin routes have different account gates, hardware, actors, implementation steps, and terminal signals.
uncertainties.reason_unresolved: The general official guide presents all four as choices and does not designate a platform-wide canonical route.

uncertainties.question: Which server language, Web version, and client packaging route should be used for the representative online implementation?
uncertainties.impact: They change runtime prerequisites, install commands, imports, configuration syntax, and troubleshooting.
uncertainties.reason_unresolved: The dynamic Sessions guide supports seven API-library languages, npm and embedded client loading, and version selection without naming one canonical combination.

uncertainties.question: What exact fields, verification checks, and wait states appear in the current Adyen free test-account signup?
uncertainties.impact: These screens may add business data, email verification, anti-abuse checks, or delays before the Customer Area is accessible.
uncertainties.reason_unresolved: The official getting-started page links to signup but does not document the form field-by-field.

uncertainties.question: Which test payment method and region should define the workshop's first authorized transaction?
uncertainties.impact: Payment-method availability, required fields, 3D Secure, redirects, and asynchronous states can change the number and kind of steps.
uncertainties.reason_unresolved: Adyen supports many payment methods and says to add those needed by the integration; no single method is labeled universal or canonical.

uncertainties.question: Can a workshop participant expose a public webhook endpoint that satisfies Adyen's port, TLS, security, and response-time requirements?
uncertainties.impact: Without it, the browser can demonstrate a client result but cannot complete the documented asynchronous order-outcome proof.
uncertainties.reason_unresolved: The requirement is documented, but the participant environment and network policy are outside the documentation.

Step 4. Sign the contract and get your live account
Step 5. Get ready to accept live payments

evidence_supported: Authorised client and completed session states

sources.title: Configure and manage webhooks

Set up webhooks in your Customer Area

sources.title: Adyen for Platforms: Get started

excludedAfterSuccess: Live-account eligibility review, Sales qualification for live processing, application requirements, legal checks, approval wait, contract, and PCI attestation
Recreating all test settings, keys, client keys, allowed origins, webhooks, and payment methods in the live Customer Area
Live endpoint prefixes, HTTPS production checkout, production card processing, pricing, settlement, payouts, reporting, and reconciliation
Refunds, cancellations, capture, tokenization, account updater, auto rescue, donations, network tokenization, partial payments, and other post-authorization features
Full refused-payment matrix, every payment method, regional configuration, localization, styling, express checkout, recurring payments, and 3D Secure optimization
In-person terminal deployment, Adyen for Platforms buildout, plugin-specific installations, Issuing, Capital, business accounts, and other product-family journeys
Production hardening, secret rotation, credential segmentation, webhook failover, replay protection, monitoring, and long-term order-management design
doesNotProve: Platform records reconstruct official documentation. They do not contain product telemetry, conversion rates, observed completion times, or proven causes of abandonment.
Friction gates are documented requirements or transitions. They are not confirmed drop-off points.
Blocker taxonomy entries are candidate hypotheses. Individual reasons remain not diagnosis eligible unless separate case evidence supports them.
A documented journey analysis is not a diagnosis of a real developer attempt.

developerGoal: Create an Agora account and secured project, then build and run the official Web Video SDK sample until two clients join the same channel and display realtime video.

entryPoint.developer_intent: Create an Agora account and secured project, then build and run the official Web Video SDK sample until two clients join the same channel and display realtime video.
assumed_prior_state: No Agora account, project, App ID, token, Vite project, SDK installation, source files, browser client, channel, or prior usage is assumed.

Node.js, npm, two camera-and-microphone clients or one client plus the official Web demo, a supported browser, and permission to grant media access are available; no signup, policy acceptance, verification, project creation, token generation, package installation, or call was performed during research.

selectedSurface.name: Secured Web Video SDK Quickstart with two clients
selectedSurface.selection_basis: Workshop selection policy (2026-07-19): when official docs present peer routes, commit to the fastest and most commonly used documented route. Prefer the vendor's first-listed or recommended quickstart, dashboard/console over CLI when both are peers, and the route with an explicit vendor time claim or fewest documented steps when tied. Selected route: Secured Web Video SDK Quickstart with two clients. This candidate follows the Web tab's recommended secured project, complete JavaScript sample, Vite setup, and explicit second-client test without substituting an undocumented shortcut. Prior surface before policy commit: Agora Video SDK Web Quickstart through a two-client basic video call.

alternatives_considered.name: Voice Calling
alternatives_considered.reason_not_primary: It is a peer realtime-media product and the platform-level docs do not rank it below Video Calling.

alternatives_considered.name: Interactive Live Streaming
alternatives_considered.reason_not_primary: It is a peer product for host and audience workflows rather than the selected all-participants Video Calling route.

alternatives_considered.name: Chat
alternatives_considered.reason_not_primary: It is a peer messaging surface with its own SDK quickstart and account enablement path.

alternatives_considered.name: Android Video SDK
alternatives_considered.reason_not_primary: It is a peer platform tab. The current structured Quickstart identifies Web as canonical and Web avoids a mobile IDE and physical-device build.

firstMeaningfulSuccess.official_milestone: Build a basic Video Calling app with the Agora Video SDK.
firstMeaningfulSuccess.normalized_outcome: Two Web Video SDK clients have joined the same Agora channel with the same App ID, channel name, and temporary token, published their local audio and video, and displayed local and remote video.
firstMeaningfulSuccess.observable_completion_signal: Each client displays its local video; after the peer publishes, the registered `user-published` handler subscribes and plays the remote track, while the first client's console logs `Publish success!` after its local publish completes.

boundary_evidence.official_label_or_terminal_state: The Quickstart test uses two clients in the same channel, and the complete sample logs `Publish success!` and renders local and remote video containers.

firstMeaningfulSuccess.why_this_is_the_boundary: The Quickstart defines a basic Video Calling app as initialization, channel join, and sending and receiving audio and video. The two-client test exercises all three before the guide moves to security and production next steps.

prerequisites.requirement: Create an Agora account with email, phone number, or a third-party account. The email route requires a verification code plus name, company name, phone number, and password.
Read and accept the Terms of Service, Privacy Policy, and Acceptable Use Policy during account signup.
Create an Agora project with a project name, use case, and Secured mode: APP ID + Token (Recommended).
Copy the project's App ID and generate a temporary RTC token for a chosen channel name.
Have Node.js and npm, and use a supported browser; Agora strongly recommends the latest stable Google Chrome.
Have a camera and microphone plus two devices, or use the documented official Web demo as the second client.
The current free package includes 10,000 participant minutes and suspends service at overuse, so payment is not a first-call prerequisite while free minutes remain.

stages.failure_or_wait: On-screen field rules must be satisfied.

stages.failure_or_wait: None documented.

stages.action: Create the documented vanilla JavaScript Vite project.
details: Run `npm create vite@latest agora_web_quickstart -- --template vanilla`.

stages.failure_or_wait: Package download or scaffold errors stop setup.

stages.action: Enter the new project, install its dependencies, and install the Agora Web SDK.
details: Run `cd agora_web_quickstart`.
Run `npm install`.
Run `npm install agora-rtc-sdk-ng`.
stages.success_signal: All three commands complete successfully.
stages.failure_or_wait: Package installation failures must be resolved before coding.

stages.action: Create or replace the JavaScript source with the complete sample code, including SDK import, client initialization, event listeners, join and leave handlers, local track creation and publishing, and local and remote playback containers.
details: Set `mode` to `rtc` and `codec` to `vp8`.
Keep the sample's `user-published` subscription handler and `Publish success!` log.
stages.input: The complete JavaScript sample from the Quickstart.
stages.output: The application's JavaScript implements the basic call lifecycle.
stages.success_signal: The source contains `startBasicCall()` and references Join and Leave controls.
stages.failure_or_wait: Omitted event listeners can cause published peer media to be missed.

stages.action: Replace the sample connection placeholders with the copied App ID, the selected channel name, and the generated temporary token; leave uid as 0 for a generated user ID.
stages.input: App ID, channel name, and temporary token.
stages.output: The source is configured for the secured test channel.
stages.success_signal: No connection placeholder remains.
stages.failure_or_wait: A mismatched channel name invalidates the temporary token for the test.

stages.action: Replace the root `index.html` with the documented Web SDK Video Quickstart UI containing Join and Leave buttons and the module script for `/src/main.js`.
stages.output: The page can load the call code and expose its controls.
stages.success_signal: Join and Leave elements have the IDs expected by the JavaScript.
stages.failure_or_wait: Mismatched IDs prevent the sample handlers from attaching.

stages.action: Start the Vite development server with `npm run dev`.
stages.output: Vite prints a local URL such as `http://localhost:5173`.
stages.success_signal: The development server remains running and the URL is displayed.
stages.failure_or_wait: A port conflict or build error prevents the URL from serving.

stages.action: Open the printed localhost URL, click Join, and grant camera and microphone access if the browser requests it.
stages.input: The first configured client and browser media permission.
stages.output: The client joins, creates local tracks, publishes them, displays local video, and logs `Publish success!`.
stages.success_signal: The local video container is visible and the console contains `Publish success!`.
stages.failure_or_wait: Denied permission, invalid credentials, unsupported browser behavior, or non-localhost HTTP access can block media or connection.

stages.action: On a second device, repeat installation and launch, or open the official Web demo, and configure it with the same App ID, channel name, and temporary token.
stages.input: A second supported client with matching channel credentials.
stages.output: The peer is ready to join the same channel.
stages.success_signal: The second client shows its Join control with matching values.
stages.failure_or_wait: Using different credentials or an expired token prevents the clients from meeting.

stages.action: Click Join on the second client and verify that each client receives and plays the other's published video and audio.
stages.input: Two joined clients in the same secured channel.
stages.output: Both clients participate in the basic realtime video call.
stages.success_signal: Local and remote video are displayed, and the remote track arrives through the `user-published` subscription handler.
stages.failure_or_wait: If the peer does not appear, verify matching App ID, channel, token, media permission, supported browser, and HTTPS or localhost requirements.

branches.condition: Choose another Agora product
branches.path: Follow that product's own account enablement, credential, SDK, and first-success guide.
branches.effect_on_first_success: Changes the meaning and terminal of the platform's first mile, which is why platform-level human judgment remains required.

branches.condition: Use a third-party account
branches.path: Select the provider on the Console login page, complete verification, click Create a new account, accept the policies, and click Continue.
branches.effect_on_first_success: Replaces email verification and profile-password steps before converging on Console.

branches.condition: Add to an existing Web project
branches.path: Create a JavaScript source in `src`, add an HTML UI, and include it with a module script instead of scaffolding Vite.
branches.effect_on_first_success: Skips new-project initialization but preserves SDK, credentials, and call behavior.

branches.condition: No second development device
branches.path: Use Agora's official Web demo or clone the official API Examples Web project as the peer.
branches.effect_on_first_success: Provides the documented second participant without duplicating the local scaffold.

frictionGates.description: Account creation requires accepting three named policies.
The email route requires a received verification code before profile completion.
The account guide recommends secured App ID plus Token mode, and production should use a token server rather than temporary tokens.
The first call requires an App ID, channel name, and a temporary token generated for that channel.
Camera and microphone permission is required to create and publish local media tracks.
The full receive-and-display test requires a second client, although the official Web demo can substitute for a second local install.
The free package suspends service after its 10,000 included participant minutes rather than charging overage.

uncertainties.question: Which Agora product should define a platform-wide first mile: Video Calling, Voice Calling, Interactive Live Streaming, Chat, Signaling, Whiteboard, or an AI surface?
uncertainties.impact: Each product has different resources, credentials, implementation steps, and success terminals.
uncertainties.reason_unresolved: The official platform and docs expose peer product families without naming one global default; this record supplies Video Calling as a complete candidate, not an assumed universal answer.
