developerGoal: Create an Authorize.net sandbox account and run the Java Hello World program that submits a test card charge.

entryPoint.developer_intent: Create an Authorize.net sandbox account and run the Java Hello World program that submits a test card charge.
assumed_prior_state: The developer has no Authorize.net sandbox account or merchant API credentials.
The developer chooses Java because it is the first language implementation shown.
A Java development environment, Maven, Git, and a terminal are available.

selectedSurface.name: Hello World Java sandbox charge
selectedSurface.selection_basis: Authorize.net labels Hello world as its step-by-step guide to make a first API call and defines that call as charging a test credit card and printing an authorization code and transaction ID. Java is the first language tab, so this record follows the Java sample plus the linked sandbox signup and credential instructions.

alternatives_considered.name: API Reference live console
alternatives_considered.reason_not_primary: It is positioned as a no-setup API explorer and does not measure the account, credentials, SDK, and local-code path.

alternatives_considered.name: C# Hello World
alternatives_considered.reason_not_primary: It is the second language tab and uses .NET tooling rather than the first-listed Java path.

alternatives_considered.name: Python, Ruby, and PHP Hello World
alternatives_considered.reason_not_primary: They are peer language implementations with different dependencies, sample repositories, and commands.

alternatives_considered.name: Accept Hosted
alternatives_considered.reason_not_primary: It adds form-token, browser payment form, customer input, redirect or iframe, and receipt behavior.

firstMeaningfulSuccess.official_milestone: Make the first API call by charging a test credit card.
firstMeaningfulSuccess.normalized_outcome: The Java sample sends an authCaptureTransaction to the Authorize.net sandbox and receives a successful transaction response.
firstMeaningfulSuccess.observable_completion_signal: The console prints Successfully created transaction with Transaction ID, response and message codes, description, and Auth Code.

boundary_evidence.official_label_or_terminal_state: The Hello World page says Time to make your first API call and describes its payment Hello World as charging a test card and printing the auth code and transaction ID.

firstMeaningfulSuccess.why_this_is_the_boundary: The successful transaction ID and authorization code are the guide's promised first-call output. Testing error conditions, settlement, refunds, payment forms, and production merchant setup are separate downstream work.

prerequisites.requirement: An Authorize.net sandbox account; a merchant account is only required to accept real payments.
The sandbox API Login ID and Transaction Key for merchant authentication.
Java, Maven, Git, and a terminal for the first-listed sample path.
Access to the sandbox Merchant Interface, GitHub or Maven dependencies, and the Authorize.net sandbox API.

stages.action: Open Authorize.net Hello world.
details: The page directs the developer to create a sandbox account and offers Java, C#, Python, Ruby, and PHP implementations.
stages.input: Intent to make a first Authorize.net API call.
stages.output: Sandbox entry and language-specific first-call instructions.
stages.success_signal: Create sandbox account and Java Step 1 are visible.
stages.failure_or_wait: None documented.

stages.action: Select Create sandbox account.
details: The sandbox simulates card-network connections and does not process real payments.
stages.input: Intent to create a test account.
stages.success_signal: The required account fields are visible.
stages.failure_or_wait: None documented.

stages.action: Enter the required personal and work context.
details: Enter first name and last name.
Select Country/Region.
Enter email.
Select Developer type: Student, Open-source, Freelance, Commercial, or Corporate.
Select Job level: Individual Contributor, Manager, Director, or Executive.
stages.success_signal: No required field message remains for these values.
stages.failure_or_wait: Missing values are rejected as required.

stages.action: Enter the required sandbox organization and currency values.
details: Enter Sandbox company name.
Select one of the listed Sandbox currencies.
The common setup guide says currency cannot be changed later; a different currency requires a new sandbox account.
stages.input: Sandbox company label and intended test currency.
stages.success_signal: Company and currency validation messages clear.
stages.failure_or_wait: Choosing the wrong currency requires another sandbox account later.

stages.action: Create the sandbox login credentials.
details: Choose a login ID of 8 to 20 characters with letters and numbers and no symbols.
Choose a password of 12 to 20 characters containing lowercase, uppercase, a number, and a special character.
Enter the same password in Confirm password.
stages.input: Unique login ID and compliant password.
stages.success_signal: Login and password validation messages clear.
stages.failure_or_wait: Invalid format, duplicate login, or mismatched passwords prevent submission.

stages.action: Accept the Authorize.net Sandbox Account Agreement.
details: Agreement acceptance is required.
stages.success_signal: The terms validation message clears.
stages.failure_or_wait: Declining the agreement blocks account creation.

stages.action: Complete reCAPTCHA.
details: The form marks reCAPTCHA as required.
stages.success_signal: The CAPTCHA requirement clears.
stages.failure_or_wait: A failed or expired challenge must be retried.

stages.action: Click Create Account.
stages.input: All required fields, agreement, and reCAPTCHA.
stages.success_signal: Account creation succeeds and sandbox access becomes available.
stages.failure_or_wait: The page does not document post-submit confirmation, email verification, or account-creation errors.

stages.action: Sign in to the sandbox Merchant Interface.
details: Use https://sandbox.authorize.net/ and the login created during signup.
stages.success_signal: Account navigation is available.
stages.failure_or_wait: Any account activation or email-verification step is not described on the signup or Hello World pages.

stages.action: Navigate to Account, Settings, then API Credentials & Keys.
details: The API Login ID and Transaction Key identify the account for gateway calls and are not Merchant Interface login credentials.
stages.success_signal: API Login ID and transaction-key controls are visible.
stages.failure_or_wait: None documented.

stages.action: Click New Transaction Key.
stages.success_signal: The key request can be submitted.
stages.failure_or_wait: None documented.

stages.action: Click Submit to request a new transaction key.
stages.success_signal: The interface offers a PIN request.
stages.failure_or_wait: None documented.

stages.action: Request and verify the PIN.
details: PIN verification is required before the Transaction Key is displayed.
stages.input: Access to the account's verification channel.
stages.success_signal: The Merchant Interface reveals the new Transaction Key.
stages.failure_or_wait: The guide does not identify the delivery channel, wait time, retry controls, or expiration behavior.

stages.action: Copy and securely store the API Login ID and Transaction Key.
details: The API Login ID is 8 to 25 alphanumeric characters.
The Transaction Key is 16 alphanumeric characters.
Both are merchant authentication secrets for API access.
stages.success_signal: Both values are available for the Java sample.
stages.failure_or_wait: Losing or mishandling the key requires generating another one.

stages.action: Select the Java Hello World tab.
details: Java is the first language option on the page.
stages.success_signal: Get the Authorize.net Java sample code is visible.
stages.failure_or_wait: Selecting another language changes SDK, code, and run commands.

stages.action: Get the Authorize.net Java sample-code project from the linked repository.
details: For an existing project, the guide instead says to add net.authorize:anet-java-sdk:LATEST to the POM.
stages.input: Git and network access, or an existing Maven project.
stages.output: Local Java sample project with Authorize.net SDK dependency.
stages.success_signal: The sample source and pom.xml are available.
stages.failure_or_wait: Clone, Maven, or repository access errors must be resolved.

stages.action: Configure the Java sample to use the sandbox API Login ID and Transaction Key.
details: The sample's run method accepts apiLoginId and transactionKey and uses them in MerchantAuthenticationType.

The current page visibly includes example credential literals; use the credentials belonging to the created sandbox account because the credential guide defines those as account authentication values.
stages.input: Own sandbox API Login ID and Transaction Key.
stages.output: Java sample configured for the developer's sandbox account.
stages.success_signal: MerchantAuthenticationType receives the developer's credentials.
stages.failure_or_wait: The Hello World prose does not explicitly state the substitution action, and using wrong credentials produces an API error.

stages.action: Confirm the sample targets Environment.SANDBOX.
details: The sample explicitly sets the Authorize.net environment to sandbox.
stages.success_signal: ApiOperationBase uses Environment.SANDBOX.
stages.failure_or_wait: A production environment with sandbox credentials fails and could risk a real transaction if live credentials were used.

stages.action: Confirm the documented test payment and transaction request.
details: Use test card 4242424242424242 and expiration 0835.
Use amount 25.00 from main.
Set transaction type to AUTH_CAPTURE_TRANSACTION.
The optional customer email is test@test.test.
Assemble MerchantAuthenticationType, PaymentType, TransactionRequestType, and CreateTransactionRequest.
stages.input: Sandbox credentials and documented test data.
stages.success_signal: The source contains merchant authentication, test card, amount, payment, transaction type, and request assembly.
stages.failure_or_wait: Malformed or unsupported request data returns a transaction or API error.

stages.action: Have the sample create the controller, execute the request, and obtain the API response.
details: Create CreateTransactionController with the request.
Call execute, then getApiResponse.
The code branches among success, failed transaction, API error, and null response.
stages.success_signal: A non-null response has resultCode OK and transaction messages.
stages.failure_or_wait: The sample prints Failed Transaction, error code and message, or Failed to get response for the documented failure branches.

stages.action: Build the Java sample with Maven.
details: Run mvn package.
stages.success_signal: Maven completes and creates target/SampleCode.jar.
stages.failure_or_wait: Compilation, dependency, or test failures stop the build.

stages.action: Run the ChargeCreditCard sample.
details: Run java -jar target/SampleCode.jar ChargeCreditCard.
stages.input: Packaged JAR, network access, and configured credentials.
stages.success_signal: The program receives the sandbox response and prints result details.
stages.failure_or_wait: Credential, network, validation, or payment errors print the sample's failure output.

stages.action: Verify that the test charge succeeded.
details: Confirm Successfully created transaction with Transaction ID is printed.
Confirm Response Code, Message Code, Description, and Auth Code are present.
stages.success_signal: A transaction ID and authorization code are printed on the success branch.
stages.failure_or_wait: Use the printed error code and error message with Authorize.net's response-code reference if the success fields are absent.

branches.condition: The developer chooses C#, Python, Ruby, or PHP.
branches.path: Follow the corresponding SDK or sample-repository setup, insert sandbox credentials, use its documented test-card transaction code, run its language-specific command, and verify its success output.
branches.effect_on_first_success: The auth-capture outcome remains a transaction ID and authorization response, while prerequisites, code, and commands change.

branches.condition: The developer adds Authorize.net to an existing Java project.
branches.path: Add net.authorize:anet-java-sdk:LATEST to pom.xml and copy or adapt the ChargeCreditCard code instead of cloning the full sample project.
branches.effect_on_first_success: Avoids the sample repository but requires project integration and source-file setup.

branches.condition: The developer uses the API Reference live console.
branches.path: Open Charge a Credit Card, select a request format, edit the out-of-box sandbox sample, send it, and inspect the response.
branches.effect_on_first_success: Avoids local account and SDK setup, so it measures the API explorer rather than owned credentials and code.

branches.condition: The developer needs to accept a real payment.
branches.path: Obtain a merchant account, generate production API credentials, change the environment and endpoint, complete go-live and PCI work, and use real payment data.
branches.effect_on_first_success: Adds underwriting, merchant, compliance, credential, and production-safety gates.

frictionGates.description: Signup requires developer type, job level, sandbox company, country, and a currency that cannot later be changed.
The sandbox login ID and password have explicit length and complexity constraints.
The Sandbox Account Agreement must be accepted.
reCAPTCHA must be completed.
A requested PIN must be received and verified before the Transaction Key is shown.
API Login ID and Transaction Key are required and must be stored securely.
The Hello World requires a language selection; this record uses first-listed Java.
Java, Maven, Git or equivalent source retrieval, and SDK dependencies are required.
Sandbox environment, account credentials, test-card data, and request construction must align.

uncertainties.question: What happens immediately after Create Account, including any email confirmation or activation?
uncertainties.impact: The sandbox account flow may add one or more steps before Merchant Interface login.
uncertainties.reason_unresolved: The signup page documents inputs and Create Account but no post-submit state.

uncertainties.question: How is the credential-verification PIN delivered and how long can it take?
uncertainties.impact: It may introduce an external-device or email wait and retry loop.
uncertainties.reason_unresolved: The setup FAQ says Request and verify a PIN but does not identify channel, expiry, timing, or retry behavior.

uncertainties.question: Why does the current Java Hello World display credential-like example literals without explicitly instructing replacement?
uncertainties.impact: A literal copy may authenticate a shared demo account, fail, or conflict with the page's create-your-account instruction.
uncertainties.reason_unresolved: The Hello World code and credential FAQ establish the credential parameters, but the prose does not explicitly connect the developer's newly generated values to the two literals in main.

developerGoal: Create a Better Stack account and monitor a public HTTP endpoint.

entryPoint.developer_intent: Create a Better Stack account and monitor a public HTTP endpoint.
assumed_prior_state: The developer begins before account creation with no platform resources or credentials.
A supported browser and internet connection are available.

selectedSurface.name: Uptime dashboard: first HTTP monitor
selectedSurface.selection_basis: The hosted dashboard path creates a working HTTP monitor without installing an agent or writing code.

alternatives_considered.name: Heartbeat monitor
alternatives_considered.reason_not_primary: A heartbeat requires an external job to call Better Stack; an HTTP monitor can check an existing URL directly.

firstMeaningfulSuccess.official_milestone: Create the first uptime monitor
firstMeaningfulSuccess.normalized_outcome: A hosted HTTP monitor checks the supplied URL and reports its current status.
firstMeaningfulSuccess.observable_completion_signal: The monitor detail page shows a completed check and an Up status for the endpoint.

boundary_evidence.official_label_or_terminal_state: The monitor detail page shows a completed check and an Up status for the endpoint.

firstMeaningfulSuccess.why_this_is_the_boundary: This is the first observable end-to-end result on the selected official surface; later production configuration and expansion follow it.

prerequisites.requirement: A Better Stack account.
A publicly reachable HTTP or HTTPS URL to monitor.

stages.action: Create or sign in to a Better Stack account and open Uptime.
stages.success_signal: The Uptime dashboard is available.
stages.failure_or_wait: Correct the documented configuration or prerequisite before continuing.

stages.action: Choose to create a monitor and select HTTP monitoring.
stages.success_signal: The monitor form is open.
stages.failure_or_wait: Correct the documented configuration or prerequisite before continuing.

stages.action: Enter the endpoint URL and save the monitor.
stages.output: The monitor is listed and checks begin.
stages.success_signal: The monitor is listed and checks begin.
stages.failure_or_wait: Correct the documented configuration or prerequisite before continuing.

stages.action: Open the monitor and confirm the first completed check.
stages.success_signal: The endpoint is reported Up.
stages.failure_or_wait: Correct the documented configuration or prerequisite before continuing.

frictionGates.description: The monitored URL must be reachable from Better Stack.

uncertainties.question: The docs do not fully expose every fresh-account signup or verification screen.
uncertainties.impact: The exact number or ordering of required onboarding actions may vary for a fresh account.
uncertainties.reason_unresolved: Public official documentation does not fully expose every authenticated or account-dependent state.

excludedAfterSuccess: Production hardening, scale configuration, and ongoing operations after the first successful result.

developerGoal: Create a Bird account and workspace, then use the official cURL quickstart until the sandbox message reaches delivered status.

entryPoint.developer_intent: Create a Bird account and workspace, then use the official cURL quickstart until the sandbox message reaches delivered status.
assumed_prior_state: No Bird account, organization, workspace, verified email, API key, sending domain, billing method, or message is assumed.
A work-email inbox, password, shell, and cURL are available; no signup, terms acceptance, key creation, or email API request was performed during research.

selectedSurface.name: Bird Send your first email through deterministic sandbox delivery
selectedSurface.selection_basis: The current documentation home labels Email as its canonical first-send quickstart, while the SMS and phone-number Verify product paths are still marked as coming later. Send your first email explicitly calls itself the fastest path from zero to a delivered email.

alternatives_considered.name: Bird CLI email quickstart
alternatives_considered.reason_not_primary: This is a peer implementation that adds CLI installation and browser OAuth; the main quickstart publishes the shorter raw HTTP route.

alternatives_considered.name: Bird Python email quickstart
alternatives_considered.reason_not_primary: This adds Python and SDK installation while reaching the same sandbox result.

alternatives_considered.name: Bird SMS
alternatives_considered.reason_not_primary: The documentation home says the SMS use-case quickstart is coming later, whereas Email has a complete canonical happy path.

firstMeaningfulSuccess.official_milestone: Send your first email
firstMeaningfulSuccess.normalized_outcome: A deterministic sandbox email sent through Bird's shared onboarding domain is accepted and then reports delivered status without reaching a real mailbox.
firstMeaningfulSuccess.observable_completion_signal: Polling the message by its `em_` ID returns `status: delivered` for `delivered@messagebird.dev`.

boundary_evidence.official_label_or_terminal_state: The quickstart shows the status moving from accepted through processed to delivered, and the sandbox guide defines `email.delivered` as the deterministic happy-path event.

firstMeaningfulSuccess.why_this_is_the_boundary: The vendor names this its first-email quickstart and demonstrates delivered as its final verification state before production domain, real-recipient, webhook, and SDK work.

prerequisites.requirement: Use a work email address that can receive Bird's verification message.
Signing up signifies agreement to Bird's Terms and Privacy Policy.
Verify the signup email by entering its code or following its confirmation link.
The account creator starts as organization owner, which can create workspace API keys; Admin and Developer workspace roles can also create keys.
Create a regional API key with `emails:write` and copy its one-time full value.
Have a shell with cURL for the main raw HTTP quickstart.
The free plan requires no credit card; the shared onboarding domain and sandbox recipient avoid production sender setup.

stages.action: Open Bird documentation and select the Email first-send quickstart.
stages.output: Send your first email opens as the current canonical product happy path.
stages.success_signal: The page identifies an API-key-to-delivered-email path.
stages.failure_or_wait: The SMS and phone-number Verify product paths are marked as coming later; a separate Verify your first customer use-case link is also present.

stages.action: Open Bird signup and select Use password instead for the documented work-email-and-password route.
details: Google, GitHub, and email sign-in-link routes are also exposed.
stages.output: The password-based signup route is selected.
stages.success_signal: The route matches Bird's account-creation guide.
stages.failure_or_wait: Selecting a different identity route changes the intermediate authentication steps.

stages.action: Enter the work email and password and submit signup.
details: Submitting signifies agreement to Bird's Terms and Privacy Policy.
stages.output: Bird creates the account and sends a verification code by email.
stages.success_signal: The confirmation screen requests email verification.
stages.failure_or_wait: The account exists but has limited use until the email is confirmed.

stages.action: Open Bird's verification email and enter its code, or follow its confirmation link.
stages.output: The email is confirmed and the developer is signed in.
stages.success_signal: The Bird dashboard opens for the first time.
stages.failure_or_wait: If the message has not arrived within a minute or two, check spam or use Resend on the confirmation screen.

stages.action: Land in the automatically available workspace.
stages.output: A ready-to-use workspace exposes Developers, API keys, and the shared onboarding sender.
stages.success_signal: The workspace dashboard is visible without DNS or domain verification.
stages.failure_or_wait: No setup wait is documented for the shared onboarding domain.

stages.action: Open Developers, API keys, choose to create a key, name it, and grant `emails:write`.
stages.input: A job-oriented name and the emails write scope
stages.output: Bird creates a workspace-bound regional key.
stages.success_signal: The full key beginning `bk_us1_` or `bk_eu1_` is displayed once.
stages.failure_or_wait: A key without the required permission returns 403 on the send endpoint.

stages.action: Copy the full key immediately and export it as `BIRD_API_KEY`.
stages.output: The shell holds the credential for the quickstart.
stages.success_signal: The key prefix identifies the matching regional API host.
stages.failure_or_wait: A lost key cannot be recovered; create a replacement and revoke the old key.

stages.action: POST the published email payload to the host matching the key's region.
details: Use `onboarding@messagebird.dev` as sender and `delivered@messagebird.dev` as recipient.
stages.input: Bearer key plus the quickstart from, to, subject, and HTML body
stages.output: Bird returns HTTP 202 with an `em_` message ID and `status: accepted`.
stages.success_signal: The request is durably accepted for asynchronous processing.
stages.failure_or_wait: A key sent to the wrong regional host returns 421; an invalid key returns 401; unsupported fields return 422.

stages.action: Process the accepted sandbox email asynchronously through the delivery-status pipeline.
stages.output: The status advances from accepted through processed toward delivered.
stages.success_signal: The deterministic delivered sandbox address selects the happy-path outcome.
stages.failure_or_wait: The shared sender is capped at roughly 50 recipients per organization per day; exceeding the cap returns 429.

stages.action: GET the message by its returned `em_` ID until its status is delivered.
stages.input: GET /v1/email/messages/{message_id} with the same bearer key
stages.success_signal: The deterministic first-email terminal is observable without a real mailbox.
stages.failure_or_wait: The sandbox never hands the message to real delivery infrastructure; delivered is the documented simulated outcome in the real event pipeline.

branches.condition: Use Google, GitHub, or an emailed sign-in link
branches.path: Select the corresponding signup control and complete that identity route.
branches.effect_on_first_success: Changes account authentication while converging on the same verified workspace; the account guide selects work email and password for its documented route.

branches.condition: Use the confirmation link instead of the code
branches.path: Follow the link in Bird's verification email.
branches.effect_on_first_success: Replaces code entry but reaches the same signed-in dashboard.

branches.condition: The key is EU-region scoped
branches.path: Use the `eu1.platform.bird.com` host for the send and status requests.
branches.effect_on_first_success: Changes only the regional host selected by the `bk_eu1_` prefix.

branches.condition: Use TypeScript instead of cURL
branches.path: Install `@messagebird/sdk`, construct `BirdClient`, call `bird.email.send`, and then fetch the message by ID.
branches.effect_on_first_success: Adds Node and package installation but reaches the same accepted and delivered statuses.

branches.condition: Test a non-happy-path sandbox outcome
branches.path: Replace the recipient with bounce, softbounce, complaint, suppressed, reject, or deferred at `messagebird.dev`.
branches.effect_on_first_success: Produces a deterministic non-delivered terminal and therefore does not satisfy the selected happy path.

frictionGates.description: Submitting signup signifies agreement to Bird's Terms and Privacy Policy.
Email confirmation is required before the account has normal dashboard use.
Key creation requires an owner, Admin, or Developer role, and the first-send key needs `emails:write`.
The full API key is displayed only once and must be copied immediately.
The API host must match the region encoded in the key prefix or the request returns 421.
HTTP 202 means accepted, not delivered; the status must progress asynchronously before verification.
The shared onboarding sender is capped at roughly 50 recipients per organization per day and returns 429 above the cap.

developerGoal: Choose the relevant Boomi product surface and, for the Data Integration candidate, create the first source-to-target data flow and run it successfully.

entryPoint.developer_intent: Choose the relevant Boomi product surface and, for the Data Integration candidate, create the first source-to-target data flow and run it successfully.
assumed_prior_state: Discovery is complete and the organization has selected Boomi, but has not said whether its first mile is Data Integration, core Integration, API Management, Flow, Master Data Hub, or another Boomi product.
For the recorded candidate, the developer has credentials for a supported source and access to a supported cloud data warehouse target.
The source and target can be reached from Data Integration, including any required firewall allowlisting or private connectivity.

selectedSurface.name: Data Integration Source to Target Flow quick start
selectedSurface.selection_basis: Workshop selection policy (2026-07-19): when official docs present peer routes, commit to the fastest and most commonly used documented route. Prefer the vendor's first-listed or recommended quickstart, dashboard/console over CLI when both are peers, and the route with an explicit vendor time claim or fewest documented steps when tied. Selected route: Data Integration Source to Target Flow quick start. The public Data Integration guide explicitly names this sequence a quick start and carries it from account setup through a successful first data-flow run. Source to Target Flow is selected from the documented flow types because it is the direct route using the required source and warehouse target prerequisites. Prior surface before policy commit: Boomi product-surface choice, with a Data Integration first-data-flow candidate.

alternatives_considered.name: Boomi Integration first process
alternatives_considered.reason_not_primary: The official page recommends the Training and Certification portal for the first process and separately points to process-library, Quick Start wizard, Boomi GPT, runtime setup, testing, and deployment routes. It is a peer product journey with different prerequisites and terminals.

alternatives_considered.name: Boomi Integration jumpstart
alternatives_considered.reason_not_primary: This route requires a process-building method and a configured runtime environment before test or deployment, unlike the fully managed Data Integration candidate.

alternatives_considered.name: Data Integration Kit
alternatives_considered.reason_not_primary: The quick-start guide labels Kits optional and places them after the first successful data-flow run.

firstMeaningfulSuccess.official_milestone: Data Integration candidate: Once your data flow runs successfully
firstMeaningfulSuccess.normalized_outcome: The developer creates reusable source and target connections, configures and saves a Source to Target Flow, runs it manually, and sees a successful run in the Activity panel.
firstMeaningfulSuccess.observable_completion_signal: The opened Data Flow's Activity panel reports that the manual run completed successfully and exposes its run logs.

boundary_evidence.official_label_or_terminal_state: The Activity panel reports that the data flow ran successfully.

firstMeaningfulSuccess.why_this_is_the_boundary: A successful manual run is the first explicit, observable terminal in the selected Data Integration quick start. Scheduling and Kits follow it. A human still must confirm that Data Integration, rather than core Integration or another product, is the representative Boomi surface.

prerequisites.requirement: A valid active Data Integration account, or authority to sign up. Existing users can contact their account administrator.
Valid authentication details for the intended data source, such as database hostname and port, username and password, or API keys.
Access to a supported target cloud data warehouse or data lake, with Snowflake, Google BigQuery, Amazon Redshift, and Azure Synapse Analytics listed as examples.
Permission to create and save connections and Data Flows in the selected Data Integration workspace.
For an on-premise or firewall-protected source, network rules that allow outbound connections to the designated Data Integration IP addresses.

stages.action: Open Boomi's product documentation and identify the product surface that owns the intended first outcome.
details: Data Integration documents Data Flows and connections, while core Integration documents processes, a Build Canvas, runtimes, testing, and deployment.
stages.success_signal: The workload can be mapped to a named Boomi product's getting-started documentation.
stages.failure_or_wait: If the workload is only described as Boomi, the official docs do not select one product-wide canonical first journey.

stages.action: For the recorded candidate, choose Data Integration and its Data Integration Flow quick-start guide.
details: The guide explicitly says to follow its steps to run the first data flow.
stages.input: Data movement into a cloud warehouse as the intended workload
stages.success_signal: The Set up your account step is the next documented action.
stages.failure_or_wait: This selection is a research candidate, not a Boomi-wide default established by official documentation.

stages.action: Navigate to the Data Integration Console and select Start for Free.
stages.success_signal: Email and password, Google sign-in, and operational-region controls are available.
stages.failure_or_wait: Existing managed-account users may need their account administrator rather than a new signup.

stages.action: Create the account with an email address and unique password, choose the operational region, and select Get Started.
details: The documented regions are North America in AWS us-east-2, Europe in eu-west-1, Israel in il-central-1, and Australia in ap-southeast-2.
Sign in with Google is an alternative branch.
stages.input: Email, unique password, and region decision
stages.success_signal: The profile and workspace setup screen opens.
stages.failure_or_wait: Account validation, duplicate identity, password, or region-selection errors can block progress; detailed messages are not enumerated in the guide.

stages.action: Enter the developer's name, company name, and Data Integration workspace name, then select Next.
stages.success_signal: Data Integration sends an email-verification message.
stages.failure_or_wait: Missing or rejected profile values prevent the onboarding flow from advancing.

stages.action: Open the verification email from Data Integration and select its verification link.
details: The guide says this step does not apply to Google sign-in.
stages.success_signal: The account is verified and can return to onboarding.
stages.failure_or_wait: Delivery or access to the verification message is an external wait and can block console access.

stages.action: Select Let's Go to enter the Data Integration onboarding page.
stages.success_signal: The left navigation, including Connections and Data Flows, is available.
stages.failure_or_wait: Authentication or incomplete verification can prevent workspace entry.

stages.action: Open Connections from the left menu and select + New Connection for the source.
details: The quick start requires at least one source connection and one target connection before a data flow can be built.
stages.success_signal: The source-system selector is visible.
stages.failure_or_wait: Insufficient workspace permission can hide or block connection creation.

stages.action: Select the intended source system from the list.
details: If it is not listed, the guide directs the developer to REST API Source for a custom connection.
stages.success_signal: The required credential fields for that source appear.
stages.failure_or_wait: The exact fields and connectivity requirements vary by source connector and are not enumerated in the general quick start.

stages.action: Enter the source system's required credentials.
details: Examples in the prerequisite list include hostname, port, username, password, and API keys.
stages.success_signal: All source-required credential fields are accepted.
stages.failure_or_wait: Invalid credentials, firewall rules, or missing allowlisting can prevent the source from being usable.

stages.action: Enter a Connection Name that identifies the source and select Save.
stages.success_signal: The source connection is present for selection in Data Flows.
stages.failure_or_wait: Missing required source configuration or rejected naming prevents Save.

stages.action: Return to Connections and select + New Connection for the target.
stages.success_signal: The target-system selector is visible.
stages.failure_or_wait: A source connection alone is insufficient; the guide requires both source and target.

stages.action: Select the intended cloud data warehouse or data-lake target.
details: The guide lists Snowflake, BigQuery, Amazon Redshift, and Azure Synapse Analytics as target examples.
stages.success_signal: The required fields for the selected target appear.
stages.failure_or_wait: The exact fields, role requirements, and warehouse configuration vary by target and are not enumerated in the general quick start.

stages.action: Enter the target's required access credentials and configuration.
stages.success_signal: All target-required fields are accepted.
stages.failure_or_wait: Invalid credentials, missing target privileges, or network restrictions block use of the target.

stages.action: Enter a Connection Name that identifies the target and select Save.
stages.success_signal: Both required reusable connections are available in the workspace.
stages.failure_or_wait: Missing required target configuration or rejected naming prevents Save.

stages.action: Open Data Flows from the left menu and select + New Data Flow.
stages.success_signal: Source to Target Flow, Logic Flow, and REST Action types are offered.
stages.failure_or_wait: The flow cannot be configured without the required workspace permissions and saved connections.

stages.action: Choose Source to Target Flow.
details: This documented type extracts from a source connector and loads directly into a target warehouse or data lake.
stages.success_signal: Controls for source, target, transformations, and scheduling are available.
stages.failure_or_wait: Logic Flow and REST Action are valid peers but have different configuration journeys.

stages.action: Select and configure the saved source connection for the Data Flow.
stages.input: Saved source connection and source-specific extraction settings
stages.success_signal: The Data Flow identifies what source data to extract.
stages.failure_or_wait: Required extraction values vary by connector and must be completed before saving or running.

stages.action: Select and configure the saved target connection for the Data Flow.
stages.input: Saved target connection and destination settings
stages.success_signal: The Data Flow identifies where extracted data will be loaded.
stages.failure_or_wait: Required destination, schema, dataset, or warehouse values vary by target and must be completed.

stages.action: Configure any transformation options needed for the selected source-to-target flow and leave scheduling for after the first manual run unless required by the form.
details: The general guide groups source, target, transformation, and scheduling options in this configuration step.
Its next section runs the flow manually and schedules only after success.
stages.success_signal: All required flow fields are complete.
stages.failure_or_wait: Connector-specific schema or mapping validation can block Save; the general quick start does not enumerate those fields.

stages.action: Select Save.
stages.success_signal: The Data Flow can be opened and exposes Run.
stages.failure_or_wait: Missing required connector or flow settings must be corrected before Save succeeds.

stages.action: Open the saved Data Flow and select Run to execute it manually.
stages.success_signal: A run appears in the Activity panel with a status and logs.
stages.failure_or_wait: Authentication, connectivity, source query, target permission, mapping, or runtime failures appear in the run status or logs and require correction followed by another manual run.

stages.action: Monitor the run status and logs in the Activity panel until the run finishes.
stages.success_signal: The Activity panel reports that the data flow ran successfully.
stages.failure_or_wait: The run is an explicit wait. Any unsuccessful terminal requires using the logs to correct the configuration, connection, credentials, permissions, or network path and running again.

branches.condition: The intended product is core Boomi Integration.
branches.path: Use the Integration getting-started route, choose process library, Quick Start wizard, Boomi GPT, or manual canvas construction, then configure a basic runtime, runtime cluster, or runtime cloud before testing or deploying the process.
branches.effect_on_first_success: Replaces the entire account, connection, Data Flow, and Activity-panel route with a process-and-runtime journey.

branches.condition: The developer already has an active Data Integration account.
branches.path: Sign in or contact the account administrator, enter the workspace, and begin at Connections rather than selecting Start for Free.
branches.effect_on_first_success: Removes new-account, profile, and email-verification actions.

branches.condition: The developer uses Sign in with Google.
branches.path: Authenticate with Google, choose the operational region, complete profile and workspace setup, and skip the separate email-verification link.
branches.effect_on_first_success: Replaces password signup and removes one email handoff.

branches.condition: The source system has no listed connector.
branches.path: Choose REST API Source and build the required custom source connection before creating the Data Flow.
branches.effect_on_first_success: Adds API-specific connection configuration beyond the general quick start.

branches.condition: The first flow requires orchestration or an API action rather than direct ELT.
branches.path: Choose Logic Flow or REST Action and complete that type's SQL, Python, nested-flow, or REST configuration.
branches.effect_on_first_success: Replaces the Source to Target candidate's configuration while retaining save, run, Activity, and successful-run verification.

branches.condition: A prebuilt Kit exactly matches the use case.
branches.path: After or instead of manual construction, open Kits, preview a Kit, select Install, configure its required connections and variables, and run its included Data Flows.
branches.effect_on_first_success: Can replace manual flow construction, but the guide labels this optional and places it after the required successful run.

frictionGates.description: Boomi has peer product surfaces with materially different first journeys and no documented platform-wide default.
The Data Integration candidate is a representative selection, not an official Boomi-wide canonical route.
The candidate requires both source-system credentials and supported target-warehouse access before the first flow can be built.
On-premise or firewall-protected sources require outbound network access to Data Integration's designated IP addresses.
Password signup requires an email-verification handoff; Google sign-in skips it.
The first manual run must be monitored until the Activity panel reports a terminal status.

uncertainties.question: Which Boomi product should represent the platform in the 100-platform comparison?
uncertainties.impact: Data Integration uses a managed Data Flow and Activity-panel terminal, while core Integration uses processes, a canvas, runtime setup, test mode, and deployment.
uncertainties.reason_unresolved: Official Boomi documentation presents separate first routes and does not designate one product surface as the platform-wide default.

uncertainties.question: Which concrete source connector and target warehouse should instantiate the Data Integration candidate?
uncertainties.impact: Credential fields, network setup, extraction configuration, target permissions, schema fields, and failure recovery vary by connector pair.
uncertainties.reason_unresolved: The general quick start requires a source and target but deliberately leaves their system-specific fields to the selected connector documentation.

developerGoal: Create a Box free developer account, generate a developer token from the pre-built app, and make a first authenticated API call without installing an SDK.

entryPoint.developer_intent: Create a Box free developer account, generate a developer token from the pre-built app, and make a first authenticated API call without installing an SDK.
assumed_prior_state: The developer has arrived after discovery.
The developer does not yet have a Box Free Developer account or a developer token.
A terminal with cURL is available; no language SDK is assumed.

selectedSurface.name: Box Quick Start developer token and Get Current User via cURL
selectedSurface.selection_basis: Box Quick Start documents free developer signup, email confirmation, a pre-built application, and Generate Developer Token as the path to the first API call. The Developer Tokens guide shows the minimal authenticated call as curl GET https://api.box.com/2.0/users/me with Authorization: Bearer [DEVELOPER_TOKEN]. That cURL call is preferred over the Quick Start SDK upload-and-list tabs, which require a language SDK and a sample file.

alternatives_considered.name: Quick Start SDK upload file and list root folder
alternatives_considered.reason_not_primary: Quick Start demonstrates upload plus get_folder_items / getFolderItems via Python, Node, Swift, or Java SDKs. Those tabs add SDK install and a sample PDF; the same developer token authenticates the lighter curl /users/me call documented on the Developer Tokens page.

alternatives_considered.name: Free Developer Plan default CCG app token exchange
alternatives_considered.reason_not_primary: The Free Developer Plan still documents an automatically created and authorized CCG app, but current Quick Start centers Generate Developer Token on the pre-built application rather than a CCG client-credentials exchange.

alternatives_considered.name: Postman or Box CLI quick starts
alternatives_considered.reason_not_primary: Those are separate tooling routes after the Quick Start foundations path.

firstMeaningfulSuccess.official_milestone: Make your first API call
firstMeaningfulSuccess.normalized_outcome: After free developer signup, email confirmation, and Generate Developer Token on the pre-built application, an authenticated GET /users/me returns the logged-in console user.
firstMeaningfulSuccess.observable_completion_signal: `curl https://api.box.com/2.0/users/me -H "authorization: Bearer [DEVELOPER_TOKEN]"` returns HTTP 200 and a user JSON object (fields such as `id`, `type`, `name`, and `login`).

boundary_evidence.official_label_or_terminal_state: Quick Start frames the journey as sign up, generate the developer token, and make your first API call. The Developer Tokens guide demonstrates curl GET /users/me with the developer token in the Authorization header.

firstMeaningfulSuccess.why_this_is_the_boundary: Quick Start defines signup, activation, pre-built app, and developer token. The Developer Tokens page supplies the shortest documented authenticated HTTP success with that token. An SDK upload-and-list is optional heavier tooling for the same credential.

prerequisites.requirement: A Box Free Developer account: name, email, password, terms acceptance, and Get Started on the developer signup page.
Email confirmation to activate the developer account, as stated on Quick Start.
A developer token generated from the pre-built application in the Developer Console. Tokens expire one hour after generation and are for development or testing only.
A terminal with cURL (or any HTTP client) able to send an authenticated HTTPS request. No language SDK is required for the compact first call.

stages.action: Open Box Quick Start and start free developer signup (Get started for free).
details: Quick Start states a free developer account gives access to the Developer Console, APIs, and AI Units.
stages.input: Intent to call the Box API
stages.success_signal: The signup form accepts name, email, and password.
stages.failure_or_wait: Existing accounts use Log in instead of signup.

stages.action: Enter name and email, create a password, accept the terms, and select Get Started.
details: Free Developer Plan documents these signup fields and Get Started.
stages.success_signal: Signup completes and the developer is directed to activate or use the account.
stages.failure_or_wait: Invalid email or rejected terms block completion.

stages.action: Confirm the email address to activate the developer account.
details: Quick Start: Confirm your email address to activate your developer account.
stages.input: Confirmation message delivered to the signup email
stages.success_signal: The developer account is activated and Developer Console access is available.
stages.failure_or_wait: Delivery delay or missed confirmation leaves the account inactive per Quick Start.

stages.action: Open the Developer Console, select the pre-built application, and select Generate Developer Token under App Details / Developer Token.
details: Quick Start: a pre-built application is created for you; copy the generated token.
Developer Tokens guide: Configuration tab or My Platform Apps menu can also generate the token.
Developer tokens expire one hour after generation.
stages.success_signal: A developer token value is shown and can be copied.
stages.failure_or_wait: No manual approval wait is documented for token generation on the free pre-built app.

stages.action: Call GET https://api.box.com/2.0/users/me with Authorization: Bearer [DEVELOPER_TOKEN] via cURL.
details: Example from the Developer Tokens guide: curl https://api.box.com/2.0/users/me -H "authorization: Bearer [DEVELOPER_TOKEN]"
stages.success_signal: HTTP 200 with a user JSON object for the console user associated with the token.
stages.failure_or_wait: Expired, revoked, or missing tokens return auth errors; tokens cannot be refreshed programmatically.

stages.action: Confirm the response body identifies the authenticated user.
stages.success_signal: User fields such as id, type, name, and login are present in a successful response.
stages.failure_or_wait: An error body means the first authenticated call did not succeed.

branches.condition: The developer follows a Quick Start SDK tab instead of cURL.
branches.path: Install the language SDK, initialize BoxClient with BoxDeveloperTokenAuth, upload the sample file to parent id 0, then list root folder items.
branches.effect_on_first_success: Adds SDK install and file upload before listing; the developer token credential is the same.

frictionGates.description: Quick Start requires email confirmation before the developer account is activated.
A developer token must be generated from the pre-built app; it expires after one hour and is not for production.

uncertainties.question: Does Free Developer Plan 'activates immediately' still apply when Quick Start requires email confirmation?
uncertainties.impact: If confirmation is optional or already satisfied in some flows, step 3 may be skippable for some accounts.
uncertainties.reason_unresolved: Quick Start says confirm email to activate; Free Developer Plan says the account activates immediately. Both pages were current on 2026-07-22.

uncertainties.question: Is the automatically created free-account app labeled only as pre-built, or still described as the default CCG app in the console UI?
uncertainties.impact: Console copy may still mention CCG while Quick Start directs developers to Generate Developer Token.
uncertainties.reason_unresolved: Free Developer Plan documents automatic CCG app creation; Quick Start documents a pre-built application and developer token without requiring a CCG token exchange for first success.

Developer has an existing app or website codebase

alternatives_considered.name: REST API

developerGoal: Choose one BrowserStack product and first result; the representative route runs the Cypress Kitchen Sink sample against its public URL and inspects the cloud result.

entryPoint.developer_intent: Choose one BrowserStack product and first result; the representative route runs the Cypress Kitchen Sink sample against its public URL and inspects the cloud result.
assumed_prior_state: The developer does not have a BrowserStack account, plan, Username, or Access key.
For the representative route, Node.js, Git, the BrowserStack Cypress CLI, sample repository, and configuration are not assumed.

selectedSurface.name: Automate: run your first Cypress test (Kitchen Sink) on a public URL
selectedSurface.selection_basis: Workshop selection policy (2026-07-19): when official docs present peer routes, commit to the fastest and most commonly used documented route. Prefer the vendor's first-listed or recommended quickstart, dashboard/console over CLI when both are peers, and the route with an explicit vendor time claim or fewest documented steps when tied. Committed to reconstructed surface `Unresolved BrowserStack product, framework, language, and target, with Automate Cypress Kitchen Sink on a public URL as the representative candidate`. Prior: BrowserStack offers manual web testing, browser automation, mobile manual and automation, accessibility, low-code automation, visual testing, load testing, test management, reporting, and AI products. Automate then requires a framework and language choice. The official Run your first Cypress test guide is preserved in full because it provides a concrete sample, public test URL, CLI execution, local result files, and Automate dashboard verification. BrowserStack does not establish Cypress as the company-wide default.

alternatives_considered.name: Automate with Selenium
alternatives_considered.reason_not_primary: BrowserStack labels Selenium widely used, but the path further branches by language, framework, build tool, sample versus existing suite, and local versus public app.

alternatives_considered.name: Automate with Playwright
alternatives_considered.reason_not_primary: A peer browser-automation framework with different SDK and runner setup.

alternatives_considered.name: App Automate
alternatives_considered.reason_not_primary: Mobile automation requires Appium, Espresso, XCUITest, Flutter, Detox, Maestro, or Smart TV plus an app artifact or URL.

alternatives_considered.name: Live and App Live
alternatives_considered.reason_not_primary: Manual testing starts a browser or real-device session rather than executing an automated build.

alternatives_considered.name: Low Code Automation
alternatives_considered.reason_not_primary: This downloads a desktop recorder, records a test, saves a local replay, creates a suite, and runs in the cloud.

alternatives_considered.name: Accessibility Testing
alternatives_considered.reason_not_primary: Accessibility has separate workflow-analyzer, automation, scanner, screen-reader, and framework paths.

firstMeaningfulSuccess.official_milestone: In the selected Run your first Cypress test route, first success is demonstrated when Automate shows the completed run grouped by browser with a detailed view containing video and text logs.
firstMeaningfulSuccess.normalized_outcome: Obtain BrowserStack credentials, install the Cypress CLI, clone and configure the Kitchen Sink sample for the public app, execute it on BrowserStack, and inspect its cloud build and logs.
firstMeaningfulSuccess.observable_completion_signal: The CLI reports completed test statuses, the results folder contains HTML and JSON build summaries, and Automate groups the run by browser combination with a detailed view containing video and text logs.

boundary_evidence.official_label_or_terminal_state: Video and text logs for the executed test are accessible.

firstMeaningfulSuccess.why_this_is_the_boundary: Committed under workshop fastest/most-used policy; terminal taken from the reconstructed official path.

prerequisites.requirement: A BrowserStack Free Account or purchased plan with access to Automate. The current signup page offers Google sign-in or a full-name, business-email, and password route and requires acceptance of the Terms of Service and Privacy Policy. The signup page advertises 100 automated browser-testing minutes, while the current free-trial FAQ states 60 Automate minutes.
BrowserStack Username and Access key from account settings.
Node.js version 12 or newer with npm, plus Git and a text editor or IDE.
Network access to npm, GitHub, https://example.cypress.io, BrowserStack APIs, Automate workers, and dashboard.
Permission to install a global npm CLI, clone a repository, install project packages, and store BrowserStack credentials in the local sample configuration.

stages.action: Open BrowserStack documentation and choose the product and testing mode.

stages.success_signal: The developer names web automation as the intended mode.
stages.failure_or_wait: The roster does not identify a product.

The Cypress guide uses the Kitchen Sink example.
stages.success_signal: The guide's prerequisites, run, and result sections are selected.
stages.failure_or_wait: Another framework changes dependencies, SDK, config, and command.

stages.action: Open BrowserStack's Create FREE Account page and choose Google or the email-and-password route.

stages.failure_or_wait: Identity-provider or form-access errors stop signup.

stages.success_signal: The page accepts the required identity values.
stages.failure_or_wait: Invalid business email, password, or Google authentication prevents continuation.

stages.action: Open account settings and obtain the BrowserStack Username and Access key.
details: Both values authenticate the Cypress CLI run.
Treat the access key as a secret.
stages.output: Username and Access key available for configuration.
stages.success_signal: Both credential values can be copied.
stages.failure_or_wait: Missing Automate access or revoked credentials prevent authentication.

stages.action: Install or verify Node.js 12 or newer, npm, and Git.
details: Node.js 12+ is an explicit prerequisite.
Git is required by the documented clone command even though it is not separately listed.
stages.output: Working node, npm, and git commands.
stages.success_signal: Version checks meet the guide's minimums and commands are on PATH.
stages.failure_or_wait: Package installation or PATH issues stop setup.

stages.action: Install the BrowserStack Cypress CLI globally.
details: Run npm install -g browserstack-cypress-cli.
stages.input: Node.js, npm, network, and global install permission.
stages.success_signal: The command is available in the shell.
stages.failure_or_wait: npm registry, global prefix, permission, or PATH errors can block installation.

stages.action: Clone the Cypress Kitchen Sink repository and enter it.
details: For Cypress 10 and newer, run git clone https://github.com/cypress-io/cypress-example-kitchensink.git && cd cypress-example-kitchensink.
The Cypress 9-and-older branch uses an additional pinned commit checkout.
stages.success_signal: The repository structure includes app, cypress, and cypress.config.js.
stages.failure_or_wait: Clone, network, existing-directory, or Git errors can fail setup.

stages.action: Install the Kitchen Sink project dependencies.
details: Run npm install.
stages.output: Installed node_modules and resolved project dependencies.
stages.success_signal: npm install completes.
stages.failure_or_wait: Dependency, engine, lockfile, registry, native module, or network failures can block progress.

stages.action: Initialize BrowserStack Cypress configuration.
details: Run browserstack-cypress init.
This creates a boilerplate browserstack.json in the project.
stages.input: Installed BrowserStack Cypress CLI in the project directory.
stages.success_signal: The configuration file exists.
stages.failure_or_wait: Wrong directory, CLI, or filesystem permission can fail initialization.

stages.action: Set BrowserStack credentials in browserstack.json.
details: Under auth, set username to the account Username and access_key to the Access key.
Do not commit real credentials to source control.
stages.success_signal: auth contains usable values.
stages.failure_or_wait: Placeholder, revoked, exposed, or wrong-account credentials fail the run.

stages.action: Configure Cypress file, version, project, and build settings.
details: For current Cypress, set cypress_config_file to ./cypress.config.js; Cypress 9 and lower uses ./cypress.json.
The guide's sample uses cypress_version 13.latest, project_name Cypress Kitchen Sink Example, build_name Build no: 1, and parallels 5.
Add external npm packages under npm_dependencies only if required.
stages.output: Run settings for the sample build.
stages.success_signal: The config points to the actual Cypress config and uses a supported Cypress version.
stages.failure_or_wait: Version or file mismatch prevents the runner from loading tests.

stages.action: Configure browser and operating-system combinations.
details: The sample includes Chrome on Windows 10, Firefox on OS X Mojave, and Edge on OS X Catalina with current or prior browser versions.
Use BrowserStack's supported devices and browsers rather than assuming every example combination remains available.
stages.success_signal: At least one supported browser combination is configured.
stages.failure_or_wait: Unsupported OS/browser/version combinations fail scheduling.

stages.action: Choose public URL instead of localhost for the representative route.
details: The guide supports the public Cypress Kitchen Sink app or a locally hosted app.
The public URL is https://example.cypress.io/.
stages.input: Preference to avoid a local tunnel and app server.
stages.success_signal: The public setup instructions are selected.
stages.failure_or_wait: Local testing has additional server and connection settings.

stages.action: Open the Kitchen Sink project in an IDE.
details: Use a project-wide search and replacement in the next action.
stages.success_signal: The project root is open.
stages.failure_or_wait: Opening a subdirectory can miss spec files.

stages.action: Replace every localhost test URL with the public Cypress URL.
details: Search for http://localhost:8080.
Replace every instance with https://example.cypress.io.
Ensure all *.spec.js files are updated.
stages.success_signal: No required spec still points to localhost:8080.
stages.failure_or_wait: A missed occurrence makes that test target an unavailable local server.

stages.action: Disable BrowserStack Local for the public route.
details: Set connection_settings.local to false in browserstack.json.
stages.success_signal: The configuration explicitly has local false.
stages.failure_or_wait: Leaving local enabled can add an unnecessary tunnel dependency.

stages.action: Run the BrowserStack Cypress build.
details: Run browserstack-cypress run.
The CLI uploads configuration and tests, schedules the browser matrix, and streams completed test status.
stages.input: Configured project, credentials, supported matrix, and network.
stages.success_signal: The CLI starts and reports test execution across the configured browsers.
stages.failure_or_wait: Credential, quota, parallel limit, configuration, upload, browser availability, or test failures can affect the build.

stages.action: Verify command-line completion and local result artifacts.
details: The CLI displays completed tests and their current status while the build runs.
After execution, results contains HTML and JSON files with the build summary.
stages.success_signal: Completed tests are reported and summary files exist.
stages.failure_or_wait: A failed or interrupted run may produce incomplete results.

stages.action: Open the BrowserStack Automate dashboard.
details: Use the same account as the configured credentials.
stages.success_signal: The Cypress build appears.
stages.failure_or_wait: Wrong organization, project, or credentials can hide it.

stages.action: Inspect the build grouped by browser combination.
details: Each spec's tests are grouped by browser combination.
Status is visible for each group.
stages.success_signal: Configured browser groups and test statuses are shown.
stages.failure_or_wait: Missing groups can indicate unsupported configuration or scheduling failure.

stages.action: Open one browser combination and inspect its detailed logs.
details: The detailed view includes video and text logs.
Failed tests additionally show relevant screenshots in the Screenshots tab.
stages.success_signal: Video and text logs for the executed test are accessible.
stages.failure_or_wait: Missing evidence or no completed session means the first-test result is incomplete.

branches.condition: The developer chooses Continue with Google rather than the direct form.
branches.path: Authenticate with an eligible Google identity, then complete any BrowserStack profile prompts and accept the linked Terms of Service and Privacy Policy.
branches.effect_on_first_success: Replaces manual full-name, business-email, and password entry with an external identity-provider handoff.

branches.condition: The sample must use Cypress 9 or older.
branches.path: Clone the same repository, enter it, check out commit ab10094ef7b199ae7febafec413a0626414bcd3c, install packages, set cypress_config_file to ./cypress.json, and use the corresponding integration-directory layout.
branches.effect_on_first_success: Pins older sample code and changes configuration and test-directory structure.

branches.condition: The developer tests the locally hosted Kitchen Sink app.
branches.path: Keep the http://localhost:8080 test URLs, set connection_settings.local true and local_identifier My_Sample_Tutorial, run npm start, verify the app at http://localhost:8080, then run browserstack-cypress run with Local connectivity.
branches.effect_on_first_success: Adds a local app server, verification, BrowserStack Local tunnel behavior, and related network/firewall gates.

branches.condition: The developer selects Selenium, Playwright, Puppeteer, JS Testing API, Appium, Espresso, XCUITest, Flutter, Detox, Maestro, or another framework.
branches.path: Use the framework and language selector, then follow its official sample-build guide through account credentials, SDK or dependencies, sample repository, capabilities, run command, and dashboard verification.
branches.effect_on_first_success: Changes the code, runtime, framework, device/browser matrix, and result grouping.

branches.condition: The intended product is Live, App Live, Low Code Automation, accessibility, Percy, load testing, test management, or another BrowserStack product.
branches.path: Use that product's official get-started flow and terminal session, build, scan, report, or test result instead of Automate Cypress.
branches.effect_on_first_success: Replaces the entire setup and observable result.

frictionGates.description: BrowserStack has many products with unrelated first results.
Automate requires a framework, language, and sample-versus-existing-suite choice.
The guide requires a Free Account or purchased plan with Automate access, and signup requires Google identity or full name, business email, and password.
Account creation requires acceptance of BrowserStack's Terms of Service and Privacy Policy.
Two current official pages disagree on the free automated-browser allowance: the signup page says 100 minutes, while the FAQ says 60 Automate minutes.
The run requires BrowserStack Username and Access key.
The selected route requires Node.js 12+, npm, Git, the global BrowserStack Cypress CLI, and project dependencies.
The sample config stores account credentials in browserstack.json, creating a secret-handling risk.
The developer must choose supported browser, version, operating-system, and parallel combinations.
Public versus localhost changes URL editing, tunnel, and app-server work.
The remote browser matrix must schedule and complete before detailed results exist.

uncertainties.question: Which BrowserStack product and test result should represent the platform?
uncertainties.impact: A manual session, automated browser build, mobile app session, accessibility scan, visual diff, low-code recording, load test, or report has different prerequisites and success.
uncertainties.reason_unresolved: The company and docs expose many products, and the roster supplies no test intent.

uncertainties.question: Which Automate framework, language, browser matrix, and application target applies?
uncertainties.impact: These choices change dependencies, credentials placement, sample code, commands, scheduling, and result evidence.
uncertainties.reason_unresolved: The official overview requires framework and language selection; Cypress and public Kitchen Sink are representative only.

uncertainties.question: Which official free automated-browser allowance applies to a newly created account?
uncertainties.impact: A 60-minute rather than 100-minute allowance changes how much of a sample matrix can complete before quota exhaustion.
uncertainties.reason_unresolved: The current signup page advertises 100 automated browser-testing minutes, while the current free-trial FAQ lists 60 Automate minutes.

uncertainties.question: Does the live signup flow add an email verification, organization-creation, or entitlement screen after submission?
uncertainties.impact: Any post-submit gate would add another access or wait step before BrowserStack credentials are available.
uncertainties.reason_unresolved: The public signup page exposes its current identity fields and policy acceptance, but the authenticated post-submit state cannot be observed without creating an account.

uncertainties.question: Are all operating-system examples in the current sample browserstack.json still schedulable for the account?
uncertainties.impact: An obsolete or plan-restricted matrix can fail before a first result.
uncertainties.reason_unresolved: The guide tells the developer to consult supported browsers and devices rather than guaranteeing every sample combination for every account.

Running tests in parallel beyond the sample configuration and optimizing concurrency.
Testing a private or local application when the public branch is selected.
Advanced capabilities, geolocation, network conditions, browsers and devices, CI integration, debugging, analytics, AI agents, and production secret management.

entryPoint.developer_intent: The Buildkite Starter example pipeline runs and its build completes successfully.
assumed_prior_state: The developer has no prior Buildkite setup for this journey.
The journey starts at account creation and stops at the first documented successful outcome.

selectedSurface.name: Getting started with Pipelines stage 1: Starter example build
selectedSurface.selection_basis: The official documentation presents this as the hosted path from account setup to Create your first pipeline. It reaches the requested first-success boundary without extending into post-success production work.

prerequisites.requirement: A Buildkite account and organization.
A connected GitHub account or the repository access requested by the Starter flow.

stages.action: Create or sign in to Buildkite and create or select an organization.
stages.input: A new developer starting at account creation
stages.output: The Buildkite organization dashboard is accessible.
stages.success_signal: The Buildkite organization dashboard is accessible.
stages.failure_or_wait: The path cannot continue until this documented requirement succeeds.

stages.action: Start Pipelines getting started stage 1 and select the Buildkite Starter example.
stages.output: A Starter example pipeline is created.
stages.success_signal: A Starter example pipeline is created.
stages.failure_or_wait: The path cannot continue until this documented requirement succeeds.

stages.action: Run the example build as directed by the getting-started flow.
stages.output: A build is queued and runs.
stages.success_signal: A build is queued and runs.
stages.failure_or_wait: The path cannot continue until this documented requirement succeeds.

stages.action: Open the completed build and inspect its jobs and log output.
