developerGoal: Reach a first Clerk authentication result without a selected application stack, SDK, hosted or embedded UI model, or authentication method.

entryPoint.developer_intent: Reach a first Clerk authentication result without a selected application stack, SDK, hosted or embedded UI model, or authentication method.
assumed_prior_state: The developer has arrived after discovery.
The application stack, existing versus new project, Account Portal versus embedded components versus custom flow, and authentication identifiers are not selected.

selectedSurface.name: Use the Clerk CLI to scaffold Next.js and create the first signed-in user
selectedSurface.selection_basis: Workshop selection policy (2026-07-19): when official docs present peer routes, commit to the fastest and most commonly used documented route. Prefer the vendor's first-listed or recommended quickstart, dashboard/console over CLI when both are peers, and the route with an explicit vendor time claim or fewest documented steps when tied. Selected route: Use the Clerk CLI to scaffold Next.js and create the first signed-in user. The platform router has no universal SDK default, but Clerk's current official Next.js AI prompt defines a CLI-first sequence. This candidate selects its fresh empty-directory npm branch, authenticated Clerk account path, visible prebuilt controls, doctor check, and first-user profile-icon terminal. Prior surface before policy commit: Clerk SDK, application stack, and integration model not selected.

alternatives_considered.name: Next.js App Router quickstart
alternatives_considered.reason_not_primary: This complete candidate is explicitly default only after Next.js and its App Router have been selected.

alternatives_considered.name: Account Portal
alternatives_considered.reason_not_primary: Clerk calls this the default implementation model, but it is a hosted user-management interface and does not define the same SDK integration result as the stack quickstarts.

alternatives_considered.name: Clerk CLI automatic framework integration
alternatives_considered.reason_not_primary: The CLI can auto-detect supported frameworks and use temporary keys, but the docs note incomplete scaffolding support for some SDKs and still depend on the target repository.

firstMeaningfulSuccess.official_milestone: No single Clerk first-user path is selected by the Quickstarts router.
firstMeaningfulSuccess.normalized_outcome: The profile icon is visible in place of Sign in and Sign up.
firstMeaningfulSuccess.observable_completion_signal: The profile icon is visible in place of Sign in and Sign up.

boundary_evidence.official_label_or_terminal_state: The profile icon is visible in place of Sign in and Sign up.

firstMeaningfulSuccess.why_this_is_the_boundary: Selected under workshop fastest/most-used policy; terminal taken from the committed official candidate route.

prerequisites.requirement: Select application stack, SDK, new or existing project, and Account Portal, prebuilt component, or custom-flow integration model.
The Dashboard route requires a Clerk account and application; CLI temporary development keys are a documented alternative for supported project types.
Choose identifiers and social providers for the development application's sign-up and sign-in flow.
The selected SDK guide determines runtime, package manager, framework project, browser, emulator, device, or extension tooling.

stages.action: Choose the application stack and Clerk implementation model, then select its official quickstart or Account Portal guide.
details: The Quickstarts router is grouped into full-stack, frontend, and backend SDKs and includes native mobile and browser extension routes.
Core concepts calls Account Portal the default, prebuilt components recommended for most use cases, and custom API flows the higher-control advanced route.
stages.input: Existing application stack, runtime target, UI control needs, repository state, and authentication requirements.
stages.success_signal: No shared code or authentication page sequence exists at this router.
stages.failure_or_wait: The path cannot be atomic until SDK and implementation model are selected.

stages.action: Follow the selected route through account or temporary-key setup, application and authentication configuration, SDK installation, credentials, framework integration, runtime launch, and first-user verification.
details: The complete authenticated CLI-first Next.js App Router candidate is preserved below.
Other SDKs have different project scaffolds, environment variables, native or browser permissions, middleware, components, and test interfaces.
stages.input: Selected Clerk route, application project, authentication choices, and required local toolchain.
stages.output: Route-specific Clerk authentication UI or API result and, where documented, a first authenticated user.
stages.success_signal: The selected guide's documented sign-up, sign-in, session, or SDK result is visible in its runtime and Clerk development application.
stages.failure_or_wait: Account creation, unclaimed temporary keys, invalid credentials, framework version, middleware placement, callback URL, identity verification, provider configuration, native API, build, or browser policy can block different routes.

stages.action: Open an empty writable project directory and select npm as the package manager for the fresh Next.js route.
details: The current Next.js CLI prompt says an empty directory must choose a package manager; npm is the default when there is no preference.
stages.input: Empty directory and local npm environment.
stages.success_signal: The shell is in the intended empty directory and npm is available.
stages.failure_or_wait: A nonempty directory follows the existing-project `clerk init` branch; a leftover lockfile or package-manager configuration changes the package-manager choice.

stages.action: Run `command -v clerk && clerk --version || echo "Clerk CLI not installed"`.
stages.output: Installed Clerk CLI path and version, or the documented not-installed message.
stages.success_signal: The CLI state is known before setup continues.
stages.failure_or_wait: A shell that cannot run the check must be corrected before the install or update decision.

stages.action: If the CLI is missing, run `npm install -g clerk`; if it is already available, run `clerk update --yes`.
details: pnpm, yarn, bun, Homebrew, and curl are official install alternatives, but this candidate selects npm.
stages.input: The CLI state from the preceding check.
stages.success_signal: `clerk` is available at the current version.
stages.failure_or_wait: Package download, permissions, network, or updater errors must be corrected before authentication.

stages.action: Run `clerk auth login` from the project directory.
details: The current Next.js CLI prompt requires this immediately after install or update and before app listing or initialization.
stages.input: Current Clerk CLI and browser-capable environment.
stages.success_signal: The CLI opens or provides the browser login flow.
stages.failure_or_wait: Browser launch, callback, network, or CLI session errors block the authenticated route.

stages.action: Complete the Clerk login flow in the browser and return control to the CLI.
details: If the developer lacks a Clerk identity, account creation is an access gate, but the CLI-first prompt does not enumerate its current form fields.
No completion duration is documented.
stages.input: Clerk identity and the browser authentication flow.
stages.success_signal: The CLI confirms authentication and can proceed to initialization.
stages.failure_or_wait: Uncompleted account creation, login, browser callback, or identity recovery leaves the CLI unauthenticated.

stages.action: Run `clerk init --framework next --pm npm` in the empty directory.
details: Do not add `--app` by default and do not list applications before init. Only use `--app <application_id>` when the developer already supplied an ID or explicitly chose an existing application.
stages.input: Empty directory, npm, and authenticated Clerk CLI session.
stages.success_signal: The CLI begins scaffolding the fresh Next.js project and Clerk integration.
stages.failure_or_wait: Project-generation, framework, package-manager, application-linking, network, or filesystem errors can stop initialization.

stages.action: Let `clerk init` scaffold Next.js, install `@clerk/nextjs`, link the project to a Clerk application, pull environment variables, and apply the Next.js provider, middleware, and authentication setup.
details: If init explicitly asks for an existing application but no ID was supplied, run `clerk apps list --json`, show the names and IDs, and let the developer choose.
For Next.js 16 or later the middleware file is `proxy.ts`; for Next.js 15 or earlier it is `middleware.ts`.
stages.input: Authenticated init session and any prompt responses requested by the CLI.
stages.success_signal: Initialization completes and reports that Clerk is set up in the project.
stages.failure_or_wait: If init errors or does not finish, the documented fallback is `npm install @clerk/nextjs`, a correctly named file exporting `clerkMiddleware()`, and `ClerkProvider` using the full Next.js quickstart.

stages.action: Ensure the layout visibly renders `SignInButton` and `SignUpButton` inside `Show when="signed-out"`, and `UserButton` inside `Show when="signed-in"`, all within `ClerkProvider` inside the body.
details: Reuse generated controls when they are already present instead of duplicating them.
Never expose `CLERK_SECRET_KEY` in client code.
stages.output: Visible signed-out and signed-in authentication controls.
stages.success_signal: The project contains the provider and the three state-appropriate controls without duplicate UI.
stages.failure_or_wait: Wrong imports, provider placement, missing controls, or exposed secret configuration must be corrected before runtime testing.

stages.action: Run `clerk doctor`.
stages.success_signal: Doctor reports no unresolved integration issue.
stages.failure_or_wait: Fix every issue reported by the CLI, then rerun doctor before starting the app.

stages.action: Run `npm run dev` from the generated Next.js project.
stages.success_signal: The app starts without build, environment, or runtime errors.
stages.failure_or_wait: Compile, dependency, environment, port, or runtime errors must be corrected and the server restarted.

stages.action: Open `http://localhost:3000` and confirm that the signed-out sign-in and sign-up controls are visible.
stages.input: Running local Next.js app without a user session.
stages.success_signal: Sign in and Sign up are visible before authentication.
stages.failure_or_wait: Missing controls indicate an init, environment, provider, component, middleware, or runtime problem that must be fixed before signup.

stages.action: Select Sign up and complete the Clerk-hosted authentication flow to create the first test user.
details: The exact identifier, verification, or social-provider screens depend on the Clerk development application's configuration and are not fixed by the CLI-first prompt.
stages.input: Test-user identity and access to the configured verification method.
stages.output: First Clerk user and active application session.
stages.success_signal: Signup succeeds and the browser returns to the app in the signed-in state.
stages.failure_or_wait: Identity verification, provider, callback, browser, session, or application-configuration failure blocks first-user creation; no universal wait or recovery transcript is documented.

stages.action: Verify that the signed-out controls are replaced by the profile icon rendered by `UserButton`.
details: The current CLI-first prompt names successful signup plus the visible profile icon as its completion signal.
stages.input: Completed signup and active Clerk session.
stages.output: Verified first signed-in Clerk user in the Next.js application.
stages.success_signal: The profile icon is visible in place of Sign in and Sign up.
stages.failure_or_wait: Signup without the signed-in profile icon does not establish the documented terminal; inspect the session, keys, provider, middleware, callback, and browser state.

branches.condition: Next.js App Router with embedded prebuilt components is selected.
branches.path: Create or enter the Next.js app using the documented npm, pnpm, yarn, or bun command; install `@clerk/nextjs`; create or sign into Clerk and create the development application, choosing its identifiers and social providers; copy the Publishable Key and Secret Key from Dashboard Quick Copy into `.env`; create `proxy.ts` in the root or `/src` location and export `clerkMiddleware()` with the supplied matcher, using `middleware.ts` for Next.js 15 or earlier; wrap the app layout with `ClerkProvider` and add signed-out SignIn and SignUp controls plus the signed-in UserButton; run the selected package manager's development command; open `http://localhost:3000`; select Sign up and complete the configured authentication flow; verify that the first user exists and the UI shows the signed-in UserButton.
branches.effect_on_first_success: The local Next.js application completes a real Clerk development sign-up and creates its first user.

branches.condition: Another web, mobile, extension, or backend SDK quickstart is selected.
branches.path: Use that guide's project generator or existing project, package manager and SDK, publishable or secret key placement, provider or middleware, hosted or embedded components, callback and route configuration, runtime launch, and documented sign-up, sign-in, or API verification. Native mobile and extensions can add Native API, development-build, OAuth, extension ID, manifest, permission, and emulator or device steps.
branches.effect_on_first_success: The first authenticated result remains stack-specific and may not use the Next.js URL or components.

branches.condition: The default hosted Account Portal is selected without embedding SDK UI.
branches.path: Create the Clerk account and application, select identifiers and social providers, use the application's hosted Account Portal sign-up or sign-in pages, complete the selected identity verification, and confirm the user and session in the hosted interface and Dashboard.
branches.effect_on_first_success: The first user can be created without application SDK integration, so this is a different boundary from a working in-app component.

branches.condition: The general Clerk CLI no-account temporary-development-key route is selected instead of the authenticated Next.js CLI prompt.
branches.path: Run `clerk init` without a Clerk account in a supported project, or use `clerk init --starter` to bootstrap from a starter template; let the CLI generate temporary development keys, detect the framework, install the appropriate SDK, and scaffold authentication; start the app with the generated instructions; complete first-user signup; if Configure your application appears, select it to claim the generated keys and associate the application with a Clerk account.
branches.effect_on_first_success: Defers the account and browser-login gate until key claiming, but creates an unclaimed temporary development instance rather than the authenticated account-linked candidate.

frictionGates.description: Application stack, SDK, implementation model, project state, and authentication identifiers must be selected.
Dashboard setup creates a Clerk account and development application; temporary CLI keys can defer claiming for supported projects.
Email, phone, username, social providers, middleware, routes, provider components, and key placement depend on the chosen route.
SDK routes use publishable and sometimes secret keys; key names and exposure rules differ between frontend and backend contexts.
Each quickstart adds a stack-specific package, toolchain, and application scaffold or existing-project requirements.
Package installation, build or dev-server startup, and email, phone, or social-provider authentication can introduce waits.
The first-user candidate requires completing the configured authentication flow and observing the signed-in state, not only rendering Sign up.

uncertainties.question: Which Clerk SDK, application stack, and Account Portal, prebuilt component, custom API, or CLI route should define first success?
uncertainties.impact: The choice changes account timing, project scaffold, dependencies, credentials, files, runtime, authentication pages, and verification.
uncertainties.reason_unresolved: The official quickstart router presents multiple stacks and the core concepts page presents multiple implementation models without a universal platform route.

uncertainties.question: Which verification pages and waits will the first user's chosen identity method require?
uncertainties.impact: Email, phone, username, social OAuth, and multifactor configuration add different screens, callbacks, and waits.
uncertainties.reason_unresolved: The account setup guide requires identifier and provider selection but cannot prescribe one universal authentication transcript.

uncertainties.question: Should CLI onboarding follow the authenticated Next.js prompt or the general CLI's no-account temporary-development-key route?
uncertainties.impact: The choice moves the Clerk account and browser-login gate before initialization or defers it until temporary keys are claimed.
uncertainties.reason_unresolved: The current Next.js CLI-first prompt requires `clerk auth login` before `clerk init`, while the general CLI guide separately permits `clerk init` without an account using temporary development keys.

sources.title: Set up your Clerk account
evidence_supported: Routing to one of multiple quickstarts

sources.title: AI prompts, Next.js App Router
sections_used: Step 1: Install or update the Clerk CLI
Step 4: Fall back to manual setup when init is incomplete
Step 5: Ensure clear auth controls are visible
evidence_supported: Exact CLI check, update, install, login, and init sequence

excludedAfterSuccess: Production domains, key rotation, security hardening, and deployment
doesNotProve: Platform records reconstruct official documentation. They do not contain product telemetry, conversion rates, observed completion times, or proven causes of abandonment.
Friction gates are documented requirements or transitions. They are not confirmed drop-off points.
Blocker taxonomy entries are candidate hypotheses. Individual reasons remain not diagnosis eligible unless separate case evidence supports them.
A documented journey analysis is not a diagnosis of a real developer attempt.

developerGoal: A ClickHouse Cloud service accepts SHOW databases in its hosted SQL console and returns database rows.

entryPoint.developer_intent: A ClickHouse Cloud service accepts SHOW databases in its hosted SQL console and returns database rows.
assumed_prior_state: The developer has no prior ClickHouse setup for this journey.
The journey starts at account creation and stops at the first documented successful outcome.

selectedSurface.name: ClickHouse Cloud quick start: service + SQL console SHOW databases
selectedSurface.selection_basis: The official documentation presents this as the hosted path from account setup to Run a query in the SQL console. It reaches the requested first-success boundary without extending into post-success production work.

firstMeaningfulSuccess.official_milestone: Connect using SQL console and run SHOW databases
firstMeaningfulSuccess.normalized_outcome: A ClickHouse Cloud service accepts SHOW databases in its hosted SQL console and returns database rows.
firstMeaningfulSuccess.observable_completion_signal: The SQL console displays the result set for SHOW databases.

boundary_evidence.official_label_or_terminal_state: The official documentation leads the developer to: Run a query in the SQL console.

firstMeaningfulSuccess.why_this_is_the_boundary: This is the first observable happy-path result on the selected ClickHouse Cloud quick start: service + SQL console SHOW databases surface; later configuration and production hardening occur after success.

prerequisites.requirement: A ClickHouse Cloud account.
A ClickHouse Cloud service created through the Cloud quick start.

stages.action: Create or sign in to ClickHouse Cloud.
stages.input: A new developer starting at account creation
stages.output: The ClickHouse Cloud console is accessible.
stages.success_signal: The ClickHouse Cloud console is accessible.
stages.failure_or_wait: The path cannot continue until this documented requirement succeeds.

stages.action: Create a ClickHouse Cloud service and wait until it is ready.
stages.output: The service reports a ready/running state.
stages.success_signal: The service reports a ready/running state.
stages.failure_or_wait: The path cannot continue until this documented requirement succeeds.

stages.action: Open the service SQL console and run SHOW databases.
stages.success_signal: The query executes successfully.
stages.failure_or_wait: The path cannot continue until this documented requirement succeeds.

stages.action: Confirm that the SQL console displays database names.
stages.output: A result set containing databases is visible.
stages.success_signal: A result set containing databases is visible.
stages.failure_or_wait: The path cannot continue until this documented requirement succeeds.

frictionGates.description: Service creation has a platform-managed provisioning wait before the SQL console is usable.

uncertainties.question: Does the current fresh-account UI exactly match every navigation label in the documented Run a query in the SQL console path?
uncertainties.impact: Navigation wording may differ while the required outcome and boundary remain the same.
uncertainties.reason_unresolved: The official documentation establishes the journey but account-specific UI state can vary.

sources.title: Run a query in the SQL console
sections_used: Run a query in the SQL console
evidence_supported: A ClickHouse Cloud service accepts SHOW databases in its hosted SQL console and returns database rows.
The SQL console displays the result set for SHOW databases.

doesNotProve: Platform records reconstruct official documentation. They do not contain product telemetry, conversion rates, observed completion times, or proven causes of abandonment.
Friction gates are documented requirements or transitions. They are not confirmed drop-off points.
Blocker taxonomy entries are candidate hypotheses. Individual reasons remain not diagnosis eligible unless separate case evidence supports them.
A documented journey analysis is not a diagnosis of a real developer attempt.

developerGoal: Build the first Cloudflare Developer Platform application after selecting Cloudflare but before selecting an onboarding interface.

entryPoint.developer_intent: Build the first Cloudflare Developer Platform application after selecting Cloudflare but before selecting an onboarding interface.
assumed_prior_state: Discovery is complete and the developer has selected Cloudflare.
No Workers onboarding interface has been selected.

selectedSurface.name: Workers CLI edited Hello Worker deployment
selectedSurface.selection_basis: Workshop selection policy (2026-07-19): when official docs present peer routes, commit to the fastest and most commonly used documented route. Prefer the vendor's first-listed or recommended quickstart, dashboard/console over CLI when both are peers, and the route with an explicit vendor time claim or fewest documented steps when tied. Selected route: Workers CLI edited Hello Worker deployment. The CLI guide is the official end-to-end code-first route that creates, runs, edits, deploys, and verifies a first Worker. It is a complete candidate, not a platform-wide default over Dashboard, Prompting, Templates, or Playground. Prior surface before policy commit: Cloudflare Workers getting-started router.

alternatives_considered.name: Workers CLI
alternatives_considered.reason_not_primary: It is one of four parallel getting-started routes and requires a Cloudflare account and Node.js.

alternatives_considered.name: Workers Dashboard
alternatives_considered.reason_not_primary: It is a parallel route through a Git provider and deployment from the dashboard.

alternatives_considered.name: Workers Prompting
alternatives_considered.reason_not_primary: It is a parallel agent-assisted route whose path and result depend on an AI tool and the developer's prompt.

alternatives_considered.name: Workers Playground
alternatives_considered.reason_not_primary: Cloudflare calls it the quickest experiment, but it is an instant preview without the account, project, and deployment journey used by the CLI and Dashboard guides.

alternatives_considered.name: Workers Templates
alternatives_considered.reason_not_primary: It requires choosing among many application templates and does not define one universal Worker outcome.

firstMeaningfulSuccess.official_milestone: No single first-Worker terminal path is selected by the official Workers getting-started router.
firstMeaningfulSuccess.normalized_outcome: The public workers.dev endpoint returns Hello Worker!.
firstMeaningfulSuccess.observable_completion_signal: The public workers.dev endpoint returns Hello Worker!.

boundary_evidence.official_label_or_terminal_state: The public workers.dev endpoint returns Hello Worker!.

firstMeaningfulSuccess.why_this_is_the_boundary: Selected under workshop fastest/most-used policy; terminal taken from the committed official candidate route.

prerequisites.requirement: Select a Workers onboarding route: CLI, Dashboard, Prompting, Templates, or the separate Playground experiment.

A Cloudflare account is required for the CLI and Dashboard deployment routes but not for a Playground preview; Playground deployment routes an unauthenticated developer through first-time user onboarding.
Node.js 16.17.0 or later is required for the CLI route.
A supported Git provider and a template, owned repository, or public repository choice are part of the Dashboard deployment route.

Cloudflare automatically sends an email-verification message after new account creation; verification requires opening the email link and logging in, with resend and alternate-browser retries documented.
Workers Free is the default account plan. Workers Paid is a separate deliberate purchase with a minimum $5 monthly account charge and is not required for the documented first Worker.

stages.action: Open the Cloudflare Developer Platform learning path and follow its Workers path.
details: Cloudflare states that its Developer Platform builds full-stack applications with Workers.
stages.success_signal: The Build applications with Cloudflare Workers route is identified.
stages.failure_or_wait: No wait is documented.

stages.action: Open Workers Getting started, which asks the developer to build the first Worker.
details: The page lists CLI, Dashboard, Prompting, and Templates.
stages.success_signal: The interface choices are visible.
stages.failure_or_wait: The page does not name one choice as the default.

stages.action: Select the onboarding interface whose path will define first success.
details: CLI uses C3, Wrangler, local preview, a code edit, and Wrangler deployment.
Dashboard uses a template or Git repository, Git-provider connection, dashboard configuration, build, deployment, and preview.
Prompting uses an AI tool or editor, optionally connects Cloudflare documentation MCP servers, and requires choosing or writing a prompt.
Templates requires choosing among many GitHub starter repositories and an npm, yarn, pnpm, or deploy-button route.
Playground is a no-setup browser experiment with optional raw HTTP testing, logs, sharing, and deployment.
stages.input: Desired interface and durability of the first result
stages.success_signal: A human has selected one official route.
stages.failure_or_wait: Without this choice, prerequisites and atomic steps conflict.

stages.action: Open Cloudflare's signup page.
stages.success_signal: Email and password fields are visible.
stages.failure_or_wait: An existing account can sign in instead and continue at Node.js setup.

stages.action: Enter an email address and password, then select Create Account.
details: Workers Free is the default; Workers Paid is a separate purchase and is not required.
stages.success_signal: Cloudflare creates the account and automatically sends a verification email.
stages.failure_or_wait: Form or identity validation must be corrected before account creation succeeds.

stages.action: Wait for Cloudflare's automatic verification email.
stages.success_signal: The recent Cloudflare message appears in the inbox or Spam folder.
stages.failure_or_wait: If missing, sign in, open My Profile, and select Send verification email.

stages.action: Open the verification link and log in to Cloudflare to verify the address.
stages.success_signal: My Profile shows the verified label.
stages.failure_or_wait: If the link is expired or fails, resend it and retry in another browser or private window.

stages.action: Install Node.js 16.17.0 or later.
details: Cloudflare recommends a version manager such as Volta or nvm to avoid permissions problems and switch versions.
stages.success_signal: The installed Node.js version is 16.17.0 or later.
stages.failure_or_wait: An older or unavailable Node.js runtime blocks C3 and Wrangler.

stages.action: Run npm create cloudflare@latest -- my-first-worker.
details: The guide also displays equivalent yarn and pnpm commands; npm is selected for this candidate.
stages.success_signal: C3 displays the project prompts.
stages.failure_or_wait: Package download, npm, permissions, or network errors must be corrected and the command rerun.

stages.action: Choose Hello World example.
stages.success_signal: C3 advances to application type.
stages.failure_or_wait: Selecting another example changes generated code and the verification text.

stages.action: Choose Worker only.
stages.success_signal: C3 advances to language selection.
stages.failure_or_wait: A different application type changes files and deployment behavior.

stages.action: Choose JavaScript.
stages.success_signal: C3 advances to Git setup.
stages.failure_or_wait: TypeScript changes source files and code.

stages.action: Choose Yes for Git version control and No for immediate deployment.
details: The guide delays deployment so the Worker can be edited first.
stages.success_signal: C3 finishes and reports project files including wrangler.jsonc, src/index.js, package.json, lockfile, and node_modules.
stages.failure_or_wait: Choosing immediate deployment changes the order and skips the documented edit-before-deploy route.

stages.action: Run cd my-first-worker.
stages.success_signal: wrangler.jsonc and src/index.js are present.
stages.failure_or_wait: A different project name requires the matching directory.

stages.action: Run npx wrangler dev.
stages.success_signal: Wrangler starts the server for http://localhost:8787.
stages.failure_or_wait: If this is the first Wrangler use, browser authentication must complete; dependency, port, or configuration errors must be corrected before the server stays running.

stages.action: If Wrangler opens a browser login, authenticate with the verified Cloudflare account and authorize Wrangler.
stages.success_signal: wrangler dev continues and serves the local Worker.
stages.failure_or_wait: If browser login is unavailable or fails, follow wrangler login documentation and retry wrangler dev.

stages.action: Open http://localhost:8787 and verify the generated response is Hello World!.
stages.success_signal: The browser displays Hello World!.
stages.failure_or_wait: A missing response requires checking Wrangler output, the local port, and generated source.

stages.action: Edit src/index.js so the fetch handler returns Hello Worker!, then save the file.
stages.success_signal: src/index.js contains the new response text.
stages.failure_or_wait: Editing a different file or not saving leaves the preview unchanged.

stages.action: Reload http://localhost:8787 and verify the response changes to Hello Worker!.
stages.input: Saved source and running wrangler dev
stages.success_signal: The browser displays Hello Worker!.
stages.failure_or_wait: Confirm the file was saved, wrangler dev is running, and the page was reloaded, then retry.

stages.action: Run npx wrangler deploy.
stages.success_signal: Wrangler reports a deployed workers.dev URL.
stages.failure_or_wait: If no workers.dev subdomain or custom domain is configured, answer Wrangler's publish-time prompt to create one; deployment errors must be corrected and retried.

stages.action: Open the reported <YOUR_WORKER>.<YOUR_SUBDOMAIN>.workers.dev URL and verify Hello Worker!.
stages.success_signal: The public workers.dev endpoint returns Hello Worker!.
stages.failure_or_wait: The first workers.dev push can return 523; wait about a minute and reload before diagnosing another cause.

branches.condition: CLI is selected, action 1 of 13.
branches.path: Create or sign in to the required Cloudflare account. New-account setup follows the separate account and email-verification branch in this record.
branches.effect_on_first_success: Adds the authenticated account gate before local Worker creation.

branches.condition: CLI is selected, action 2 of 13.
branches.path: Install Node.js. Wrangler requires Node.js 16.17.0 or later; Cloudflare recommends a version manager such as Volta or nvm to avoid permission problems and switch versions.
branches.effect_on_first_success: Adds a local runtime installation and version gate.

branches.condition: CLI is selected, action 3 of 13.
branches.path: Choose npm, yarn, or pnpm and run its displayed C3 command to create my-first-worker: npm create cloudflare@latest -- my-first-worker, yarn create cloudflare my-first-worker, or pnpm create cloudflare@latest my-first-worker.
branches.effect_on_first_success: Adds a package-manager choice while preserving the same C3 project target.

branches.condition: CLI is selected, action 4 of 13.
branches.path: Answer the five C3 prompts in order: choose Hello World example; choose Worker only; choose JavaScript; choose Yes for Git version control; choose No for immediate deployment because the guide edits the Worker first.
branches.effect_on_first_success: Makes template, application shape, language, Git, and deployment timing explicit and reproducible.

branches.condition: CLI is selected, action 5 of 13.
branches.path: Change into the generated project with cd my-first-worker and verify the C3 output includes wrangler.jsonc, src/index.js, package.json, the package lock, and node_modules.
branches.effect_on_first_success: Moves execution into the generated project and provides a local scaffold check.

branches.condition: CLI is selected, action 6 of 13.
branches.path: Run npx wrangler dev from the project directory to start the local development server.
branches.effect_on_first_success: Starts the local Worker runtime before any hosted deployment.

branches.condition: CLI is selected, action 7 of 13.
branches.path: If Wrangler has not been used before, allow it to open a browser and log in to the Cloudflare account. If browser login is unavailable or fails, follow the separate wrangler login documentation before retrying wrangler dev.
branches.effect_on_first_success: Adds first-use browser authentication and a documented non-browser troubleshooting branch.

branches.condition: CLI is selected, action 8 of 13.
branches.path: Open http://localhost:8787 and verify the generated Worker returns Hello World!.
branches.effect_on_first_success: Provides the first local execution signal, before the guide's required edit and hosted success.

branches.condition: CLI is selected, action 9 of 13.
branches.path: Open src/index.js and replace its response text so the fetch handler returns Hello Worker! instead of Hello World!.
branches.effect_on_first_success: Demonstrates that the generated Worker code is editable.

branches.condition: CLI is selected, action 10 of 13.
branches.path: Save src/index.js and reload the localhost page; verify the output changes to Hello Worker!. If it does not, confirm the file was saved, wrangler dev is still running, and the browser was reloaded, then retry.
branches.effect_on_first_success: Provides the documented edit-save-reload verification and its exact retry checks.

branches.condition: CLI is selected, action 11 of 13.
branches.path: Run npx wrangler deploy to deploy the edited Worker.
branches.effect_on_first_success: Moves the verified local Worker to Cloudflare's hosted runtime.

branches.condition: CLI is selected, action 12 of 13.
branches.path: If no workers.dev subdomain or custom domain is configured, answer Wrangler's publish-time prompt to set one up.
branches.effect_on_first_success: Adds first-deployment hostname configuration only when the account lacks one.

branches.condition: CLI is selected, action 13 of 13.
branches.path: Open <YOUR_WORKER>.<YOUR_SUBDOMAIN>.workers.dev and verify the deployed Hello Worker! response. If the first workers.dev push returns 523, wait a minute or so for it to resolve, then reload the same URL before diagnosing another cause.
branches.effect_on_first_success: Produces the CLI route's hosted terminal signal and preserves the ordered first-subdomain propagation wait.

branches.condition: Dashboard is selected, action 1 of 8.
branches.path: Create or sign in to the required Cloudflare account. New-account setup follows the separate account and email-verification branch in this record.
branches.effect_on_first_success: Adds the authenticated Dashboard gate.

branches.condition: Dashboard is selected, action 2 of 8.
branches.path: In the Cloudflare dashboard, open Workers & Pages.
branches.effect_on_first_success: Enters the Dashboard surface that creates Workers applications.

branches.condition: Dashboard is selected, action 3 of 8.
branches.path: Select Create application.
branches.effect_on_first_success: Opens the three documented application-source choices.

branches.condition: Dashboard is selected, action 4 of 8.
branches.path: Choose exactly one source: a production-ready gallery template, an existing Git repository in the developer's account, or a public Workers repository that Cloudflare will clone and bootstrap.
branches.effect_on_first_success: Changes repository ownership and initial code while preserving the Dashboard deployment route.

branches.condition: Dashboard is selected, action 5 of 8.
branches.path: Connect the chosen Git provider for the selected repository or bootstrapped source.
branches.effect_on_first_success: Adds external Git authorization before build configuration.

branches.condition: Dashboard is selected, action 6 of 8.
branches.path: Configure the project in the Dashboard and select Deploy.
branches.effect_on_first_success: Commits the selected source and build settings to hosted execution.

branches.condition: Dashboard is selected, action 7 of 8.
branches.path: Wait while Cloudflare starts and completes the new build and deployment.
branches.effect_on_first_success: Adds an explicit hosted build and deployment wait.

branches.condition: Dashboard is selected, action 8 of 8.
branches.path: Open the provided workers.dev subdomain and verify the deployed Worker preview.
branches.effect_on_first_success: Produces the Dashboard route's terminal hosted preview.

branches.condition: Prompting is selected.
branches.path: Choose a supported agent or editor; optionally connect the cloudflare-docs MCP server and the observability MCP server; choose an example prompt or copy Cloudflare's base prompt; paste it into the AI tool; insert the developer request between the user-prompt tags; and have the tool create the requested Workers application. The result is prompt-specific, and Cloudflare does not demonstrate one universal terminal application on this page.
branches.effect_on_first_success: Adds an AI-tool and prompt choice, with an application shape and execution path that are not shared by the CLI or Dashboard routes.

branches.condition: Templates is selected.
branches.path: Choose one of the listed Workers starter repositories, then start it with its displayed npm, yarn, or pnpm create-cloudflare command or its Deploy to Cloudflare action. Each template has a different application and may introduce different services, credentials, and terminal states.
branches.effect_on_first_success: Introduces a template and package-manager or deployment choice before a concrete first-success path exists.

branches.condition: Playground is selected.
branches.path: Use Firefox or Chrome on desktop, open the Workers Playground without authentication, inspect or edit the default Worker and its automatic preview, optionally send a raw HTTP request and inspect console logs, then optionally copy a share link or deploy. Deployment requires account onboarding when not already signed in and produces a unique public URL.
branches.effect_on_first_success: Removes account and local-setup gates for preview, but sharing and deployment create later branches with different completion signals.

branches.condition: A CLI, Dashboard, or Playground deployment requires a new Cloudflare account, account action 1 of 6.
branches.path: Open the Cloudflare signup page.
branches.effect_on_first_success: Begins the common authenticated-deployment gate.

branches.condition: A CLI, Dashboard, or Playground deployment requires a new Cloudflare account, account action 2 of 6.
branches.path: Enter an email address and password, then select Create Account.
branches.effect_on_first_success: Creates the Cloudflare account; its default name is based on the email address.

branches.condition: A CLI, Dashboard, or Playground deployment requires a new Cloudflare account, account action 3 of 6.
branches.path: Wait for Cloudflare to automatically send the verification message to the provided email address.
branches.effect_on_first_success: Adds email-delivery latency immediately after account creation.

branches.condition: A CLI, Dashboard, or Playground deployment requires a new Cloudflare account, account action 4 of 6.
branches.path: Open the email provider, find the recent Cloudflare message, and check the Spam folder if it is missing.
branches.effect_on_first_success: Requires inbox access and a documented missing-message check.

branches.condition: A CLI, Dashboard, or Playground deployment requires a new Cloudflare account, account action 5 of 6.
branches.path: Open the verification link in the Cloudflare email and log in to Cloudflare to verify the address.
branches.effect_on_first_success: Completes email ownership verification required for certain Dashboard tasks.

branches.condition: The Cloudflare verification email is missing, expired, or its link fails, account action 6 of 6.
branches.path: Log in to the Dashboard, go to My Profile, select Send verification email, and repeat the email-link login. If the link still fails, try it in a different browser or private window, resend again, and check My Profile for the verified label.
branches.effect_on_first_success: Adds the complete documented resend and browser-retry path before verification can be confirmed.

branches.condition: The new or existing account has not deliberately purchased Workers Paid.
branches.path: Use the Workers Free plan that Cloudflare provides by default. It includes limited Workers, Pages Functions, Workers KV, and Hyperdrive usage; the current Workers allowance includes 100,000 requests per day and 10 milliseconds of CPU time per invocation.
branches.effect_on_first_success: Keeps the first Worker deployment on the default no-purchase route with documented usage limits.

branches.condition: The developer deliberately chooses Workers Paid for increased allotments or paid-only capabilities.
branches.path: Purchase the separate Workers Paid plan for a minimum account charge of $5 USD per month, then use its Standard usage model and paid allotments. Do not treat this purchase as part of default account creation or the Free-plan first deployment.
branches.effect_on_first_success: Adds an explicit paid subscription and billing boundary that is conditional, not automatic.

frictionGates.description: The official Workers router requires an interface choice that changes the entire first-mile path.

CLI and Dashboard deployments require a Cloudflare account; Playground preview does not, but deploying from Playground requires login or first-time onboarding. New account creation asks for email and password and automatically starts email verification.
The CLI path requires Node.js and installs Wrangler through C3.
The Prompting route requires choosing an AI tool and prompt, and the Templates route requires choosing a starter repository and launch method.
The CLI guide states that first-time workers.dev 523 errors may require waiting a minute or so to resolve, then reloading the deployed URL.
New accounts receive an automatic verification email; missing or expired messages require Dashboard resend, and failing links can require another browser or private window.
The CLI route requires npm, yarn, or pnpm plus exact C3 choices for example, Worker-only template, JavaScript, Git, and deferred deployment.
The Dashboard route waits for Cloudflare's hosted build and deployment before the workers.dev preview exists.
Workers Free is the default plan; Workers Paid is a separate optional purchase with a minimum $5 monthly account charge.

uncertainties.question: Should Cloudflare first success be a no-setup Playground preview, an agent-generated application, a chosen template, a locally edited Worker, or a deployed workers.dev Worker?
uncertainties.impact: The answer changes browser support, account, software, AI-tool, prompt, template, Git, authentication, code-edit, deployment, and wait gates.
uncertainties.reason_unresolved: The official Workers getting-started page presents CLI, Dashboard, Prompting, and Templates as parallel routes, and the Dashboard and Playground pages separately call Playground the quickest experiment without making it the universal first path.

developerGoal: Create a Cloudinary account, find product-environment credentials, complete the Image and Video Developer Kickstart, and run one official SDK quick start through its demonstrated media-delivery terminal.

entryPoint.developer_intent: Create a Cloudinary account, find product-environment credentials, complete the Image and Video Developer Kickstart, and run one official SDK quick start through its demonstrated media-delivery terminal.
assumed_prior_state: No Cloudinary account, product environment credentials, SDK, sample project, uploaded asset, or delivered transformation is assumed.
The developer can receive account email, can accept Cloudinary terms, and has a local development environment for whichever SDK route they choose.
No registration submission, email verification, credential retrieval, upload, API request, or asset delivery was performed during research.

selectedSurface.name: Email registration and Node.js backend SDK quick start
selectedSurface.selection_basis: Workshop selection policy (2026-07-19): when official docs present peer routes, commit to the fastest and most commonly used documented route. Prefer the vendor's first-listed or recommended quickstart, dashboard/console over CLI when both are peers, and the route with an explicit vendor time claim or fewest documented steps when tied. Selected route: Email registration and Node.js backend SDK quick start. This is one complete official peer route. Email registration has a documented verification requirement, and Node.js is the first backend SDK listed, but neither is asserted to be the universal default. Prior surface before policy commit: Image and Video Developer Kickstart through a completed SDK quick start.

alternatives_considered.name: SDK quick starts index
alternatives_considered.reason_not_primary: The index is also recommended for onboarding but immediately requires a choice among backend, frontend, and mobile SDKs; the Developer Kickstart provides the broader official common spine before the same choice.

alternatives_considered.name: Developer Kickstart with Python SDK
alternatives_considered.reason_not_primary: Python is a peer language option and the official route does not rank it above Node.js or the other SDKs.

alternatives_considered.name: Google or GitHub registration
alternatives_considered.reason_not_primary: These are peer account-creation branches; the documented email route was used for the reconstructable candidate because Cloudinary also documents its email-verification requirement.

firstMeaningfulSuccess.official_milestone: I successfully ran my first SDK quick start project!
firstMeaningfulSuccess.normalized_outcome: Run a selected official SDK quick start end to end and observe its documented media output.
firstMeaningfulSuccess.observable_completion_signal: For the fully reconstructed Node.js candidate, the script logs configuration, upload and resource responses, prints an image tag, and the transformed image appears when its URL is opened in a browser.

boundary_evidence.official_label_or_terminal_state: I successfully ran my first SDK quick start project!

firstMeaningfulSuccess.why_this_is_the_boundary: The Developer Kickstart uses successful execution of a selected SDK quick start as its final required progress marker. The Node.js candidate demonstrates the corresponding end-to-end output as a transformed image loaded from the generated URL.

prerequisites.requirement: A free Cloudinary account created with Google, GitHub, or an email address; the email route requires clicking the emailed verification link before the account can be used.
Accept Cloudinary's Terms of Service and Privacy Policy when creating the account.
Cloud name, API key, API secret, and API environment-variable format from Console Settings > API Keys; keep the API secret out of client code and version control.
A supported development environment for the chosen official SDK quick start; the reconstructed candidate requires Node.js and npm.

stages.action: Open the Developer Kickstart and begin its ordered onboarding tasks.
details: The route is presented as a collection of quick tasks covering optimization, transformation, asset management, and an SDK project.
stages.output: The Kickstart progress sequence from Intro through Step 5 is visible.
stages.success_signal: The page displays the progress control 'Got it - I'm ready to get started'.
stages.failure_or_wait: No navigation recovery behavior is documented.

stages.action: Register for a free Cloudinary account using Google, GitHub, or email.
details: The email form asks for full name, email, and a password with the displayed composition rules.
Submitting the email form accepts the Terms of Service and Privacy Policy.
stages.input: A selected registration method and its required identity information
stages.success_signal: For email registration, Cloudinary sends a verification email; the account becomes usable only after its link is opened.
stages.failure_or_wait: The registration surface documents country restrictions, existing organization-managed accounts, invitations, and a generic retry or alternate-method error.

stages.action: Open Console Settings, navigate to API Keys, and locate the product environment cloud name, API key, API secret, and API environment variable.
details: Optionally customize a new product environment's cloud name before it is used in production; changing an in-use cloud name can break delivered asset URLs.
stages.output: Product-environment credentials are available for the SDK route.
stages.success_signal: The Kickstart progress markers for having an account and knowing where to find credentials can be checked.
stages.failure_or_wait: The API secret may require password verification before it can be viewed or copied.

stages.action: Complete the Kickstart's ordered optimization, first-transformation, and resize tasks, then continue to Step 5.
details: Step 2 contains four browser tasks, Step 3 contains four transformation tasks, and Step 4 contains two resize tasks; the complete Node.js candidate below preserves those actions in order.
stages.input: The account cloud name and the Kickstart sample delivery URLs
stages.output: A prepared developer reaches the final SDK-project task.
stages.success_signal: The Step 5 page presents backend, frontend, and mobile quick-start groups.
stages.failure_or_wait: A missing URL slash produces an error in Step 2; the guide says to inspect the URL and restore the missing slash. The generative-fill resize may take a few seconds on its first request.

stages.action: Choose Sign Up with Email, enter full name, email, and a compliant password, then select Get Started Now.
details: The password requires at least eight characters, lowercase, uppercase, number, special character, and no spaces.
Selecting the button accepts the Terms of Service and Privacy Policy.
stages.input: Full name, email, and compliant password
stages.success_signal: The official help article says the verification email is sent automatically.
stages.failure_or_wait: If the email is missing, request a new verification email or password-reset email.

stages.action: Open the verification link from the Cloudinary email to complete signup and activate the account.
stages.success_signal: The account can use Cloudinary APIs and tools after verification.
stages.failure_or_wait: Email link rewriting can interfere; Cloudinary says to copy and paste the original link directly if needed.

stages.action: Open Console Settings > API Keys and copy the API environment-variable format, replacing its API-key and API-secret placeholders with the displayed values.
details: The cloud name is already included in the format.
Do not expose the API secret or commit it to version control.
stages.success_signal: Cloud name, API key, API secret, and environment-variable format are visible.
stages.failure_or_wait: Viewing the secret can require password verification.

stages.action: Open the Step 2 sample image delivery URL with the account cloud name and inspect the original image.
details: The guide describes the starting sample as an approximately 800 KB, 1870 by 1250 JPG included with the account.
stages.input: The Step 2 image URL with `<your_cloud_name>` replaced
stages.success_signal: The high-resolution JPG is visible in the browser.
stages.failure_or_wait: No recovery behavior is documented for the initial image request.

stages.action: Insert `/q_auto/f_auto` after `/upload`, reload the image, inspect its size and format, and compare it with the original.
stages.output: An automatically optimized image variant loads.
stages.success_signal: The guide says inspection shows roughly one third or less of the original file size without an obvious visual-quality difference.
stages.failure_or_wait: If a URL component is missing a slash, inspect the URL and restore the slash before reloading.

stages.action: Insert `/c_scale,w_500` before `/q_auto/f_auto`, reload the image, and inspect the delivered size and format.
stages.output: A 500-pixel scaled and optimized image loads.
stages.success_signal: The resized delivery is visible and its file size and format can be inspected.
stages.failure_or_wait: If a URL component is missing a slash, inspect the URL and restore the slash before reloading.

stages.action: Open the sample video with the account cloud name, inspect the original, add the same scale and automatic optimization parameters, reload it, then let it play to the end before checking the result.
details: The guide requires waiting until playback ends so progressive optimization can complete.
stages.output: The optimized video variant completes playback.
stages.success_signal: The developer can compare the optimized video's format and file size with the original.
stages.failure_or_wait: The required playback wait is documented; no separate recovery behavior is stated.

stages.action: Add `/e_grayscale` before `/c_scale,w_500` and reload the Step 3 image URL.
stages.input: The optimized and resized image URL
stages.success_signal: The effect transformation is visible.
stages.failure_or_wait: No recovery behavior is documented for this task.

stages.action: Add `/bo_40px_solid_green` after `/e_grayscale` and reload the URL.
stages.output: The black-and-white image loads with a solid green border.
stages.success_signal: The border transformation is visible.
stages.failure_or_wait: No recovery behavior is documented for this task.

stages.action: Add the published Good morning text-layer and layer-apply components after the border, remove `e_grayscale`, and reload the URL.
details: The text is orange, bottom-right aligned, and offset by 60 pixels in each direction.
stages.output: The color image loads with the text layer.
stages.success_signal: The border and Good morning text are visible on the color image.
stages.failure_or_wait: No recovery behavior is documented for this task.

stages.action: Remove `/f_auto`, change the extension from `.jpg` to `.png`, reload and inspect the PNG, then restore `/f_auto` and reload again.
stages.output: A forced PNG and then an automatically selected delivery format load in sequence.
stages.success_signal: Inspection first shows PNG; after restoring `f_auto`, Cloudinary selects a browser-appropriate format.
stages.failure_or_wait: No recovery behavior is documented for this task.

stages.action: Replace `c_scale,w_500` with each documented automatic crop or pad transformation, then keep the predominant-gradient variant and test it against each listed sample public ID.
details: The required variants include automatic banner and portrait crops, orange padding, generative fill, and predominant-gradient padding.
stages.output: Each crop or pad variant loads across the listed sample assets.
stages.success_signal: The developer sees the impact of the different automatic resize transformations.
stages.failure_or_wait: The first generative-fill request may take a few seconds; no other recovery behavior is documented.

stages.action: Return to the simple scale-cropping URL, replace its resize component with each documented face-detection crop, and inspect both results.
details: The guide uses `g_faces` with a portrait fill and with a rounded square automatic crop.
stages.success_signal: The developer sees how face-detection gravity preserves people during resizing.
stages.failure_or_wait: No recovery behavior is documented for this task.

stages.action: In a supported Node.js environment, run `npm install cloudinary`.
stages.success_signal: npm completes the package installation.
stages.failure_or_wait: No installation recovery behavior is documented in the quick start.

stages.action: Set the CLOUDINARY_URL environment variable for the current platform using the copied credential values.
details: Use the documented export command on macOS or Linux or set command on Windows.
stages.output: The SDK can read product-environment credentials.
stages.success_signal: The later script logs the expected cloud_name, api_key, api_secret, and secure configuration.
stages.failure_or_wait: An incorrect value is exposed by a mismatching or failed configuration output.

stages.action: Create index.js, require Cloudinary v2, enable secure URLs, and add the published uploadImage, getAssetInfo, and createImageTag functions.
details: The upload uses the happy_people.jpg remote URL, filename-derived public ID, overwrite, and no random suffix.
The resource call requests detected colors.
The transformation creates a 250 by 250 circular face thumbnail with outline and background colors.
stages.output: A complete Node.js script ready to execute.
stages.success_signal: The main function uploads the sample, retrieves its colors, creates an image tag, and logs it.
stages.failure_or_wait: The sample catches and prints upload or resource errors but does not document a recovery sequence.

stages.action: Save index.js and run `node index.js`.
stages.output: Configuration, upload response, resource response, and generated image tag are printed.
stages.success_signal: The upload response includes public_id and secure_url, the resource response includes colors, and the generated tag contains the transformation URL.
stages.failure_or_wait: Errors are logged by the sample; no retry sequence is documented.

stages.action: Open the transformed URL from the generated image tag in a browser.
stages.output: The transformed happy_people image is delivered.
stages.success_signal: The browser displays the circular face thumbnail with the documented outline and background transformations.
stages.failure_or_wait: No delivery retry behavior is documented in the quick start.

branches.condition: Choose Google or GitHub signup
branches.path: Continue through the selected provider's authentication and consent flow instead of the email form and email-verification sequence.

branches.condition: Customize a new cloud name
branches.path: In Product Environments, edit the environment, choose a unique cloud name, and save before using delivered asset URLs.
branches.effect_on_first_success: Adds a recommended configuration step and changes the base identifier in delivered URLs.

branches.condition: Choose another SDK surface or runtime
branches.path: Follow the corresponding backend, frontend, or mobile quick start rather than the Node.js candidate.
branches.effect_on_first_success: Changes installation, configuration, code, execution, and terminal details.

frictionGates.description: A Cloudinary account is required for the Kickstart and SDK route.
Email registration requires clicking a verification link before APIs and tools can be used.
The backend SDK route requires a product-environment cloud name, API key, and API secret, with the secret protected from exposure.
The official final Kickstart task requires a human choice among materially different backend, frontend, and mobile SDK paths.

uncertainties.question: Which backend, frontend, or mobile SDK and language should represent Cloudinary's universal first mile?
uncertainties.impact: The installation, credential use, code, terminal artifact, and exact step count differ by selection.
uncertainties.reason_unresolved: The Developer Kickstart says to pick a favorite language and presents the SDKs as peer routes without choosing one.

developerGoal: Create a Cohere account, obtain the trial API key, and receive a Chat API v2 response over HTTPS.

entryPoint.developer_intent: Create a Cohere account, obtain the trial API key, and receive a Chat API v2 response over HTTPS.
assumed_prior_state: No Cohere account or API key yet.
A terminal with cURL is available; no language SDK is assumed.

selectedSurface.name: Cohere Chat API v2 via cURL (trial API key)
selectedSurface.selection_basis: Cohere documents Chat API access with Bearer authentication. The Chat API guide includes a cURL example POSTing to https://api.cohere.ai/v2/chat with Authorization: bearer $CO_API_KEY, so installing the Python SDK is not required for first success. Account creation issues a trial API key.

alternatives_considered.name: Python / TypeScript SDK hello-world
alternatives_considered.reason_not_primary: SDK snippets are convenience clients; the same guide documents cURL to /v2/chat.

alternatives_considered.name: OpenAI Compatibility API
alternatives_considered.reason_not_primary: Migration path for OpenAI SDK apps, not the shortest native first call.

alternatives_considered.name: Playground
alternatives_considered.reason_not_primary: In-browser try-out rather than the documented programmatic Chat API call.

firstMeaningfulSuccess.official_milestone: Send a Chat API request and receive an assistant message
firstMeaningfulSuccess.normalized_outcome: An authenticated cURL POST to https://api.cohere.ai/v2/chat returns an assistant message for the prompt.
firstMeaningfulSuccess.observable_completion_signal: The JSON response includes an assistant message content text block.

boundary_evidence.official_label_or_terminal_state: The Chat API guide documents cURL to /v2/chat with Bearer auth and shows an assistant message response structure.

firstMeaningfulSuccess.why_this_is_the_boundary: First success is an authenticated Chat API response. The official Chat guide's cURL example proves no SDK install is required. Trial keys are free but limited; production keys are a later paid path.

prerequisites.requirement: A Cohere account; account creation automatically issues a Trial API key.
The trial API key from the API keys page, exported for HTTP requests.
A terminal with cURL (or any HTTP client). No language SDK is required.

stages.action: Create a Cohere account and open the dashboard API keys page.

stages.failure_or_wait: Complete any displayed signup verification.

stages.action: Export the trial key as CO_API_KEY.
stages.success_signal: The environment variable is set.
stages.failure_or_wait: Shell persistence depends on the OS.

stages.action: POST https://api.cohere.ai/v2/chat with cURL using Authorization: bearer $CO_API_KEY.
details: Send JSON with a Command model and a user message as in the Chat API cURL example.
stages.success_signal: HTTP success with an assistant message content text.
stages.failure_or_wait: Invalid keys or rate limits return API errors.

stages.action: Confirm the assistant message text in the Chat API JSON response.
stages.success_signal: Assistant text is present.
stages.failure_or_wait: An error body means the call failed.
