developerGoal: Choose the CDP product and developer outcome whose first mile should be measured, then follow its account, credential, SDK or CLI, and first-success path.

entryPoint.developer_intent: Choose the CDP product and developer outcome whose first mile should be measured, then follow its account, credential, SDK or CLI, and first-success path.
assumed_prior_state: The developer has not supplied a CDP product, custody model, runtime, network, account type, or endpoint.
The developer may not yet have a CDP account, project, API key, Wallet Secret, or verified business.

selectedSurface.name: Create and verify a first EVM API Key Wallet account with Node TypeScript
selectedSurface.selection_basis: Workshop selection policy (2026-07-19): when official docs present peer routes, commit to the fastest and most commonly used documented route. Prefer the vendor's first-listed or recommended quickstart, dashboard/console over CLI when both are peers, and the route with an explicit vendor time claim or fewest documented steps when tied. Selected route: Create and verify a first EVM API Key Wallet account with Node TypeScript. The official API Key Wallet quickstart defines a non-custodial Node route with a printed EVM address as its first observable result, before funding or transactions. Prior surface before policy commit: Unresolved at CDP custodial model, product family, authentication model, and implementation selection.

alternatives_considered.name: API Key Wallet quickstart
alternatives_considered.reason_not_primary: It is a non-custodial server-owned wallet path that requires API key, Wallet Secret, runtime, EVM versus Solana, and wallet-operation choices.

alternatives_considered.name: User Wallet quickstart
alternatives_considered.reason_not_primary: It is an end-user authentication and embedded-wallet route with different actors and custody.

alternatives_considered.name: CDP CLI setup and connectivity check
alternatives_considered.reason_not_primary: It verifies CLI authentication and wallet access but does not represent every CDP product or API.

alternatives_considered.name: Trade API quickstart
alternatives_considered.reason_not_primary: It requires wallet model, chain, token pair, account, price or quote, and execution choices.

alternatives_considered.name: Coinbase Prime quickstart
alternatives_considered.reason_not_primary: It is an institutional API path requiring a Coinbase Prime account and portfolio.

alternatives_considered.name: Agentic Wallet CLI quickstart
alternatives_considered.reason_not_primary: It is a specialized email-authenticated agent wallet with a separately claimed two-minute path.

firstMeaningfulSuccess.official_milestone: The selected EVM API Key Wallet route demonstrates first success when the terminal displays the Created EVM account label and a 0x-prefixed address.
firstMeaningfulSuccess.normalized_outcome: The terminal displays the Created EVM account label and a 0x-prefixed address.
firstMeaningfulSuccess.observable_completion_signal: The terminal displays the Created EVM account label and a 0x-prefixed address.

boundary_evidence.official_label_or_terminal_state: The terminal displays the Created EVM account label and a 0x-prefixed address.

firstMeaningfulSuccess.why_this_is_the_boundary: Selected under workshop fastest/most-used policy; terminal taken from the committed official candidate route.

prerequisites.requirement: A CDP Portal account and project for the authenticated CDP routes.
A verified business account linked to CDP for custodial API groups.
The credential set required by the selected route, such as a Secret API key, Client API key, Wallet Secret, end-user authentication, OAuth credential, or institutional key.

stages.action: Open the Coinbase Developer Platform API overview.
details: The page describes CDP as trusted crypto infrastructure and presents non-custodial and custodial API sets.
stages.input: Intent to build with Coinbase Developer Platform.
stages.output: CDP API model, product navigation, and shared get-started links.
stages.success_signal: Non-custodial APIs, custodial APIs, Get an API key, and Authentication guide are visible.
stages.failure_or_wait: None documented.

stages.action: Choose non-custodial or custodial capabilities.
details: Non-custodial User Wallets, API Key Wallets, Onramp, and related tools are available to a CDP entity after signup.
Custodial transfers, trading orders, payment acceptance, reporting, and related groups require a verified business account linked to CDP.
stages.success_signal: The selected model matches who controls assets and which service is needed.
stages.failure_or_wait: The roster identifies only CDP, so the custody model is unresolved.

stages.action: Create or sign in to a CDP Portal account and use its project.
details: CDP authentication documentation assumes an existing CDP account and says a project is included by default.
The CLI setup page says a project is auto-created on first sign-in.
stages.input: Eligible Coinbase identity and account details.
stages.output: Authenticated CDP Portal and selected project.
stages.success_signal: The project and API Keys area are accessible.
stages.failure_or_wait: The current account-creation, email verification, entity, geographic, and compliance screens are not enumerated by the developer guides.

stages.action: Choose the CDP product family and first result.
details: Current documentation families include wallets, payments, stablecoins, trading, onchain tools, customers, webhooks, consumer APIs, business APIs, and institutional APIs.
The choice determines whether an API key alone is sufficient or business verification, OAuth, Wallet Secret, end-user authentication, or institutional access is required.
stages.output: One product quickstart or API group.
stages.success_signal: A guide with an explicit target result is selected.
stages.failure_or_wait: No product family can be inferred from the company name.

stages.action: Choose the authentication model and configure the matching credential type.
details: Secret API keys are for server-to-server REST APIs.
Client API keys are for client-side JSON-RPC.
Non-custodial API Key Wallets additionally use a Wallet Secret.
User wallets authenticate end users, and other Coinbase product families have distinct credentials.
stages.input: Selected product architecture and trust boundary.
stages.success_signal: The credential type matches the chosen API and client location.
stages.failure_or_wait: Selecting the wrong key or signature algorithm produces authentication or permission errors.

stages.action: Choose the implementation, network, resource type, and concrete API or SDK operation.
details: The API Key Wallet quickstart alone branches by Node TypeScript versus Python, EVM versus Solana, and account creation, faucet, or transaction.
The generic authentication guide requires a request method, host, and path before it can generate and use a request-specific JWT.
stages.input: Runtime, chain or network, selected resource, and terminal outcome.
stages.output: Atomic commands, code, waits, and verification for one first-mile journey.
stages.success_signal: Every placeholder and branch required by the selected quickstart is resolved.
stages.failure_or_wait: Research stops rather than imposing financial, custody, network, and runtime choices.

stages.action: Create or sign in to a CDP Portal account, open its project, and confirm Node.js 22 or later is available locally.
details: The quickstart requires a CDP Portal account and Node 22 or later; CDP documentation says a project is included or auto-created on first sign-in.
stages.input: An eligible Coinbase identity and local Node environment.
stages.output: An authenticated CDP project and compatible runtime.
stages.success_signal: The project's API Keys area is accessible and Node satisfies the version requirement.
stages.failure_or_wait: The developer guides do not enumerate current account-creation, email, entity, geographic, or compliance screens.

stages.action: Create a CDP API key and copy its API key ID and secret.
details: The server-side SDK uses the CDP secret API-key credentials.
stages.output: A CDP API key ID and secret.
stages.success_signal: Both key values are available for local configuration.
stages.failure_or_wait: Wrong key type, permissions, or lost secret prevents SDK authentication.

stages.action: Generate and copy the Wallet Secret for the project.
details: API Key Wallet operations require this additional wallet credential.
stages.success_signal: The Wallet Secret is generated and stored for local use.
stages.failure_or_wait: A missing or mismatched Wallet Secret prevents wallet-account creation.

stages.action: Create the cdp-sdk-example Node project, set package type to module, and create main.ts and .env.
details: The guide uses mkdir, npm init -y, npm pkg set type=module, and empty main.ts and .env files.
stages.success_signal: package.json, main.ts, and .env exist in the project directory.
stages.failure_or_wait: Filesystem or npm initialization errors block setup.

stages.action: Add CDP_API_KEY_ID, CDP_API_KEY_SECRET, and CDP_WALLET_SECRET to .env.
details: Each variable must contain the matching value created in the CDP Portal.
stages.output: The complete local CDP credential environment.
stages.success_signal: All three variables are present without placeholder values.
stages.failure_or_wait: Missing, swapped, or malformed credential values cause authentication or wallet errors.

stages.action: Install @coinbase/cdp-sdk and dotenv.
details: These packages provide the CDP client and load .env credentials.
stages.output: The CDP SDK and dotenv dependencies.
stages.success_signal: npm completes installation without error.
stages.failure_or_wait: Dependency or supported-runtime errors prevent execution.

stages.action: Configure main.ts to load the environment, instantiate CdpClient, create an EVM account, and print its address.
details: Use cdp.evm.createAccount and log the resulting account address.
stages.input: The official Node sample and installed dependencies.
stages.success_signal: main.ts contains client initialization, createAccount, and output logging.
stages.failure_or_wait: An omitted Wallet Secret or incorrect SDK configuration prevents wallet creation.

stages.action: Run npx tsx main.ts.
details: The SDK authenticates to CDP and creates the EVM account.
stages.input: The program, dependencies, credentials, and network access.
stages.output: A newly created EVM account or an SDK error.
stages.success_signal: The command prints Created EVM account followed by a 0x address.
stages.failure_or_wait: Invalid API credentials, Wallet Secret, project permissions, or network access prevents account creation.

stages.action: Verify that the printed value is the newly created EVM account address.
details: The 0x-prefixed address is the first observable wallet result; faucet funding and sending a transaction are later steps in the quickstart.
stages.output: A confirmed first CDP API Key Wallet account.
stages.success_signal: The terminal displays the Created EVM account label and a 0x-prefixed address.
stages.failure_or_wait: No address or only an error means the candidate terminal was not reached.

branches.condition: The selected capability is custodial.
branches.path: Complete CDP Initial Setup, verify and link the required business account, select the custodial API group, create credentials with the needed permissions, then follow that group's resource-specific first request.
branches.effect_on_first_success: Adds business verification and product eligibility before any custodial API result.

branches.condition: The selected product is non-custodial API Key Wallets with Node and EVM.
branches.path: Sign in to CDP Portal, create a CDP API key, generate a Wallet Secret, initialize a TypeScript project and .env, add CDP_API_KEY_ID, CDP_API_KEY_SECRET, and CDP_WALLET_SECRET, install @coinbase/cdp-sdk and dotenv, instantiate CdpClient, run the empty client program, add cdp.evm.createAccount, run it, and verify Created EVM account plus a 0x address. Continue only if the selected boundary includes faucet funding and a sent testnet transaction.
branches.effect_on_first_success: Defines the first application result as a server-controlled EVM account address and adds credential, secret, SDK, and wallet-operation work.

branches.condition: The selected product is non-custodial API Key Wallets with Python and Solana.
branches.path: Create the same portal keys, initialize and activate a Python 3.10+ virtual environment, create main.py and .env, add all three secrets, install cdp-sdk and python-dotenv, instantiate CdpClient, call cdp.solana.create_account, run the program, and verify a Solana address.
branches.effect_on_first_success: Changes runtime, package manager, asynchronous code, account API, and address format.

branches.condition: The developer uses the CDP CLI for a non-custodial connectivity check.
branches.path: Confirm Node 22+, install Linux keyring support if applicable, install @coinbase/cdp-cli, sign in and let CDP auto-create a project, create and download an API key file, run cdp env live --key-file, generate and download a Wallet Secret, run cdp env live --wallet-secret-file, run cdp env, then run cdp evm accounts list and confirm JSON without an auth error.
branches.effect_on_first_success: Defines success as authenticated CLI connectivity, which may return an empty accounts list rather than creating a wallet.

branches.condition: The selected route uses a generic Secret API key and direct REST authentication.
branches.path: Choose project, Secret API Keys, and Create API key; name the key, optionally set an IP allowlist and granular permissions, select Ed25519 or ECDSA as supported, create it, copy the key ID and secret, choose a runtime, export key and request method/host/path variables, install its JWT dependency, create and run the signing script, export the two-minute JWT, send the selected endpoint with Authorization: Bearer, and inspect its API-specific response.
branches.effect_on_first_success: Requires a concrete endpoint and runtime, and its JWT must be regenerated after two minutes or for changed request details.

branches.condition: The selected product is Agentic Wallet CLI.
branches.path: Install the skill package, initiate email login, receive the six-digit OTP and flow ID, verify them, run status, and confirm Authenticated before using wallet commands.
branches.effect_on_first_success: Uses email OTP and a wallet server instead of CDP Portal API keys.

frictionGates.description: The developer must choose non-custodial versus custodial APIs.
Custodial API groups require a verified business account linked to CDP.
Most authenticated CDP routes require a CDP Portal account and project.
Wallets, payments, trading, onchain tools, customers, webhooks, consumer, business, and institutional APIs have different first results.
The developer must choose Secret key, Client key, Wallet Secret, user authentication, OAuth, CLI, or another product-specific model.
Secret API key material and any Wallet Secret must be copied or downloaded and stored securely.
API-key permissions and optional IP allowlists can allow or block the selected operation.
Runtime, chain, network, account type, and endpoint choices are required before atomic implementation steps exist.
Direct-request JWTs expire after two minutes, and faucet or blockchain transactions introduce confirmation waits in wallet paths.

uncertainties.question: Which CDP product and custody model should define platform first success?
uncertainties.impact: It changes eligibility, actors, credentials, financial actions, cost, and terminal verification.
uncertainties.reason_unresolved: Coinbase documents multiple peer product families and explicitly separates non-custodial and custodial APIs.

uncertainties.question: Which authentication model, runtime, network, and first operation should be measured?
uncertainties.impact: It changes portal fields, secrets, dependencies, code, waits, and response.
uncertainties.reason_unresolved: The generic entry stops at API key and authentication guidance, while each product quickstart requires additional choices.

uncertainties.question: What exact live screens create and verify a new CDP Portal account and entity?
uncertainties.impact: The common account gate cannot be counted page by page.
uncertainties.reason_unresolved: The guides say create or sign in to an account but do not enumerate signup, email verification, entity, region, or compliance screens.

uncertainties.question: Is the current v2 authentication page intentionally login-gated for unauthenticated readers?
uncertainties.impact: A new developer may face an unexpected documentation access step.
uncertainties.reason_unresolved: The current overview links to /api-reference/v2/authentication, while unauthenticated retrieval redirected to a docs login page; the accessible authentication guide at /api-reference/authentication supplies the detailed workflow.

assumed_prior_state: The developer begins before account creation with no platform resources or credentials.
A supported browser and internet connection are available.

selectedSurface.name: Confluent Cloud Console quick start: cluster, topic, and message

alternatives_considered.name: Local Confluent CLI quick start

firstMeaningfulSuccess.official_milestone: Produce and consume a message in Confluent Cloud

firstMeaningfulSuccess.why_this_is_the_boundary: This is the first observable end-to-end result on the selected official surface; later production configuration and expansion follow it.

developerGoal: Deploy an application or service on a self-hosted server using Coolify

assumed_prior_state: Developer has access to a server with SSH
Developer has a Git repository or Docker image for deployment
entryPoint.developer_intent: Deploy an application or service on a self-hosted server using Coolify

alternatives_considered.name: CLI
alternatives_considered.reason_not_primary: While SSH and CLI are used for initial server setup and activation, the main developer interaction surface for deployment and management is the web UI.

alternatives_considered.name: API
alternatives_considered.reason_not_primary: API exists for automation but is secondary to the web UI for first-mile onboarding and deployment.

selectedSurface.name: Web UI
selectedSurface.selection_basis: Coolify is primarily accessed and managed through a web interface for deploying and managing applications and services on self-hosted servers, as documented in official Coolify docs.

boundary_evidence.official_label_or_terminal_state: Start Deploying - Create your first project and deploy applications, databases, or services

firstMeaningfulSuccess.normalized_outcome: First application or service is deployed and accessible through Coolify with automatic SSL and domain configuration optionally set
firstMeaningfulSuccess.observable_completion_signal: Application status shows as deployed and accessible in Coolify dashboard; optionally domain resolves with SSL
firstMeaningfulSuccess.official_milestone: Successfully deploy first application or service and access it via Coolify web interface
firstMeaningfulSuccess.why_this_is_the_boundary: This milestone marks the transition from setup to actual deployment and usage of Coolify as intended, documented as the first success point in official docs.

prerequisites.requirement: Have an account on your server or hosting provider with SSH access
Access to a server or VPS with minimum 2 CPU cores and 2GB RAM recommended
Docker installed or use Coolify 1-Click App which includes Docker

stages.action: Read introduction and prerequisites for Coolify self-hosting
details: Understand Coolify's purpose and requirements
Learn about server and SSH access needs
stages.failure_or_wait: Developer lacks server or SSH access
stages.success_signal: Developer proceeds to server preparation

stages.action: Deploy Coolify on server using 1-Click App or manual Docker installation
details: Select server size and region
Add SSH key for access
Create and deploy server instance
stages.failure_or_wait: Server deployment or Docker installation fails
stages.input: Use DigitalOcean Marketplace or manual Docker commands
stages.output: Coolify platform installed and running on server
stages.success_signal: Server is accessible and Coolify service is running

stages.action: SSH into the server for initial setup
details: Run first login to trigger setup wizard
Activate localhost server for deployments
stages.failure_or_wait: SSH connection issues or setup errors
stages.output: Coolify setup wizard runs and localhost activated
stages.success_signal: Setup wizard completes and Coolify restarts

stages.action: Open Coolify web interface in browser
details: Navigate to http://your-droplet-ip:8000
Redirected to registration page
stages.failure_or_wait: Network or server issues prevent access
stages.success_signal: Registration page loads successfully

stages.action: Create admin account on Coolify
details: Fill registration form with username and strong password
First user gets full admin access
stages.failure_or_wait: Form validation errors or server errors
stages.output: Admin account created and logged in
stages.success_signal: Dashboard shows localhost server connected

stages.action: Create first project and add application or service resource
details: Choose application type or database
Connect Git repository or Docker image
stages.failure_or_wait: Configuration errors or missing inputs
stages.success_signal: Resource ready for deployment

stages.action: Deploy the application or service
details: Trigger deployment from Coolify dashboard
Monitor deployment logs and status
stages.failure_or_wait: Deployment errors or build failures
stages.success_signal: Deployment status shows success and app accessible

stages.action: Optionally configure custom domain and SSL
details: Add DNS A record pointing to server IP
Update instance URL in Coolify settings
Coolify automatically provisions SSL certificate
stages.failure_or_wait: DNS propagation delay or configuration errors
stages.success_signal: Application accessible via custom domain with SSL

developerGoal: Create a Coralogix account and team, obtain a Send-Your-Data API key, POST a first log via HTTPS, and see it in the product.

entryPoint.developer_intent: Create a Coralogix account and team, obtain a Send-Your-Data API key, POST a first log via HTTPS, and see it in the product.
assumed_prior_state: No Coralogix account, team, Send-Your-Data API key, or ingested log is assumed.
A work email inbox, browser, and terminal with cURL are available.

selectedSurface.name: Account setup + Send-Your-Data key + REST /logs/v1/singles via cURL
selectedSurface.selection_basis: Common questions say a one-off test can post directly to the logs API. The REST /singles guide documents cURL with a Send-Your-Data Bearer key and ends at viewing logs in the Coralogix dashboard. Integration packages are an alternate guided path.

alternatives_considered.name: Integration packages
alternatives_considered.reason_not_primary: Docs call packages the fastest guided send path, but they add source-specific setup. Direct /singles is the documented one-off API test.

alternatives_considered.name: OpenTelemetry Collector
alternatives_considered.reason_not_primary: Adds collector install and pipeline configuration.

firstMeaningfulSuccess.official_milestone: View your logs in your Coralogix dashboard
firstMeaningfulSuccess.normalized_outcome: An authenticated POST to the region ingress /logs/v1/singles endpoint is accepted and the submitted log is visible in Coralogix (Explore, Livetail, or dashboard).
firstMeaningfulSuccess.observable_completion_signal: The submitted application/subsystem/text appear in Coralogix after a successful /singles request.

boundary_evidence.official_label_or_terminal_state: REST /singles: View your logs, with all metadata fields, in your Coralogix dashboard. Common questions: Livetail is the quickest way to confirm data is arriving.

firstMeaningfulSuccess.why_this_is_the_boundary: Dashboard/Explore visibility is the documented proof that Coralogix accepted the first ingested event.

prerequisites.requirement: A Coralogix organization and team created with a work email.
A Send-Your-Data API key (not Query/Team/Personal).
The ingress hostname must match the team's Coralogix domain/region.
A terminal with cURL (or any HTTPS client). No SDK is required.

stages.action: Sign up with a work email, activate from the verification message, create the organization, and create the first team.
details: The first user becomes Organization Administrator.
If the email domain already belongs to an organization, team join or Org Admin approval can replace new-org creation.
stages.success_signal: The team URL and console are available.
stages.failure_or_wait: Wait for the activation email; existing-domain accounts may need Org Admin access.

stages.action: Open Data Flow → API Keys (or Settings → Users and Teams → API Keys), create a Send-Your-Data key, and copy it immediately.
details: Wrong key types fail ingestion authentication.
stages.success_signal: The key value is stored locally.
stages.failure_or_wait: With API key security enabled, the value may be shown only once.

stages.action: Map the team login hostname to the matching Coralogix domain and form `https://ingress.<domain>/logs/v1/singles`.
stages.success_signal: The domain matches a Quick reference row.
stages.failure_or_wait: Region mismatch prevents ingestion.

stages.action: POST a JSON array to `/logs/v1/singles` with Content-Type: application/json and Authorization: Bearer <Send-Your-Data API key>.
details: Required body fields: applicationName, subsystemName, and text.
Example host in docs: https://ingress.eu2.coralogix.com/logs/v1/singles
stages.input: Ingress URL, API key, and log JSON
stages.success_signal: The request is accepted.
stages.failure_or_wait: Check key type, payload size (2MB limit), and endpoint region.

stages.action: Confirm the submitted log in Livetail or Explore (filter by application name if needed).
details: Livetail is the quickest confirmation path; Explore searches historical logs.
Data usually appears within a few minutes.
stages.success_signal: The event text and metadata match the POST body.
stages.failure_or_wait: Widen the time range or check shipper clock if nothing appears.

branches.condition: Work-email domain already belongs to a Coralogix organization
branches.path: Join an existing team, request Org Admin access, or use SSO as configured.
branches.effect_on_first_success: May replace self-service org creation with an approval wait.

branches.condition: Prefer an integration package instead of cURL
branches.path: Use the guided integration package flow that sets key, endpoint, and application/subsystem.
branches.effect_on_first_success: Same visibility terminal with package-specific setup steps.

frictionGates.description: Work-email activation is required before organization and team setup continue.
A Send-Your-Data API key is required; other key types fail ingestion auth.
The ingress endpoint must match the team's Coralogix domain/region.

uncertainties.question: Will a given work-email domain create a new organization or join an existing one?

uncertainties.reason_unresolved: Depends on prior registrations and admin policy outside public docs.

Friction gates are documented requirements or transitions. They are not confirmed drop-off points.
Blocker taxonomy entries are candidate hypotheses. Individual reasons remain not diagnosis eligible unless separate case evidence supports them.

developerGoal: Create a no-cost Capella environment and execute the first query documented by Couchbase against included sample data.

entryPoint.developer_intent: Create a no-cost Capella environment and execute the first query documented by Couchbase against included sample data.
assumed_prior_state: The developer has an accessible email address, or a qualifying GitHub or Google account.
The developer can accept Couchbase's Terms of Service and Privacy Policy.
The developer is creating the first free tier operational cluster in the organization or has access to the organization's existing free tier slot.
The developer can choose an available cloud provider, region, and cluster CIDR block.
The developer is the initial account creator, or otherwise holds Organization Owner or Project Creator permission to create or turn on the cluster.

selectedSurface.name: Create a Capella free tier operational cluster and run Couchbase's first SQL++ query
selectedSurface.selection_basis: Workshop selection policy (2026-07-19): when official docs present peer routes, commit to the fastest and most commonly used documented route. Prefer the vendor's first-listed or recommended quickstart, dashboard/console over CLI when both are peers, and the route with an explicit vendor time claim or fewest documented steps when tied. Selected route: Create a Capella free tier operational cluster and run Couchbase's first SQL++ query. Couchbase's current welcome page, free-tier deployment guide, and sample-data tutorial form a direct chronological path to an explicitly named first query with an observable JSON result. Prior surface before policy commit: Create a Couchbase Capella free tier operational cluster and run the documented first SQL++ query.

alternatives_considered.name: Guided Setup with App Services
alternatives_considered.reason_not_primary: Couchbase recommends this interactive route for new users, but it provisions both an operational cluster and App Services, imports sample data, creates App Service credentials, and demonstrates service integration. Those mobile-sync steps are not necessary for the general database first-query milestone.

alternatives_considered.name: Documents viewer update
alternatives_considered.reason_not_primary: This is an explicit data-success path that edits airport_1261 from Amiens to Rouen, but the same official page names a separate first-query procedure that is a more representative database interaction.

alternatives_considered.name: SDK connection
alternatives_considered.reason_not_primary: The SDK route requires cluster credentials, an allowed IP address, SDK selection and installation, and replacement of a password placeholder. Couchbase lists it after the first query.

alternatives_considered.name: Paid operational cluster
alternatives_considered.reason_not_primary: The paid path adds a support plan, support time zone, availability-zone and service configuration, plus support-plan billing exposure. Its documentation explicitly says it does not apply to free tier operational clusters.

alternatives_considered.name: Couchbase Shell, command-line tools, or IDE extension
alternatives_considered.reason_not_primary: Each route requires credentials and an allowed IP, and some add local downloads, certificates, configuration files, or extension installation before a connection.

firstMeaningfulSuccess.official_milestone: Run Your First Query with the Query Tab
firstMeaningfulSuccess.normalized_outcome: A Couchbase Capella free tier operational cluster exists with the included travel-sample bucket, and the developer executes Couchbase's documented SQL++ query against it.
firstMeaningfulSuccess.observable_completion_signal: After the developer presses Enter or selects Run, Capella automatically displays the query results in JSON format.

boundary_evidence.official_label_or_terminal_state: Run Your First Query with the Query Tab

firstMeaningfulSuccess.why_this_is_the_boundary: The official page labels the procedure `Run Your First Query`, supplies the exact SQL++ statement, and specifies an observable result. Its Next Steps section places SDKs, data import, App Services, credentials, allowed IPs, code generation, and SDK installation after this point.

prerequisites.requirement: A full name, accessible email address, and password with at least 12 characters including uppercase, lowercase, number, and special characters for the direct email route.
A confirmation code delivered to the email address for direct signup. GitHub requires a verified public primary email; a Google account using a non-Google email must be verified.
Agreement to Couchbase's Terms of Service and Privacy Policy before Create Account. Marketing subscription is optional.
An organization can have only one free tier operational cluster at a time.
Organization Owner or Project Creator permission is required to create or turn on an operational cluster.
No payment method or credit card is documented as a requirement for the forever-free operational cluster path.

stages.action: Open Welcome to Couchbase Capella and choose the free tier operational-cluster path.
stages.output: The official signup and free-cluster sequence is identified.
stages.success_signal: The Explore Couchbase Capella list shows Sign Up, account and cluster creation, and sample-data exploration.
stages.failure_or_wait: Capella Analytics does not offer this free tier; the documented route is Capella Operational.

stages.action: Open the Capella signup page and choose Email Address for the primary documented route.
stages.success_signal: Full Name, Email Address, and Password fields are available.
stages.failure_or_wait: GitHub and Google are supported branches with provider-specific email verification conditions.

stages.action: Enter full name, email address, and a password meeting every published rule, then select Get Started.
stages.input: Full name, accessible email, and a password of at least 12 characters with uppercase, lowercase, number, and special character.
stages.output: Couchbase sends a confirmation code by email.
stages.success_signal: The signup interface requests the confirmation code.
stages.failure_or_wait: The guide states the password rules and email-code requirement but does not document validation-error or recovery behavior for this form.

stages.action: Retrieve and enter the confirmation code sent by Couchbase.
stages.output: The documented confirmation-code action is completed.
stages.success_signal: The procedure next requires review of the Terms of Service and Privacy Policy.
stages.failure_or_wait: Email delivery time and any resend behavior are not specified in the guide.

stages.action: Review and agree to the Terms of Service and Privacy Policy, choose whether to receive optional marketing, and select Create Account.
stages.input: Required legal acceptance and optional marketing choice.
stages.success_signal: Capella creates an organization and default project labeled My First Project.
stages.failure_or_wait: The guide requires agreement before Create Account and marks marketing as optional, but does not document a declined-terms state.

stages.action: Choose Getting Started for manual cluster configuration rather than the App Services Guided Setup.
stages.input: The choice between Guided Setup and Getting Started.
stages.output: The direct free tier operational-cluster creation path is active.
stages.success_signal: A Create Cluster control is available for the operational environment.
stages.failure_or_wait: Guided Setup is valid and recommended for new mobile users, but adds App Services, App Service credentials, sample import selection, and an integration demonstration.

stages.action: Select Create Cluster and choose My First Project.
stages.output: The cluster will belong to My First Project.
stages.success_signal: Cluster Option and configuration fields are shown.
stages.failure_or_wait: A user without Organization Owner or Project Creator permission cannot create or turn on the cluster.

stages.action: Under Cluster Option, select Free.
stages.output: The perpetual free tier operational-cluster configuration is selected.
stages.success_signal: Free is selected before cluster deployment.
stages.failure_or_wait: Only one free tier operational cluster is available per organization, and the offer is unavailable in Capella Analytics.

stages.action: Enter a cluster name or accept the default, and optionally provide a description.
stages.output: The cluster has an identity in the project.
stages.success_signal: The name field contains a value.
stages.failure_or_wait: The documentation does not publish the default cluster name or validation rules.

stages.action: Enter a CIDR block or accept the default.
stages.input: A custom cluster CIDR block or the UI-provided default.
stages.output: The cluster network range is configured.
stages.success_signal: The CIDR field contains the chosen or default value.
stages.failure_or_wait: The guide links a separate CIDR configuration reference but does not document validation or recovery behavior in this quick start.

stages.action: Select Create Cluster.
stages.input: Project, Free option, name, optional description, provider, region, and CIDR block.
stages.output: The documented free tier cluster deployment action is submitted.
stages.success_signal: The guide does not name an immediate post-submit state; readiness is established only when the deployed cluster can be used in the next tutorial.
stages.failure_or_wait: The guide documents the one-free-cluster organization limit and required role but does not state a creation-error or retry sequence.

stages.action: Provision the operational cluster and make its included travel-sample bucket available to Data Tools.
stages.input: The free tier cluster configuration submitted through Create Cluster.
stages.output: A free tier operational cluster with travel-sample data is available for exploration.
stages.success_signal: Data Tools can open the cluster's Query tab and the travel-sample bucket is present.
stages.failure_or_wait: The free-tier account guide does not publish an exact provisioning status label or duration. Timing and any transient deployment states remain undocumented for this route.

stages.action: Go to Data Tools, then Query.
stages.input: The deployed free tier operational cluster.
stages.output: The SQL++ query editor opens for the included sample data.
stages.success_signal: The query editor and Run control are visible.
stages.failure_or_wait: If the cluster was turned off after inactivity, an authorized user must turn it back on before querying.

stages.action: Replace the editor text with the exact SQL++ query from Couchbase's Run Your First Query procedure.
stages.input: `SELECT route.airlineid, airline.name, route.sourceairport, route.destinationairport FROM `travel-sample` route INNER JOIN `travel-sample` airline ON route.airlineid = META(airline).id WHERE route.type = "route" AND route.destinationairport = "SFO" ORDER BY route.sourceairport;`
stages.output: The query editor contains the documented join and SFO destination filter.
stages.success_signal: The full statement is present and ready to run.
stages.failure_or_wait: The tutorial does not document query-editor validation or recovery behavior for an altered statement.

stages.action: Press Enter or select Run.
stages.output: Capella executes the query against travel-sample.
stages.success_signal: Query results are automatically displayed in JSON format, completing the documented first-query milestone.
stages.failure_or_wait: Couchbase does not publish a first-query failure or recovery sequence in this tutorial.

branches.condition: GitHub signup
branches.path: Make the GitHub primary email verified and public, choose GitHub, complete provider prompts, accept Couchbase terms and privacy policy, optionally choose marketing, and select Create Account.
branches.effect_on_first_success: Replaces name, password, and Couchbase email-code steps with provider login, but requires a public verified GitHub primary email.

branches.condition: Google signup
branches.path: Choose Google, complete provider prompts, accept Couchbase terms and privacy policy, optionally choose marketing, and select Create Account. Verify any non-Google email used by the Google account first.
branches.effect_on_first_success: Replaces direct password and confirmation-code steps with provider authentication.

branches.condition: Existing 30-day Capella trial account
branches.path: An unexpired trial converts to free tier after the trial period; an expired trial user logs in and selects Activate Free Tier before deploying the operational cluster.
branches.effect_on_first_success: Skips new account creation but adds a state-dependent activation or conversion route.

branches.condition: New mobile or edge developer chooses Guided Setup
branches.path: Open App Services, start Guided Setup, create the free tier cluster, choose sample-data import, create App Services, set an App Service username, endpoint name, and user credentials, then select Connect.
branches.effect_on_first_success: Adds App Services infrastructure and an integrated mobile-sync demonstration before the database query.

branches.condition: Cloud placement choice
branches.path: Choose AWS, Google Cloud, or Azure, then an available provider-specific region and a custom or default CIDR block.
branches.effect_on_first_success: Changes infrastructure placement and networking choices but converges on the same Data Tools query boundary.

branches.condition: Document-oriented exploration
branches.path: Open Data Tools, Documents; select travel-sample, inventory, and airport; set Limit to 8; Get Documents; open airport_1261; change city from Amiens to Rouen; and Save Document.
branches.effect_on_first_success: Produces an alternate explicit first data mutation before or instead of the documented first query.

branches.condition: Application connection after first query
branches.path: Create cluster access credentials, add the current IP to allowed IPs, generate a snippet, select and install an SDK, replace the password placeholder, and optionally download a certificate.
branches.effect_on_first_success: Moves beyond the browser query into application integration and adds secrets, networking, local installation, and code.

branches.condition: Paid cluster instead of free tier
branches.path: Use the separate paid-cluster creation path with project role, service sizing, support plan, time zone, availability zones, and cloud placement.
branches.effect_on_first_success: Adds paid-plan decisions and cannot reuse the free-tier provisioning timing or assumptions because the paid guide explicitly excludes free clusters.

frictionGates.description: Direct signup requires at least 12 characters and four character classes.
The primary route leaves the browser to retrieve a confirmation code, and the guide does not publish delivery or resend timing.
The GitHub branch requires the primary email to be both verified and public, adding a GitHub profile-setting change for developers who keep addresses private.

The recommended Guided Setup combines database and App Services onboarding, while manual Getting Started is the direct route to the general database query. The user must choose based on workload intent.
Capella allows only one free tier operational cluster per organization at any time.
The free path still asks the developer to choose provider, region, and CIDR without naming a universal default combination.
The free-tier guide does not document an exact readiness status or deployment duration before Data Tools can query travel-sample.

After more than 72 hours without activity, Couchbase turns off the free cluster and linked App Services while preserving data and state. An authorized user must select Turn On Cluster before continuing.
The tutorial specifies the successful JSON result but does not provide a dedicated error-recovery sequence for a failed first query.

uncertainties.question: What exact status label and duration indicate that a newly created free tier operational cluster is ready for Data Tools?
uncertainties.impact: The wait between Create Cluster and opening Query cannot be timed or mapped to a named status from the free-tier guide.
uncertainties.reason_unresolved: The free-tier account guide says to deploy the cluster and the next tutorial assumes travel-sample is available, but neither publishes a readiness status or duration. The paid-cluster timing is explicitly inapplicable.

uncertainties.question: Which cloud provider, region, and CIDR values appear as defaults for a specific new account?
uncertainties.impact: Those three configuration choices cannot be reduced to one universal workshop answer.
uncertainties.reason_unresolved: The guide requires a provider and available region and permits the default CIDR without documenting account-wide default values.

uncertainties.question: What exact resend, expiration, and recovery behavior applies to the signup confirmation code?
uncertainties.impact: Email-verification failure recovery and wait time cannot be fully enumerated.
uncertainties.reason_unresolved: The current account guide says Couchbase sends a confirmation code and requires entry, but documents no code lifetime or resend procedure.

uncertainties.question: Does every current account land directly in the default organization and My First Project view after Create Account, or does an intermediate welcome screen appear?
uncertainties.impact: A possible transitional click between account creation and Create Cluster cannot be counted from documentation alone.
uncertainties.reason_unresolved: The guide guarantees automatic organization and default-project creation but does not enumerate the post-submit navigation state.

developerGoal: Send a notification using Courier's API to verify integration and delivery.

assumed_prior_state: Developer has internet access and a modern development environment.
Developer can sign up for a Courier account.
Developer can access Courier dashboard to retrieve API key.
entryPoint.developer_intent: Send a notification using Courier's API to verify integration and delivery.

alternatives_considered.name: Visual Dashboard and Design Studio
alternatives_considered.reason_not_primary: While the visual dashboard and Design Studio are important for content design and lifecycle marketing, the documented first success for developers is sending a notification via the API.

alternatives_considered.name: AI Agent Integration via MCP Server and CLI
alternatives_considered.reason_not_primary: Targeted at AI coding agents, this is a specialized integration path rather than the primary onboarding for general developers.

selectedSurface.name: REST API with SDKs and CLI
selectedSurface.selection_basis: The official documentation emphasizes sending notifications via a single REST API call, supported by multiple server SDKs (Node.js, Python, Ruby, Go) and a CLI tool, making this the primary developer surface for first-mile onboarding.

boundary_evidence.official_label_or_terminal_state: Message delivered as shown in Message Logs timeline

firstMeaningfulSuccess.normalized_outcome: A notification message is successfully sent using the API key and appears as delivered in the Courier dashboard logs.
firstMeaningfulSuccess.observable_completion_signal: Message appears in Message Logs with timeline stages: accepted, routed, rendered, sent, and delivered.
firstMeaningfulSuccess.official_milestone: Send a notification message via the Courier API and verify its delivery in the dashboard logs.
firstMeaningfulSuccess.why_this_is_the_boundary: The documentation explicitly defines sending a message and seeing it delivered in the dashboard logs as the first success milestone for developers integrating Courier.

prerequisites.requirement: Sign up for a Courier account at https://app.courier.com/signup.
Obtain an API key from the Courier dashboard under Settings > API Keys.
Have a development environment capable of making HTTP requests or running SDKs/CLI (e.g., curl, Node.js, Python).

stages.action: Sign up or log in to Courier dashboard.
details: Access https://app.courier.com/signup or https://app.courier.com/login.
stages.failure_or_wait: Authentication failure or account creation errors.
stages.success_signal: Access to Courier dashboard.

stages.action: Copy API key from Settings > API Keys in the dashboard.
details: Locate API key in dashboard settings.
Copy key for use in API calls.
stages.failure_or_wait: API key not found or permission denied.
stages.success_signal: API key copied successfully.

stages.action: Set environment variable COURIER_API_KEY with the copied API key.
details: Export environment variable in shell or set in development environment.
stages.failure_or_wait: Environment variable not set or inaccessible.
stages.success_signal: Environment variable accessible to SDK or CLI.

stages.action: Send a notification message using curl, CLI, or SDK with the API key.
details: Use provided example payload with recipient email, message content, and routing method.
Replace placeholders YOUR_API_KEY and you@example.com appropriately.
stages.failure_or_wait: API errors such as authentication failure or malformed request.
stages.input: API key, recipient email, message content JSON.
stages.success_signal: Response includes a requestId indicating message accepted.

stages.action: Open Message Logs in Courier dashboard to verify message delivery status.
details: Navigate to https://app.courier.com/logs.
Check timeline stages: accepted, routed, rendered, sent, delivered.
stages.failure_or_wait: Message not found or delivery failed.
stages.output: Message delivery timeline visible in dashboard.
stages.success_signal: Message shows delivered status in logs.

candidatePaths.name: AI Agent Integration Path
candidatePaths.relationship_to_primary_path: Alternative path targeting AI agents rather than general developers; shares steps like API key setup and sending messages but includes agent-specific tooling installation.
candidatePaths.selection_basis: Documented onboarding for AI coding agents using Courier's MCP server and CLI tooling.

steps.action: Sign up or log in to Courier dashboard.
details: Access https://app.courier.com/signup or https://app.courier.com/login.

steps.action: Copy API key from Settings > API Keys.
details: Locate API key in dashboard settings.

steps.action: Set environment variable COURIER_API_KEY with the API key.
details: Export environment variable.

steps.action: Install AI agent tooling such as Courier CLI and configure MCP server.
details: Run npm install -g @trycourier/cli.
Add MCP server configuration with API key.
steps.output: Configured MCP server for AI agent.
steps.success_signal: Agent tooling ready to send messages.

steps.action: Send first message using CLI or curl with AI agent tooling.
details: Use example commands to send message with routing method single.

frictionGates.description: User must create a Courier account to obtain API key.
API key is required to authenticate API calls.
Correctly format and send API request with valid recipient and message content.
Access to Courier dashboard Message Logs to verify delivery.

developerGoal: Run the CyberArk Conjur OSS Docker Compose demo, create its account and policy identities, store a generated secret, and retrieve it from the demo application.

entryPoint.developer_intent: Run the CyberArk Conjur OSS Docker Compose demo, create its account and policy identities, store a generated secret, and retrieve it from the demo application.
assumed_prior_state: Docker Desktop with Docker Compose, Git, OpenSSL, a shell, and permission to run local containers are available.
No CyberArk account, Conjur installation, Conjur account, data key, policy, identity, API key, stored secret, or running demo is assumed.
The route is explicitly a local Conjur OSS learning demo, not a production setup or a product-neutral CyberArk onboarding path.
No repository clone, container start, key generation, policy load, authentication, secret write, or retrieval was performed during research.

selectedSurface.name: Conjur OSS Docker Compose store-and-retrieve demo
selectedSurface.selection_basis: Workshop selection policy (2026-07-19): when official docs present peer routes, commit to the fastest and most commonly used documented route. Prefer the vendor's first-listed or recommended quickstart, dashboard/console over CLI when both are peers, and the route with an explicit vendor time claim or fewest documented steps when tied. Selected route: Conjur OSS Docker Compose store-and-retrieve demo. The CyberArk-owned repository publishes every required command, generated credential, expected intermediate signal, stored variable, retrieval program, and final congratulations terminal. Prior surface before policy commit: CyberArk Conjur Open Source Docker Compose quickstart.

alternatives_considered.name: CyberArk commercial developer portal
alternatives_considered.reason_not_primary: The current URL redirects away from a CyberArk developer start and does not establish a reproducible current product-neutral onboarding path.

alternatives_considered.name: Production Conjur deployment
alternatives_considered.reason_not_primary: The official repository explicitly says the quickstart is for learning and demonstration only and must not be used in production.

firstMeaningfulSuccess.official_milestone: Run the demo application and retrieve the secret from Conjur.
firstMeaningfulSuccess.normalized_outcome: The local BotApp authenticates to Conjur OSS, fetches the generated value stored in `BotApp/secretVar`, displays it, and reaches the quickstart congratulations message.
firstMeaningfulSuccess.observable_completion_signal: `/tmp/program.sh` displays the stored secret and the repository says, `Congratulations! You are ready to secure your own apps with Conjur.`

boundary_evidence.official_label_or_terminal_state: The demo application displays the secret followed by the published congratulations message.

firstMeaningfulSuccess.why_this_is_the_boundary: The repository's ordered walkthrough installs Conjur, defines policy, stores a secret, and runs an application that retrieves it. That retrieval completes the candidate before cleanup and production guidance.

prerequisites.requirement: Install Docker Desktop with Docker Compose, Git, and OpenSSL.
Have a shell that supports the published environment-variable and command-substitution syntax.
Allow cloning from GitHub and pulling the published container images.

stages.action: Open the CyberArk Conjur quickstart repository and accept its demo-only scope and support caveat.
stages.output: Requirements and the complete Docker Compose walkthrough are visible.
stages.success_signal: The repository identifies a sample Conjur OSS install and warns against production use.
stages.failure_or_wait: Human judgment is required to accept Conjur OSS as the CyberArk surface because it is not designated as CyberArk's universal first product.

stages.action: Pull the quickstart container images.
details: Run `docker compose pull`.
stages.success_signal: Compose reports the images as Pulled.
stages.failure_or_wait: Registry, network, disk, or Docker errors must be resolved.

stages.action: Generate the Conjur data key and save it to `data_key`.
details: Run `docker compose run --no-deps --rm conjur data-key generate > data_key`.
stages.output: A Compose network is created and `data_key` contains the generated key.
stages.success_signal: The data-key command completes and the file exists.
stages.failure_or_wait: Container or filesystem errors stop initialization.

stages.action: Export the generated key and start the quickstart services.
details: Run `export CONJUR_DATA_KEY="$(< data_key)"`.
Run `docker compose up -d`.
stages.output: The quickstart containers start in the background.
stages.success_signal: Compose reports containers Started.
stages.failure_or_wait: Missing key, port, health, or Docker errors prevent startup.

stages.action: Run `docker ps` and verify the expected services are running.
stages.output: Docker lists the active quickstart containers.
stages.success_signal: The expected Conjur, proxy, client, database, and demo services are running.
stages.failure_or_wait: Stopped or unhealthy services must be diagnosed before account creation.

stages.action: Create the Conjur account and save its administrator data.
details: Run `docker compose exec conjur conjurctl account create myConjurAccount > admin_data`.
stages.output: Account output, including administrator credentials, is written to `admin_data`.
stages.success_signal: The terminal reports `Created new account 'myConjurAccount'`.
stages.failure_or_wait: Service or account-creation errors stop initialization.

stages.action: Initialize the client for the local self-signed Conjur OSS service.
details: Run `docker compose exec client conjur init oss -u https://proxy -a myConjurAccount --self-signed`.
At the trust prompt, enter `y`.
Select Conjur Open Source when prompted.
stages.input: Proxy URL, account name, and acceptance of the local self-signed certificate.
stages.output: The client writes its certificate and configuration.
stages.success_signal: Initialization completes and the Conjur configuration is written.
stages.failure_or_wait: Rejecting certificate trust or choosing the wrong product prevents this demo configuration.

stages.action: Log in as the Conjur administrator using the API key stored in `admin_data`.
details: Run `docker compose exec client conjur login -i admin`.
Supply the administrator API key when prompted.
stages.output: The client establishes an administrator session.
stages.success_signal: The CLI prints `Logged in`.
stages.failure_or_wait: An incorrect API key prevents policy loading.

stages.action: Load the BotApp policy and save its generated identity keys.
details: Run `docker compose exec client conjur policy load -b root -f policy/BotApp.yml > my_app_data`.
stages.input: Administrator session and published policy file.
stages.output: The policy is loaded and generated keys for Dave and BotApp are written to `my_app_data`.
stages.success_signal: The CLI reports `Loaded policy 'root'`.
stages.failure_or_wait: Authentication, policy, or file errors stop identity creation.

stages.action: Log out the administrator, log in as `Dave@BotApp` with the saved API key, and verify the identity.
details: Run the published logout command.
Run `docker compose exec client conjur login -i Dave@BotApp` and supply Dave's API key from `my_app_data`.
Run `docker compose exec client conjur whoami`.
stages.output: The CLI returns the authenticated identity as JSON.
stages.success_signal: Login succeeds and `whoami` identifies Dave in BotApp.
stages.failure_or_wait: A wrong saved key prevents the secret write.

stages.action: Generate a random secret and store it in the policy variable.
details: Run `secretVal=$(openssl rand -hex 12 | tr -d '\r\n')`.
Run `docker compose exec client conjur variable set -i BotApp/secretVar -v ${secretVal}`.
stages.input: The authenticated Dave identity and OpenSSL.
stages.output: The generated value is stored in `BotApp/secretVar`.
stages.success_signal: The CLI reports `Value added`.
stages.failure_or_wait: Identity, authorization, variable-path, or shell errors prevent storage.

stages.action: Enter the BotApp container and request a short-lived Conjur authentication token with the BotApp API key.
details: Run `docker exec -it bot_app bash`.
Run the published `curl` authentication command against `https://proxy/authn/myConjurAccount/host%2FBotApp%2FmyDemoApp/authenticate`, writing the response to `/tmp/conjur_token`.
stages.output: A Conjur authentication token is saved inside the demo container.
stages.success_signal: The token file is created without an authentication error.
stages.failure_or_wait: The token expires after eight minutes, so delay requires regeneration before secret retrieval.

stages.action: Run the demo program and verify that it retrieves and displays the stored secret.
details: Run `/tmp/program.sh` inside `bot_app`.
stages.input: A valid short-lived token and the stored `BotApp/secretVar` value.
stages.output: The demo fetches and displays the secret.
stages.success_signal: The stored value is displayed and the quickstart reaches `Congratulations! You are ready to secure your own apps with Conjur.`
stages.failure_or_wait: If no secret appears, regenerate the authentication token and retry within its eight-minute window.

stages.action: Clone the repository, select a project version compatible with the current Conjur OSS suite release when possible, enter it, and run `docker compose pull`.
details: The repository strongly recommends a version from the latest Conjur OSS suite release because suite-release versions receive additional compatibility testing.
stages.output: Quickstart files and images are local.
stages.success_signal: Images report Pulled.
stages.failure_or_wait: Clone, version-selection, or pull failures stop setup.

stages.action: Generate the Conjur data key into `data_key`, export `CONJUR_DATA_KEY`, and run `docker compose up -d`.
stages.output: Services start with the generated key.
stages.success_signal: Containers report Started.
stages.failure_or_wait: Key or Docker errors prevent startup.

stages.action: Create `myConjurAccount` into `admin_data` and initialize the client for `https://proxy` with the self-signed OSS option.
stages.output: Account credentials and client configuration exist.
stages.success_signal: The account is created and client config is written.
stages.failure_or_wait: Account, trust, or product-selection errors stop setup.

stages.action: Log in as admin and load `policy/BotApp.yml` into root, saving generated keys to `my_app_data`.
stages.success_signal: The CLI reports `Loaded policy 'root'`.
stages.failure_or_wait: Authentication or policy errors stop setup.

stages.action: Log in as `Dave@BotApp`, verify with `whoami`, generate a random value, and set `BotApp/secretVar`.
stages.success_signal: The CLI reports `Value added`.
stages.failure_or_wait: Identity or authorization errors block the write.

stages.action: Enter `bot_app`, authenticate the application into `/tmp/conjur_token`, and run `/tmp/program.sh`.
stages.input: BotApp API key and stored secret.
stages.output: The demo retrieves and displays the secret.
stages.success_signal: The value and congratulations message appear.
stages.failure_or_wait: Regenerate the token if more than eight minutes elapsed.

branches.condition: The goal is a commercial CyberArk product or production deployment
branches.path: Stop and select that product's current supported documentation and entitlement path instead of using this demo.
branches.effect_on_first_success: Changes the product, access model, prerequisites, and terminal; the Conjur OSS candidate cannot be generalized.

branches.condition: The application token is older than eight minutes or no secret is returned
branches.path: Repeat the published authentication request to regenerate `/tmp/conjur_token`, then run the program again.
branches.effect_on_first_success: Adds a documented recovery cycle before retrieval.

frictionGates.description: Conjur OSS is one CyberArk product candidate and is not established as the product-neutral CyberArk first mile.
The repository labels the route Community level, community contributed, not reviewed or supported by CyberArk, and unsuitable for production.
The route pulls multiple Docker images before initialization.
The demo runs several local containers and depends on Docker resources, ports, and health.
The local client requires explicit trust of the demo's self-signed certificate.
Administrator, Dave, and BotApp API keys are generated into local files and must be selected at the correct steps.
The application authentication token is usable for only eight minutes before retrieval must reauthenticate.

uncertainties.question: Should a CyberArk-wide first mile use this Conjur OSS demo or a different current commercial product surface?
uncertainties.impact: The selected product changes entitlements, account access, credentials, setup, implementation, and the meaning of first success.
uncertainties.reason_unresolved: The fully documented candidate is product-specific and its own CyberArk-owned source labels it community contributed, not reviewed, and unsupported rather than a universal supported onboarding path.

developerGoal: Start without a Dagster account or project, install the local prerequisites, scaffold the official quickstart project, define its sample ETL asset, materialize it in the local Dagster UI, and verify the transformed CSV.

entryPoint.developer_intent: Start without a Dagster account or project, install the local prerequisites, scaffold the official quickstart project, define its sample ETL asset, materialize it in the local Dagster UI, and verify the transformed CSV.
assumed_prior_state: No Dagster account, Dagster installation, project, virtual environment, asset definition, sample data, pipeline run, or cloud deployment is assumed.
A supported Mac, Windows, or Linux computer, terminal, browser, text editor, network connection, and permission to install local software are available.
No installation, project scaffolding, code editing, local server start, or materialization was performed during research.

selectedSurface.name: Dagster OSS quickstart first local ETL pipeline
selectedSurface.selection_basis: The official documentation home presents Build your first Dagster pipeline as its general quickstart. That guide selects Dagster OSS for local development, creates one asset containing a complete CSV extract, transform, and load operation, runs it from the Dagster UI, and explicitly congratulates the developer for building and running a first pipeline.

alternatives_considered.name: Dagster+ Serverless quickstart
alternatives_considered.reason_not_primary: The OSS quickstart is the general next page from the documentation home and does not require a Dagster+ account or hosted project.

alternatives_considered.name: Dagster+ Hybrid deployment
alternatives_considered.reason_not_primary: The quickstart makes Hybrid deployment optional after the local pipeline has run.

alternatives_considered.name: Dagster basics tutorial
alternatives_considered.reason_not_primary: The longer tutorial is a next step after the bounded quickstart's first pipeline.

firstMeaningfulSuccess.official_milestone: Build and run your first pipeline with Dagster.
firstMeaningfulSuccess.normalized_outcome: The official local project materializes its `processed_data` ETL asset and writes the transformed sample rows, including the new `age_group` column, to `processed_data.csv`.
firstMeaningfulSuccess.observable_completion_signal: Running `cat src/dagster_quickstart/defs/data/processed_data.csv` shows the documented header `id,name,age,city,age_group` and the four transformed rows ending in Young, Middle, Senior, and Middle.

boundary_evidence.official_label_or_terminal_state: The guide is titled Build your first Dagster pipeline and ends the local run and verification sequence with 'Congratulations! You've just built and run your first pipeline with Dagster.'

firstMeaningfulSuccess.why_this_is_the_boundary: The materialized asset and verified output are the guide's first complete data-pipeline result. Production deployment, additional assets, automation, testing, and observability are later steps.

prerequisites.requirement: Install Python 3.10 or higher; the installation guide recommends Python 3.13.
Install the `uv` Python package manager for the recommended quickstart branch; installing it also provides `uvx`.
Allow the scaffold and dependency commands to download the current Dagster tooling, project dependencies, and pandas.

stages.action: Open the Dagster documentation home and follow the Quickstart link to Build your first Dagster pipeline.
details: The home page describes the quickstart as the way to build a first Dagster pipeline.
The selected guide uses Dagster OSS for local development.
stages.output: The official local quickstart and its prerequisites are visible.
stages.success_signal: The guide begins with Step 1: Scaffold a new Dagster project.

stages.action: Install Python 3.10 or higher and install `uv` with the operating-system command shown in the Dagster installation guide.
details: Mac and Linux use `curl -LsSf https://astral.sh/uv/install.sh | sh`.
Windows uses the documented PowerShell installer command.
The `uv` installation supplies `uvx`.
stages.input: A supported computer with terminal and installation permission
stages.output: Python and the recommended `uv` and `uvx` commands are available.
stages.success_signal: The environment can invoke `uvx` for the next scaffold command.

stages.action: Run `uvx create-dagster@latest project dagster-quickstart` and answer `y` when prompted to run `uv sync`.
details: The command scaffolds the project and the accepted prompt creates its virtual environment and installs dependencies.
stages.output: A `dagster-quickstart` project with `pyproject.toml`, source package, definitions, tests, and lock file is created.
stages.success_signal: The generated project has the structure displayed by the guide.

stages.action: Change to `dagster-quickstart`, activate `.venv` with the operating-system command, and run `uv add pandas`.
details: Mac and Unix activate with `source .venv/bin/activate`.
Windows activates with `.venv\Scripts\activate`.
stages.output: The project's virtual environment is active and pandas is added.
stages.success_signal: The environment provides `dg` and the project can import pandas.

stages.action: Run `dg scaffold defs dagster.asset assets.py`.
details: The command adds `assets.py` under `src/dagster_quickstart/defs`.
stages.output: An asset-definition file is scaffolded in the project.
stages.success_signal: The `defs` directory contains `assets.py`.

stages.action: Create `src/dagster_quickstart/defs/data/sample_data.csv` and copy the guide's four-row sample dataset into it.
details: Create the directory and file with `mkdir src/dagster_quickstart/defs/data && touch src/dagster_quickstart/defs/data/sample_data.csv`.
The input columns are `id,name,age,city` for Alice, Bob, Charlie, and Diana.
stages.input: The exact sample CSV shown in the quickstart
stages.output: The source CSV exists at the path used by the asset.
stages.success_signal: The file contains the documented header and four rows.

stages.action: Replace `assets.py` with the guide's `processed_data` asset definition.
details: The code reads the sample CSV with pandas, adds `age_group` using bins and labels, writes `processed_data.csv`, and returns `Data loaded successfully`.
stages.output: The project defines one asset representing the entire ETL operation.
stages.success_signal: The file contains the documented `processed_data` asset definition and its input and output paths.

stages.action: From the project root, run `dg dev` and open `http://localhost:3000`.
details: The command starts the local Dagster development service and UI.
stages.output: The local Dagster UI displays the project.
stages.success_signal: The browser shows the Dagster UI and its Assets tab.

stages.action: Open Assets, click View lineage, and click Materialize.
details: The guide uses the lineage page's Materialize action to run the pipeline.
stages.input: The `processed_data` asset in the local UI
stages.output: Dagster launches a materialization run for the ETL asset.
stages.success_signal: A run is available from the Runs tab.

stages.action: Open the Runs tab and click View for the executing run.
details: The run view exposes the execution of the materialization selected by the guide.
stages.output: The developer can inspect the run in Dagster's UI.
stages.success_signal: The run view is displayed.

stages.action: Run `cat src/dagster_quickstart/defs/data/processed_data.csv`.
details: The guide says the output should contain the added `age_group` column and the four transformed rows.
stages.output: The transformed CSV is printed in the terminal.
stages.success_signal: The output matches the documented header and Young, Middle, Senior, and Middle values, establishing the first pipeline result.

branches.condition: The developer uses Windows
branches.path: Install `uv` with the documented PowerShell command and activate the generated environment with `.venv\Scripts\activate`.
branches.effect_on_first_success: Changes installation and activation syntax only.

branches.condition: The developer uses Mac or Linux
branches.path: Install `uv` with the documented shell installer and activate with `source .venv/bin/activate`.
branches.effect_on_first_success: Uses the Unix shell commands shown in the guide.

branches.condition: The developer uses pip instead of recommended `uv`
branches.path: Install `create-dagster`, scaffold the project, create and activate `.venv`, install pandas, and install the project editable with the documented pip commands; pip earlier than 25.1 requires an upgrade or separate CLI installation.
branches.effect_on_first_success: Adds manual environment and editable-project installation before the same asset steps.

branches.condition: The developer wants pre-run validation
branches.path: Run `dg list defs` to see `processed_data`, then `dg check defs` to load and validate definitions.
branches.effect_on_first_success: Adds an optional definition check before starting the UI.

branches.condition: The developer runs from the CLI instead of the UI
branches.path: Run `dg launch --assets "*"`.
branches.effect_on_first_success: Replaces UI materialization with the documented CLI launch while keeping file verification.

frictionGates.description: The local route requires Python 3.10 or higher and the selected package-manager tooling.
Installation and virtual-environment activation commands differ between Windows and Mac or Linux.
Project scaffolding and `uv sync` install the generated project's dependencies.
The developer must create the exact sample data file and Python asset at the paths used by the published code.
The developer must run the local service and open its browser UI at localhost.

selectedSurface.name: Daily room via cURL REST API then Prebuilt room URL

stages.action: Create or sign in to a Daily account and open the dashboard.
