developerGoal: Choose one Datadog product and first telemetry result; the representative path installs a Linux host Agent and verifies its default Agent metric in Datadog.

entryPoint.developer_intent: Choose one Datadog product and first telemetry result; the representative path installs a Linux host Agent and verifies its default Agent metric in Datadog.
assumed_prior_state: No Datadog account, organization, site, API key, product, monitored environment, or platform is assumed.
For the representative route, the developer has a supported Linux host but has not installed the Agent.

selectedSurface.name: Linux host Agent reporting via Fleet Automation
selectedSurface.selection_basis: Workshop selection policy (2026-07-19): when official docs present peer routes, commit to the fastest and most commonly used documented route. Prefer the vendor's first-listed or recommended quickstart, dashboard/console over CLI when both are peers, and the route with an explicit vendor time claim or fewest documented steps when tied. Committed to reconstructed surface `Unresolved product and platform, with Linux host Agent reporting as the representative infrastructure route`. Prior: Datadog's Getting Started page describes a many-product observability platform and directs developers to separate guides for Agent, API, integrations, tracing, logs, RUM, Synthetic Monitoring, CI Visibility, security, and other products. The docs homepage lists Install the Agent first, and the Agent guide provides an observable default-metric result, so a Linux host Agent is preserved as the representative candidate. Neither the product nor Linux platform is a Datadog-wide default.

alternatives_considered.name: Datadog HTTP API
alternatives_considered.reason_not_primary: This route uses API and application keys and a first API request rather than installed telemetry.

alternatives_considered.name: Getting Started with APM Tracing
alternatives_considered.reason_not_primary: This explicitly sends a first trace but requires a language, sample application, Agent, and instrumentation choice.

alternatives_considered.name: Logs
alternatives_considered.reason_not_primary: First logs use source-specific collection and processing instead of default infrastructure metrics.

alternatives_considered.name: Synthetic Monitoring
alternatives_considered.reason_not_primary: This creates API, browser, mobile, or network tests and has different location, assertion, and billing choices.

alternatives_considered.name: Container, cloud, and configuration-management Agents
alternatives_considered.reason_not_primary: These are peer Agent platforms with different credentials, manifests, permissions, and verification.

alternatives_considered.name: Datadog Learning Center Quick Start
alternatives_considered.reason_not_primary: This is a hosted training route with a trial account and real cloud compute rather than onboarding the developer's actual environment.

firstMeaningfulSuccess.official_milestone: In the selected Linux host Agent route, first success is demonstrated when Metrics Summary shows datadog.agent.started or datadog.agent.running and the metric panel includes the reporting host and Agent version tags.
firstMeaningfulSuccess.normalized_outcome: Obtain Datadog organization access, select the correct Datadog site and Linux host platform, use Fleet Automation's in-app generated script to install and configure Agent 7, then verify an Agent metric with host and version metadata.
firstMeaningfulSuccess.observable_completion_signal: Metrics Summary shows datadog.agent.started or datadog.agent.running, and the opened Metric panel includes the reporting host and Agent version tags.

boundary_evidence.official_label_or_terminal_state: The panel shows host and Agent version metadata, confirming the Agent is reporting.

firstMeaningfulSuccess.why_this_is_the_boundary: Committed under workshop fastest/most-used policy; terminal taken from the reconstructed official path.

prerequisites.requirement: An authenticated user in a Datadog organization. The current self-service route is a 14-day free trial with no credit card; it requires a business email, password, first and last name, job title, company, acceptance of the Datadog Subscription Agreement and Privacy Policy, and optionally a phone number.
The correct Datadog site or region for the organization, such as datadoghq.com, datadoghq.eu, or a government site. The trial form warns that data cannot be migrated between regions after signup.
For the representative route, a Linux host on a version supported by the current Agent.
Administrative access to the Linux host to execute the in-app installation script, install packages, create services and files, and start the Agent.
An organization API key for the Agent to submit metrics and events.
Outbound network access from the host to Datadog package, key, intake, and selected-site endpoints, directly or through a configured proxy.

stages.action: Open Datadog Getting Started and choose a product area.
details: The platform lists separate guides for UI, sites, Agent, API, integrations, containers, tracing, logs, CI, testing, security, RUM, and more.
stages.input: Intent to gain a first Datadog result.
stages.success_signal: The developer names the product and observable data type.
stages.failure_or_wait: No product-wide default is documented.

stages.action: Open the Datadog free-trial form or use an administrator invitation.
details: The self-service trial lasts 14 days and does not require a credit card.
The form links the Datadog Subscription Agreement and Privacy Policy.
Invited users instead receive an email with a Join Account link valid for 48 hours.
stages.input: Eligible business identity, trial route, or administrator invite.
stages.output: Trial signup form or invitation route.
stages.success_signal: The correct account-creation route is open.
stages.failure_or_wait: An invitation can expire; organization policy can constrain the login method.

stages.action: Enter a business email address for the self-service trial.
details: The form labels this field Business Email.
stages.output: Email value accepted by the form.
stages.success_signal: The account details step becomes available.
stages.failure_or_wait: An invalid or ineligible email prevents continuation.

stages.action: Choose the Datadog data region for the trial organization.
details: The form warns to choose carefully because data cannot be migrated from one region to another.
stages.success_signal: The region is recorded in the signup form.
stages.failure_or_wait: Choosing the wrong region creates a non-migratable organization placement for this signup.

stages.action: Enter the remaining trial-account details.
details: Create a password and enter first name, last name, job title, and company.
A phone number can be entered but is marked optional.
stages.input: Password, first and last name, job title, company, and optional phone number.
stages.success_signal: All required identity and company fields pass form validation.
stages.failure_or_wait: Missing required fields or an unacceptable password prevents submission.

stages.action: Review the linked agreements and create the trial account.
details: Submitting the form indicates agreement to the Datadog Subscription Agreement and acknowledgment of the Privacy Policy.
stages.input: Completed form and acceptance of the linked legal terms.
stages.success_signal: The account is created and the Datadog app can be opened.
stages.failure_or_wait: Form rejection or any post-submit verification required by the live flow delays access.

stages.action: Confirm the Datadog organization and site.
details: API keys are organization-specific.
The Agent site defaults to datadoghq.com but must match the organization's region or government site.
stages.success_signal: The app and later Agent configuration target the same organization and site.
stages.failure_or_wait: A mismatched site can install an Agent that does not report to the viewed organization.

stages.action: Choose Agent and then choose the Agent platform.
details: Agent choices include host-based, container platforms, cloud platforms, and configuration management.
For the representative route, choose host-based Linux.
stages.input: Infrastructure telemetry intent and supported Linux host.
stages.success_signal: The Linux installation instructions are selected.
stages.failure_or_wait: Another platform changes installation and configuration.

stages.action: Open Fleet Automation's Agent installation workflow for the Linux host.
details: Datadog calls Fleet Automation the primary in-app workflow for installing, upgrading, configuring, and troubleshooting the Agent at scale.
The Linux docs direct the developer to follow in-app instructions and run the generated script.
stages.input: Datadog session, correct organization/site, and Linux target.
stages.output: Platform-specific Agent installation form and generated script.
stages.success_signal: The page generates a Linux installation command for the organization.
stages.failure_or_wait: App permissions or unsupported platform selection can block command generation.

stages.action: Include the organization API key and target site in the generated Agent configuration.
details: The Agent needs an API key to submit metrics and events.
Linux datadog.yaml stores api_key and site, while the generated script normally carries the required organization context.
stages.input: Organization API key and Datadog site.
stages.success_signal: The generated command is scoped to the intended organization and site.
stages.failure_or_wait: A missing, revoked, or incorrect API key prevents reporting.

stages.action: Run the generated Fleet Automation installation script on the Linux host.
details: Execute the exact in-app command rather than copying a generic command from another account.
The installation writes the Agent package and configuration and sets up the service.
stages.input: Administrative shell on supported Linux, generated command, and outbound network.
stages.success_signal: The package and service installation complete without an error.
stages.failure_or_wait: Package repository, signing key, network, proxy, sudo, distribution, architecture, or API-key errors can fail installation.

stages.action: Start the Agent service and begin default checks.
details: The Agent collects its own health metrics plus default CPU, disk, IO, memory, network, NTP, uptime, file-handle, and load checks where supported.
datadog.agent.running has value 1 when the Agent is running and reporting; datadog.agent.started increments on start.
stages.success_signal: The Agent service is running and can connect to Datadog.
stages.failure_or_wait: Service startup or intake connectivity can fail even after package installation.

stages.action: Inspect local Agent status if the installation workflow requests or metrics do not appear.
details: Linux Agent commands require appropriate permissions.
The Agent docs provide status, help, check, log, and troubleshooting routes.
stages.success_signal: Agent status indicates a running service and successful checks.
stages.failure_or_wait: Configuration, service, or connectivity errors require troubleshooting.

stages.action: Open Metrics Summary in the Datadog app.
details: This is the Agent guide's documented UI verification route.
stages.input: Authenticated Datadog session in the correct organization.
stages.success_signal: The metric search is available.
stages.failure_or_wait: Wrong organization or site hides the expected metric.

stages.action: Search for datadog.agent.started or datadog.agent.running.
details: If metrics are not visible immediately, the guide says it may take a few minutes for the Agent to send data.
Other default examples include ntp.offset and system.cpu.idle.
stages.success_signal: At least one default Agent metric appears.
stages.failure_or_wait: Wait a few minutes, then investigate service, API key, site, network, or host if no metric arrives.

stages.action: Open the Agent metric and inspect its metadata.
details: Select either matching metric to open the Metric panel.
Without custom host tags, the panel should show Datadog's default version and host tags.
stages.output: Metric panel for the reporting Agent.
stages.success_signal: The panel shows host and Agent version metadata, confirming the Agent is reporting.
stages.failure_or_wait: Missing host/version data or an unexpected host means the documented result has not been verified.

branches.condition: An administrator invites the developer to an existing organization.
branches.path: The administrator opens Organization Settings > Users, selects Invite Users, enters email, assigns permitted roles, and sends the invite. The developer opens the email within 48 hours, selects Join Account, and signs in using an organization-allowed login method.
branches.effect_on_first_success: Adds an administrator actor, email handoff, role choice, and invitation-expiry wait.

branches.condition: The developer uses Google, password, or SAML login.
branches.path: Use a login method enabled by the organization. Google users are automatically email-verified at first login; password users verify while setting the first password; SAML users manually verify email for the additional account-management features described in the docs.
branches.effect_on_first_success: Changes identity, verification, and possible administrator coordination.

branches.condition: The monitored target is a container, Kubernetes cluster, cloud integration, configuration-managed fleet, or another operating system.
branches.path: Select that Agent or integration platform and follow its official installation guide, credentials, permissions, manifest or package steps, then verify its documented telemetry in Datadog.
branches.effect_on_first_success: Changes required infrastructure permissions, installation artifact, API-key injection, service model, and success telemetry.

branches.condition: The intended first success is API, APM, logs, RUM, Synthetic Monitoring, CI Visibility, security, or another Datadog product.
branches.path: Follow that product's official getting-started path and define its own credential, source, setup, send/test, and UI verification sequence.
branches.effect_on_first_success: Replaces Agent installation and default metrics with another data type and terminal signal.

frictionGates.description: Datadog exposes many product-specific starting paths without one platform-wide success.
The developer needs a 14-day no-credit-card trial or organization membership and an enabled login method.
Existing-organization access can require an administrator invitation and role assignment.
The self-service trial requires a data region choice that the form says cannot later be migrated.
Creating the account requires agreement to the Datadog Subscription Agreement and acknowledgment of the Privacy Policy.
The Agent must report to the organization's correct Datadog site.
Agent installation branches across host, container, cloud, and configuration-management platforms.
The Agent requires an organization API key.
The developer must execute a privileged, platform-specific in-app installation script on the monitored host.
Package installation and telemetry submission require supported Linux and outbound access, or a proxy.
New Agent metrics may take a few minutes to become visible.

uncertainties.question: Which Datadog product should represent the platform?
uncertainties.impact: Agent metrics, API requests, traces, logs, RUM sessions, Synthetic tests, CI data, and security findings have unrelated first paths and success signals.
uncertainties.reason_unresolved: The official Getting Started page routes to many product areas, and the roster gives no intended telemetry.

uncertainties.question: Which Agent platform and operating-system version applies?
uncertainties.impact: Host, container, Kubernetes, cloud, and configuration-management routes use different installation commands, permissions, credentials, and verification.
uncertainties.reason_unresolved: Agent documentation requires a platform choice; Linux is only the representative candidate.

uncertainties.question: Does the live self-service trial require any post-submit email verification or additional onboarding screen before the app opens?
uncertainties.impact: A post-submit verification or onboarding screen would add another access step before Agent setup.
uncertainties.reason_unresolved: The public trial form exposes the required input fields and create-account action, but the authenticated post-submit state cannot be observed without creating an account.

uncertainties.question: What exact command will Fleet Automation generate for the account?
uncertainties.impact: The script varies by Datadog site, API key, operating system, Agent options, and current release.
uncertainties.reason_unresolved: Current Linux docs intentionally delegate to the authenticated in-app generated script; fabricating a generic command would risk wrong credentials or region.

developerGoal: Start without dbt software or a project, follow the official dbt Core and DuckDB quickstart through either its Local or Web browser route, build the Jaffle Shop project, and observe the documented successful build summary.

entryPoint.developer_intent: Start without dbt software or a project, follow the official dbt Core and DuckDB quickstart through either its Local or Web browser route, build the Jaffle Shop project, and observe the documented successful build summary.
assumed_prior_state: No dbt platform account, dbt Core installation, DuckDB installation, Jaffle Shop repository, virtual environment, profile, database file, models, seeds, or tests are assumed.
For the fully reconstructed Local Mac candidate, a Mac with Python, Git, terminal knowledge, network access, and permission to create a virtual environment and install packages is available.
No repository clone, GitHub login, Codespaces creation, package installation, database build, or query was performed during research.

selectedSurface.name: Local Mac Jaffle Shop DuckDB build
selectedSurface.selection_basis: Workshop selection policy (2026-07-19): when official docs present peer routes, commit to the fastest and most commonly used documented route. Prefer the vendor's first-listed or recommended quickstart, dashboard/console over CLI when both are peers, and the route with an explicit vendor time claim or fewest documented steps when tied. Selected route: Local Mac Jaffle Shop DuckDB build. This is one complete official tab with explicit commands and no dbt platform account or external database. It is recorded as a candidate, not silently promoted over the Web browser peer. Prior surface before policy commit: dbt Core DuckDB quickstart first successful Jaffle Shop build.

alternatives_considered.name: Web browser with GitHub Codespaces
alternatives_considered.reason_not_primary: This peer tab requires GitHub login, template-repository creation, Codespaces creation, and a build wait; the guide does not prefer it over Local.

alternatives_considered.name: dbt platform quickstart
alternatives_considered.reason_not_primary: The selected DuckDB guide states that DuckDB is not supported in dbt platform and explicitly uses dbt Core CLI.

alternatives_considered.name: dbt Fusion engine DuckDB setup
alternatives_considered.reason_not_primary: The beginner guide identifies this as a separate Fusion-specific setup, while its own path is dbt Core CLI.

firstMeaningfulSuccess.official_milestone: Run the Jaffle Shop DuckDB project successfully with `dbt build`.
firstMeaningfulSuccess.normalized_outcome: dbt Core seeds the sample DuckDB database, creates the Jaffle Shop view and table models, runs the data tests, and completes all 28 nodes without warnings, errors, or skips in the guide's demonstrated terminal.
firstMeaningfulSuccess.observable_completion_signal: The terminal shows `Completed successfully` followed by `Done. PASS=28 WARN=0 ERROR=0 SKIP=0 TOTAL=28`.

boundary_evidence.official_label_or_terminal_state: The quickstart displays the complete successful `dbt build` output with three seeds, three view models, twenty data tests, two table models, `Completed successfully`, and PASS=28.

firstMeaningfulSuccess.why_this_is_the_boundary: The successful build is the guide's first proof that dbt Core, its DuckDB adapter, the provided project, seeds, models, and tests work together. Query exploration, documentation, larger datasets, version control, scheduling, and hosted deployment are later work.

prerequisites.requirement: Have a GitHub account, which the guide lists as a prerequisite and uses in the Web browser branch.
For the Local candidate, provide Git and Python commands capable of cloning the repository, creating a virtual environment, and installing its requirements.
Allow cloning `dbt-labs/jaffle_shop_duckdb` and installing packages from `requirements.txt`.

stages.action: Open Quickstart for dbt Core using DuckDB and review its prerequisites and stated first build.
details: The guide is marked dbt Core, Quickstart, and Beginner.
It provides Local and Web browser setup tabs without designating either as preferred.
stages.output: The common DuckDB quickstart and its two setup routes are visible.
stages.success_signal: The Set up DuckDB for dbt Core section offers Local and Web browser tabs.

stages.action: Choose Local or Web browser before beginning setup.
details: Local clones the public Jaffle Shop DuckDB repository and installs requirements in a virtual environment.
Web browser creates a GitHub repository from a template and a Codespace, then waits for `postCreateCommand`.
stages.input: Host environment and willingness to use GitHub Codespaces
stages.output: One environment-specific candidate route is selected.
stages.success_signal: The selected route can proceed to its documented `dbt build` command.
stages.failure_or_wait: The guide does not name one route as the default, and the Web browser route crosses GitHub's authenticated interface.

stages.action: Run `git clone https://github.com/dbt-labs/jaffle_shop_duckdb.git`.
stages.input: The official public Jaffle Shop DuckDB repository URL
stages.success_signal: The repository directory is available locally.

stages.action: Run `cd jaffle_shop_duckdb`.
stages.output: The terminal's working directory is the dbt project root.
stages.success_signal: `requirements.txt` and `dbt_project.yml` are available from the current directory.

stages.action: Create the virtual environment with `python3 -m venv venv` and activate it with `source venv/bin/activate`.
stages.output: The project's isolated Python environment is active.
stages.success_signal: The shell prompt uses the `venv` environment for package installation.

stages.action: Run `python3 -m pip install --upgrade pip`, then `python3 -m pip install -r requirements.txt`, and activate the environment again as shown.
details: The repository requirements install dbt Core, DuckDB, and the other dependencies needed by the guide.
stages.output: dbt Core and the DuckDB project dependencies are installed in the environment.
stages.success_signal: The environment can invoke `dbt`.

stages.action: Run `dbt build` from the project root.
details: The command compiles, runs, and tests the provided project.
The demonstrated run processes three seeds, three view models, twenty data tests, and two table models.
stages.input: The installed and configured Jaffle Shop DuckDB project
stages.output: dbt builds the local sample warehouse and reports the result of all project nodes.
stages.success_signal: The terminal prints `Completed successfully`.
stages.failure_or_wait: If DuckDB reports `Could not set lock on file`, close sessions that lock the database; the guide names DBeaver and warns that deleting the database file as a last resort loses its data.

stages.action: Read the final `dbt build` summary.
stages.output: The terminal reports the full passing node count.
stages.success_signal: The documented terminal is `Done. PASS=28 WARN=0 ERROR=0 SKIP=0 TOTAL=28`.

branches.condition: Use Local on Windows Command Prompt
branches.path: Clone and enter the repository, create `venv` with `python -m venv venv`, activate with `venv\Scripts\activate.bat`, upgrade pip, install `requirements.txt`, activate again, and run `dbt build`.
branches.effect_on_first_success: Changes Python and activation syntax while converging on the same build terminal.

branches.condition: Use Local on Windows PowerShell
branches.path: Use `python -m venv venv`, activate with `venv\Scripts\Activate.ps1`, upgrade pip, install `requirements.txt`, activate again, and run `dbt build`.
branches.effect_on_first_success: Uses the PowerShell activation variant before the same build.

branches.condition: Use the Web browser route
branches.path: After GitHub login, open the guide's template repository, select Use this template > Create new repository, choose repository options, select Create repository from template, open Code > Codespaces > Create codespace on main, wait for `postCreateCommand` to complete and a new terminal prompt, then run `dbt build`.
branches.effect_on_first_success: Adds GitHub authentication, repository creation, Codespaces provisioning, and a several-minute documented wait.

branches.condition: The local DuckDB file is locked
branches.path: Disconnect or close sessions locking the file; the guide specifically says DBeaver may need to be shut down. Deleting the database file is a last resort that loses its data.
branches.effect_on_first_success: Requires releasing the lock before rerunning the build.

frictionGates.description: The guide presents Local and Web browser as peer setup tabs and then presents operating-system-specific local commands.
The guide lists a GitHub account as a prerequisite, and the Web browser route requires GitHub login before repository and Codespaces creation.
The Local route requires cloning the project, creating a virtual environment, upgrading pip, and installing all requirements.
The Web browser route says the Codespaces `postCreateCommand` can take several minutes before a new terminal prompt appears.
A lock held on `jaffle_shop.duckdb` prevents the documented build until the locking session is closed or the database is deleted with acknowledged data loss.

uncertainties.question: Should Local or Web browser represent the canonical quickstart route?
uncertainties.impact: The route changes account, repository, environment, installation, provisioning, and wait steps.
uncertainties.reason_unresolved: The guide exposes both tabs and does not label either one as preferred; the Local Mac route is therefore preserved only as a fully reconstructable candidate.

uncertainties.question: Why does the guide list a GitHub account as a global prerequisite when its Local clone command targets a public repository?
uncertainties.impact: A local user may encounter an unnecessary stated account prerequisite, but removing it would contradict the guide.
uncertainties.reason_unresolved: The prerequisite is explicit while the Local steps do not contain a GitHub login action.

uncertainties.question: What exact GitHub authentication sequence precedes the Web browser candidate?
uncertainties.impact: That candidate cannot be verified from no account as a complete screen-by-screen route.
uncertainties.reason_unresolved: The quickstart says to go to the template after logging in and then documents repository and Codespaces actions, but it does not enumerate GitHub signup or login transitions.

developerGoal: Create a Deepgram account and transcribe sample audio with the Listen API.

entryPoint.developer_intent: Create a Deepgram account and transcribe sample audio with the Listen API.
assumed_prior_state: The developer begins before account creation with no platform resources or credentials.
A supported browser and internet connection are available.

selectedSurface.name: Listen API first request via cURL
selectedSurface.selection_basis: The official fundamentals guide documents a direct Listen API cURL request using an account API key, without an SDK or playground.

alternatives_considered.name: API playground
alternatives_considered.reason_not_primary: The playground is interactive, but the documented cURL request is directly reproducible and exercises the API boundary.

firstMeaningfulSuccess.official_milestone: Make the first Listen API request
firstMeaningfulSuccess.normalized_outcome: An authenticated cURL request sends audio to the Listen API and returns a transcript.
firstMeaningfulSuccess.observable_completion_signal: The successful JSON response contains a transcript under the channel alternatives.

boundary_evidence.official_label_or_terminal_state: The successful JSON response contains a transcript under the channel alternatives.

firstMeaningfulSuccess.why_this_is_the_boundary: This is the first observable end-to-end result on the selected official surface; later production configuration and expansion follow it.

prerequisites.requirement: A Deepgram account with usable API credit.
A Deepgram API key.
A terminal with cURL.

stages.action: Create or sign in to Deepgram and open the console.
stages.success_signal: The Deepgram console is available.
stages.failure_or_wait: Correct the documented configuration or prerequisite before continuing.

stages.action: Create an API key and copy it securely.
stages.success_signal: A Deepgram token is available.
stages.failure_or_wait: Correct the documented configuration or prerequisite before continuing.

stages.action: Store the API key in a shell variable for the request.
stages.success_signal: cURL can read the token.
stages.failure_or_wait: Correct the documented configuration or prerequisite before continuing.

stages.action: Run the documented cURL request against the Listen API with the sample audio source.
stages.success_signal: Deepgram returns transcription JSON.

developerGoal: Create a Descope account and project, embed the recommended Descope Flow in an application, and test a sign-up or sign-in until the SDK reports an authenticated user and session.

entryPoint.developer_intent: Create a Descope account and project, embed the recommended Descope Flow in an application, and test a sign-up or sign-in until the SDK reports an authenticated user and session.
assumed_prior_state: No Descope account, project, Project ID, configured Flow, SDK, application integration, user, or authenticated session is assumed.
The developer has a local application environment appropriate to the framework they select and can complete the end-user authentication method contained in the project's sign-up-or-in Flow.
No account signup, onboarding wizard, Flow modification, package installation, authentication, user creation, or session issuance was performed during research.

selectedSurface.name: Descope Flows Quickstart through a tested sign-up or sign-in
selectedSurface.selection_basis: Under the workshop policy, this is the fastest commonly used documented route: the official guide recommends Descope Flows as the easiest integration approach, and the React quickstart provides a concrete native path. Account creation is treated as one opaque step at the official Console URL; only documented post-login actions are reconstructed.

alternatives_considered.name: Hosted Application with Descope Flows
alternatives_considered.reason_not_primary: The guide presents Hosted Application and Native Implementation as two Flow-based options without selecting one for every developer.

alternatives_considered.name: Client SDKs without Flows
alternatives_considered.reason_not_primary: The guide recommends Flows as easiest and places Client SDKs under Not Using Flows for developers who build their own frontend login experience.

alternatives_considered.name: Backend SDKs without Flows
alternatives_considered.reason_not_primary: This peer alternative requires the developer to own frontend and session behavior, while the guide recommends Flows.

alternatives_considered.name: React Flow integration
alternatives_considered.reason_not_primary: React is one of many web, mobile, full-stack, backend, library, and generic choices. It is preserved as a downstream candidate outline, not asserted as universal.

firstMeaningfulSuccess.official_milestone: Application is working
firstMeaningfulSuccess.normalized_outcome: A React app renders the Descope `sign-up-or-in` Flow and completes a test sign-up or sign-in, producing an authenticated user and session.
firstMeaningfulSuccess.observable_completion_signal: The `onSuccess` callback reports the user, the session hook reports an authenticated state, and the React quickstart checkpoint can be marked `Application is working`.

boundary_evidence.official_label_or_terminal_state: Application is working

firstMeaningfulSuccess.why_this_is_the_boundary: The React quickstart identifies this checkpoint immediately after the first working authentication test. Backend session validation and customization are later work.

prerequisites.requirement: A Descope account and Console access.
A Descope project with a Project ID and a `sign-up-or-in` Flow available to embed.
A supported application framework and package manager for the selected quickstart; the documented candidate continuation uses React and `@descope/react-sdk`.

stages.action: Open Descope's Getting Started Quickstart Guide and select the recommended Descope Flows approach.
details: Flows are described as the easiest, recommended, straightforward, and extensible approach.
stages.output: Flow-based implementation choices and framework quickstarts are available.
stages.success_signal: The guide identifies Native Implementation and Hosted Application as Flow-based paths.
stages.failure_or_wait: No navigation recovery behavior is documented.

stages.action: Choose Native Implementation or Hosted Application and select the application's framework route.
details: The Getting Started navigation includes full-stack, web, mobile, backend, library, generic, and web-platform options.
stages.output: A framework-specific or hosted Flow integration guide.
stages.success_signal: No single implementation and framework combination is selected for all developers.
stages.failure_or_wait: Installation, embedding, session handling, and terminal details vary by selection.

stages.action: Sign up for or sign in to Descope.
details: Treat signup as one opaque workshop-policy step; do not infer form fields.
stages.success_signal: The Console project settings are accessible.
stages.failure_or_wait: Use the Console's displayed account recovery path.

stages.action: Open the project settings and copy the Project ID for the project containing the pre-created `sign-up-or-in` Flow.
details: The onboarding wizard pre-creates Flows; the React guide uses `flowId="sign-up-or-in"`.
stages.output: Project ID and Flow ID available for the app.
stages.success_signal: The Project ID is copied and the Flow is available.
stages.failure_or_wait: Create or select a project and verify the Flow in the Console.

stages.action: Install the Descope React SDK.
details: Install the `@descope/react-sdk` package in the React application.
stages.success_signal: The package installation completes.
stages.failure_or_wait: Resolve package-manager errors before continuing.

stages.action: Wrap the app with `AuthProvider` and render the Descope component.
details: Pass the copied Project ID to `AuthProvider`.
Render the Descope component with `flowId="sign-up-or-in"` and the documented success and error callbacks.
Use `useSession` and `useUser` to display authenticated content.
stages.output: A React authentication UI backed by the Descope Flow.
stages.success_signal: The app compiles and displays the Flow.
stages.failure_or_wait: The error callback reports Flow or configuration failures.

stages.action: Start the React app and complete a test sign-up or sign-in through the embedded Flow.
stages.output: An authenticated Descope user and session.
stages.success_signal: The success callback reports the user and authenticated UI is visible.
stages.failure_or_wait: Use the Flow error callback and browser console to diagnose failure.

stages.action: Mark the React quickstart checkpoint as complete.
stages.success_signal: The checkpoint reads `Application is working`.
stages.failure_or_wait: Repeat the test after correcting the reported integration error.

branches.condition: Choose Native Implementation with React
branches.path: Embed the sign-up-or-in Flow using `@descope/react-sdk`, AuthProvider, and the Descope component, then test sign-up or sign-in until the app reports an authenticated user.
branches.effect_on_first_success: Requires a local React application and package installation; the terminal is observed through component callbacks and session hooks.

branches.condition: Choose Hosted Application
branches.path: Use Descope's hosted Flow application instead of embedding the Flow natively.
branches.effect_on_first_success: Changes domain, application, and integration steps while retaining a Flow-based authentication terminal.

branches.condition: Do not use Flows
branches.path: Use Client SDKs for custom frontend screens with Descope session management, or Backend SDKs for a developer-owned frontend and custom backend authentication APIs.
branches.effect_on_first_success: Replaces the recommended Flow embedding route with materially different UI, API, and session work.

branches.condition: Building a B2B app that requires SAML or OIDC SSO
branches.path: Use the dedicated SSO quickstart to configure a connection, add SSO, and test with an identity provider or mock IdP.
branches.effect_on_first_success: Introduces tenant and SSO connection configuration before the authentication terminal.

frictionGates.description: The recommended Flow route still requires Native versus Hosted implementation and framework choices that change required actions.
A Descope account and project are required before a Project ID and Flow can be used.
The public Console shell does not expose signup, verification, onboarding-wizard, project, or Project ID convergence states.
The code route depends on an existing project and sign-up-or-in Flow before the SDK can show an authentication experience.

developerGoal: Deploy a first application to DigitalOcean App Platform and open its public URL.

entryPoint.developer_intent: Deploy a first application to DigitalOcean App Platform and open its public URL.
assumed_prior_state: The developer can use a web browser and the DigitalOcean Control Panel.
A DigitalOcean personal account with Control Panel access exists, or the developer can complete the separate signup gate.
For the general Quickstart, the developer has source code in GitHub, GitLab, or Bitbucket, or a container image in a supported registry.
For the fully reconstructed sample candidate, the developer can deploy directly from a DigitalOcean sample repository without creating a GitHub repository.

selectedSurface.name: Deploy a DigitalOcean sample directly from its template repository
selectedSurface.selection_basis: Workshop selection policy (2026-07-19): when official docs present peer routes, commit to the fastest and most commonly used documented route. Prefer the vendor's first-listed or recommended quickstart, dashboard/console over CLI when both are peers, and the route with an explicit vendor time claim or fewest documented steps when tied. Selected route: Deploy a DigitalOcean sample directly from its template repository. This vendor-documented sample branch supplies the source and explicitly says no additional information is required at repository selection, while retaining the full control-panel configuration, creation, deployment, and browser-verification journey. Prior surface before policy commit: App Platform Getting Started, with App Platform Quickstart as the general route and Deploy Sample Apps as the fully reconstructable candidate.

alternatives_considered.name: App Platform API or doctl automation
alternatives_considered.reason_not_primary: The general Quickstart starts with the Control Panel and presents repository or image selection. The automation route additionally requires doctl or API credentials and an app spec.

alternatives_considered.name: Droplets
alternatives_considered.reason_not_primary: Droplets are a separate infrastructure product, while the frozen category and official App Platform Getting Started surface provide a direct application deployment journey.

alternatives_considered.name: Functions
alternatives_considered.reason_not_primary: Functions are a separate product surface and are not selected by the App Platform Getting Started index.

firstMeaningfulSuccess.official_milestone: A deployed sample app's basic Hello World style website is viewable in the browser.
firstMeaningfulSuccess.normalized_outcome: App Platform has automatically deployed the selected starter application and exposed it at the URL shown on the app Overview page.
firstMeaningfulSuccess.observable_completion_signal: The deployment completes, a URL appears at the top of the app Overview page, and opening it displays the sample app's basic Hello World style website.

boundary_evidence.official_label_or_terminal_state: A deployed sample app's basic Hello World style website is viewable in the browser.

firstMeaningfulSuccess.why_this_is_the_boundary: The official Quickstart stops the Create an App path after deployment and directs the developer to view the app at the Overview URL. The sample-app guide states that each starter deploys a basic Hello World style website viewable after deployment. Scaling, databases, domains, customization, and redeployment are later work.

prerequisites.requirement: A DigitalOcean personal account with Control Panel access. The public App Platform quickstart does not document the atomic signup sequence.
The account may require a payment method for identity verification. App Platform has a free tier only for qualifying static-site apps; service components are paid according to selected resources.
If the GitHub repository route is used, the developer must have the Owner or Maintainer permission role for the repository and may need to grant DigitalOcean read access.
For the sample candidate, access to a starter in the Control Panel Templates tab. Deploying directly from the template repository avoids a GitHub account requirement.

stages.action: Open the official App Platform Getting Started page.
stages.output: The page presents App Platform Quickstart first and also links the sample-app deployment guide.
stages.success_signal: The App Platform Quickstart and Deploy Sample Apps routes are visible.
stages.failure_or_wait: None documented.

stages.action: Open Deploy Sample Apps to follow a fully specified starter path.
stages.output: The control-panel sample deployment instructions are available.
stages.success_signal: The guide identifies starter apps that deploy a basic Hello World style website.
stages.failure_or_wait: The general Quickstart instead requires the developer to supply a repository or container image.

stages.action: Open the DigitalOcean Control Panel and authenticate to an existing account.
stages.output: The authenticated Control Panel is available.
stages.success_signal: The Create menu is accessible.
stages.failure_or_wait: A new developer must pass signup, possible verification, and possible payment-method gates. The public quickstart does not document the atomic signup screens, so that precondition remains unresolved.

stages.action: Select the Templates tab and choose or search for a starter app.
stages.input: A starter app from the template grid.
stages.output: The selected starter app is ready for deployment setup.
stages.success_signal: The starter app presents a Deploy action.
stages.failure_or_wait: Starter availability may vary; the guide does not promise a particular language template.

stages.action: Click Deploy and choose Deploy from template repository.
stages.output: App Platform uses the sample app repository directly.
stages.success_signal: The creation flow proceeds without requesting repository ownership information.
stages.failure_or_wait: The peer Create new repository in GitHub branch requires selecting an owner, naming the repository, creating it, returning to DigitalOcean, refreshing the page, selecting GitHub and the new repository, and clicking Next.

stages.action: Detect the starter's app resources and prefill resource settings.
stages.input: The selected sample repository and its app spec and language-specific files.
stages.output: A Resource settings table is populated.
stages.success_signal: Detected component name, resource type, branch, build and run configuration, and related settings are available for review.
stages.failure_or_wait: The developer can edit detected fields. The exact fields shown depend on resource type.

stages.action: Review the Resource settings table and edit any settings required by the selected starter, then continue.
stages.input: Detected settings, including component name, resource type, branch, instance size, commands, ports, routes, and environment variables where applicable.
stages.output: Resource settings are accepted for the app.
stages.success_signal: The flow advances beyond resource configuration.
stages.failure_or_wait: Web services must bind their HTTP port on 0.0.0.0 rather than localhost. Build or runtime requirements can vary by starter.

stages.action: Optionally add app-level environment variables and encrypt sensitive values.
stages.input: Any variables required by the chosen starter.
stages.output: Optional variables are attached to the app configuration.
stages.success_signal: The flow can continue with or without optional variables when the starter has none.
stages.failure_or_wait: A starter that requires external credentials can add undocumented, starter-specific prerequisites.

stages.action: If the Datacenter region selector is available, choose the region for the app.

stages.output: The selected deployment region is attached to the app configuration.

stages.failure_or_wait: Region selection is not available for static sites because static resources use DigitalOcean's global CDN.

stages.action: Review the app name and project, and create a new project only if desired.

stages.success_signal: The Finalize section accepts the selected app and project information.
stages.failure_or_wait: Creating a new project adds the name, purpose, and environment fields.

stages.action: Review the configuration and pricing summary.
stages.input: The detected or selected resource sizes and app settings.
stages.output: The developer can see the resources and applicable price before creation.
stages.success_signal: The Create App action is available after review.
stages.failure_or_wait: Only qualifying static-site apps use the free tier. Other component sizes are billable, and account verification may require a payment method.

stages.input: The confirmed starter, resources, app information, and pricing.
stages.output: App Platform creates the app and starts deployment automatically.

branches.condition: The developer does not yet have a DigitalOcean account
branches.path: Use the external signup flow. Official support pages establish that email, Google, and GitHub login methods exist and that verification, account lock, and payment-method checks can occur, but the public App Platform docs do not reconstruct their current order.
branches.effect_on_first_success: Adds an unresolved account-creation and possible verification or billing sequence before the Control Panel path.

branches.condition: The developer uses the general Quickstart rather than a sample template
branches.path: Select GitHub, GitLab, Bitbucket, DigitalOcean Container Registry, Docker Hub, or GitHub Container Registry; choose the repository and branch or tag, optionally a monorepo directory and Autodeploy, then proceed to resource configuration.
branches.effect_on_first_success: Uses developer-supplied code or an image and can add repository authorization, language detection, Dockerfile, and app-specific configuration requirements.

branches.condition: The developer wants a customizable copy of the starter
branches.path: Choose Create new repository in GitHub, select an owner, enter a repository name, click Create repository, return to Create an app, refresh, select GitHub and the new repository, then click Next.
branches.effect_on_first_success: Adds GitHub ownership and repository-creation steps before converging on resource configuration.

branches.condition: The selected starter needs environment variables or external credentials
branches.path: Edit app-level or resource-level variables, add the required keys and values, select their scope, and encrypt sensitive values before continuing.
branches.effect_on_first_success: Adds starter-specific credential and configuration gates that the generic guide cannot enumerate.

branches.condition: The selected starter is a static site
branches.path: Skip manual region selection because static resources are served through DigitalOcean's global CDN.
branches.effect_on_first_success: Removes the region-selection action before Finalize without changing the deployment and browser-verification terminal.

branches.condition: The app contains only qualifying static-site components and the team is within free-tier limits
branches.path: Use the static-site free tier. Otherwise select a paid component size and accept usage-based billing.
branches.effect_on_first_success: Determines whether the first deployment is free or billable.

frictionGates.description: The documented app flow requires the authenticated Control Panel, but current public App Platform docs do not enumerate the signup screens.

Signup or login can trigger verification or account lock handling. The official support surface directs a locked signup to support and discusses emailed verification codes, but does not locate those states in the App Platform path.
DigitalOcean may require a payment method to verify identity. The exact eligibility and position of this gate are not stated by the App Platform quickstart.
The sample flow branches between creating a GitHub repository and deploying directly from the template repository.
Detected resource settings depend on the starter and resource type, and a web service must bind its HTTP port to 0.0.0.0.
The developer must review pricing. Only qualifying static sites have a free tier; other service resources are billed by selected size and usage.
App Platform builds and deploys before the public URL can be verified. The quickstart does not document a typical duration.

uncertainties.question: What is the complete current signup, verification, and first-team sequence for a new DigitalOcean developer?
uncertainties.impact: The number, order, and possible waiting time of steps before the Control Panel cannot be reconstructed from the public App Platform quickstart.
uncertainties.reason_unresolved: The public official pages establish that email, Google, GitHub, verification-code, account-lock, and payment-method states exist, but they do not present one chronological signup route.

uncertainties.question: Which exact starter template is selected for the shortest sample journey, and does it use only free static-site resources?
uncertainties.impact: Resource settings, environment requirements, price, build behavior, and rendered content vary by starter.
uncertainties.reason_unresolved: The official guide presents a template grid and language list but does not name one canonical default starter.

uncertainties.question: Does every new account need to provide a payment method before a first App Platform deployment?
uncertainties.impact: A billing and identity-verification gate may appear before resource creation.
uncertainties.reason_unresolved: The support page explains why payment information is requested, but the App Platform docs do not state account eligibility rules or the gate's position.

developerGoal: Build and test Discord's complete JavaScript rock-paper-scissors bot tutorial through slash commands, buttons, select menus, and a posted game result.

entryPoint.developer_intent: Build and test Discord's complete JavaScript rock-paper-scissors bot tutorial through slash commands, buttons, select menus, and a posted game result.
assumed_prior_state: The developer has a Discord user account and can create or administer a quiet test server.
The developer has not yet created the tutorial application.

selectedSurface.name: Building your first Discord Bot
selectedSurface.selection_basis: Discord's platform overview says bots are the most common Discord app type and the Bots & Companion Apps page routes to an end-to-end Getting Started guide. The guide is a step-by-step first-app tutorial and explicitly redirects iframe game builders to the Activity alternative.

alternatives_considered.name: Building your first Activity
alternatives_considered.reason_not_primary: The bot quickstart explicitly identifies this as the alternative for games or social experiences in an iframe.

alternatives_considered.name: Discord Social SDK Getting Started
alternatives_considered.reason_not_primary: The platform overview presents the Social SDK as a game-specific app type, while bots are identified as most common.

alternatives_considered.name: Webhook-only integration
alternatives_considered.reason_not_primary: The Bots page says webhooks can be simpler for push-only messaging, but they cannot handle the interactive first-app outcome.

firstMeaningfulSuccess.official_milestone: Build your first Discord app.
firstMeaningfulSuccess.normalized_outcome: A configured and installed Discord bot receives interactions through a public endpoint, runs the /challenge game across two users, and posts the calculated result to the original channel.
firstMeaningfulSuccess.observable_completion_signal: After User A runs /challenge, User B accepts and selects an object, and the app posts the readable game result in the original channel; the guide then says to test everything and congratulates the developer.

boundary_evidence.official_label_or_terminal_state: The guide is labeled a step-by-step tutorial for building your first Discord app and its Next steps begin with Congrats on building your first Discord app after the complete game test.

firstMeaningfulSuccess.why_this_is_the_boundary: The terminal game flow exercises every capability promised at the start: slash commands, messages, and component interactions. The earlier /test response is an intermediate check because the same guide explicitly continues to add the finished rock-paper-scissors app.

prerequisites.requirement: A Discord account.
A test server where the developer can authorize server installation with MANAGE_GUILD permission, or permission to create a free server.
Node.js, Git, npm, a code editor, and a terminal.
ngrok for exposing the local Express server; installation may occur when the guide reaches public-endpoint setup.

stages.action: Open Overview of Discord Apps and select Bots & Companion Apps, then Getting Started.
details: Bots are the most common app type; the guide is the end-to-end first-bot tutorial.
stages.input: Intent to build a Discord bot.
stages.output: Building your first Discord Bot guide.
stages.success_signal: Step 0 and promised finished game are visible.
stages.failure_or_wait: Activity and Social SDK builders follow separate routes.

stages.action: Clone discord/discord-example-app.
stages.success_signal: git clone completes.
stages.failure_or_wait: Network, Git, or filesystem errors are not decomposed.

stages.action: Change into discord-example-app.
stages.success_signal: package.json and tutorial files are present.
stages.failure_or_wait: Wrong directory blocks dependency installation.

stages.action: Run npm install.
stages.success_signal: npm completes successfully.
stages.failure_or_wait: Dependency or network failures require local recovery not described.

stages.action: Open the project in a code editor and review its structure.
details: The guide identifies app.js, commands.js, game.js, utils.js, .env, examples, and package files.
stages.success_signal: The listed files are visible.
stages.failure_or_wait: None documented.

stages.action: Create a Discord application, enter a name, and press Create.
stages.output: New Discord application and General Information page.
stages.success_signal: Application ID and Interactions Endpoint URL fields are visible.
stages.failure_or_wait: An existing app can be used instead.

stages.action: Rename .env.sample to .env.
stages.success_signal: The project contains .env.
stages.failure_or_wait: Accidentally committing the file can expose credentials.

stages.action: Copy Application ID into the APP_ID placeholder in .env.
stages.success_signal: APP_ID contains the portal value.
stages.failure_or_wait: Copy errors break registration and webhook operations.

stages.action: Copy Public Key into the PUBLIC_KEY placeholder in .env.
details: The sample uses it to verify that interaction requests come from Discord.
stages.success_signal: PUBLIC_KEY contains the portal value.
stages.failure_or_wait: Wrong key causes endpoint verification failure.

stages.action: On Bot, click Reset Token, copy the new bot token into .env, and store it securely.
details: The token cannot be viewed again without regeneration and must not be shared or committed.
stages.success_signal: BOT_TOKEN contains the newly generated value and a secure copy exists.
stages.failure_or_wait: Loss requires another reset; exposure requires regeneration.

stages.action: On Installation, enable both User Install and Guild Install.
details: Discord recommends both contexts by default.
stages.success_signal: Both toggles are active.
stages.failure_or_wait: A server-only or user-only app is a documented specialization.

stages.action: Select Discord Provided Link in Install Link.
stages.success_signal: Default Install Settings appears.
stages.failure_or_wait: None documented.

stages.action: Add the applications.commands scope for User Install.
stages.success_signal: The scope is listed for User Install.
stages.failure_or_wait: User-installed apps are limited to commands.

stages.action: For Guild Install, add applications.commands and bot scopes, then select Send Messages permission.
details: Selecting bot reveals the Permissions menu.
stages.success_signal: The settings show both scopes and Send Messages.
stages.failure_or_wait: Additional permissions are optional and use-case-specific.

stages.action: Copy the default Install Link, open it, choose Add to server, select the test server, and complete authorization.
stages.input: Install link and MANAGE_GUILD permission on a quiet test server.
stages.success_signal: The bot appears in the server member list.
stages.failure_or_wait: Missing server permission or choosing the wrong server blocks safe testing.

stages.action: Open the same link, choose Add to my apps, and complete user installation.
stages.input: Install link and Discord user account.
stages.success_signal: A direct message with the app can be opened.
stages.failure_or_wait: Installation prompts can be denied or cancelled.

stages.action: Run npm run register from the project directory.
details: The script uses the HTTP API to PUT the commands in ALL_COMMANDS as global application commands.
stages.success_signal: The command completes without API error.
stages.failure_or_wait: Invalid credentials, permissions, or request errors prevent registration.

stages.action: Return to the test server and verify that the slash commands appear.
details: They do not work yet because the app is not receiving requests.
stages.success_signal: The commands appear in Discord.
stages.failure_or_wait: Propagation delay is possible but not quantified.

stages.action: Run npm run start in the project directory.
details: The Express server defaults to port 3000 and verifies request headers and PING requests.
stages.success_signal: Terminal output says the app is running on port 3000.
stages.failure_or_wait: Port conflicts or application errors block the server.

stages.action: Confirm the local server reports port 3000.
stages.success_signal: The terminal reports the expected port.
stages.failure_or_wait: A custom PORT requires the tunnel and endpoint to match.

stages.action: Install ngrok if it is not already available.
stages.success_signal: The ngrok command is available.
stages.failure_or_wait: The guide delegates installation details to ngrok.

stages.action: Open a second terminal and run ngrok http 3000.
stages.input: Running local server and installed ngrok.
stages.success_signal: ngrok reports Tunnel Status online and an HTTPS Forwarding URL to localhost:3000.
stages.failure_or_wait: Authentication, network, or port mismatch can prevent a tunnel.

stages.action: Paste the ngrok HTTPS URL plus /interactions into Interaction Endpoint URL.
stages.input: Online tunnel forwarding to the sample app.
stages.success_signal: The field contains the full public interactions URL.
stages.failure_or_wait: Wrong URL or missing suffix fails verification.

stages.action: Click Save Changes and verify the interaction endpoint.
details: The sample verifies Discord request signatures with PUBLIC_KEY and answers PING.
stages.input: Running app, running tunnel, correct URL, and public key.
stages.success_signal: The portal accepts and saves the endpoint.
stages.failure_or_wait: If verification fails, check that app and ngrok use the same port and that the URL is copied correctly.

stages.action: Trigger /test in the server.
details: The existing app.js handler returns hello world plus a random emoji.
stages.success_signal: The app posts hello world and an emoji in the originating context.
stages.failure_or_wait: This is an intermediate check, not the guide's final app.

stages.action: Add the documented /challenge command handler after the /test block.
details: Parse context, user ID, and selected object; store the active game by interaction ID; return a channel message with an Accept button and unique custom_id.
stages.success_signal: The code matches the guide or examples/app.js.
stages.failure_or_wait: Syntax or placement errors stop the server or handler.

stages.action: Run /challenge, select an object, and verify that the channel receives an Accept button.
stages.input: Running updated app and registered challenge command.
stages.success_signal: The channel shows a challenge message with Accept.
stages.failure_or_wait: The app may need restart after code changes; the guide does not state a specific restart command here.

stages.action: Add the documented message-component handler for Accept.
details: Match accept_button_, recover game ID, send an ephemeral select menu with shuffled choices, delete the original challenge message, and catch request errors.
stages.success_signal: The code can respond to an Accept component.
stages.failure_or_wait: Errors are logged as Error sending message.

stages.action: As a second user, press Accept and verify the ephemeral object-choice menu.
stages.input: Active challenge and updated running app.
stages.success_signal: The accepting user sees What is your object of choice? and a select menu.
stages.failure_or_wait: Another user cannot complete the game if the button handler fails.

stages.action: Extend the component handler for select_choice interactions.
details: Recover game ID; read second user and choice; call getResult; delete active game; post result; patch the ephemeral message to Nice choice; catch errors.
stages.input: Active-game state and tutorial select-handler code.
stages.success_signal: The source contains both button and select branches.
stages.failure_or_wait: Missing state or request failures prevent a result.

stages.action: Run the complete two-user game flow.
details: User A runs /challenge and chooses an object; Discord posts the invitation; User B accepts and chooses an object.
stages.input: Running app, verified endpoint, two installations/users, and complete handlers.
stages.success_signal: Discord delivers all interactions to the app and receives valid responses.
stages.failure_or_wait: Tunnel, server, state, permissions, or handler errors can interrupt the flow.

stages.action: Verify the final result is posted to the original channel and the ephemeral message updates.
details: The guide then says that is it, directs the developer to test everything, and begins Next steps with a congratulations.
stages.success_signal: A readable winner/result appears publicly and the second user's ephemeral message says Nice choice with an emoji.
stages.failure_or_wait: If either message is absent, the full tutorial outcome is incomplete.

branches.condition: The intended application is an iframe game or social experience.
branches.path: Follow Building your first Activity instead of the bot tutorial.
branches.effect_on_first_success: Changes the SDK, runtime, installation, and terminal interaction.

branches.condition: Node.js is not installed.
branches.path: Install Node.js before cloning and installing dependencies.
branches.effect_on_first_success: Adds a prerequisite installation gate before Step 0 commands.

branches.condition: The app intentionally supports only server or only user installation.
branches.path: Enable only the applicable context and adjust scopes and later installation tests.
branches.effect_on_first_success: The tutorial's both-context verification no longer applies.

branches.condition: The bot needs permissions beyond Send Messages.
branches.path: Select the additional granular permissions required by the use case.
branches.effect_on_first_success: Adds permission review and broader authorization; not needed for the tutorial.

branches.condition: Port 3000 is unavailable.
branches.path: Set PORT in .env and use that same port in ngrok.
branches.effect_on_first_success: Changes the local and tunnel configuration together.

branches.condition: Endpoint verification fails.
branches.path: Confirm both processes use the same port, the forwarding URL is correct, /interactions is appended, and PUBLIC_KEY matches the application.
branches.effect_on_first_success: Adds diagnostic and retry work before commands can respond.

frictionGates.description: The tutorial requires Node.js, Git, npm dependencies, and a code editor.
A Discord developer account and application are required.
Application ID, Public Key, and a one-time bot token must be copied accurately and protected.
Server installation requires a quiet test server and MANAGE_GUILD authorization.
An ngrok installation is required for the documented local-development route.
The local server and public tunnel must remain running on matching ports.
Discord must verify the public interaction endpoint before the commands work.
The terminal game verification requires a second participant or user context to accept and choose an object.

uncertainties.question: What exact Discord account registration steps precede Developer Portal access for a new person?
uncertainties.impact: The account gate cannot be decomposed to screen level.
uncertainties.reason_unresolved: The bot guide assumes an available Discord user account and starts at project setup and application creation.

uncertainties.question: How long do global slash commands take to appear under current propagation conditions?
uncertainties.impact: The developer may wait after registration without knowing whether it failed.
uncertainties.reason_unresolved: The guide says the commands should appear but provides no duration or retry threshold.

sources.title: Overview of Discord Apps
sections_used: The Three Types of Discord Apps

sources.title: Bots & Companion Apps
evidence_supported: Bots as most common app type

excludedAfterSuccess: Persistent database for active game state
doesNotProve: Platform records reconstruct official documentation. They do not contain product telemetry, conversion rates, observed completion times, or proven causes of abandonment.
Friction gates are documented requirements or transitions. They are not confirmed drop-off points.
Blocker taxonomy entries are candidate hypotheses. Individual reasons remain not diagnosis eligible unless separate case evidence supports them.
A documented journey analysis is not a diagnosis of a real developer attempt.

category: Frameworks, mobile, games, hardware, and creative ecosystems
developerGoal: Install Django, create the tutorial project and polls app, and serve the first view locally.

entryPoint.developer_intent: Install Django, create the tutorial project and polls app, and serve the first view locally.
assumed_prior_state: Discovery is complete and the developer has selected Django.
The developer has a computer, shell, browser, and permission to install Python packages and create local files.

selectedSurface.name: Writing your first Django app, part 1
selectedSurface.selection_basis: The Django 6.0 documentation places the quick install guide immediately before its official first-app tutorial. Part 1 is a bounded guide that ends by verifying the first polls view in a browser and then directs the reader to part 2.

alternatives_considered.name: Quick install guide
alternatives_considered.reason_not_primary: This is required setup and ends by directing the reader to the tutorial; it does not create or run an application.

alternatives_considered.name: Django at a glance
alternatives_considered.reason_not_primary: This is an architectural overview rather than the named first-app tutorial.

firstMeaningfulSuccess.official_milestone: Write your first view
firstMeaningfulSuccess.normalized_outcome: A locally running Django 6.0 development server routes /polls/ to the tutorial's index view.
firstMeaningfulSuccess.observable_completion_signal: The browser at http://localhost:8000/polls/ displays: Hello, world. You're at the polls index.

boundary_evidence.official_label_or_terminal_state: Part 1 explicitly says to verify the wired index view in the browser, gives the expected text, and then sends the reader to part 2.

firstMeaningfulSuccess.why_this_is_the_boundary: This is the final verification in the official part 1 journey. The earlier rocket page verifies only the project scaffold; the final polls response verifies an application, view, URL configuration, server, and browser request together.

prerequisites.requirement: Python 3.12 or later for the Django 6.0 tutorial, plus a shell and browser.
Django 6.0 installed in the selected Python environment; the official release is the recommended installation choice for most users.

stages.action: Install the latest compatible Python if it is not already available, then verify it from the shell.
details: Django 6.0 supports Python 3.12 and later.
SQLite ships with Python, so no external database is needed for this first path.
stages.input: A supported operating system and package installation access
stages.success_signal: The Python shell starts and prints its version.
stages.failure_or_wait: An older Python version requires either a compatible older Django documentation version or a Python upgrade.

stages.action: Install an official Django release in the Python environment.
details: The quick install guide recommends an official release for most users.
Operating-system packages and the development version are documented alternatives.
stages.success_signal: Python can import Django and print its version.
stages.failure_or_wait: Package installation or import errors block the tutorial.

stages.action: Run python -m django --version, or py -m django --version on Windows, and confirm the result matches the Django 6.0 tutorial.
details: No module named django means Django is not installed in that Python environment.
stages.success_signal: The command prints a Django version compatible with the selected documentation.
stages.failure_or_wait: If the version differs, use the version switcher or update Django before continuing.

stages.action: Change to the intended parent directory and run django-admin startproject mysite djangotutorial.
details: Do not use names such as django or test because they conflict with built-in packages.
stages.output: djangotutorial directory containing manage.py and the mysite package
stages.success_signal: The documented project file tree is created.
stages.failure_or_wait: If django-admin fails, use the linked troubleshooting guidance before continuing.

stages.action: Change into djangotutorial and run python manage.py runserver, or py manage.py runserver on Windows.
details: Ignore the initial unapplied-migrations warning for this part of the tutorial.
The development server is only for development, not production.
stages.success_signal: System checks report no issues and the server start address is printed.
stages.failure_or_wait: A port or environment error must be resolved; adding files later can require restarting because not every new file triggers automatic reload.

stages.action: Open http://127.0.0.1:8000/ in a browser.
details: This is the intermediate project-scaffold verification.
stages.success_signal: A Congratulations page with a rocket appears and states that it worked.
stages.failure_or_wait: No specific retry is documented beyond checking the running server and URL.

stages.action: From the directory containing manage.py, run python manage.py startapp polls, or the Windows py equivalent.
details: The command scaffolds the polls Python package with views.py and other app files.
stages.success_signal: The documented polls file tree exists.
stages.failure_or_wait: If a newly added file is not detected, restart runserver as the guide notes.

stages.action: Replace polls/views.py with the documented HttpResponse index view.
details: Import HttpResponse and return the exact tutorial greeting from index(request).
stages.success_signal: views.py defines index and its response text.
stages.failure_or_wait: Python syntax or import errors prevent the server from loading the view.

stages.action: Create polls/urls.py and map the empty app route to views.index with the name index.
details: Import path from django.urls and views from the current app.
stages.success_signal: polls/urls.py contains the documented urlpatterns list.
stages.failure_or_wait: A missing file, import, or route prevents URL resolution.

stages.action: Update mysite/urls.py to import include and mount polls.urls at polls/.
details: Keep the existing admin route and add path('polls/', include('polls.urls')).
stages.input: Project URL configuration and polls URL configuration
stages.output: Root route connected to the polls app
stages.success_signal: The root urlpatterns list contains both polls and admin routes.
stages.failure_or_wait: A missing include import or incorrect module string causes startup or routing errors.

stages.action: Ensure runserver is running, then open http://localhost:8000/polls/ in the browser.
details: If the server stopped or did not reload after new files were added, run the documented runserver command again.
stages.input: Running server and wired polls route
stages.output: HTTP response from the first Django view
stages.success_signal: The browser shows: Hello, world. You're at the polls index.
stages.failure_or_wait: If a page-not-found response appears, confirm the URL includes /polls/ rather than opening only the site root.

branches.condition: The developer uses an older supported Python release.
branches.path: Select the Django version and matching documentation compatible with that Python version instead of following the 6.0 route.
branches.effect_on_first_success: The same conceptual journey applies, but commands or generated code may differ by documentation version.

branches.condition: The developer does not choose the recommended official release.
branches.path: Install a distribution-provided package or the development version and use matching documentation.
branches.effect_on_first_success: Adds platform-specific installation behavior or development-version compatibility risk.

branches.condition: The browser shows a 404 page.
branches.path: Open http://localhost:8000/polls/ exactly, not only http://localhost:8000/, and retry.
branches.effect_on_first_success: Corrects the requested route without changing the app.

frictionGates.description: Django 6.0 requires Python 3.12 or later and version-matched documentation.
Python and Django must be installed locally before the first-app tutorial can begin.
A local TCP port and long-running development-server process must remain available through verification.
The developer must create and connect a view, an app URLconf, and the project URLconf before the first application response appears.

evidence_supported: Complete ordered project and app path

excludedAfterSuccess: Database migration and model setup in part 2
