Friction gates are documented requirements or transitions. They are not confirmed drop-off points.
Blocker taxonomy entries are candidate hypotheses. Individual reasons remain not diagnosis eligible unless separate case evidence supports them.
A documented journey analysis is not a diagnosis of a real developer attempt.

developerGoal: Docker Hub exchanges a username and PAT for a JWT, then returns the authenticated user's repository list.

entryPoint.developer_intent: Docker Hub exchanges a username and PAT for a JWT, then returns the authenticated user's repository list.
assumed_prior_state: The developer has no prior Docker setup for this journey.
The journey starts at account creation and stops at the first documented successful outcome.

selectedSurface.name: Docker Hub PAT + Hub API JWT auth + list repositories (cURL)
selectedSurface.selection_basis: The official documentation presents this as the hosted path from account setup to Authenticate and list Docker Hub repositories. It reaches the requested first-success boundary without extending into post-success production work.

firstMeaningfulSuccess.official_milestone: Authenticate with Docker Hub API and list repositories
firstMeaningfulSuccess.normalized_outcome: Docker Hub exchanges a username and PAT for a JWT, then returns the authenticated user's repository list.
firstMeaningfulSuccess.observable_completion_signal: The repository-list request returns successful JSON for the Docker Hub namespace.

boundary_evidence.official_label_or_terminal_state: The official documentation leads the developer to: Authenticate and list Docker Hub repositories.

firstMeaningfulSuccess.why_this_is_the_boundary: This is the first observable happy-path result on the selected Docker Hub PAT + Hub API JWT auth + list repositories (cURL) surface; later configuration and production hardening occur after success.

prerequisites.requirement: A Docker account with access to Docker Hub.
A Docker Hub personal access token with suitable permissions.
A terminal with cURL and a JSON-handling method for the returned JWT.

stages.action: Create or sign in to Docker Hub.
stages.input: A new developer starting at account creation
stages.output: Docker Hub account settings are accessible.
stages.success_signal: Docker Hub account settings are accessible.
stages.failure_or_wait: The path cannot continue until this documented requirement succeeds.

stages.action: Create a personal access token and copy it securely.
stages.output: A Docker Hub PAT is available.
stages.success_signal: A Docker Hub PAT is available.
stages.failure_or_wait: The path cannot continue until this documented requirement succeeds.

stages.action: Use cURL to exchange the Docker ID and PAT for a Docker Hub API JWT as documented.
stages.output: The authentication response contains a JWT.
stages.success_signal: The authentication response contains a JWT.
stages.failure_or_wait: The path cannot continue until this documented requirement succeeds.

stages.action: Send the documented authenticated request to list repositories for the Docker Hub namespace.
stages.success_signal: Docker Hub returns repository-list JSON.
stages.failure_or_wait: The path cannot continue until this documented requirement succeeds.

stages.action: Confirm the list request succeeded and inspect the repositories array, including an empty list for a new namespace.
stages.output: A valid repository-list response is visible.
stages.success_signal: A valid repository-list response is visible.
stages.failure_or_wait: The path cannot continue until this documented requirement succeeds.

frictionGates.description: The PAT value is only available for secure use after creation and must carry the required permissions.

uncertainties.question: Does the current fresh-account UI exactly match every navigation label in the documented Authenticate and list Docker Hub repositories path?
uncertainties.impact: Navigation wording may differ while the required outcome and boundary remain the same.
uncertainties.reason_unresolved: The official documentation establishes the journey but account-specific UI state can vary.

developerGoal: Start without a Docusign account, create the free developer sandbox account, generate the main eSignature REST API Node.js Quickstart, run its preconfigured project locally, authenticate through its documented first-run Authorization Code Grant route, and open the embedded-signing example.

entryPoint.developer_intent: Start without a Docusign account, create the free developer sandbox account, generate the main eSignature REST API Node.js Quickstart, run its preconfigured project locally, authenticate through its documented first-run Authorization Code Grant route, and open the embedded-signing example.
assumed_prior_state: No Docusign account, developer sandbox, app, integration key, secret, RSA key, user GUID, redirect URI, consent, Quickstart ZIP, Node.js environment, signer identity, or envelope is assumed.
A supported computer, browser, terminal, npm environment, email address, and a signer name and email are available.

No account form was submitted, no verification or login was completed, and no app, integration key, secret, RSA key, consent, Quickstart, package installation, local server, or signing request was created during research.

selectedSurface.name: Docusign eSignature REST API main Node.js Quickstart first run
selectedSurface.selection_basis: Under the workshop policy, the official main Node.js Quickstart is the fastest commonly used documented route: it downloads a personalized, preconfigured project and documents its first-run destination. Developer signup is one opaque step at the official signup URL; the public post-login generator and local run steps are reconstructed from official docs.

alternatives_considered.name: Authorization Code Grant embedded signing Quickstart
alternatives_considered.reason_not_primary: This smaller specialized project includes one embedded-signing workflow, while the overview labels Multiple code examples as the main Quickstart and documents its first-run behavior.

alternatives_considered.name: JWT Grant remote signing Quickstart
alternatives_considered.reason_not_primary: This smaller specialized project includes one remote-signing workflow and uses JWT, while the main project's documented first run uses Authorization Code Grant and opens embedded signing.

alternatives_considered.name: Manually configured Node.js code launcher
alternatives_considered.reason_not_primary: The launcher exposes the same broad code examples but requires manual account configuration. The main Quickstart ZIP is preconfigured and has a documented first-run example.

firstMeaningfulSuccess.official_milestone: On the first run of the main Quickstart project, authenticate with Authorization Code Grant and open the embedded-signing example.
firstMeaningfulSuccess.normalized_outcome: The intended first success is the preconfigured main Node.js Quickstart running locally, authenticating with Authorization Code Grant, and opening its embedded-signing code example.
firstMeaningfulSuccess.observable_completion_signal: The local Quickstart opens the embedded-signing example after authentication, which the official overview identifies as its first-run demonstration of basic Docusign functionality; the docs do not identify a later completed-envelope response as part of this default terminal.

boundary_evidence.official_label_or_terminal_state: On first run, authenticate with Authorization Code Grant and open the embedded-signing example.

firstMeaningfulSuccess.why_this_is_the_boundary: The official overview labels Multiple code examples as the main Quickstart and directly states what its first run opens. A completed signature or envelope response would go beyond that published terminal.

prerequisites.requirement: Create a free Docusign developer account for the sandbox environment and be its owner or account admin, because Quickstart creates a new app and integration key.

Name the app, select Node.js, and choose the main Multiple code examples, Authorization Code Grant and JWT Grant project; Quickstart creates and preconfigures its app, integration key, account settings, and authentication code.

For a manually configured launcher instead of the selected Quickstart, provide an integration key and secret for Authorization Code Grant, or an integration key, RSA key pair, and impersonated user's User ID GUID for JWT Grant.
For a manually configured local launcher, register `http://localhost:3000/ds/callback` as the redirect URI.
Have Node.js 8.10 or later and npm 5 or later according to the official repository's stated prerequisite.
For manual launcher configuration, provide the signer name and email in `appsettings.json`.

stages.action: Open the eSignature REST API Quickstart and overview, then identify the main Node.js project and its documented first-run behavior.
details: The generator requires an account owner or admin because it creates a new app and integration key.
The overview calls Multiple code examples, Authorization Code Grant and JWT Grant the main project; its first run authenticates with Authorization Code Grant and opens the embedded-signing example.
Smaller Authorization Code Grant embedded-signing and JWT remote-signing projects remain selectable alternatives.
stages.output: The main Quickstart surface, generation choices, prerequisites, run commands, and first-run terminal are identified.
stages.success_signal: The official overview enumerates login, app naming, language and project selection, ZIP download, extraction and run, plus the main project's first-run destination.

branches.condition: Select a build-intent answer
branches.path: Choose Workflow, API Integration, Extension App, or Not Sure before submitting the account form.
branches.effect_on_first_success: May influence the hidden post-signup experience, but no public mapping is documented.

branches.condition: Generate the smaller Authorization Code Grant embedded-signing Quickstart
branches.path: Choose that project in the generator, download and extract the personalized ZIP, enter `quick_acg`, run `npm install` and `npm start`, open localhost, and authenticate through Authorization Code Grant to use its single embedded-signing workflow.
branches.effect_on_first_success: Narrows the project to authentication and one embedded-signing workflow instead of the selected main Quickstart.

branches.condition: Generate the smaller JWT Grant remote-signing Quickstart
branches.path: Choose that project in the generator, download and extract the personalized ZIP, enter `jwt_console_project`, run `npm install` and `npm start`, and use its single remote-signing workflow.
branches.effect_on_first_success: Changes authentication and the signing experience; first-time JWT impersonation consent may still be required.

branches.condition: Use the main Multiple code examples Quickstart
branches.path: Name the app, select Node.js and the main project, download and extract the personalized ZIP, run `npm install` and `npm start`, then authenticate with Authorization Code Grant and let the first run open embedded signing.
branches.effect_on_first_success: Follows the selected official main-project default and exposes broader examples only on later runs.

branches.condition: Configure the project manually instead of downloading a preconfigured Quickstart
branches.path: Create the app and integration key in Apps and Keys and populate the grant-specific settings and signer details yourself.
branches.effect_on_first_success: Adds app creation, credential generation, callback configuration, and secret-handling steps.

frictionGates.description: The developer-account form requires personal identity, company, country or region, and build-intent fields.
Get Started accepts Docusign's Terms and Conditions and Privacy Policy.

Verification, sandbox provisioning, and authenticated Quickstart access are hidden after form submission, and Quickstart requires the account owner or admin because it creates an app and integration key.
The generator requires an app name, language, and project-type choice; the overview identifies Multiple code examples as the main project and documents its first-run default.
Quickstart creates and preconfigures the selected route's app, integration key, account settings, and authentication code; manual credential setup applies only to the unconfigured launcher branch.
The selected main project first authenticates interactively with Authorization Code Grant; JWT alternatives can require first-time impersonation consent.
The selected Node.js Quickstart requires ZIP extraction, Node.js 8.10 or later, npm 5 or later, `npm install`, `npm start`, and a local browser session.

prerequisites.requirement: Create and sign in to an OptiView Real-time account to access the Streaming Dashboard; the account-creation sequence is not publicly enumerated.
Review and accept the Millicast Terms of Service through the required account-creation checkbox.
Have a web camera and microphone available for dashboard capture.
Grant the dashboard broadcaster access to the microphone and camera when starting the stream.
Create a publish token that authenticates the dashboard broadcast.
Public official getting-started documentation does not establish whether plan selection, payment details, or free entitlement is required before the first dashboard broadcast.

branches.condition: Account access originates in a restricted territory
branches.path: Check the current Platform Requirements territory table before attempting registration. Cuba, Iran, and North Korea are documented as unavailable for account creation, portal access, Streaming APIs, and payment; China permits account creation, portal access, and Streaming APIs but payment requires consultation.
branches.effect_on_first_success: Can block the journey before registration or add payment consultation, depending on territory.

branches.condition: Build a custom web application
branches.path: Continue to Part 2 after completing the dashboard exercise, then replace broadcast or playback with the Streaming Web SDK.
branches.effect_on_first_success: Adds code after the dashboard-first milestone.

branches.condition: Secure viewer is enabled
branches.path: Create and provide a subscribe token when opening the hosted viewer.
branches.effect_on_first_success: Adds a viewer credential; by default the subscriber does not need one.

branches.condition: Broadcast longer than four hours
branches.path: Use a software encoder, hardware encoder, or Dolby.io SDK instead of the dashboard broadcaster.
branches.effect_on_first_success: Avoids the dashboard's automatic four-hour logout but is unnecessary for the first stream.

frictionGates.description: The required account-creation and authentication transitions are not publicly enumerated.
Account creation requires checking that the developer has read and agrees to the Millicast Terms of Service.
Current territory restrictions can prohibit account creation and portal access or require consultation for payment.
The public first-mile sources do not establish whether a plan or payment state blocks the dashboard broadcast.
Publishing requires a dashboard-created publish token.
The browser must grant microphone and camera access when Start is clicked.
The first broadcast becomes observable when the LIVE box and timecode appear, then playback opens through the hosted viewer.

developerGoal: Get started with the Elastic platform, with no solution use case or deployment model supplied.

The developer has not selected managed cloud, local Docker, or self-managed deployment.

selectedSurface.name: Elastic Cloud trial to first Elasticsearch Console search
selectedSurface.selection_basis: Workshop selection policy (2026-07-19): when official docs present peer routes, commit to the fastest and most commonly used documented route. Prefer the vendor's first-listed or recommended quickstart, dashboard/console over CLI when both are peers, and the route with an explicit vendor time claim or fewest documented steps when tied. Selected route: Elastic Cloud trial to first Elasticsearch Console search. The current Search getting-started page recommends Elastic Cloud Serverless for simplicity and speed and points to Index and search basics. This is a complete representative Search candidate, not a platform-wide choice over Observability or Security. Prior surface before policy commit: Elastic platform, solution and deployment type not selected.

alternatives_considered.name: Get started with Elasticsearch
alternatives_considered.reason_not_primary: This is the Search solution path, while Elastic presents Observability and Security as peer platform solutions.

alternatives_considered.name: Get started with Elastic Observability
alternatives_considered.reason_not_primary: This is the Observability solution path, with additional source and ingestion-mode branches.

alternatives_considered.name: Get started with Elastic Security
alternatives_considered.reason_not_primary: This is the Security solution path, not a platform-wide default.

alternatives_considered.name: Local Elasticsearch and Kibana in Docker
alternatives_considered.reason_not_primary: Elastic lists local development alongside Elastic Cloud Hosted and Serverless quick-start options without making it the platform-wide default.

firstMeaningfulSuccess.official_milestone: The selected Elasticsearch Console route demonstrates first success when the expected book appears under hits.hits with its original _source JSON.
firstMeaningfulSuccess.normalized_outcome: The expected book appears under hits.hits with its original _source JSON.
firstMeaningfulSuccess.observable_completion_signal: The expected book appears under hits.hits with its original _source JSON.

boundary_evidence.official_label_or_terminal_state: The expected book appears under hits.hits with its original _source JSON.

firstMeaningfulSuccess.why_this_is_the_boundary: Selected under workshop fastest/most-used policy; terminal taken from the committed official candidate route.

prerequisites.requirement: A selected deployment model: Elastic Cloud Serverless, Elastic Cloud Hosted, local Docker, self-managed, Elastic Cloud Enterprise, or Elastic Cloud on Kubernetes.

stages.action: Open Elastic's Solutions overview.
details: The overview maps different developer needs to Elasticsearch, Elastic Observability, and Elastic Security.
stages.success_signal: The solution comparison and Get started links are visible.
stages.failure_or_wait: No universal route continues without a use-case choice.

stages.action: Open Elastic Cloud Sign Up and register with email and password, Google, Microsoft, or a supported marketplace.
details: First signup creates an Elastic Cloud organization and starts a free 14-day trial.
An email address can belong to only one Elastic Cloud account and a user can belong to only one organization at a time.
stages.input: Eligible email identity or supported federated or marketplace identity.
stages.output: Elastic Cloud account, organization, and trial.
stages.success_signal: The Elastic Cloud home page is available.
stages.failure_or_wait: A reused account email or existing organization membership can require leaving the organization or using another email.

stages.action: From the Elastic Cloud home page, choose to create a serverless project and select the Elasticsearch project type.
details: The trial currently permits one active serverless project.
Project creation requires the admin predefined role or an equivalent custom role.
Elastic says to choose Elasticsearch when unsure because it provides core search capabilities and search-building UI tools.
stages.input: Elastic Cloud trial organization with project-creation permission.
stages.success_signal: The project settings can be reviewed and submitted.
stages.failure_or_wait: Missing admin-equivalent permission, the active-project trial limit, the broader organization project limit, or blocked Elastic CDN domains can prevent creation or leave the UI blank.

stages.action: Review the serverless project settings and launch the project.
details: The public generic project page does not enumerate every current Elasticsearch settings field.
stages.input: Elasticsearch project selection and accepted settings.
stages.success_signal: Elastic creates the project and exposes entry to the project UI.
stages.failure_or_wait: Invalid project settings or provisioning failures block project access; no duration is documented for this candidate.

stages.action: Open the Elasticsearch project and navigate to Dev Tools, Console.
details: Console can also be opened as Persistent Console from the footer on supported Search and Elasticsearch Serverless pages.
stages.output: Interactive Console request and response panels.
stages.success_signal: Console accepts Elasticsearch API syntax.
stages.failure_or_wait: Blocked kibana.estccdn.com or cloud.elastic.co can make the project UI blank; Console can also be disabled by configuration.

stages.action: Run PUT /books in Console.
stages.success_signal: The JSON response contains acknowledged true, shards_acknowledged true, and index books.
stages.failure_or_wait: A non-success HTTP status or JSON error means the index was not created.

stages.action: Run POST books/_doc with the documented Snow Crash JSON document.
details: The document includes name, author, release_date, and page_count.
stages.input: Created books index and sample JSON.
stages.success_signal: The response result is created, failed shard count is zero, and a document ID is returned.
stages.failure_or_wait: Mapping, request-format, authorization, or shard failures prevent the document from becoming searchable.

stages.action: Make the indexed document available for search in near real time.
details: Elastic documents near-real-time search availability rather than immediate transactional visibility.
stages.success_signal: A subsequent search can return the document.
stages.failure_or_wait: A search issued before refresh may not immediately return the new document; no candidate-specific wait duration is given.

stages.action: Run GET books/_search in Console.
stages.success_signal: The response has timed_out false and a hits array containing the indexed book document.
stages.failure_or_wait: An empty result can mean refresh delay, wrong index, or a failed earlier indexing request; an error response must be corrected before retrying.

stages.action: Inspect the response panel and verify the returned hit contains the Snow Crash source fields.
details: Console also displays the HTTP status code and request execution time.
stages.output: Verified first Elasticsearch index, document, and search result.
stages.success_signal: The expected book appears under hits.hits with its original _source JSON.
stages.failure_or_wait: Missing or different source data means the documented first-search terminal has not been reached.

branches.condition: The goal is to build a search experience.
branches.path: Follow Get started with Elasticsearch, then choose Serverless, Stack, or local Docker and continue through data ingestion and query construction.
branches.effect_on_first_success: Candidate success becomes a documented indexing and search result, but the guide still offers multiple search quickstarts.

branches.condition: The goal is infrastructure or application observability.
branches.path: Follow Get started with Elastic Observability, choose a project tier and telemetry source, collect data, then explore the relevant UI.
branches.effect_on_first_success: Candidate success becomes visible logs, metrics, or traces, with source-specific setup branches.

branches.condition: The goal is threat detection and response.
branches.path: Follow Get started with Elastic Security, create a project, ingest data through a selected integration, and continue to a use-case quickstart.
branches.effect_on_first_success: Candidate success becomes a Security-specific data or use-case outcome.

frictionGates.description: Elastic requires a solution choice among Search, Observability, and Security before the documented routes converge on any task.
Elastic also requires a deployment-model choice, with managed, local, and self-managed options carrying different account and environment gates.

uncertainties.question: Which Elastic solution defines the intended first-mile journey?
uncertainties.impact: It changes the actor, prerequisites, steps, gates, and completion signal.
uncertainties.reason_unresolved: The official platform overview presents Elasticsearch, Observability, and Security as use-case-dependent peers.

uncertainties.question: Which deployment model should the selected solution use?
uncertainties.impact: It determines whether the journey begins with trial signup, project creation, Docker installation, Kubernetes, or self-managed infrastructure.
uncertainties.reason_unresolved: The deployment guide offers multiple quick-start and advanced models without a platform-wide default.

developerGoal: Create an ElevenLabs Free account and API key, then receive synthesized audio over HTTPS without installing an SDK.

entryPoint.developer_intent: Create an ElevenLabs Free account and API key, then receive synthesized audio over HTTPS without installing an SDK.
assumed_prior_state: No ElevenLabs account, API key, or TTS request is assumed.
A terminal with cURL is available; no language SDK is assumed.

selectedSurface.name: Text to Speech API via cURL (API key + Free-tier credits)
selectedSurface.selection_basis: ElevenLabs API Authentication documents xi-api-key header auth and a first cURL request. The Create speech API reference documents POST https://api.elevenlabs.io/v1/text-to-speech/{voice_id} returning audio. Billing docs assign Free tier automatically at signup with included credit quota. An SDK install is not required.

alternatives_considered.name: ElevenAPI Python/TypeScript Quickstart
alternatives_considered.reason_not_primary: Requires SDK install and speaker playback helpers; the same TTS endpoint works with cURL.

alternatives_considered.name: Speech Synthesis web UI only
alternatives_considered.reason_not_primary: In-browser generate path; not the programmatic API first success.

alternatives_considered.name: cURL GET /v1/models auth probe only
alternatives_considered.reason_not_primary: Proves authentication but does not produce text-to-speech audio.

firstMeaningfulSuccess.official_milestone: Create speech HTTP 200 audio response
firstMeaningfulSuccess.normalized_outcome: An authenticated cURL POST to https://api.elevenlabs.io/v1/text-to-speech/{voice_id} returns audio bytes (for example written to an mp3 file).
firstMeaningfulSuccess.observable_completion_signal: The HTTP response is successful and the body is playable audio for the requested text.

boundary_evidence.official_label_or_terminal_state: Create speech documents POST /v1/text-to-speech/{voice_id} with xi-api-key and a JSON body containing text and model_id, returning application/octet-stream audio.

firstMeaningfulSuccess.why_this_is_the_boundary: The first meaningful TTS API success is returned speech audio. Authentication docs establish the xi-api-key header pattern with cURL. Free-tier credits are assigned at signup; paid plans and Pay As You Go follow after quota exhaustion.

prerequisites.requirement: An ElevenLabs account (email verification for email signup, or OAuth).
Usable Free-tier credit quota (assigned automatically at signup). Paid subscription or Pay As You Go is required only after Free quota is exhausted.
An API key included as xi-api-key on every request.
A terminal with cURL (or any HTTP client). No language SDK is required.

stages.action: Create an ElevenLabs account and verify email if using email signup.
details: Account docs: Get started free, then verify emailed link for email/password signup.
OAuth (Google, Facebook, GitHub) is an alternative signup route.
stages.input: Intent to use the ElevenLabs API
stages.success_signal: The dashboard and API key settings are reachable.
stages.failure_or_wait: Email verification is required before email-signup accounts can use the service.

stages.action: Confirm Free-tier credits are available (automatic at signup), or purchase Pay As You Go / upgrade if quota is exhausted.
details: Billing docs: new accounts are automatically assigned to the free tier with included credit quota.
API requests deduct from the quota.
stages.success_signal: Subscription shows Free tier with remaining credits.
stages.failure_or_wait: Exhausted credits require Pay As You Go or a paid plan before further generation.

stages.action: Create an API key in the dashboard and store it securely.
details: Authentication docs: every request must include xi-api-key.
stages.success_signal: The key value is available locally.
stages.failure_or_wait: Keys may be scope-restricted; Text to Speech permission must be allowed for this route.

stages.action: Export the key as ELEVENLABS_API_KEY (or substitute directly into xi-api-key).
stages.output: Key available to HTTP clients in the shell
stages.success_signal: The environment variable is set for the current shell.
stages.failure_or_wait: Shell persistence depends on the OS.

stages.action: POST text-to-speech with cURL to https://api.elevenlabs.io/v1/text-to-speech/{voice_id}.
details: Include xi-api-key and Content-Type: application/json.
Send text and model_id in the JSON body; optionally set output_format=mp3_44100_128 and write the response to a file with --output.
stages.input: API key, Free-tier credits, and voice_id
stages.success_signal: HTTP 200 with audio bytes for the requested text.
stages.failure_or_wait: 401 invalid key; 422 invalid parameters; 429 rate limit or exhausted quota.

stages.action: Confirm the response body is playable audio (for example open the saved mp3).
stages.success_signal: Audio plays or file size is non-zero for the sample text.
stages.failure_or_wait: JSON error body means the first call did not succeed.

branches.condition: The developer prefers the official SDK Quickstart.
branches.path: Install the Python or TypeScript SDK and call text_to_speech.convert for the same voice and text.
branches.effect_on_first_success: Adds package install and optional speaker playback without changing the TTS endpoint boundary.

branches.condition: Free-tier credits are exhausted.
branches.path: Upgrade subscription or purchase Pay As You Go credits before further API generation.
branches.effect_on_first_success: Billing purchase becomes required after Free quota is gone.

frictionGates.description: Email signup requires email verification before service use.
Free-tier quota is automatic; paid plans or Pay As You Go are required after credits are exhausted.
xi-api-key must authenticate every API request.

uncertainties.question: Exact Free-tier character/credit allotment and commercial-use restrictions for API-generated audio on Free.
uncertainties.impact: May affect how far demos can go before requiring Pay As You Go.
uncertainties.reason_unresolved: Billing points to the pricing/subscription page for plan tables rather than embedding current Free numbers in the billing article.

developerGoal: Start building a backend application with Encore by installing the CLI and creating a new app.

assumed_prior_state: Developer has a compatible local development environment (e.g., macOS with Homebrew or equivalent shell environment).
entryPoint.developer_intent: Start building a backend application with Encore by installing the CLI and creating a new app.

selectedSurface.name: Encore CLI and local development environment
selectedSurface.selection_basis: The CLI is the primary interface for developers to create, run, and manage Encore applications locally and deploy to cloud environments, as documented in the official quick start and overview guides.

boundary_evidence.official_label_or_terminal_state: Quick Start Guide: 'If you see this JSON response, you've successfully made an API call to your very first Encore application.'

firstMeaningfulSuccess.normalized_outcome: Developer runs the app locally and successfully calls the API endpoint, receiving the expected JSON response
firstMeaningfulSuccess.observable_completion_signal: Receiving the JSON response {"Message": "Hello, world!"} from the API endpoint call
firstMeaningfulSuccess.official_milestone: Successfully run a local Encore app and receive a valid API response from the local development environment
firstMeaningfulSuccess.why_this_is_the_boundary: This milestone marks the first time the developer has a running Encore app locally and can successfully interact with it, demonstrating the platform's core functionality working end-to-end.

prerequisites.requirement: Install the Encore CLI appropriate for your system (e.g., via Homebrew on macOS)
Create a free Encore Cloud account when prompted during app creation to enable cloud deployment and secret management

stages.action: Install Encore CLI
details: Run the appropriate install command for your system, e.g., 'brew install encoredev/tap/encore' on macOS
stages.failure_or_wait: Installation errors or missing dependencies
stages.success_signal: Encore CLI command is recognized and runs

stages.action: Create a new Encore app
details: Run 'encore app create'
When prompted, create a free Encore Cloud account
Select TypeScript as the app language
Choose the 'Hello World' starter template
Optionally install AI instructions for enhanced tooling
Provide a name for the app
stages.failure_or_wait: Account creation or network issues
stages.output: New app folder created with starter code
stages.success_signal: App folder with starter code is created

stages.action: Open and review the starter app code
details: Open 'hello.ts' file in the app folder
Understand the API endpoint definition and service structure
stages.failure_or_wait: None
stages.success_signal: Developer can identify the API endpoint code

stages.action: Run the Encore app locally
details: Navigate to the app folder
Run 'encore run' to start the local development environment
stages.failure_or_wait: Errors in starting local environment
stages.output: Local development environment running with infrastructure
stages.success_signal: Local environment is up and running without errors

stages.action: Open the Local Development Dashboard
details: Open http://localhost:9400 in a browser
stages.failure_or_wait: Dashboard not reachable
stages.success_signal: Dashboard shows API explorer and service catalog

stages.action: Call the API endpoint
details: Use curl or API explorer to call the endpoint, e.g., 'curl http://localhost:4000/hello/world'
stages.failure_or_wait: No response or error response
stages.success_signal: Response contains {"Message": "Hello, world!"}

branches.condition: Developer already has an Encore Cloud account
branches.effect_on_first_success: Speeds up onboarding by skipping account creation step

developerGoal: Follow Esri's general developer getting-started guidance to create credentials and display the first documented map.

entryPoint.developer_intent: Follow Esri's general developer getting-started guidance to create credentials and display the first documented map.
assumed_prior_state: A browser, an accessible email inbox, and CodePen are available.
No ArcGIS account, subscription, organization, portal, API key, authentication choice, or client SDK is assumed.

selectedSurface.name: ArcGIS Location Platform API key and JavaScript Display a map
selectedSurface.selection_basis: Workshop selection policy (2026-07-19): when official docs present peer routes, commit to the fastest and most commonly used documented route. Prefer the vendor's first-listed or recommended quickstart, dashboard/console over CLI when both are peers, and the route with an explicit vendor time claim or fewest documented steps when tied. Selected route: ArcGIS Location Platform API key and JavaScript Display a map. This combines the first-listed account and Display a map client with the tutorial's required API key route. It is a reproducible candidate, not a company-wide default. Prior surface before policy commit: Esri mapping and location services getting started, with an ArcGIS Location Platform and JavaScript map candidate.

alternatives_considered.name: ArcGIS Online account route
alternatives_considered.reason_not_primary: ArcGIS Online is a peer account type with a multi-user organization, organization-admin controls, and different billing ownership.

alternatives_considered.name: OAuth 2.0 user authentication
alternatives_considered.reason_not_primary: It is a peer authentication route for private applications and changes credential creation, user consent, and billing ownership.

alternatives_considered.name: Other Maps SDKs and open-source clients
alternatives_considered.reason_not_primary: The Display a map tutorial is offered for multiple web and native SDKs without a universal client default.

firstMeaningfulSuccess.official_milestone: Display a map with the ArcGIS Basemap Styles service.
firstMeaningfulSuccess.normalized_outcome: In the JavaScript candidate, the CodePen application displays the topographic basemap for the Santa Monica Mountains and allows pan and zoom.
firstMeaningfulSuccess.observable_completion_signal: Running the pen displays the topographic basemap centered near `-118.805, 34.020` at zoom 13 with the zoom component.

boundary_evidence.official_label_or_terminal_state: Display a map

firstMeaningfulSuccess.why_this_is_the_boundary: The general page labels Display a map as a first-application tutorial, and the JavaScript tutorial ends its required steps by running the app and observing the named topographic map before listing later tutorials.

prerequisites.requirement: Choose an eligible ArcGIS account route; the candidate uses a free ArcGIS Location Platform account and its single-user organization.

For the email candidate, provide a unique email, compliant unique username and password, unique 3-to-16-character portal URL, and security question and answer, then accept the platform agreement and privacy policy.
Activate the account from the confirmation email within 24 hours.
Create API key credentials for a public application with Basemap styles service privilege and retain the one-time-visible API key.
Use a browser and a CodePen pen for the selected ArcGIS Maps SDK for JavaScript route.

stages.action: Open Esri's mapping and location services Get started page.
stages.output: Account, authentication, service, and client-API choices plus first-application tutorials are visible.
stages.success_signal: The page offers ArcGIS Location Platform and ArcGIS Online accounts, API key and OAuth routes, and multiple Display a map clients.
stages.failure_or_wait: No universal combination is selected.

stages.action: Open ArcGIS Location Platform signup and complete the email form with the documented identity, portal URL, security question, agreement, and privacy inputs.
details: The email must not already belong to another Location Platform account.
The portal URL cannot be changed after account creation.
stages.input: Unique email, compliant username and password, unique portal URL, security answer, and legal acceptance
stages.success_signal: The signup form is complete and Esri sends activation instructions.
stages.failure_or_wait: Username and portal URL must be unique and meet the documented character rules.

stages.action: Follow the confirmation-email instructions within 24 hours.
stages.output: An activated ArcGIS Location Platform account and subscription
stages.success_signal: The account can sign in to the Location Platform dashboard.
stages.failure_or_wait: The activation window is 24 hours.

stages.action: Go to `https://location.arcgis.com`, sign in, and click My portal from the dashboard.
stages.success_signal: Portal content management is available.
stages.failure_or_wait: None documented for this step.

stages.action: Click Content > My content > New item, then Developer credentials > API key credentials > Next.
stages.output: The API key credential wizard opens.
stages.success_signal: The application-type screen is shown.
stages.failure_or_wait: If credential options are absent, the account lacks the documented role or privileges.

stages.action: Choose Public application and click Next, then select No item access and click Next.
details: Private-item access is optional and not required for this basemap tutorial.
stages.output: A public credential without private item access
stages.success_signal: The service-privilege screen opens.
stages.failure_or_wait: A private application must use the corresponding private option instead.

stages.action: Select Location services > Basemaps, then click Next.
stages.output: Basemap styles service privilege is assigned.
stages.success_signal: The expiration and referrer screen opens.
stages.failure_or_wait: The map cannot access its basemap without the required privilege.

stages.action: Set the expiration date to one month from today, enter allowed referrer domains, and click Next.
details: The tutorial highly recommends referrer restrictions.
stages.output: Token lifetime and web-origin restrictions are configured.
stages.success_signal: The item-details screen opens.
stages.failure_or_wait: Changing expiration or referrers later invalidates the token.

stages.action: Set title `My API key credentials`, create or select folder `Developer credentials`, add tags and a description, click Next, review the summary, and click Next again.
stages.output: The API key credential item is saved.
stages.success_signal: The Generate API key window appears.
stages.failure_or_wait: Review privileges and item access before generating a key because they determine its reach.

stages.action: Select Generate the API key and go to item details page, confirm readiness to copy and save it, click Next, and copy the displayed key.
stages.output: A long-lived API key access token
stages.success_signal: The Copy your key window displays the token and it is retained for the application.
stages.failure_or_wait: The value is visible only once; losing it requires regeneration.

stages.action: Create a new CodePen pen and add the tutorial's basic HTML and full-page CSS.
stages.success_signal: The HTML body is ready for the map component.
stages.failure_or_wait: Outside CodePen, the document also needs complete HTML tags.

stages.action: Define global `esriConfig` before the SDK libraries and set its `apiKey` property to the copied access token.
stages.output: The application is configured to authenticate location-service requests.
stages.success_signal: `esriConfig.apiKey` contains the token value.
stages.failure_or_wait: The token needs the Basemap privilege used by the tutorial.

stages.action: Add `<script type="module" src="https://js.arcgis.com/5.1/"></script>` in the head.
stages.output: ArcGIS Maps SDK for JavaScript is loaded from Esri's CDN.
stages.success_signal: The custom map elements are available to the page.
stages.failure_or_wait: None documented for this step.

stages.action: Add an `arcgis-map` component with basemap `arcgis/topographic`, center `-118.805, 34.020`, and zoom `13`, then add an `arcgis-zoom` component in the top-left slot.
stages.output: The map and zoom controls are declared.
stages.success_signal: The page contains the complete tutorial markup.
stages.failure_or_wait: None documented for this step.

stages.action: Run the CodePen application.
stages.success_signal: The Santa Monica Mountains basemap displays and can be panned and zoomed.
stages.failure_or_wait: No run recovery is documented in the selected tutorial.

branches.condition: Use ArcGIS Online
branches.path: Use an organization account with the correct user type and role, then follow the ArcGIS Online variant of API key creation.
branches.effect_on_first_success: Adds organization-admin and role dependencies and changes which organization bears service usage.

branches.condition: Build a private app with signed-in users
branches.path: Create OAuth credentials and implement OAuth 2.0 user authentication instead of embedding an API key.
branches.effect_on_first_success: Adds redirect, user sign-in, consent, and token-flow work.

branches.condition: Choose another Display a map client
branches.path: Follow that SDK or open-source library's own Display a map tutorial.
branches.effect_on_first_success: Changes runtime prerequisites, project setup, code, and execution environment.

branches.condition: Choose an alternate Location Platform identity method
branches.path: Use an available organization-specific login route rather than the email candidate.
branches.effect_on_first_success: Introduces external identity and possible administrator or support dependencies; the same account page also contains conflicting statements about social signup availability for new accounts.

frictionGates.description: Account, authentication, and client choices materially change the route and no universal combination is selected.
Email signup requires unique account and portal identifiers, a security answer, legal acceptance, and email activation.
The key must have the Basemap styles service privilege.
The generated API key is visible only once and later regeneration invalidates the prior key.
Expiration and referrer restrictions are configured before generation, and changing them later invalidates the key.

uncertainties.question: Which account, authentication method, and client should define Esri Developer's company-wide first mile?
uncertainties.impact: The answer changes organization requirements, billing ownership, credential and consent work, runtime setup, and code.
uncertainties.reason_unresolved: The general page presents these as purpose-dependent peers; the candidate uses first-listed options rather than an official universal default.

uncertainties.question: Can a brand-new Location Platform user currently sign up with a social identity?
uncertainties.impact: The new-user identity path and verification steps differ.
uncertainties.reason_unresolved: The account page first says social signup is supported, but its Social media section says new social signup is not currently supported and limits it to migrated pre-June-2024 accounts.

developerGoal: Create an Exa account, obtain free credits and an API key, and complete a Search API request over HTTPS without installing an SDK.

entryPoint.developer_intent: Create an Exa account, obtain free credits and an API key, and complete a Search API request over HTTPS without installing an SDK.
assumed_prior_state: No Exa account, free credits, API key, or prior request is assumed.
A terminal with cURL is available; no language SDK is assumed.

selectedSurface.name: Get started with Exa via cURL Search
selectedSurface.selection_basis: Exa's Get started guide states its outcome is making the first request to an Exa API endpoint. The same page documents a cURL POST to https://api.exa.ai/search with x-api-key and says cURL needs no installation. Search and crawl is the first listed use case. SDK install is optional.

alternatives_considered.name: Python or JavaScript SDK Search
alternatives_considered.reason_not_primary: Optional client libraries; the quickstart documents an equivalent cURL Search request and says cURL needs no installation.

alternatives_considered.name: Answer or Chat Completions first request
alternatives_considered.reason_not_primary: Peer use cases on the same page; Search and crawl is listed first for the first request.

alternatives_considered.name: API Playground
alternatives_considered.reason_not_primary: Separate exploration route; QuickStart defines first success as an API request.

firstMeaningfulSuccess.official_milestone: Make your first request
firstMeaningfulSuccess.normalized_outcome: An authenticated cURL POST to https://api.exa.ai/search returns search results JSON.
firstMeaningfulSuccess.observable_completion_signal: The Search API returns a successful JSON body containing search results for the requested query.

boundary_evidence.official_label_or_terminal_state: Get started with Exa documents Make your first request, includes a cURL POST to https://api.exa.ai/search with x-api-key, and states cURL needs no installation.

firstMeaningfulSuccess.why_this_is_the_boundary: The first meaningful Exa success is a successful authenticated Search API response. cURL is documented on the same quickstart as the SDKs, and the page says cURL needs no installation. New accounts receive $10 free credits after onboarding; a payment card is not required for that first funded request while free credits remain.

prerequisites.requirement: An Exa account that can open the Dashboard.
A positive credit balance. New accounts receive $10 in free credits when onboarding is completed; requests are blocked when the balance runs out.
An Exa API key from the Dashboard, exported as EXA_API_KEY.
A terminal with cURL (or any HTTP client). No language SDK is required.

stages.action: Open Get started with Exa (QuickStart).
details: The page states Make your first request to one of Exa's API endpoints.
stages.output: Documented first-request path with cURL and SDK tabs
stages.success_signal: The cURL Search example is visible.
stages.failure_or_wait: No navigation recovery is documented.

stages.action: Create or sign in to an Exa account and open the Dashboard.
details: Treat signup as one opaque step at the official Dashboard entry.
stages.input: Intent to use the Exa API
stages.success_signal: API Keys and Billing are reachable.
stages.failure_or_wait: Follow the displayed account recovery path.

stages.action: Complete new-account onboarding to receive the documented $10 in free credits.
details: Billing docs state new accounts receive $10 in free credits when you complete onboarding.
A payment method is not required for this free-credit first request while the balance remains positive.
stages.success_signal: Billing shows a positive credit balance from free credits.
stages.failure_or_wait: Incomplete onboarding leaves credits unavailable; exhausted credits block API requests until more credits are added.

stages.action: Get an Exa API key from the Dashboard and store it securely.
details: The quickstart says to get the key from the Exa Dashboard.
stages.success_signal: The key value is available locally.
stages.failure_or_wait: No manual approval wait is documented for key creation.

stages.action: Export the key as EXA_API_KEY in the terminal.
details: macOS/Linux: export EXA_API_KEY=...; Windows: setx EXA_API_KEY ...
stages.output: Shell environment ready for authenticated HTTP
stages.success_signal: EXA_API_KEY is set for the current shell.
stages.failure_or_wait: Shell persistence depends on the OS.

stages.action: POST a Search request with cURL to https://api.exa.ai/search.
details: Include accept and content-type application/json headers.
Include x-api-key: ${EXA_API_KEY}.
Send a JSON body with query, type auto, and contents.highlights as shown in the quickstart cURL Search example.
stages.success_signal: The API returns a successful JSON body containing search results.
stages.failure_or_wait: Missing key, exhausted credits (402), or invalid JSON produce API errors.

stages.action: Confirm the Search JSON includes search results for the query.
stages.success_signal: Search results are present in the successful response.
stages.failure_or_wait: An error body means the first call did not succeed.

branches.condition: The developer prefers the Python or JavaScript SDK.
branches.path: Install exa-py or exa-js (and dotenv if using a .env file), then run the published Search example with the same EXA_API_KEY and credit balance.
branches.effect_on_first_success: Adds optional local package installation without changing the authenticated Search boundary.

branches.condition: The developer prefers Answer or Chat Completions instead of Search.
branches.path: Use the documented cURL examples for https://api.exa.ai/answer or https://api.exa.ai/chat/completions with the same API key.
branches.effect_on_first_success: Peer first-request endpoints on the same quickstart page.

branches.condition: Free credits are exhausted before or during the first request.
branches.path: Add credits from Billing (payment via Stripe) or enable auto recharge before retrying the API call.
branches.effect_on_first_success: Billing purchase becomes required once the free balance is gone.

frictionGates.description: An Exa account and Dashboard access are required before an API key can be created.
New accounts must complete onboarding to receive the documented $10 free credits; requests fail when credits are exhausted.
A secret API key must be obtained and supplied as x-api-key on the Search request.

developerGoal: Create and run the simplest FastAPI application and observe its first HTTP response.

entryPoint.developer_intent: Create and run the simplest FastAPI application and observe its first HTTP response.
assumed_prior_state: The developer has Python installed and can use a terminal and editor.
No FastAPI project or package installation is assumed.

selectedSurface.name: Official First Steps Hello World API
selectedSurface.selection_basis: FastAPI's Tutorial begins by installing FastAPI and its next page is explicitly titled `First Steps`. That page supplies the simplest complete `main.py`, runs it with the development server, and has a `Check it` section whose first observable result is the JSON response `{'message': 'Hello World'}` at the root URL.

alternatives_considered.name: Automatic interactive API documentation
alternatives_considered.reason_not_primary: The docs visit `/docs` only after the root JSON response has already established a working API.

alternatives_considered.name: FastAPI Cloud deployment
alternatives_considered.reason_not_primary: The page marks deployment optional and places it after local first success.

alternatives_considered.name: Manual Uvicorn server command
alternatives_considered.reason_not_primary: The current first-steps route uses the FastAPI CLI installed by the standard package.

firstMeaningfulSuccess.official_milestone: Check it
firstMeaningfulSuccess.normalized_outcome: A locally running FastAPI development server returns the documented Hello World JSON object for a GET request to `/`.
firstMeaningfulSuccess.observable_completion_signal: Opening `http://127.0.0.1:8000` shows the JSON response `{'message': 'Hello World'}`.

boundary_evidence.official_label_or_terminal_state: The official First Steps page places the root response under the heading `Check it` immediately after the server command.

firstMeaningfulSuccess.why_this_is_the_boundary: This is the first end-to-end proof that installation, import, application construction, route registration, server startup, HTTP handling, and serialization all work. Interactive docs and deployment follow afterward.

prerequisites.requirement: Python, its package installer, a terminal, and an editor are required.
Create and activate a project-specific Python virtual environment before installation.
Package installation requires access to download FastAPI and its dependencies.

stages.action: Create a project directory, create a virtual environment with `python -m venv .venv`, and activate it for the current shell.
details: On macOS, Linux, or Windows Bash, activation is `source .venv/bin/activate` or the documented Windows path.
On Windows PowerShell, use the documented `.venv\Scripts\Activate.ps1` command.
stages.input: A working Python installation and writable project directory.
stages.success_signal: The shell uses the Python interpreter inside `.venv`; the optional official check is `which python` or `Get-Command python`.
stages.failure_or_wait: Missing Python, an unavailable `venv` module, or shell execution-policy restrictions block activation.

stages.action: Install the documented standard FastAPI package with `pip install "fastapi[standard]"` in the active environment.
details: The standard extra includes the `fastapi` command and Uvicorn server dependencies.
The tutorial lists non-standard alternatives, but the primary first-steps command depends on the standard CLI installation.
stages.input: Active virtual environment and package-network access.
stages.output: FastAPI and its standard dependencies installed locally.
stages.success_signal: The installation completes and the `fastapi` command is available.
stages.failure_or_wait: Package index, proxy, certificate, permission, or incompatible Python problems can interrupt installation; the virtual-environment guide recommends upgrading or repairing pip for pip-specific errors.

stages.action: Create `main.py` containing the official simplest application: import `FastAPI`, instantiate `app`, register `GET /`, and return `{'message': 'Hello World'}` from `root`.
details: The official example defines `root` with `async def`.
The recap explains the import, instance, path operation decorator, function, and returned content as five conceptual steps.
stages.output: A saved `main.py` with a root path operation.
stages.success_signal: The file is present in the working directory with the `app` object and route exactly defined.
stages.failure_or_wait: A syntax error, wrong filename, or unsaved file prevents import at server startup.

stages.action: From the project directory, run `fastapi dev` and wait for application startup.
details: The CLI searches the package structure, imports `main.py`, finds the `app` object, starts the development server, and prints the local URL.
Passing `main.py` explicitly is supported but is not needed for this primary route.
stages.input: Saved `main.py` and active environment with the FastAPI CLI.
stages.success_signal: The terminal reports `Application startup complete` and the local server URL.
stages.failure_or_wait: Import errors, missing CLI, wrong directory, occupied port, or invalid code prevent the server from becoming ready.

stages.action: Open `http://127.0.0.1:8000` in a browser and inspect the returned JSON.
details: The route is a GET request to `/`.
The response body is the documented Hello World object.
stages.success_signal: The browser shows `{'message': 'Hello World'}` as documented under `Check it`.
stages.failure_or_wait: Connection refusal means the server is not listening; a 404 indicates a path mismatch; a 500 response or traceback indicates application failure.

branches.condition: The developer uses `uv` instead of Python `venv` and pip.
branches.path: Create the environment with `uv venv`, activate it, and install with `uv pip install "fastapi[standard]"`; the application, server, and verification steps remain the same.
branches.effect_on_first_success: Only environment creation and package installation syntax change.

branches.condition: The developer intentionally excludes FastAPI Cloud CLI or all optional standard dependencies.
branches.path: Use the documented `fastapi[standard-no-fastapi-cloud-cli]` alternative, or install bare `fastapi` and separately provide the needed ASGI server and invocation path.
branches.effect_on_first_success: The no-cloud standard variant preserves the `fastapi` command; bare installation can require a manual Uvicorn setup not used by the primary tutorial.

branches.condition: The developer uses a normal synchronous function instead of `async def`.
branches.path: Define `def root()` as the page's documented alternative and return the same object.
branches.effect_on_first_success: The root response and completion signal are unchanged.

frictionGates.description: Python and a project-specific virtual environment are required before the framework installation.
The recommended standard package downloads FastAPI, its CLI, Uvicorn, and dependencies.
The module filename, exported `app` object, decorator, function, and response content must be importable by the CLI.
The server must finish importing and report application startup complete before the browser check.
First success is not server output alone; the root URL must return the documented JSON object.

The representative developer is using Windows and does not yet have Git, VS Code, or Flutter configured.

selectedSurface.name: Set up and test drive Flutter
selectedSurface.selection_basis: Flutter's install documentation labels this page Quick start, recommends its VS Code route, and states a bounded achievement: install prerequisites and Flutter, create an app, run it on the web, and try stateful hot reload. The page is configured to Windows by default, which supplies the representative primary path.

alternatives_considered.name: Custom Flutter setup
alternatives_considered.reason_not_primary: The custom setup page tells VS Code users to consider the quick start instead and is intended for other editors, manual setup, or particular target platforms.

alternatives_considered.name: Create a new Flutter app
alternatives_considered.reason_not_primary: This reference assumes Flutter is already set up, while the selected quick start covers a new developer from installation through a running and hot-reloaded app.

alternatives_considered.name: Write your first app
alternatives_considered.reason_not_primary: This longer codelab assumes setup is complete and claims about 90 minutes, while the quick start is the recommended first setup and test-drive surface.

firstMeaningfulSuccess.official_milestone: Try hot reload
firstMeaningfulSuccess.normalized_outcome: The generated Flutter counter app runs in Chrome, retains its current counter state across a source edit, and uses the edited decrement behavior after stateful hot reload.
firstMeaningfulSuccess.observable_completion_signal: After incrementing the counter, changing _counter++ to _counter--, and saving or clicking Hot Reload, the existing value remains and the next click decreases it.

boundary_evidence.official_label_or_terminal_state: The quick start's achievement list includes trying stateful hot reload, and its hot-reload section ends by telling the developer to notice that the value decreases instead of increasing.

firstMeaningfulSuccess.why_this_is_the_boundary: This is the last observable behavior in the page's stated quick-start achievements. The following Flutter-sidebar exploration is product orientation after the app already runs and hot reload has succeeded.

prerequisites.requirement: A Windows computer supported by the current Flutter toolchain, with internet access and permission to install software and write the SDK and project files.
Git for Windows and Visual Studio Code, or another Code OSS-based editor compatible with the Flutter extension.
Google Chrome for the selected web execution route.

stages.action: Open the Flutter quick start and keep Windows selected as the development platform.
details: The guide also offers macOS, Linux, and ChromeOS variants.
stages.success_signal: Windows is shown as the configured platform.
stages.failure_or_wait: Choosing another operating system changes prerequisite installation actions but not the later VS Code test drive.

stages.action: Download and install the latest Git for Windows.
details: Skip only if Git is already installed.
stages.success_signal: Git is available for Flutter SDK cloning.
stages.failure_or_wait: Git installation or PATH problems block the VS Code SDK download.

stages.action: Download and install Visual Studio Code.
details: A compatible Code OSS-based editor may substitute for VS Code.
stages.success_signal: VS Code launches.
stages.failure_or_wait: Installation policy or permissions can block the editor setup.

stages.action: Open VS Code and install the Flutter extension from its Marketplace page.
details: Installing the Flutter extension also provides the Dart tooling used by the guide.
stages.output: Flutter commands available in VS Code
stages.success_signal: The command palette can surface Flutter commands.
stages.failure_or_wait: Marketplace, network, or extension-installation failures block the guided SDK setup.

stages.action: Open View > Command Palette, type flutter, and select Flutter: New Project.
details: Because no SDK is configured yet, VS Code asks the developer to locate it.
stages.success_signal: Download SDK is offered.
stages.failure_or_wait: If the command is absent, verify the Flutter extension installation.

stages.action: Select Download SDK, choose an installation folder, and click Clone Flutter.
details: VS Code downloads the SDK into the selected folder.
stages.success_signal: The SDK download completes.
stages.failure_or_wait: The download can take a few minutes; if it appears hung, click Cancel and restart the installation.

stages.action: Click Add SDK to PATH after the SDK clone completes.
details: VS Code displays a success notification when the path update succeeds.
stages.success_signal: The notification says the Flutter SDK was added to PATH.
stages.failure_or_wait: A failed PATH update prevents new terminals from finding flutter.

stages.action: Respond to the optional Google Analytics notice, then close and reopen all terminals and restart VS Code.
details: Consent is optional; the restart is required so all terminals receive the updated PATH.
stages.success_signal: A new terminal can resolve Flutter tooling.
stages.failure_or_wait: An Android Studio warning can be ignored for the selected web route; run flutter doctor to verify installation if needed.

stages.action: Open the command palette again and select Flutter: New Project.
details: An operating-system or editor documents-access prompt may appear; agree to proceed.
stages.success_signal: Which Flutter template? appears.
stages.failure_or_wait: Denied filesystem access prevents project creation.

stages.action: Choose the Application template.
details: The guide says this bootstraps a simple counter app.
stages.success_signal: VS Code asks for the project parent directory.
stages.failure_or_wait: Choosing a different template changes the documented output.

stages.action: Select or create the parent directory and confirm Select a folder to create the project in.
details: Select the parent directory, not a pre-created project folder.
stages.success_signal: VS Code prompts for an app name.
stages.failure_or_wait: Filesystem permission errors block scaffolding.

stages.action: Enter trying_flutter or another lowercase_with_underscores project name and press Enter.
stages.success_signal: VS Code starts project initialization.
stages.failure_or_wait: An invalid name must be replaced with one following the documented naming convention.

stages.action: Wait while VS Code runs Flutter project initialization and retrieves dependencies.
details: Progress appears in a notification and the Output panel.
stages.output: Generated counter app opened in VS Code
stages.success_signal: Initialization finishes and lib/main.dart is available.
stages.failure_or_wait: Dependency download and project initialization can take time; inspect the Output panel for failures.

stages.action: Open lib/main.dart.
details: The generated comments explain the counter sample.
stages.success_signal: main.dart is open in the editor.
stages.failure_or_wait: No wait is documented.

stages.action: Open the command palette, select Flutter: Select Device, and choose Chrome.
details: The quick start deliberately uses the web route even though Flutter targets several platforms.
stages.output: Chrome selected as the Flutter target
stages.success_signal: Chrome is the active device.
stages.failure_or_wait: If Chrome is unavailable, install it before selecting the web target.

stages.action: Select Run > Start Debugging or press F5.
details: Flutter builds and starts the app with flutter run, then opens a new Chrome instance.
stages.output: Running Flutter counter app in Chrome
stages.success_signal: Chrome opens and displays the newly created counter app.
stages.failure_or_wait: Build, browser, or toolchain errors must be resolved before the app can open.

stages.action: Click the increment button several times while the app is running.
details: This creates nonzero state that the hot-reload check must preserve.
stages.success_signal: The displayed counter increases.
stages.failure_or_wait: If the button does not update, the app is not running as expected.

stages.action: In lib/main.dart, change _counter++ to _counter--, then save all files or click Hot Reload.
details: Do not restart the app; the check relies on retaining its current state.
stages.input: Running app with nonzero counter state
stages.success_signal: The existing counter value stays the same immediately after hot reload.
stages.failure_or_wait: Compilation errors prevent the hot reload from applying.

stages.action: Click the counter button again and observe the value.
stages.success_signal: The retained counter value now decreases instead of increasing.
stages.failure_or_wait: If it still increases, confirm the edited line was saved and hot reload completed successfully.

branches.condition: The development computer runs macOS.
branches.path: Install Git through the recommended Xcode command-line-tools route, install VS Code, then rejoin the shared VS Code SDK setup.
branches.effect_on_first_success: Changes prerequisite installation and OS prompts but preserves the same generated web app and hot-reload boundary.

branches.condition: The development computer runs Linux or ChromeOS.
branches.path: Install the listed curl, git, unzip, xz-utils, zip, and libglu1-mesa packages; on ChromeOS first enable and update Linux support; then install VS Code and rejoin the shared route.
branches.effect_on_first_success: Adds package-manager work and, for ChromeOS, Linux-environment setup.

branches.condition: The developer prefers another environment or manual installation.
branches.path: Use the custom setup guide and its editor, SDK, and target-platform instructions instead of this recommended VS Code flow.
branches.effect_on_first_success: Replaces much of the primary installation path and may select a different runtime target.

branches.condition: The SDK download appears hung.
branches.path: Click Cancel and restart the Flutter installation sequence.
branches.effect_on_first_success: Adds a failed download attempt and repeat before PATH configuration.

branches.condition: VS Code warns that Android Studio is not installed.
branches.path: Ignore the warning for web, iOS, or macOS targets and optionally run flutter doctor to verify the rest of the installation.
branches.effect_on_first_success: Avoids an unnecessary Android Studio installation for the selected Chrome route.

frictionGates.description: Git, VS Code, the Flutter extension, and the Flutter SDK must all be installed before project creation.
Cloning the Flutter SDK takes a few minutes and may require cancellation and restart if it hangs.
The SDK must be added to PATH, and terminals plus VS Code must be restarted to receive the new environment.
The operating system or VS Code can request access to the selected documents folder before scaffolding.
Project initialization retrieves dependencies and can take time before main.dart is available.
Chrome must be installed and selectable for the quick start's web target.

stages.action: Install flyctl for the local OS.

stages.action: Run fly auth signup (or fly auth login) and complete browser signup, including payment details when prompted.

stages.action: Run fly launch --image flyio/hellofly:latest and accept defaults to deploy.

stages.action: Run fly status and fly apps open to confirm the HTTPS demo.
