developerGoal: Create a free InfluxDB Cloud Serverless account and use the official browser tutorial to write its published home-sensor line protocol sample successfully to the automatically created default bucket.

entryPoint.developer_intent: Create a free InfluxDB Cloud Serverless account and use the official browser tutorial to write its published home-sensor line protocol sample successfully to the automatically created default bucket.
assumed_prior_state: No InfluxDB Cloud account, organization, bucket, token, billing instrument, local CLI, or client library is assumed.
The developer has a name, email address, password, company name, and access to the email inbox used for signup.
The developer is authorized to accept the Cloud Services Subscription Agreement and Global Data Processing Agreement.
The developer is evaluating a small workload that fits the Cloud Serverless Free Plan.
No account, agreement, resource, token, data write, or query was executed during research.

selectedSurface.name: InfluxDB Cloud Serverless Free Plan through the InfluxDB UI
selectedSurface.selection_basis: InfluxData's current product and pricing pages position Cloud Serverless as the fully managed, no-card route for smaller workloads and early evaluation. The official Cloud Serverless tutorial covers setup, writing, and querying, and its browser UI write branch reaches an explicit successful-write confirmation without local software.

alternatives_considered.name: InfluxDB 3 Core
alternatives_considered.reason_not_primary: This is a self-managed local installation rather than the fully managed Cloud Serverless surface selected by the Cloud signup and tutorial.

alternatives_considered.name: InfluxDB 3 Enterprise
alternatives_considered.reason_not_primary: This is a self-managed production product with a 30-day trial and infrastructure requirements.

alternatives_considered.name: InfluxDB Cloud Dedicated
alternatives_considered.reason_not_primary: This is a single-tenant, custom-priced managed offering rather than the instant Free Plan route.

alternatives_considered.name: AWS Marketplace
alternatives_considered.reason_not_primary: This creates a Usage-Based subscription billed through AWS and adds marketplace subscription and account setup.

alternatives_considered.name: Google or Microsoft social sign-on
alternatives_considered.reason_not_primary: These are documented account alternatives, but the email route is fully enumerated through email verification.

alternatives_considered.name: HTTP API, influx CLI, Telegraf, or client library write
alternatives_considered.reason_not_primary: These are peer write branches that add credentials, local tools, code, or process configuration, while the UI branch provides an explicit write confirmation in the browser.

alternatives_considered.name: Data Explorer SQL query
alternatives_considered.reason_not_primary: This is tutorial part 3 and requires data written in part 2, so it occurs after the first successful write terminal.

firstMeaningfulSuccess.official_milestone: Get started writing data
firstMeaningfulSuccess.normalized_outcome: The developer writes InfluxData's published home-sensor line protocol sample to the automatically created default bucket through the InfluxDB Cloud Serverless UI.
firstMeaningfulSuccess.observable_completion_signal: After Write Data is selected, the UI confirms that the data has been written successfully.

boundary_evidence.official_label_or_terminal_state: Get started writing data

firstMeaningfulSuccess.why_this_is_the_boundary: Writing data is part 2 of the official three-part get-started tutorial and supplies an explicit browser success confirmation. The separate SQL query journey is part 3 and depends on the data already written, so it is after the first successful platform operation.

prerequisites.requirement: Name, email address, and a user-selected password for the documented email signup route.
Access to the signup inbox to select Verify Your Email.
Choose AWS and either US East (Virginia) or EU Frankfurt for the documented InfluxData subscription route.
A company name entered during Cloud Serverless setup.
Review and affirm the InfluxDB Cloud Services Subscription Agreement and InfluxData Global Data Processing Agreement.
Choose Keep to remain on the Free Plan instead of entering payment details for Usage-Based or contacting sales for Annual.
Use the default organization and bucket that InfluxDB Cloud Serverless creates automatically from the signup email address.

branches.condition: Google or Microsoft social sign-on
branches.path: Select Google or Microsoft instead of entering email credentials; social sign-on does not support email aliases.
branches.effect_on_first_success: Changes account authentication and skips the documented email-verification branch, then converges on Cloud setup.

branches.condition: European region
branches.path: Choose AWS EU Frankfurt instead of AWS US East (Virginia).
branches.effect_on_first_success: Changes the Cloud instance region and region URL while preserving Free Plan and UI write steps.

branches.condition: Usage-Based Plan
branches.path: Select Upgrade Now, set a limit of at least $1, optionally enable overage email, enter payment and billing details, select Upgrade, observe Ready To Rock, and select Start building your team.
branches.effect_on_first_success: Adds payment and billing steps before the same default organization and bucket state.

branches.condition: Annual Plan
branches.path: Select Contact Sales, enter the requested information, and submit Send.
branches.effect_on_first_success: Introduces a human sales dependency and does not provide the immediate Free Plan route.

branches.condition: AWS Marketplace Usage-Based subscription
branches.path: Subscribe in AWS Marketplace, select Set Up Your Account, enter credentials, and select Start Now.
branches.effect_on_first_success: Bills all usage through AWS and does not support linking an existing InfluxDB Cloud account.

branches.condition: Create the tutorial's get-started bucket
branches.path: Open Load Data, then Buckets, create get-started, and choose a supported retention period before writing.
branches.effect_on_first_success: Follows the later query tutorial's assumed bucket name but introduces a retention choice that is unnecessary for the first UI write.

branches.condition: Non-UI write tool
branches.path: Generate an API token, configure credentials, and use influx CLI, Telegraf, v1 or v2 HTTP API, Python, Go, Node.js, C#, or Java with the documented sample.
branches.effect_on_first_success: Adds secret handling, tool installation, code, or process configuration and supplies a tool-specific success result such as HTTP 204.

frictionGates.description: Email signup requires opening a verification message before Cloud setup can continue.
The documented direct subscription requires choosing AWS and either US East (Virginia) or EU Frankfurt.
The direct route requires affirmative acceptance of two agreements.
Keep, Upgrade Now, and Contact Sales lead to materially different free, paid, and human-assisted paths.
The sample timestamps use seconds, so the UI precision must be changed to Seconds before writing.
Line protocol is whitespace-sensitive and requires its measurement, tag, field, and timestamp structure to remain intact.
If a Free Plan write or query rate is exceeded, the API returns HTTP 429 and Retry-After; wait for the fixed five-minute window or upgrade to raise organization limits.

uncertainties.question: What password complexity rules are enforced in the email signup form?
uncertainties.impact: The developer may need to adjust the chosen password, but the required input and overall account sequence do not change.
uncertainties.reason_unresolved: The official signup guide says to enter a password but does not publish its validation rules.

uncertainties.question: How long does the verification email take to arrive, and how is a missing or expired email recovered?
uncertainties.impact: Email delivery can add unknown wait time before provider and region setup.
uncertainties.reason_unresolved: The guide requires verification but publishes no timing, expiration, or resend procedure.

uncertainties.question: What exact name is assigned to the automatically created default bucket for email addresses that contain characters the bucket name does not accept?
uncertainties.impact: The label selected in Load Data may be normalized, but no resource choice or extra creation step is introduced.
uncertainties.reason_unresolved: The signup guide says the default bucket is created from the email address but does not document normalization rules.

excludedAfterSuccess: The part 3 Data Explorer SQL query, query results, and visualization occur after the UI confirms the first data write.
Creating the tutorial's optional get-started bucket, generating a token, configuring a CLI, or choosing another write tool is outside the selected default-bucket UI route.
Dashboards, alerts, tasks, Telegraf pipelines, application client libraries, API integrations, additional organization changes, and production tuning are after first success.
Usage-Based billing, Annual sales contact, Cloud Dedicated, InfluxDB 3 Core, InfluxDB 3 Enterprise, and AWS Marketplace procurement are outside the selected Free Plan path.

developerGoal: Create the minimum QuickBooks Online developer setup, obtain a temporary sandbox access token in the OAuth 2.0 Playground, call the Accounting API CompanyInfo entity, and inspect the sample-shaped response.

entryPoint.developer_intent: Create the minimum QuickBooks Online developer setup, obtain a temporary sandbox access token in the OAuth 2.0 Playground, call the Accounting API CompanyInfo entity, and inspect the sample-shaped response.
assumed_prior_state: Discovery is complete and the developer has selected the QuickBooks Online Accounting API rather than QuickBooks Payments or QuickBooks Desktop.
The developer does not yet have an Intuit Developer account, workspace, app, sandbox company, development credentials, OAuth token, or realm ID.
The developer can use a browser and a CLI or API client capable of sending cURL-equivalent HTTPS requests.
The first success uses Intuit's sandbox and sample data, not a live QuickBooks Online company.

selectedSurface.name: QuickBooks Online Accounting API CompanyInfo call through the OAuth 2.0 Playground
selectedSurface.selection_basis: Workshop selection policy (2026-07-19): when official docs present peer routes, commit to the fastest and most commonly used documented route. Prefer the vendor's first-listed or recommended quickstart, dashboard/console over CLI when both are peers, and the route with an explicit vendor time claim or fewest documented steps when tied. Committed to already-reconstructed surface `QuickBooks Online Accounting API CompanyInfo call through the OAuth 2.0 Playground`. Prior basis: The QuickBooks Online Accounting API getting-started guide contains a highlighted quick-sample path that says full application authentication is not needed for testing, directs the developer through the OAuth 2.0 Playground, and supplies a complete CompanyInfo cURL request and sample server response. A separate Build a demo app guide also calls the CompanyInfo route the generic jump-right-in API call. QuickBooks Desktop and QuickBooks Payments have separate first miles, so the organization-level selection still needs human judgment.

alternatives_considered.name: QuickBooks Online API Explorer
alternatives_considered.reason_not_primary: It is a co-equal no-code testing option: choose a sandbox company, entity, and operation, then select Try it, but it does not demonstrate the access token and realm ID that the cURL path exposes.

alternatives_considered.name: QuickBooks Online demo application
alternatives_considered.reason_not_primary: It is the more robust app-development route, with .NET, Java, and PHP variants, repository setup, redirect URI configuration, browser authorization, and an automatic CompanyInfo call.

alternatives_considered.name: Full OAuth 2.0 implementation
alternatives_considered.reason_not_primary: It is required for a real application, but the official getting-started guide explicitly provides the Playground for a quick first sample call before the complete authorization implementation.

alternatives_considered.name: QuickBooks Payments API
alternatives_considered.reason_not_primary: It uses the payments scope and a charge or payment-processing outcome rather than the Accounting API CompanyInfo result.

alternatives_considered.name: QuickBooks Desktop SDK
alternatives_considered.reason_not_primary: It is a separate desktop integration surface with a different SDK, local QuickBooks environment, and connection model.

firstMeaningfulSuccess.official_milestone: Want to make a quick sample API call?
firstMeaningfulSuccess.normalized_outcome: The developer creates the required QuickBooks Online developer resources, uses the OAuth 2.0 Playground to obtain a temporary access token and sandbox realm ID, sends the documented GET CompanyInfo request, and receives a CompanyInfo object for the sandbox company.
firstMeaningfulSuccess.observable_completion_signal: The sandbox endpoint returns a JSON response with a top-level CompanyInfo object, sandbox company fields such as CompanyName and Id, and a response time value that matches the documented sample response shape.

boundary_evidence.official_label_or_terminal_state: The Create and start developing your app guide labels the inset Want to make a quick sample API call?, provides the GET CompanyInfo command, and says the response should match the sample server response.

firstMeaningfulSuccess.why_this_is_the_boundary: It is Intuit's explicitly documented first request, proving developer access, app and scope setup, sandbox availability, temporary OAuth authorization, bearer-token use, realm selection, and a successful Accounting API response. SDK integration, durable OAuth handling, writes, webhooks, publication, and production access come later.

prerequisites.requirement: An Intuit Developer account and developer profile are required; account setup asks for basic company information and a workspace name.
A QuickBooks Online sandbox company is required. Intuit automatically gives a new developer profile one sandbox company with sample data.
A developer app with the accounting scope is required to authorize access to the CompanyInfo entity.
The developer app has separate Development Client ID and Client Secret credentials for sandbox use; the Playground uses the app to produce a temporary access token and realm ID.
A web browser plus cURL or another API client is required for the documented quick sample route.
The selected first success uses the automatically provided sandbox company and sample data; the official route does not document a payment-card or QuickBooks subscription purchase gate.

stages.action: Open Create and start developing your app and select the QuickBooks Online Accounting API path.
details: The guide separately links API Explorer, use-case workflows, QuickBooks Desktop, and QuickBooks Payments.
If unfamiliar, review the linked API-platform, REST, bookkeeping, and QuickBooks Online basics before continuing; the guide says familiar developers can skip this learning step.

stages.failure_or_wait: Choosing QuickBooks Desktop, QuickBooks Payments, or a specific workflow changes the first-mile outcome and documentation sequence.

Sandbox content is for non-commercial development and testing.

stages.action: Confirm the app's Development Client ID and Client Secret in Keys and credentials.
details: Open My Hub, App dashboard, select the app, choose Keys and credentials, choose Development, turn on Show credentials, and copy Client ID and Client Secret.
Development credentials work with sandbox companies; Production has a separate credential set and questionnaire approval.

stages.failure_or_wait: Selecting Production introduces a questionnaire and approval gate and yields credentials that are not for the sandbox route.

stages.action: Open the OAuth 2.0 Playground, select the sandbox app or company, select the accounting scope, obtain an authorization code, and exchange it for tokens.
details: Choose the relevant sandbox selection in the Select app dropdown.
Select com.intuit.quickBooks.accounting or the displayed Accounting scope.
Select Get authorization code and, if prompted, select Connect.
Review the generated authorization code and Realm ID, then select Get tokens and review the sample token request and response.
stages.input: Developer app, sandbox, and accounting scope
stages.output: Temporary access token and sandbox realm ID
stages.success_signal: The Playground response contains accessToken and the UI exposes the Realm ID.
stages.failure_or_wait: A missing app, unavailable sandbox, omitted scope, denied Connect prompt, or failed code exchange prevents the access token from appearing and requires correcting that selection before retrying.

stages.action: Copy the documented GET CompanyInfo cURL command and replace both sandbox-company placeholders with the Realm ID and the bearer placeholder with the access token.
details: The sandbox URL is https://sandbox-quickbooks.api.intuit.com/v3/company/{realmId}/companyinfo/{realmId}.
Set accept and content-type to application/json.
Set authorization to Bearer followed by the temporary access token.
Keep the access token out of source control and command history where possible.
stages.input: Access token, realm ID, and official CompanyInfo command
stages.success_signal: No REPLACE_WITH placeholders remain and the realm ID is identical in both URL positions.
stages.failure_or_wait: A malformed Authorization header, wrong realm ID, expired token, missing scope, or production-versus-sandbox mismatch prevents a successful response.

stages.action: Send the GET CompanyInfo request to the sandbox QuickBooks Online Accounting API.
details: The request reads the CompanyInfo entity for the sandbox company selected in the Playground.
stages.output: HTTP response from the sandbox Accounting API
stages.success_signal: The server returns JSON rather than an OAuth, scope, realm, or endpoint error.
stages.failure_or_wait: If the access token is older than its documented 60-minute lifetime, return to the Playground to refresh it or generate a new authorization and token before retrying.

stages.action: Compare the response shape with Intuit's sample server response.
details: Verify the top-level CompanyInfo object.
Verify sandbox company fields such as CompanyName, CompanyAddr, Country, Id, SyncToken, and MetaData are present as applicable.
Verify the response includes the server time value.
stages.output: Verified first QuickBooks Online Accounting API response
stages.success_signal: The response matches the sample's CompanyInfo response structure for the selected sandbox.
stages.failure_or_wait: An error payload or response for another entity, company, or environment does not satisfy the documented first-call boundary.

branches.condition: The developer chooses the API Explorer route.
branches.path: Sign in to the developer account, open API Explorer, choose the sandbox company, find an Accounting API entity and operation, select Try it, and inspect the generated request and response in the browser.
branches.effect_on_first_success: Avoids cURL and manual bearer-header construction but changes the terminal experience to an in-browser tool and permits an entity other than CompanyInfo.

branches.condition: The developer wants a runnable demo application rather than a raw first API request.
branches.path: Use Build a demo app and choose .NET, Java, or PHP; meet the language-specific prerequisites; clone the matching official Hello World repository; create or use the demo app with accounting scope; get Development credentials; add the repository's localhost redirect URI in app Settings; place credentials, redirect URI, and sandbox environment in the sample configuration; run the app; select Connect to QuickBooks; sign in, select a sandbox, authorize; follow the callback; and verify that the sample automatically calls CompanyInfo.
branches.effect_on_first_success: Adds local tooling, repository, SDK, configuration file, redirect URI, browser consent, callback, and language-specific failure modes before the same CompanyInfo outcome.

branches.condition: QuickBooks Payments is the intended product.
branches.path: Use the QuickBooks Payments workflow, create an app with the payments scope, use the Payments sandbox and test card data, obtain a scoped OAuth token, submit the documented charge or payment request, and verify the Payments API response.
branches.effect_on_first_success: Changes scope, endpoint, test data, billing semantics, and terminal result from company metadata to a payment-processing object.

branches.condition: QuickBooks Desktop is the intended product.
branches.path: Leave the QuickBooks Online docs and use the QuickBooks Desktop SDK getting-started route, install the required desktop SDK and local QuickBooks environment, create and authorize the desktop connection, run its sample request, and verify the local company-data response.
branches.effect_on_first_success: Replaces the cloud sandbox and OAuth 2.0 model with a local desktop integration and separate SDK.

branches.condition: The app also needs QuickBooks payment data.
branches.path: Add the payments scope in addition to accounting, obtain a token authorized for both intended scopes, and use only the endpoints corresponding to granted permissions.
branches.effect_on_first_success: Adds user-consent scope and product surface but is unnecessary for CompanyInfo.

branches.condition: The automatic sandbox is unavailable or a different SKU or region is needed.
branches.path: Open My Hub, Sandboxes, select Add, choose QuickBooks Online Plus or Advanced; for Plus choose the country and note that it cannot later be changed; select Create; then use the new sandbox in the Playground. Up to 10 sandboxes can be created and they remain active for two years.
branches.effect_on_first_success: Adds SKU and irreversible region choices plus explicit sandbox creation before authorization.

branches.condition: The Playground's Example API calls workflow is preferred over the copied command in the getting-started page.
branches.path: After obtaining tokens, go to Example API calls, select Get Company Info from Call APIs for, select Get Curl, copy the generated command into a CLI or API client, execute it, and compare its request and response with the Playground sample.
branches.effect_on_first_success: Lets the Playground populate the accessToken authorization value and command rather than manually replacing placeholders.

branches.condition: The temporary access token has expired.
branches.path: Return to the first Playground server response, obtain refreshToken, open Refresh Access Token, confirm the refresh token is populated, select Refresh Access Token, copy the new accessToken, replace the bearer value in the CompanyInfo command, and retry. Alternatively repeat authorization and Get tokens.
branches.effect_on_first_success: Adds a token-refresh or reauthorization cycle after the documented 60-minute access-token lifetime.

branches.condition: The request fails because accounting scope was not authorized.
branches.path: Return to the Playground, select the accounting scope, obtain a new authorization code, approve Connect, exchange for a new token, update the bearer token, and resend the request.
branches.effect_on_first_success: Repeats OAuth authorization because scopes determine which API request types the token can make.

branches.condition: The realm ID or environment is wrong.
branches.path: Copy the Realm ID associated with the selected sandbox from the Playground, use the sandbox base URL, replace both URL placeholders with the same ID, and resend the request. Do not use Production credentials or a live-company URL for the selected test route.
branches.effect_on_first_success: Corrects company and environment routing before the response can match the sandbox sample.

frictionGates.description: QuickBooks Online Accounting, QuickBooks Payments, QuickBooks Desktop, API Explorer, generic cURL, and language-specific demo routes have different first-mile paths.
The developer account requires basic company information and a workspace name; the current form sequence is not documented field-by-field.
A developer app and accounting scope must exist before the Playground can issue a CompanyInfo-capable token.
The first request depends on a selectable sandbox company and its immutable region if a new sandbox must be created.
Development and Production credentials are separate; Production credentials introduce a questionnaire and approval and do not belong in the sandbox path.
The Playground requires app and sandbox selection, accounting scope, a Connect authorization prompt when shown, code generation, and token exchange.
The bearer token, sandbox realm ID in two URL positions, sandbox hostname, and JSON headers must all be correct in the raw cURL request.
Playground access tokens are temporary and valid for 60 minutes; expiry requires refresh or reauthorization before retrying.

uncertainties.question: Which QuickBooks product and first-success surface should define the workshop's canonical Intuit QuickBooks journey?
uncertainties.impact: Accounting API, Payments API, Desktop SDK, API Explorer, cURL, and demo-app choices change account resources, credentials, tools, scopes, code, and success signals.
uncertainties.reason_unresolved: The official getting-started page links these as separate valid routes and does not designate one organization-wide canonical path.

uncertainties.question: What exact Intuit Developer signup and current app-creation screens appear before the workspace and app are ready?
uncertainties.impact: Additional identity verification, agreements, company fields, use-case questions, or validation can add pages and waiting before the documented dashboard steps.
uncertainties.reason_unresolved: The official guide names basic company information, workspace name, and on-screen app steps but does not enumerate the live forms.

uncertainties.question: Does the current OAuth Playground label the first selector as an app, sandbox company, or both in the user's account?
uncertainties.impact: The exact user action and displayed object affect the page count and comprehension of app-to-company authorization.
uncertainties.reason_unresolved: One official guide says select a sandbox company from Select app, while the Playground guide says select one of your sandbox companies from the same dropdown and also requires the app to exist.

uncertainties.question: Can the automatically created default sandbox be absent, delayed, or region-dependent for some new developer profiles?
uncertainties.impact: If so, the path gains manual SKU and country selection before authorization.
uncertainties.reason_unresolved: The docs state that a sandbox is created automatically and document manual additional creation, but do not describe exceptions or provisioning time.

developerGoal: Choose a JetBrains developer product and reach the first terminal state that its official getting-started material defines; for the retained candidate, run a generated IntelliJ Platform plugin locally.

entryPoint.developer_intent: Choose a JetBrains developer product and reach the first terminal state that its official getting-started material defines; for the retained candidate, run a generated IntelliJ Platform plugin locally.
assumed_prior_state: No JetBrains account, subscription, Toolbox App, IntelliJ IDEA installation, Plugin DevKit, JDK selection, Gradle project, source code, run configuration, or Marketplace listing is assumed.
A supported Windows, macOS, or Linux computer, browser, network connection, and permission to install desktop software are available.
No software download, installation, account login, plugin creation, Gradle dependency download, or IDE execution was performed during research.

selectedSurface.name: IntelliJ Platform Plugin SDK first local plugin run
selectedSurface.selection_basis: Workshop selection policy (2026-07-19): when official docs present peer routes, commit to the fastest and most commonly used documented route. Prefer the vendor's first-listed or recommended quickstart, dashboard/console over CLI when both are peers, and the route with an explicit vendor time claim or fewest documented steps when tied. Selected route: IntelliJ Platform Plugin SDK first local plugin run. The Plugin SDK fully documents a minimal Gradle project and an explicit local execution terminal. Prior surface before policy commit: JetBrains product catalog, with the IntelliJ Platform Plugin SDK first local plugin run as one complete candidate.

alternatives_considered.name: TeamCity
alternatives_considered.reason_not_primary: It is a peer CI product with cloud and self-hosted onboarding rather than an IntelliJ Platform plugin-development journey.

alternatives_considered.name: YouTrack
alternatives_considered.reason_not_primary: It is a peer project-management product with cloud and server onboarding rather than a local IDE plugin run.

alternatives_considered.name: IntelliJ Platform Plugin Template
alternatives_considered.reason_not_primary: Inside the selected plugin surface, the template is a documented peer with CI configuration. The New Project Wizard is the workflow described directly by the Plugin SDK.

alternatives_considered.name: Create a visible custom action
alternatives_considered.reason_not_primary: Adding an action provides visible behavior but follows project creation. The generated run configuration already defines the earliest documented local plugin execution boundary.

alternatives_considered.name: Publish to JetBrains Marketplace
alternatives_considered.reason_not_primary: Publication requires packaging, signing, Marketplace access, review, and distribution after the plugin has run locally.

firstMeaningfulSuccess.official_milestone: For the selected IntelliJ Platform candidate, execute the generated plugin with Run IDE with Plugin or the `runIde` Gradle task.
firstMeaningfulSuccess.normalized_outcome: The wizard-generated Gradle plugin project starts the target IntelliJ Platform IDE in a standalone development instance with the plugin loaded.
firstMeaningfulSuccess.observable_completion_signal: A separate IDE instance launches from the generated Run IDE with Plugin configuration or `runIde` task without a build or launch error.

boundary_evidence.official_label_or_terminal_state: The Plugin SDK has an Executing the Plugin section and states that the generated project contains a Run IDE with Plugin configuration that can be executed.

firstMeaningfulSuccess.why_this_is_the_boundary: Local execution is the earliest documented proof that the generated plugin project builds and can be loaded by its target IDE. Adding visible actions, tests, compatibility verification, signing, packaging, and Marketplace publication are later work.

prerequisites.requirement: Use a computer and operating system supported by the current IntelliJ IDEA installation guide and select the matching Toolbox App installer.
Install the JetBrains Toolbox App and then install IntelliJ IDEA; Toolbox is the recommended installation method.
Install and enable Plugin DevKit from JetBrains Marketplace and ensure Gradle support is enabled, because Plugin DevKit is no longer bundled.
Select a JDK compatible with the target IntelliJ Platform version, such as Java 21 for platform 2024.2 or later.
Choose a project name and location, plugin Group, Artifact, target platform, and compatible JDK in the IDE Plugin wizard.

stages.action: Open the JetBrains product catalog and choose the product surface whose first mile is being measured.
details: The catalog presents IDEs, team tools, language and framework tools, and other developer products as peers.
stages.success_signal: A product and its own documentation can be selected.
stages.failure_or_wait: JetBrains does not name one company-wide developer first mile.

stages.action: For the retained candidate, choose IntelliJ Platform plugin development and open Developing a Plugin.
details: The page recommends the Gradle-based model and exposes the New Project Wizard and Plugin Template as project-creation routes.
stages.output: The local plugin-development route is selected.
stages.success_signal: Creating a Plugin Gradle Project is listed as the first workflow step.
stages.failure_or_wait: Choosing a different JetBrains product changes the complete first-success journey.

stages.action: Open Developing a Plugin and select the recommended Gradle-based New Project Wizard workflow.
details: The page recommends the IntelliJ Platform Gradle Plugin for current platform versions.
It identifies the New Project Wizard and IntelliJ Platform Plugin Template as the two main project-creation routes.
stages.output: The local wizard route and its required IntelliJ IDEA, Gradle, and Plugin DevKit tooling are identified.
stages.success_signal: Creating a Plugin Gradle Project is listed as the first workflow step.
stages.failure_or_wait: A theme plugin or Scala plugin uses an alternative documented model.

stages.action: Install the JetBrains Toolbox App using the operating-system-specific instructions, launch it, and select IntelliJ IDEA to install.
details: Windows uses the installer wizard, macOS mounts the matching Intel or Apple Silicon image and moves Toolbox to Applications, and Linux extracts and runs the Toolbox distribution.
A JetBrains Account login is documented for automatic license activation but is not stated as required to install the product.
stages.output: IntelliJ IDEA is installed and available to launch.
stages.success_signal: IntelliJ IDEA appears as an installed Toolbox product and launches.
stages.failure_or_wait: The selected guide does not document an installation recovery path.

stages.action: In IntelliJ IDEA, install and enable Plugin DevKit from JetBrains Marketplace and verify Gradle support is enabled.
details: Plugin DevKit must be installed separately because it has not been bundled since IntelliJ IDEA 2023.3.
stages.output: The IDE Plugin project wizard is enabled.
stages.success_signal: IDE Plugin is available as a New Project type.
stages.failure_or_wait: The wizard requires both Gradle and Plugin DevKit to be installed and enabled.

stages.action: Open File > New > Project, select IDE Plugin, and configure the plugin project.
details: Enter Name and Location, choose Plugin type, provide Group and Artifact, and select the target-compatible JDK.
For IntelliJ IDEA 2023.1 and later, the generated project supports Kotlin and Java sources; older IDEs also ask for language.
Click Create.
stages.input: Project identity, location, Group, Artifact, and compatible JDK
stages.output: A Gradle plugin project is generated with wrapper files, `plugin.xml`, build configuration, and Run IDE with Plugin configuration.
stages.success_signal: The project opens and contains the documented generated structure and run configuration.
stages.failure_or_wait: The selected guide does not document a project-creation recovery path.

stages.action: Execute Run IDE with Plugin from Run > Run or the Gradle Run Configurations node.
details: The same result can be started by locating and double-clicking `runIde` in the Gradle Tasks node.
stages.output: Gradle builds the project and launches a standalone target IDE instance with the plugin.
stages.success_signal: The standalone IDE opens without a build or launch error.
stages.failure_or_wait: If `runIde` is absent, click Sync All Gradle Projects, locate the task again, and double-click it.

branches.condition: The host is Windows
branches.path: Download the Toolbox `.exe`, run it, follow the installer wizard, launch Toolbox from the notification area, and select IntelliJ IDEA.
branches.effect_on_first_success: Changes only desktop installation steps.

branches.condition: The host is macOS
branches.path: Download the correct Intel or Apple Silicon `.dmg`, mount it, drag Toolbox to Applications, launch it, and select IntelliJ IDEA.
branches.effect_on_first_success: Adds an architecture-specific installer choice.

branches.condition: The host is Linux
branches.path: Install required system packages, extract the Toolbox archive, run its executable, and select IntelliJ IDEA.
branches.effect_on_first_success: Adds package and archive steps before the common IDE workflow.

branches.condition: Inside the candidate, the target platform is 2024.2 or later
branches.path: Use the IntelliJ Platform Gradle Plugin 2.x and Java 21-compatible project settings.
branches.effect_on_first_success: Determines Gradle plugin and JDK compatibility.

branches.condition: Inside the candidate, use the Gradle task directly
branches.path: Open the Gradle tool window, find `runIde` under Tasks, sync if it is absent, and double-click it.
branches.effect_on_first_success: Replaces the generated run-configuration menu action with the equivalent Gradle task.

frictionGates.description: The installer and exact Toolbox procedure differ by operating system and, on macOS and Linux, processor architecture.
The path requires Toolbox and IntelliJ IDEA desktop installation with permission to run downloaded software.
Inside the selected candidate, Plugin DevKit is no longer bundled and must be installed from JetBrains Marketplace.
Inside the selected candidate, target platform, Gradle plugin generation, and JDK versions must be compatible.
Inside the selected candidate, the generated wrapper can download its specified Gradle version automatically.

uncertainties.question: Which JetBrains product surface should represent the umbrella company for a user with no stated goal?
uncertainties.impact: An IDE user, plugin developer, YouTrack integrator, and TeamCity user have different first miles.
uncertainties.reason_unresolved: JetBrains publishes several independent developer products without naming one default; the record retains IntelliJ Platform extensibility only as a complete candidate.

uncertainties.question: Does a generated plugin without custom code expose a visible in-product behavior?
uncertainties.impact: The terminal proves build and loading but not a user-facing feature.
uncertainties.reason_unresolved: The project guide treats plugin execution as the terminal and sends developers to the separate Creating Actions tutorial for basic visible functionality.

developerGoal: Start a JFrog Platform Cloud trial, configure a Docker repository, and push hello-world.

entryPoint.developer_intent: Start a JFrog Platform Cloud trial, configure a Docker repository, and push hello-world.
assumed_prior_state: No JFrog Cloud environment exists.
Docker CLI is available locally.

selectedSurface.name: JFrog Platform Cloud trial Docker hello-world push
selectedSurface.selection_basis: JFrog Cloud trial onboarding creates a hosted Artifactory environment. Official Docker docs and Cloud testing guidance walk through repository setup, docker login to *.jfrog.io, and docker push of hello-world as the first artifact success.

alternatives_considered.name: Self-hosted Artifactory
alternatives_considered.reason_not_primary: Self-hosted path; Cloud trial is the hosted onboarding route.

alternatives_considered.name: Other package types (npm/Maven)
alternatives_considered.reason_not_primary: Peer repository types; Docker hello-world is the documented Cloud test path.

firstMeaningfulSuccess.official_milestone: Push hello-world to Artifactory Cloud

firstMeaningfulSuccess.observable_completion_signal: Artifactory Artifacts view shows the pushed hello-world image under the Docker repository.

developerGoal: Create a Klarna playground account, generate test API credentials, integrate the recommended web in-line widget, and complete one test payment until Klarna returns an order ID.

entryPoint.developer_intent: Create a Klarna playground account, generate test API credentials, integrate the recommended web in-line widget, and complete one test payment until Klarna returns an order ID.
assumed_prior_state: No Klarna merchant account, Merchant portal access, merchant ID, API credential, payment session, checkout integration, authorization token, or order is assumed.
The developer has a web checkout and server capable of API requests and serving JavaScript.
The test uses Klarna's sample customer and payment data, not real personal data.
No signup, terms acceptance, credential generation, API request, or test purchase was performed during research.

selectedSurface.name: Klarna Payments playground web integration with the in-line widget
selectedSurface.selection_basis: Klarna's web prerequisites explicitly call the in-line widget the recommended solution and state that its guide covers initiation, checkout, and order creation. The official testing guide publicly documents playground account creation and key generation, allowing the full route to be reconstructed without assuming an existing merchant account.

alternatives_considered.name: Hosted Payment Page
alternatives_considered.reason_not_primary: Klarna presents browser redirect as an alternative and explicitly recommends the in-line widget for the best user experience.

alternatives_considered.name: Platform integrations
alternatives_considered.reason_not_primary: These are platform-specific plugin routes rather than Klarna's general direct web-payment guide.

alternatives_considered.name: Mobile SDK
alternatives_considered.reason_not_primary: This is the strongly recommended route for mobile applications, not a website integration.

firstMeaningfulSuccess.official_milestone: Create a one-time payment order in the Klarna playground
firstMeaningfulSuccess.normalized_outcome: A test customer completes the Klarna widget flow and the server exchanges the authorization token for a Klarna order.
firstMeaningfulSuccess.observable_completion_signal: The create-order request succeeds, closes the payment session, charges the test customer, and returns `order_id`, `fraud_status`, and `authorized_payment_method`.

boundary_evidence.official_label_or_terminal_state: A create-order success response containing `order_id`, `fraud_status`, and `authorized_payment_method`.

firstMeaningfulSuccess.why_this_is_the_boundary: Klarna structures the direct web integration as initiate a payment, check out, and create an order. Order management is linked as the next section, so the first returned test order is the documented integration terminal.

prerequisites.requirement: A test Merchant portal account in the correct Klarna credential region.
Acceptance of Klarna's Terms & Conditions while activating the playground account.
A playground Klarna API Key ID, used as API username, and Secret, used as API password.
A server-side API client and a checkout page that can load Klarna's JavaScript SDK.

stages.action: Open Klarna's Before you test guide.
stages.output: Playground prerequisites and account steps are shown.
stages.success_signal: The guide lists Merchant portal access, test API keys, and sample data.
stages.failure_or_wait: No navigation recovery is specified.

stages.action: Click Log in in the Klarna Docs navigation.
stages.output: The credential region and environment selector opens.
stages.success_signal: Region and environment controls are available.
stages.failure_or_wait: Use the region matching the intended API endpoints.

stages.action: Select the credential region, select Playground, and click Sign up.
stages.output: The playground account signup form opens.
stages.success_signal: An email field and SIGN-UP action are available.
stages.failure_or_wait: A production environment would not create the test account used by this path.

stages.action: Enter an email address and click SIGN-UP.
stages.success_signal: The confirmation email arrives.
stages.failure_or_wait: Email delivery time is not stated.

stages.action: Open the confirmation email and click Activate account.
stages.output: Account activation continues in the browser.
stages.success_signal: Terms, password, and name setup are presented.
stages.failure_or_wait: No expiry or resend path is documented on this page.

stages.action: Accept the Terms & Conditions, choose a new password, enter a name, and log in to the Merchant portal.
stages.success_signal: The developer is authenticated in the test Merchant portal.
stages.failure_or_wait: Password requirements are not stated in the guide.

stages.action: Open Payment settings, select Klarna API Keys, and click Generate new Klarna API key.
stages.output: A downloadable API credential file is generated.
stages.success_signal: The key dialog offers the .txt file.
stages.failure_or_wait: The guide does not document generation latency.

stages.action: Download the .txt file and close the dialog.
details: The secret is displayed only once.
stages.output: The developer has Key ID and Secret for playground API URLs.
stages.success_signal: Both credential values are stored securely.
stages.failure_or_wait: If the secret is lost, a new key is required.

stages.action: Create a one-time payment session with an authenticated POST to the regional playground `/payments/v1/sessions` endpoint.
details: Use HTTP Basic authentication with Key ID as username and Secret as password.
Set intent to buy and provide acquiring channel, purchase country, currency, locale, order amount, tax amount, and order lines.
stages.output: Klarna returns session_id, client_token, and payment_method_categories.
stages.success_signal: The response contains a client_token for the JavaScript SDK.
stages.failure_or_wait: The session remains open for 48 hours or until an order is placed.

stages.action: Add Klarna's JavaScript SDK script and an asynchronous callback to the checkout page.
details: Load https://x.klarnacdn.net/kp/lib/v1/api.js.
stages.output: Klarna.Payments becomes available in the callback.
stages.success_signal: The SDK can be initialized.
stages.failure_or_wait: The SDK script is asynchronous.

stages.action: Call `Klarna.Payments.init` with the session's client_token.
stages.output: The SDK is bound to the payment session.
stages.success_signal: Initialization completes before load is called.
stages.failure_or_wait: The token depends on a session that expires after 48 hours.

stages.action: Add the `klarna-payments-container` element and call `Klarna.Payments.load` with its selector.
details: Use identifier, localized name, and asset_urls from the session response when presenting the option.
stages.output: The Klarna widget loads in checkout.
stages.success_signal: The callback returns `show_form: true` and the Klarna option is displayed.
stages.failure_or_wait: If show_form is false, Klarna is unavailable for that purchase and the option must be handled accordingly.

stages.action: Select Klarna in the checkout and trigger the Buy action using Klarna's sample customer data.
details: Do not use real personal data in playground.
stages.success_signal: The test customer can log in and choose a Klarna payment option.
stages.failure_or_wait: The test must use region-appropriate sample data.

stages.action: Call `Klarna.Payments.authorize` with the required customer details when the Buy action occurs.
details: Provide billing address and, when distinct, shipping address.
Avoid delays between the click and authorize call so the browser does not block Klarna's pop-up.
stages.output: Klarna evaluates the purchase and returns an authorization token on success.
stages.success_signal: The authorize response contains an authorization_token.
stages.failure_or_wait: An authorization failure or browser-blocked pop-up stops order creation.

stages.action: Send the authorization token in an authenticated POST to the session's `/order` endpoint within 60 minutes.
details: Use the regional playground API URL and the same HTTP Basic credentials.
stages.output: Klarna creates the one-time payment order and closes the session.
stages.success_signal: The response returns order_id, fraud_status, and authorized_payment_method.
stages.failure_or_wait: Validation can fail if order creation occurs more than 60 minutes after authorization or request data is invalid.

stages.action: Verify that the create-order success response contains `order_id`, `fraud_status`, and `authorized_payment_method`.
stages.output: The first playground Klarna order is confirmed.
stages.success_signal: The documented example shows `fraud_status: ACCEPTED`, but the success contract returns the fraud-status field rather than guaranteeing that example value for every order.
stages.failure_or_wait: Use Merchant portal logs for API troubleshooting if the request fails.

branches.condition: The developer chooses browser redirect
branches.path: Use Klarna Hosted Payment Page instead of the in-line JavaScript widget.
branches.effect_on_first_success: Replaces client-side SDK integration with a server-side HPP session and customer redirect.

branches.condition: The purchase is recurring
branches.path: Use intent tokenize, buy_and_tokenize, or buy_and_default_tokenize as documented for the recurring scenario.
branches.effect_on_first_success: Changes session intent, authorization response, and order or token terminal.

branches.condition: load returns show_form false
branches.path: Hide or disable Klarna, or leave it visible with Klarna's unavailable message.
branches.effect_on_first_success: No Klarna order can be created for that test purchase; use valid supported test data before retrying.

frictionGates.description: The developer must choose the credential region and Playground environment.
A verified playground Merchant portal account is required.
Klarna requires Terms & Conditions acceptance during account activation.
The API Secret is only displayed in the generated file once.
Klarna pre-assesses availability and only displays the payment option when load returns show_form true.
The order must be created within 60 minutes of authorization.

developerGoal: Send a first notification using Knock by integrating with the backend and creating a workflow.

assumed_prior_state: Developer has a backend web application ready for integration
Developer has basic knowledge of API usage and environment variable management
entryPoint.developer_intent: Send a first notification using Knock by integrating with the backend and creating a workflow.

alternatives_considered.name: Knock Elixir SDK
alternatives_considered.reason_not_primary: While the Elixir SDK is official and documented, the primary onboarding flow documented centers on the dashboard and API usage, which is language-agnostic and more broadly applicable.

selectedSurface.name: Knock Dashboard and API
selectedSurface.selection_basis: The official Knock documentation emphasizes the use of the Knock dashboard for workflow creation and the Knock API for triggering notifications as the primary developer interaction surfaces.

boundary_evidence.official_label_or_terminal_state: Trigger your workflow to notify a set of users

firstMeaningfulSuccess.normalized_outcome: Developer sends a notification through a triggered workflow that delivers a message to specified recipients.
firstMeaningfulSuccess.observable_completion_signal: API call to trigger the workflow returns success and the notification is sent to the intended recipients.
firstMeaningfulSuccess.official_milestone: Successfully trigger a workflow to send a notification to users via the Knock API.
firstMeaningfulSuccess.why_this_is_the_boundary: This step marks the first time a developer completes the end-to-end flow of creating a workflow and successfully sending a notification, which is the core functional milestone for Knock.

prerequisites.requirement: Create a Knock account and log into the Knock dashboard.
Have a backend web application environment ready to integrate with Knock SDK or API.

Set API keys as environment variables to securely use them in backend integration.

details: Choose appropriate SDK for backend language or plan to use direct API calls.

stages.output: SDK installed or API integration planned
stages.success_signal: SDK available in project or API client ready

details: Locate API keys under Platform section in dashboard.
Set secret key as environment variable in backend environment.
stages.failure_or_wait: Missing or incorrect API keys cause authentication failure
stages.output: Environment variables configured with API keys
stages.success_signal: Backend can authenticate API calls to Knock

stages.action: Create a workflow in the Knock dashboard.
details: Click '+ Workflow' button in dashboard.
Name the workflow.
Add at least one channel step to the workflow.
Configure the message template for the channel step.
stages.failure_or_wait: Errors in workflow creation or invalid channel configuration
stages.output: Workflow with configured channel step created
stages.success_signal: Workflow appears in dashboard and is editable

stages.action: Commit the workflow to the current environment to activate it.
details: Use the Commit button in the workflow canvas header.
Review changes and add commit message.
stages.failure_or_wait: Commit errors or uncommitted changes
stages.output: Workflow committed and activated in environment
stages.success_signal: Workflow ready to be triggered

stages.action: Trigger the workflow via the Knock API to send a notification.
details: Use the trigger API call with required data: recipients and data payload.
Use sample payload from workflow trigger step as template.
stages.failure_or_wait: API errors or delivery failures
stages.input: API call with recipients and data
stages.success_signal: API call returns success and notification is delivered

stages.action: Publish workflows to production environment when ready.
details: Ensure workflows are in production environment to send real notifications.
Optionally promote workflows from development to production.
stages.failure_or_wait: Misconfiguration or missing promotion
stages.success_signal: Notifications sent to real users in production

frictionGates.description: User must create and log into a Knock account to access the dashboard.

Trigger API call requires correct recipients and data payload to successfully send notifications.

developerGoal: Install LangChain and run the vendor's basic weather-tool agent until it calls the tool and returns printed content.

entryPoint.developer_intent: Install LangChain and run the vendor's basic weather-tool agent until it calls the tool and returns printed content.
assumed_prior_state: The developer has not installed LangChain for this project.
The developer can obtain credentials for one supported model provider.

selectedSurface.name: LangChain Python Quickstart, basic agent
selectedSurface.selection_basis: The LangChain Python overview routes new users through Install and then Quickstart. The Quickstart is explicitly titled Build your first agent in minutes and begins with a basic agent before the later real-world and tracing sections.

alternatives_considered.name: Deep Agents quickstart
alternatives_considered.reason_not_primary: The LangChain overview presents Deep Agents as a separate batteries-included framework; the roster entry is LangChain and its own Get started navigation selects the LangChain quickstart.

alternatives_considered.name: LangGraph quickstart
alternatives_considered.reason_not_primary: LangGraph is a separate lower-level framework in the documentation.

alternatives_considered.name: JavaScript LangChain quickstart
alternatives_considered.reason_not_primary: The inspected overview and ordered navigation were the Python surface; the JavaScript surface is a peer language route.

firstMeaningfulSuccess.official_milestone: Build your first agent in minutes.
firstMeaningfulSuccess.normalized_outcome: A LangChain agent using a selected chat model, weather function, and system prompt is invoked with the San Francisco weather question and prints its final content blocks after calling the tool.
firstMeaningfulSuccess.observable_completion_signal: The executed example prints the agent's final content blocks and the agent calls get_weather with San Francisco.

boundary_evidence.official_label_or_terminal_state: The Quickstart is titled Build your first agent in minutes; the first runnable section says Start by creating a simple agent, then states that running it causes the agent to call the weather tool.

firstMeaningfulSuccess.why_this_is_the_boundary: The successful tool-calling response fulfills the page's explicitly named first-agent milestone. Build a real-world agent and Trace agent calls are later headings after this first runnable success and are excluded from the first mile.

prerequisites.requirement: Python 3.10 or later.
uv, pip, or Python venv tooling for dependency installation.
An API key or supported local/cloud credential configuration for one documented model provider.

stages.action: Open the LangChain Python overview and follow Get started to Install, then Quickstart.
details: The overview identifies create_agent as the LangChain agent harness and links the ordered Get started pages.
stages.input: Intent to build a first LangChain agent.
stages.success_signal: The Quickstart title Build your first agent in minutes is visible.
stages.failure_or_wait: None documented.

stages.action: Initialize a uv project.
details: Run uv init, the first installation tab in the quickstart.
stages.success_signal: uv creates project metadata in the working directory.
stages.failure_or_wait: The guide does not provide uv installation or local permission recovery.

stages.action: Add langchain and deepagents with uv.
details: Run uv add langchain deepagents.
stages.output: Declared LangChain and Deep Agents dependencies.
stages.success_signal: uv resolves and records the dependencies.
stages.failure_or_wait: Dependency resolution or network failures are not decomposed.

stages.action: Run uv sync.
stages.success_signal: uv sync completes successfully.
stages.failure_or_wait: Package download or environment errors block execution.

stages.action: Obtain an API key from one supported model provider.
details: The guide offers OpenAI, Google Gemini, Anthropic, OpenRouter, Fireworks, Baseten, Ollama, Azure, AWS Bedrock, Hugging Face, and other integrations.
stages.input: Eligible provider account or supported local model setup.
stages.success_signal: The required provider credential is available.
stages.failure_or_wait: Provider-specific account, billing, entitlement, and key-creation steps are outside this guide.

stages.action: Export the environment variable or variables required by the chosen provider.
details: The primary OpenAI tab uses OPENAI_API_KEY; other providers use their documented variable set.
stages.output: Credential available to the Python process.
stages.success_signal: The relevant environment variable is set in the current shell.
stages.failure_or_wait: Incorrect variable names, quoting, scope, or expired credentials cause authentication failure.

stages.action: Create runnable Python code that imports create_agent and defines get_weather.
details: The function accepts a city string and returns the tutorial's fixed sunny-weather string.
stages.input: Installed packages and writable Python environment.
stages.output: Imported factory and weather tool function.
stages.success_signal: The module can import LangChain and the function is defined.
stages.failure_or_wait: Import errors indicate the environment or dependency steps are incomplete.

stages.action: Create the agent with the selected model, get_weather tool, and helpful-assistant system prompt.
details: The first/default provider example uses an OpenAI model; each provider tab supplies its model identifier.
stages.input: Model identifier, tool function, and system prompt.
stages.success_signal: create_agent returns an agent instance.
stages.failure_or_wait: Unsupported model identifiers or provider integration differences can fail initialization.

stages.action: Invoke the agent with the documented San Francisco weather message.
stages.output: Agent run with a user message.
stages.success_signal: The model processes the message and selects the weather tool.
stages.failure_or_wait: Provider authentication, quota, billing, network, or model errors can stop the run.

stages.action: Print the final message content blocks.
stages.success_signal: The final message content_blocks are printed.
stages.failure_or_wait: A failed invocation produces no valid result to print.

stages.action: Verify that the agent used the weather tool for San Francisco and returned its final response.
details: The guide explains that the agent recognizes the city and calls the available weather tool.
stages.success_signal: The output shows the get_weather tool interaction and final content, completing the first-agent milestone.
stages.failure_or_wait: Model outputs can vary, but absence of the tool call means the demonstrated behavior was not reached.

branches.condition: The developer chooses pip instead of uv.
branches.path: Run pip install -U langchain deepagents and omit uv init and uv sync.
branches.effect_on_first_success: Replaces the three uv actions with one package-install command.

branches.condition: The developer wants an isolated venv.
branches.path: Create .venv with python3 -m venv, activate it using the OS-specific command, then install langchain and deepagents with pip.
branches.effect_on_first_success: Adds environment creation and activation before installation.

branches.condition: The developer selects a provider other than OpenAI.
branches.path: Use the provider tab's API key or local/cloud credentials, environment variables, model identifier, and any provider-specific initialization parameters.
branches.effect_on_first_success: Credential and model configuration change, but the weather-tool invocation remains the same.

branches.condition: The developer uses local Ollama.
branches.path: Run Ollama locally rather than supplying a hosted API key.
branches.effect_on_first_success: Replaces a hosted credential with a required local model service.

branches.condition: The developer uses an AI coding assistant.
branches.path: Optionally install the LangChain Docs MCP server and LangChain Skills before implementing the sample.
branches.effect_on_first_success: Adds assistant setup that the quickstart recommends but does not require for the manual path.

frictionGates.description: Python 3.10+ and one package-management workflow are required.

Provider secrets must be placed in the correct environment variables.
The invocation depends on provider authentication, service availability, model access, and usage capacity outside LangChain.

developerGoal: Create, evaluate, and visibly toggle a first LaunchDarkly feature flag without a preselected SDK or application stack.

entryPoint.developer_intent: Create, evaluate, and visibly toggle a first LaunchDarkly feature flag without a preselected SDK or application stack.
assumed_prior_state: The developer has arrived after discovery.
No SDK type, runtime, project, environment, feature flag, or context is selected.

selectedSurface.name: LaunchDarkly software-developer quickstart, SDK not selected
selectedSurface.selection_basis: Workshop selection policy (2026-07-19): when official docs present peer routes, commit to the fastest and most commonly used documented route. Prefer the vendor's first-listed or recommended quickstart, dashboard/console over CLI when both are peers, and the route with an explicit vendor time claim or fewest documented steps when tied. Committed to already-reconstructed surface `LaunchDarkly software-developer quickstart, SDK not selected`. Prior basis: LaunchDarkly's docs home sends new users to Get started, and the developer role path says to set up an SDK, create a first feature flag, and use the Quickstart or CLI. The in-app Quickstart is the recommended guided route but supports many client-side, server-side, mobile, and edge SDKs; installation, credentials, code, contexts, execution, and fallback behavior differ by SDK.

alternatives_considered.name: In-app Quickstart
alternatives_considered.reason_not_primary: It creates a first flag and sample application but asks the developer to choose an SDK.

alternatives_considered.name: LaunchDarkly CLI setup
alternatives_considered.reason_not_primary: The developer role page presents CLI as an alternative to the in-app Quickstart.

alternatives_considered.name: Individual SDK onboarding guides
alternatives_considered.reason_not_primary: The official onboarding index lists .NET, Android, Go, iOS, Java, JavaScript, Node.js client and server, Python, React Native, React Web, Vue, and other distinct routes.

alternatives_considered.name: Product manager flag toggle
alternatives_considered.reason_not_primary: The role router gives product managers a UI-first path without requiring the same SDK implementation sequence.

firstMeaningfulSuccess.official_milestone: Create your first flag, install an SDK, and enable your first feature
firstMeaningfulSuccess.normalized_outcome: In a selected SDK branch, an application initializes LaunchDarkly, evaluates a first flag for a context, and changes its visible behavior when the flag is turned on and off.
firstMeaningfulSuccess.observable_completion_signal: The application immediately changes to the corresponding variation after the developer toggles the flag and selects Review and save; initialization logs show `SDK successfully initialized` rather than failure.

boundary_evidence.official_label_or_terminal_state: The official docs describe the Quickstart as the workflow to create a first flag, install an SDK, and enable a first feature, and the toggle page says changes should appear immediately in the application.

firstMeaningfulSuccess.why_this_is_the_boundary: The success outcome is documented, but an atomic path cannot be fixed until a human selects the SDK and application environment.

prerequisites.requirement: Create a LaunchDarkly account through the no-card 14-day free trial. When the trial ends, the account automatically converts to the free Developer plan unless upgraded; a project and environment are required for the flag and SDK credential.
A runnable application and package manager for one supported SDK are required.
The member must be able to create flags, access environment credentials, and save targeting changes.

stages.action: Sign up for the 14-day LaunchDarkly free trial and open the initial project and Test environment.
details: No credit card is required.
After 14 days, the account automatically converts to the free Developer plan unless the developer upgrades.
stages.input: LaunchDarkly signup identity and account details.
stages.output: Active account, project, and Test environment.
stages.success_signal: The Flags list is accessible for the new account.
stages.failure_or_wait: The docs do not state a manual approval wait for self-service signup.

stages.action: Choose the language or framework used by the runnable sample and install its LaunchDarkly SDK with the corresponding package manager.
details: The Get Started page provides parallel examples for JavaScript, Node.js, React, Python, Java, .NET, and other supported SDKs.
stages.input: Runnable application, selected SDK, runtime, and package manager.
stages.output: LaunchDarkly SDK installed in the application.
stages.success_signal: The SDK package imports or loads in the selected runtime.
stages.failure_or_wait: The exact package name and installation command depend on the selected SDK.

stages.action: Create the first Boolean feature flag in LaunchDarkly and copy its flag key.
details: The Get Started examples use `my-first-feature-flag`.
stages.input: Project, Test environment, flag name, and key.
stages.output: Boolean feature flag with true and false variations.
stages.success_signal: The flag appears in the project's Flags list and its key can be copied.
stages.failure_or_wait: Insufficient flag permissions block creation.

stages.action: Copy the Test environment credential appropriate to the SDK: server-side SDK key, client-side ID, or mobile key.
details: Credentials from another project or environment do not authenticate the sample correctly.
stages.success_signal: The credential is available to the sample application.
stages.failure_or_wait: Using the wrong credential type or environment produces an authentication or initialization error.

stages.action: Initialize the selected LaunchDarkly client with the Test credential and define the documented user context.
details: The common example context identifies a user with a stable key and optional name and email.
Initialization APIs are SDK-specific.
stages.input: SDK package, matching credential, and context fields.
stages.output: Configured LaunchDarkly client and evaluation context.
stages.success_signal: The application reaches the SDK initialized callback or equivalent ready state.
stages.failure_or_wait: Package, async initialization, and credential errors vary by SDK.

stages.action: Run the application and confirm the initialization output.
details: The documented JavaScript spine prints `SDK successfully initialized!`.
stages.success_signal: The output contains `SDK successfully initialized!` rather than an initialization failure.
stages.failure_or_wait: Check the Test environment credential if initialization fails.

stages.action: Evaluate the first flag for the context with the copied flag key and a `false` fallback, then run the code.
details: The sample prints `Our first feature flag is: true` while the Test flag serves the true variation.
stages.input: Initialized client, context, flag key, and false fallback.
stages.success_signal: The application prints the documented true result.
stages.failure_or_wait: A wrong flag key, context, credential, or fallback path can return an unintended value.

stages.action: In the Test environment, toggle the flag Off, select Review and save, enter a comment explaining the change, and select Save changes.
details: The save flow commits the new targeting state.
stages.input: Feature flag, Test environment, Off setting, and comment.
stages.success_signal: The flag list shows Off for the Test environment.
stages.failure_or_wait: A missing save or wrong environment leaves the running evaluation unchanged.

stages.action: Run the evaluation code again and verify that the saved flag change changes the returned value.
details: The documented terminal output is `Our first feature flag is: false`.
stages.input: Saved Off flag and connected application.
stages.success_signal: The application prints `Our first feature flag is: false` after previously printing true.
stages.failure_or_wait: If the value does not change, verify initialization, flag key, credential, streaming connectivity, proxy, outbound authorization, and the selected environment.

branches.condition: A supported SDK is selected.
branches.path: Install its package, initialize the LaunchDarkly client with the SDK key, client-side ID, or mobile key appropriate to that SDK, create the documented context, evaluate the quickstart flag with its key and fallback value, run the application, confirm successful initialization, open the flag's Targeting tab and target environment, toggle it On, select Review and save, and verify the application's changed output. For terminating applications, shut down the client so pending events flush.
branches.effect_on_first_success: The application serves the LaunchDarkly-selected variation and changes after the saved toggle.

branches.condition: SDK initialization fails or the application does not change.
branches.path: Check logs for `SDK failed to initialize`, verify the SDK credential and flag key, confirm the app remains connected, restart it if long-lived streaming was closed, account for HTTPS proxies, and add required outbound authorization before retrying.
branches.effect_on_first_success: The documented recovery loop must succeed before a toggle can be observed.

branches.condition: The user is a product manager rather than a software developer.
branches.path: Begin with turning flags on and off and targeting variations rather than installing an SDK in a new application.
branches.effect_on_first_success: The account must already have a connected application and flag for the UI-only toggle to have a visible result.

frictionGates.description: Self-service signup starts a 14-day no-card trial that automatically converts to the free Developer plan unless upgraded.
SDK type, runtime, and application must be selected before copying the corresponding implementation.
The application must install the selected LaunchDarkly SDK through its dependency manager.
Server-side SDK key, client-side ID, or mobile key must match the SDK and environment.
Flag key, context, fallback value, and toggle environment must be consistent between application and UI.
Streaming connections, HTTPS proxies, and outbound network authorization can delay or block updates.
The application must show the corresponding flag variation after Review and save.

uncertainties.question: Which LaunchDarkly SDK and application stack should define the atomic quickstart?
uncertainties.impact: The choice changes package installation, credential type, source files, async initialization, context code, run command, shutdown, and failure recovery.
uncertainties.reason_unresolved: LaunchDarkly documents many SDK onboarding guides and makes SDK choice part of getting started.
