developerGoal: Obtain an Okta Integrator Free Plan org and choose the app/runtime whose redirect-authentication or API-protection journey should be measured.

entryPoint.developer_intent: Obtain an Okta Integrator Free Plan org and choose the app/runtime whose redirect-authentication or API-protection journey should be measured.
assumed_prior_state: The developer does not yet have an Okta Integrator Free Plan org.
No application type, language, or authentication target is supplied.

selectedSurface.name: Create an Okta Integrator Free Plan org and complete an Angular SPA redirect sign-in
selectedSurface.selection_basis: Workshop selection policy (2026-07-19): when official docs present peer routes, commit to the fastest and most commonly used documented route. Prefer the vendor's first-listed or recommended quickstart, dashboard/console over CLI when both are peers, and the route with an explicit vendor time claim or fewest documented steps when tied. Selected route: Create an Okta Integrator Free Plan org and complete an Angular SPA redirect sign-in. Okta recommends the hosted redirect model as the most secure and fastest sign-in integration, and its official Angular SPA guide ends with a signed-in profile view. Prior surface before policy commit: Unresolved at application type and language selection in Okta Quickstart.

alternatives_considered.name: Server-side web app redirect quickstart
alternatives_considered.reason_not_primary: Requires a framework choice among ASP.NET Core, Node.js, Java, Go, Python, and others.

alternatives_considered.name: SPA redirect quickstart
alternatives_considered.reason_not_primary: A peer front-end type with public-client and PKCE configuration.

alternatives_considered.name: Mobile/native redirect quickstart
alternatives_considered.reason_not_primary: A peer type with device-specific SDK steps.

alternatives_considered.name: Protect your API
alternatives_considered.reason_not_primary: Verifies API access rather than an end-user sign-in.

firstMeaningfulSuccess.official_milestone: The selected Angular SPA redirect sign-in route demonstrates first success when the Profile view displays the user's name and the signed-in controls include Sign out.
firstMeaningfulSuccess.normalized_outcome: The Profile view displays the user's name and the signed-in controls include Sign out.
firstMeaningfulSuccess.observable_completion_signal: The Profile view displays the user's name and the signed-in controls include Sign out.

boundary_evidence.official_label_or_terminal_state: The Profile view displays the user's name and the signed-in controls include Sign out.

firstMeaningfulSuccess.why_this_is_the_boundary: Selected under workshop fastest/most-used policy; terminal taken from the committed official candidate route.

prerequisites.requirement: A free Okta Integrator Free Plan org with an administrator account, created with a unique eligible business email. The org does not expire, but deactivates after 180 consecutive days with no user sign-in unless an app is submitted to the OIN, and it permits 10 active users.

stages.action: Open the Okta Quickstart.
details: The page organizes rapid integration paths by application type.
stages.success_signal: Sign users in and Protect your API are visible.
stages.failure_or_wait: None documented.

stages.action: Open Okta Sign up and select the Okta Workforce Identity tile's Sign up for Integrator Free Plan route.
details: The current signup surface distinguishes Auth0, a 30-day Okta Platform trial, and the free Okta Integrator Free Plan.
The Integrator Free Plan is the developer route for building, testing, and managing workforce integrations.
stages.input: Intent to build and test an Okta integration.
stages.success_signal: First Name, Last Name, Work Email, and Country/Region fields are visible.
stages.failure_or_wait: Selecting the 30-day Okta Platform trial or Auth0 creates a different account and product path.

stages.action: Enter first name, last name, location, and a unique business email address.
details: The live form labels the email Work Email and location Country/Region.
The same business email cannot create multiple Integrator Free Plan organizations.
Some email addresses can be rejected based on location and security considerations.
stages.output: Completed Integrator Free Plan identity fields.
stages.success_signal: All required fields pass form validation.
stages.failure_or_wait: A reused, ineligible, or security-restricted business email blocks org creation.

stages.action: Review the Integrator Free Plan Agreement and Privacy Policy, confirm authority to bind the organization, choose the optional marketing consent, and submit Sign up.
details: The signup states that clicking Sign up binds the developer or represented entity to the Integrator Free Plan Agreement and Privacy Policy.
Okta's current subscription agreement says a person accepting on behalf of a customer must have authority and may not use a free trial if they do not agree.
stages.input: Completed form and authority to accept the applicable terms.
stages.success_signal: Okta accepts the submission and sends the verification email.
stages.failure_or_wait: Lack of authority or refusal of required terms blocks signup; marketing consent is optional.

stages.action: Send the Integrator Free Plan verification email.
details: The email includes the new Okta org domain and an account-activation link.
stages.output: Verification email with org domain and activation link.
stages.success_signal: The developer receives the message and can identify the org domain.
stages.failure_or_wait: Email delivery or business-email eligibility can delay or block activation; no delivery time is documented.

stages.action: Open the activation link and set the administrator password and other authenticators.
details: The verification email is the documented transition to password and authenticator setup.
stages.success_signal: Password and required authenticators are configured and the org can be opened.
stages.failure_or_wait: Expired, missing, or already-used activation state requires account recovery, which the signup reference does not decompose.

stages.action: Open the new Okta org's Admin Console and retain its org domain.
details: The Integrator Free Plan includes SSO, Universal Directory, adaptive MFA, Lifecycle Management, API Access Management, and Workflows, subject to its documented limits.
The org is free and does not expire, but 180 days without any user sign-in deactivates it unless an app has been submitted to the OIN.
stages.input: Activated administrator account and org domain.
stages.output: Usable Integrator Free Plan org with administrator access.
stages.success_signal: The Admin Console opens and the org domain is known.
stages.failure_or_wait: The plan supports no more than 10 active users, and an inactive org can deactivate after 180 days.

stages.action: Choose sign-in or API protection, then the application type.
details: Sign-in types are server-side web, front-end web, and mobile/native.
API protection is separate.
stages.success_signal: The guide family matches the app.
stages.failure_or_wait: The roster entry supplies no app architecture.

stages.action: Choose the documented language or framework implementation.
details: Each app family offers its own runtime variants.
stages.success_signal: A guide with concrete integration and verification steps is selected.
stages.failure_or_wait: Research stops rather than imposing a runtime.

stages.action: Open Okta Sign up and choose Sign up for Integrator Free Plan from the Workforce Identity option.
details: The signup page also offers Auth0 and a 30-day Okta Platform trial, which are different routes.
stages.input: Intent to build and test a Workforce Identity integration.
stages.output: The Integrator Free Plan registration form.
stages.success_signal: The name, work email, and country or region fields are visible.
stages.failure_or_wait: Selecting another plan or Auth0 changes the organization and product path.

stages.action: Enter first name, last name, country or region, and a unique eligible business email.
details: The same business email cannot create multiple Integrator Free Plan organizations.
stages.success_signal: All required identity fields pass validation.
stages.failure_or_wait: A reused or restricted email can be rejected based on location or security considerations.

stages.action: Accept the required Integrator Free Plan agreement and privacy terms, confirm authority to bind the organization, choose optional marketing consent, and submit Sign up.
details: Marketing consent is optional, but acceptance of the applicable agreement and privacy policy is required.
stages.input: The completed form and authority to accept the terms.
stages.success_signal: Okta accepts the submission and initiates email verification.
stages.failure_or_wait: Lack of authority or refusal of the required terms blocks signup.

stages.action: Send the organization verification email with the org domain and activation link.
details: This message is the documented handoff from registration to administrator setup.
stages.success_signal: The developer receives the org domain and activation link.
stages.failure_or_wait: Email delivery is an explicit wait and no delivery time is documented.

stages.action: Open the activation link and configure the administrator password and required authenticators.
details: The activation flow establishes the first org administrator.
stages.input: The verification email and activation link.
stages.success_signal: The password and authenticators are accepted.
stages.failure_or_wait: A missing, expired, or used link requires recovery that the signup reference does not decompose.

stages.action: Sign in to the new org's Admin Console and retain the org domain.
details: The Integrator Free Plan does not expire, but an org can deactivate after 180 days with no user sign-in and is limited to 10 active users.
stages.input: The activated administrator and org domain.
stages.output: Administrator access to the Integrator Free Plan org.
stages.success_signal: The Admin Console opens.
stages.failure_or_wait: Authentication failure or a deactivated org blocks application setup.

stages.action: Open Applications, Applications, select Create App Integration, choose OIDC and Single-Page Application, then select Next.
details: OIDC SPA is the app-integration type used by the Angular redirect guide.
stages.success_signal: The form shows SPA-specific grant and URI settings.
stages.failure_or_wait: Choosing another protocol or application type produces a different integration.

stages.action: Name the integration, enable Authorization Code and Refresh Token, and configure the local sign-in and sign-out URIs.
details: Set the sign-in redirect URI to http://localhost:4200/login/callback and the sign-out redirect URI to http://localhost:4200.
stages.input: An app name and the documented local URIs.
stages.success_signal: Both grant types and exact callback URIs are present.
stages.failure_or_wait: A mismatched redirect URI prevents Okta from returning authentication to the sample.

stages.action: Choose the controlled-access assignment, save the integration, then use the General tab to verify Refresh Token, set the Initiate login URI, save again, and copy the Client ID.
details: The selected user must be allowed to access the application.
On the General tab, confirm Refresh Token is selected and set the Initiate login URI to the local application URI documented by the guide before selecting Save.
stages.input: The completed SPA settings and intended test-user assignment.
stages.output: A saved OIDC SPA integration and client ID.
stages.success_signal: The integration exists, Refresh Token and Initiate login URI match the guide, the settings are saved, and the client ID is copied.
stages.failure_or_wait: An unassigned test user, missing Refresh Token grant, or incorrect Initiate login URI prevents the documented sign-in path from working as configured.

stages.action: Add http://localhost:4200 as an Okta Trusted Origin for the required local redirect and CORS behavior.
details: The guide explicitly enables the local base URI in Trusted Origins.
stages.output: A trusted local origin in the Okta org.
stages.success_signal: http://localhost:4200 appears in Trusted Origins with the required types.
stages.failure_or_wait: A missing trusted origin can block browser requests or redirect behavior.

stages.action: Confirm a recent Node and npm environment, then create the Angular 22 app with npx @angular/cli@22 new okta-angular-sample --ssr=false.
details: The guide disables server-side rendering for this browser SPA sample.
stages.input: Node, npm, and a writable project directory.
stages.success_signal: The okta-angular-sample directory is created.
stages.failure_or_wait: Unsupported runtime, package download, or scaffolding errors block setup.

stages.action: Enter the project and install @okta/okta-angular@8.0 and @okta/okta-auth-js@8.0.
details: These documented package versions provide the Angular integration and browser authentication client.
stages.output: The Okta Angular and Auth JS dependencies.
stages.success_signal: npm completes installation without error.
stages.failure_or_wait: Dependency-resolution errors prevent configuration.

stages.action: Ensure the default custom authorization server has an access policy, then configure the Angular application with that issuer, the copied Client ID, redirect URI, scopes, and Okta authentication provider.
details: The default custom authorization server must have an access policy before it can authorize this application.
The redirect URI must resolve to http://localhost:4200/login/callback and the issuer must use the new org domain's default authorization server.
stages.input: The Okta org domain, default authorization-server access policy, client ID, and guide's Angular configuration.
stages.output: An Angular app connected to the OIDC SPA integration.
stages.success_signal: The authentication provider and OktaAuth configuration contain no placeholders.
stages.failure_or_wait: A missing authorization-server access policy or wrong issuer, client ID, callback, or scopes causes authorization or callback failure.

stages.action: Add the sign-in and sign-out controls, login callback route, and the guide's initial unguarded Profile route and view.
details: The callback component processes Okta's redirect, while the Profile route provides the visible authenticated result.
Do not label this initial Profile route as protected: the guide adds canActivateAuthGuard later, after the initial sign-in and profile test.
stages.output: A runnable SPA with authentication UI and routes.
stages.success_signal: The app includes Sign in, callback, the initial unguarded Profile route and view, and Sign out behavior.
stages.failure_or_wait: Missing callback or route configuration leaves the redirect incomplete.

stages.action: Run ng serve and open http://localhost:4200.
details: The Angular development server hosts the configured SPA on the registered origin.
stages.success_signal: The browser loads the SPA and shows the Sign in control.
stages.failure_or_wait: Build or port errors prevent the browser flow.

stages.action: Select Sign in, authenticate as an assigned org user, and allow the callback to return to the SPA.
details: Okta hosts the credential experience and returns the browser to the registered callback.
stages.input: Credentials for an assigned Okta user.
stages.success_signal: The callback completes and the SPA changes to its signed-in state.
stages.failure_or_wait: Invalid credentials, an unassigned user, mismatched URI, issuer, or client ID blocks the redirect flow.

stages.action: Open Profile and verify that the signed-in user's name is displayed, then confirm Sign out is available.
details: The profile content is the guide's observable proof that the SPA obtained and used the authenticated user state.
stages.output: A visible user profile in the Angular app.
stages.success_signal: The Profile view displays the user's name and the signed-in controls include Sign out.
stages.failure_or_wait: A completed login without the profile result does not satisfy this candidate terminal.

branches.condition: The intent is end-user sign-in.
branches.path: Use the recommended Okta-hosted redirect model, then choose server-side, SPA, or mobile and a framework.
branches.effect_on_first_success: Defines success as completing hosted sign-in and observing authenticated user state or claims.

branches.condition: The intent is API protection.
branches.path: Choose Protect your API and a server framework, configure authorization, send a token-bearing request, and verify allowed versus denied responses.
branches.effect_on_first_success: Defines success around API authorization rather than interactive sign-in.

branches.condition: The app is a single-page application.
branches.path: Create an OIDC SPA integration, configure Authorization Code with PKCE and redirect URIs, enable trusted origins, install the framework SDK, implement sign-in/out and protected routes, then sign in.
branches.effect_on_first_success: Adds public-client, CORS, PKCE, and browser SDK actions.

branches.condition: The app is server-side, mobile, or native.
branches.path: Follow the matching app type, redirect URI, credential, SDK, callback, and verification sequence.
branches.effect_on_first_success: Changes credential handling and callback implementation by runtime.

frictionGates.description: An Okta Integrator Free Plan org and administrator access are required; signup needs a unique eligible business email and a location accepted by Okta's security controls.
Signup requires acceptance of the Integrator Free Plan Agreement and Privacy Policy by someone authorized to bind the represented entity.
The developer must receive the verification email and use its activation link before setting the password and authenticators.
The free org is limited to 10 active users and deactivates after 180 consecutive days without a user sign-in unless an app is submitted to the OIN.
The administrator needs sufficient role permissions to create and configure app integrations.
Existing orgs without API Access Management may require support enablement or a Console-only alternative.
The developer must choose sign-in versus API protection and an application architecture.
A language or framework choice is required before atomic implementation steps exist.

uncertainties.question: Which application type and runtime should be measured?
uncertainties.impact: It determines integration configuration, code, and terminal success.
uncertainties.reason_unresolved: The Quickstart explicitly requires these choices and the roster gives only the company.

uncertainties.question: Which password and authenticator screens appear after the activation link?
uncertainties.impact: Authenticator policy and recovery requirements can add device, factor-enrollment, and verification steps before the Admin Console opens.
uncertainties.reason_unresolved: The current org-defaults reference establishes the activation link, password, and other authenticators but does not enumerate their exact in-product screens or factor choices.

developerGoal: Create a OneSignal account and app to send notifications via a chosen messaging channel.

assumed_prior_state: Developer has internet access and a device to access the OneSignal Dashboard
Developer has an existing app or website to integrate messaging
entryPoint.developer_intent: Create a OneSignal account and app to send notifications via a chosen messaging channel.

alternatives_considered.name: REST API only
alternatives_considered.reason_not_primary: The REST API requires prior app and channel setup in the Dashboard and is used after initial onboarding steps.

alternatives_considered.name: Third-party integrations
alternatives_considered.reason_not_primary: Integrations are for extending functionality after initial setup, not for first-mile onboarding.

selectedSurface.name: Dashboard and SDK
selectedSurface.selection_basis: The official documentation emphasizes starting from the OneSignal Dashboard to create an app and configure messaging channels, then integrating the SDK for push and in-app messages or configuring email/SMS channels without SDK. This is the primary developer surface for onboarding.

boundary_evidence.official_label_or_terminal_state: Send messages — compose campaigns, personalize content, and build automated Journeys

firstMeaningfulSuccess.normalized_outcome: Message sent and received by at least one user subscription
firstMeaningfulSuccess.observable_completion_signal: Message delivery confirmation in OneSignal Dashboard or API response indicating successful send
firstMeaningfulSuccess.official_milestone: Successfully send a message through a configured channel from the OneSignal Dashboard or API
firstMeaningfulSuccess.why_this_is_the_boundary: The documentation defines sending a message as the first major milestone after setup, marking the platform's core functionality being operational.

prerequisites.requirement: Create a OneSignal account or be invited to an existing Organization
Create a OneSignal App within the Organization to hold user and messaging data
Choose and configure at least one messaging channel (e.g., mobile push, web push, email, SMS)
Integrate the OneSignal SDK for channels requiring SDK (mobile push, web push, in-app messages)
Obtain and secure API keys and App ID for authentication and API usage

Sign up or authenticate

Select platform(s) for messaging
stages.failure_or_wait: Validation errors or network issues
stages.output: New app created with unique App ID
stages.success_signal: App visible in Dashboard with App ID

stages.action: Choose and configure at least one messaging channel
details: For push: follow SDK installation and platform-specific setup
For email/SMS: configure sender domains or phone numbers
Set up credentials and permissions as required
stages.failure_or_wait: Configuration errors or missing credentials
stages.output: Channel enabled and configured in the app
stages.success_signal: Channel status shows as active or configured

stages.action: Integrate OneSignal SDK into app code for channels requiring SDK
details: Install SDK via package manager
Initialize SDK with App ID
Implement user subscription and permission prompts
stages.failure_or_wait: Build errors or SDK initialization failures
stages.input: SDK installation commands and App ID
stages.output: SDK integrated and initialized in app
stages.success_signal: App registers subscriptions with OneSignal

stages.action: Send a test message or campaign from the Dashboard or API
details: Compose message content
Select target audience or test users
Send message
stages.failure_or_wait: Send errors or validation failures
stages.success_signal: Delivery confirmation or success response

candidatePaths.name: API-only onboarding path
candidatePaths.relationship_to_primary_path: Alternative to Dashboard UI message sending; requires prior app and channel setup
candidatePaths.selection_basis: For developers who prefer to use REST API directly after app and channel setup

steps.action: Create OneSignal account and app
details: Same as primary path step 1 and 2
steps.output: App with App ID and API keys

steps.action: Obtain API keys from Dashboard
details: Navigate to Keys & IDs
Copy REST API key

details: Construct API request with App ID and API key
Specify message content and target
Send POST request to messages endpoint

branches.condition: Developer chooses a messaging channel that does not require SDK (e.g., email or SMS)
branches.effect_on_first_success: Faster onboarding by configuring channel without code integration

frictionGates.description: Creating a OneSignal account is required to access the Dashboard and create apps.
Configuring at least one messaging channel is required before sending messages.
SDK integration is required for push and in-app messaging channels.
API keys must be secured and used properly to authenticate API requests.

uncertainties.impact: Without a vendor claim or explicit documentation, time estimates for onboarding duration remain unclear, affecting developer expectations.
uncertainties.question: What is the typical or average time to first success (sending a message) claimed or expected by OneSignal?
uncertainties.reason_unresolved: No explicit time-to-first-success or onboarding duration claim was found in the official documentation sources.

developerGoal: Create an OpenAI Platform API key, fund usable prepaid credits with a payment method, and receive a Responses API JSON result over HTTPS.

entryPoint.developer_intent: Create an OpenAI Platform API key, fund usable prepaid credits with a payment method, and receive a Responses API JSON result over HTTPS.
assumed_prior_state: The developer has no OpenAI Platform API key, prepaid credit balance, or prior Responses request.
A terminal with cURL is available; no language SDK is assumed.

selectedSurface.name: OpenAI Responses API via cURL (API key + funded credits)
selectedSurface.selection_basis: OpenAI's Developer quickstart creates an API key and demonstrates a Responses API call. The same page documents a cURL request to https://api.openai.com/v1/responses with Authorization: Bearer $OPENAI_API_KEY, so an SDK install is not required. OpenAI prepaid-billing help states that after purchasing credits you can start using the API, and billing setup includes adding payment details.

alternatives_considered.name: JavaScript / Python / other SDK tabs
alternatives_considered.reason_not_primary: SDK tabs are optional convenience clients. The quickstart also documents cURL against the same Responses endpoint with only an API key.

alternatives_considered.name: Agents SDK quickstart
alternatives_considered.reason_not_primary: This is a later specialized agent path after a basic Responses API call.

alternatives_considered.name: Chat Completions legacy path
alternatives_considered.reason_not_primary: The current Developer quickstart centers the Responses API as the first call.

firstMeaningfulSuccess.official_milestone: Run an API Call to the Responses API
firstMeaningfulSuccess.normalized_outcome: An authenticated HTTPS Responses API request to https://api.openai.com/v1/responses returns model-generated output text.
firstMeaningfulSuccess.observable_completion_signal: The Responses API returns a successful JSON body that includes model output text for the requested prompt.

boundary_evidence.official_label_or_terminal_state: The Developer quickstart documents Create an API Key, export OPENAI_API_KEY, and a cURL POST to https://api.openai.com/v1/responses with Authorization: Bearer $OPENAI_API_KEY. Prepaid billing help states that after purchasing credits you should be able to start using the API.

firstMeaningfulSuccess.why_this_is_the_boundary: The first meaningful LLM success is a successful authenticated Responses API response. An SDK is not required because the official quickstart documents the same call with cURL. Usable prepaid credits (and payment details to purchase them when free-trial credit is $0) are required for API usage per OpenAI's prepaid billing help; a key alone is not enough once free-trial credit is exhausted or unavailable.

prerequisites.requirement: An OpenAI Platform account that can open the API keys and billing settings.

Payment details and prepaid credits with a usable balance. OpenAI's prepaid billing setup shows Add payment details and states that after purchasing credits you can start using the API; exhausted credits return a billing-quota error on API requests.
An API key created in the OpenAI dashboard and stored securely.
A terminal with cURL (or any HTTP client) able to send HTTPS JSON requests. No language SDK is required.

stages.action: Create or sign in to an OpenAI Platform account and open the dashboard.
stages.input: Intent to use the OpenAI API
stages.success_signal: API keys and billing settings are reachable.
stages.failure_or_wait: Account recovery follows the dashboard sign-in flow.

stages.action: Open billing settings, add payment details, and purchase prepaid credits (or confirm a usable credit balance).
details: Prepaid billing setup includes Add payment details under the billing overview.
After purchasing credits, OpenAI states you should be able to start using the API; systems may take a couple of minutes to reflect the balance.
If free-trial credit remaining is already $0, payment details and a credit purchase are required before API usage continues.
stages.success_signal: Billing shows a positive usable credit balance (or non-zero free-trial credit still available).
stages.failure_or_wait: Payment verification delays or a $0 balance leave API requests failing with billing-quota errors.

stages.action: Create an API key in the OpenAI dashboard and store it securely.
details: The quickstart warns to keep the key in a safe location.
stages.success_signal: The key is generated and copied to secure local storage.
stages.failure_or_wait: No manual approval wait is documented for key creation.

stages.action: Export the key as OPENAI_API_KEY in the terminal.
details: macOS/Linux: export OPENAI_API_KEY=...; PowerShell: setx OPENAI_API_KEY ...
stages.output: API key available to HTTP clients in the shell
stages.success_signal: The environment variable is set for the current shell.
stages.failure_or_wait: Shell persistence depends on the OS.

stages.action: POST a Responses API request with cURL to https://api.openai.com/v1/responses.
details: Include Content-Type: application/json and Authorization: Bearer $OPENAI_API_KEY.
Send a JSON body with a model and input prompt as shown in the quickstart cURL example.
stages.success_signal: The API returns a successful JSON body containing model output text.
stages.failure_or_wait: Missing key, insufficient credits, or invalid JSON produce API errors instead of model output.

stages.action: Confirm the Responses API JSON includes the model-generated text.
stages.success_signal: Model output text is present in the successful response.
stages.failure_or_wait: An error body means the first call did not succeed.

branches.condition: The account still has usable free-trial credit remaining.
branches.path: Skip purchasing additional prepaid credits while free-trial credit remains; the quickstart may still show Congrats on running a free test API request. When free-trial credit is $0, add payment details and purchase credits before further API usage.
branches.effect_on_first_success: Billing purchase may be deferred only while free-trial credit remains usable.

branches.condition: The developer prefers an official SDK instead of cURL.
branches.path: Install the language SDK shown on the quickstart tab and run the equivalent Responses example; the API endpoint and API-key header requirements stay the same.
branches.effect_on_first_success: Adds optional local package installation without changing the authenticated Responses boundary.

frictionGates.description: Payment details and prepaid credits are required to start or continue API usage when free-trial credit is $0; exhausted credits cause billing-quota API errors.
A secret API key must be created and stored safely.
The Authorization Bearer token must be the API key for https://api.openai.com/v1/responses.

uncertainties.question: Which brand-new accounts currently receive non-zero free-trial credit versus $0 free-trial credit requiring immediate payment details?
uncertainties.impact: Some accounts may complete the first Responses call before purchasing credits; others hit billing before any successful LLM response.
uncertainties.reason_unresolved: The quickstart mentions a free test request while prepaid billing help shows Free trial credit remaining $0.00 with Add payment details; eligibility is account-state dependent and not fully enumerated on these pages.

developerGoal: Create a first OCI resource without a preselected service or interface.

entryPoint.developer_intent: Create a first OCI resource without a preselected service or interface.
assumed_prior_state: The developer has arrived after discovery.
No OCI service, region-specific resource, compartment, or interface has been selected.

selectedSurface.name: Create an OCI Free Trial tenancy and connect to the first Linux instance
selectedSurface.selection_basis: Workshop selection policy (2026-07-19): when official docs present peer routes, commit to the fastest and most commonly used documented route. Prefer the vendor's first-listed or recommended quickstart, dashboard/console over CLI when both are peers, and the route with an explicit vendor time claim or fewest documented steps when tied. Selected route: Create an OCI Free Trial tenancy and connect to the first Linux instance. OCI lists several coequal first resources, but its official Launching Your First Linux Instance tutorial supplies a complete console path from an OCI account through compartment and network creation to an authenticated shell on an Oracle Linux VM. Prior surface before policy commit: OCI resource type and interface not selected.

alternatives_considered.name: Launch your first Linux instance
alternatives_considered.reason_not_primary: This is one compute-specific choice among several first-resource tutorials.

alternatives_considered.name: Get Started with the CLI
alternatives_considered.reason_not_primary: This interface-specific walkthrough creates compartments, networks, and instances and requires separate CLI setup; OCI does not present it as the only general route.

firstMeaningfulSuccess.official_milestone: The selected Free Trial route demonstrates first success when the developer is connected to the default shell for the first Linux instance.
firstMeaningfulSuccess.normalized_outcome: The developer is connected to the default shell for the instance.
firstMeaningfulSuccess.observable_completion_signal: The developer is connected to the default shell for the instance.

boundary_evidence.official_label_or_terminal_state: The developer is connected to the default shell for the instance.

firstMeaningfulSuccess.why_this_is_the_boundary: Selected under workshop fastest/most-used policy; terminal taken from the committed official candidate route.

prerequisites.requirement: Free Trial signup requires country or territory, name, a valid email address, CAPTCHA completion, address, and phone details.
Most users must provide an accepted payment card for identity verification; Oracle may place a small temporary hold that is removed automatically.
A Home Region, resource type, and interaction interface must be selected. The Home Region cannot be changed after account creation.

stages.action: Open Oracle Cloud Free Trial signup, enter country or territory, name, valid email, and CAPTCHA, then select Verify my email.
details: Oracle sends a verification link to the supplied address.
The link is valid for 30 minutes.
stages.input: Country, name, email address, and CAPTCHA response.
stages.success_signal: The verification email arrives with a signup link.
stages.failure_or_wait: An expired link requires restarting email verification; some countries must contact Oracle Sales instead of self-service signup.

stages.action: Select the Home Region, accept the terms, and continue.
details: The Home Region determines where the tenancy is created and cannot be changed later.
Free-tier availability varies by region.
stages.success_signal: The address and phone section opens.
stages.failure_or_wait: A mistaken Home Region cannot be corrected after account creation.

stages.action: Enter address and phone details, then add an accepted credit or eligible debit card and select Finish.
details: Oracle uses the card for identity verification and may place a small temporary hold.
The Free Trial does not charge the card unless the account is upgraded.
stages.success_signal: The payment verification finishes and the trial terms are available.
stages.failure_or_wait: An unsupported card or failed verification blocks signup.

stages.action: Accept the trial terms and select Start my free trial.
details: The promotion includes $300 in credits valid for up to 30 days.
stages.input: Completed signup form and accepted terms.
stages.success_signal: Oracle begins provisioning cloud services in the tenancy.
stages.failure_or_wait: Provisioning must finish before Console access is available.

stages.action: Wait for tenancy provisioning and the redirect to the OCI Console, then retain the welcome email.
details: Oracle redirects the developer after the services are provisioned and also sends a welcome email.
stages.output: Provisioned OCI tenancy and authenticated Console session.
stages.success_signal: The OCI Console opens and the welcome email confirms the account.
stages.failure_or_wait: The documentation does not state a provisioning duration.

stages.action: Open the OCI documentation home and follow its Start route to first-resource tutorials.
details: The home distinguishes Start from Develop routes.
The first-resource index groups tutorials by resource type.
stages.input: Provisioned OCI tenancy and intent to create a resource.
stages.output: A menu of official resource-specific tutorials.
stages.success_signal: The developer reaches the documented first-resource choices.
stages.failure_or_wait: No wait is documented at the tutorial router.

stages.action: Select compute, storage, networking, database, load balancing, or another resource path and its interface.
details: OCI resources may be region-specific or availability-domain-specific.
The CLI route itself includes compartment, VCN, and instance decisions.
stages.input: A human-selected resource outcome and interface.
stages.success_signal: No common platform-level terminal signal is documented.
stages.failure_or_wait: The journey cannot continue as one canonical path without the selection.

stages.action: Open Oracle Cloud Free Trial signup, enter country or territory, name, valid email, and CAPTCHA, then select Verify my email.
details: Oracle sends a verification link that is valid for 30 minutes.
stages.input: Country, name, reachable email, and CAPTCHA response.
stages.success_signal: The verification email arrives with a signup link.
stages.failure_or_wait: An expired link requires restarting verification; some countries require contacting Oracle Sales.

stages.action: Open the verification link, create and confirm the password, optionally enter a company name, and choose a cloud account name.
stages.input: Valid email link, password, and cloud account name.
stages.success_signal: The signup form accepts the account information.
stages.failure_or_wait: Password or cloud account name validation can block continuation.

stages.action: Select the Home Region, accept the terms, and continue.
details: The Home Region cannot be changed after account creation and Free Tier availability varies by region.
stages.success_signal: The address and phone section opens.
stages.failure_or_wait: A mistaken Home Region cannot be corrected after the account is created.

stages.action: Enter the address and phone details, provide a post office box if Oracle asks for one, provide a CPF if the address is in Brazil, and select Continue.
details: The post office box and Brazil CPF fields are conditional requirements documented in the official signup instructions.
stages.input: Address, phone, and any requested post office box or Brazil CPF details.
stages.output: Accepted contact details and the payment verification section.
stages.success_signal: The Add payment verification method control becomes available.
stages.failure_or_wait: Missing or invalid address, phone, requested post office box, or Brazil CPF details blocks continuation.

stages.action: Select Add payment verification method, choose Credit Card, enter an accepted card's details, and select Finish.
details: Oracle uses the card for identity verification and may place a small temporary hold that is removed automatically.
stages.success_signal: Payment verification completes and the trial terms become available.
stages.failure_or_wait: An unsupported card or failed verification blocks signup.

stages.action: Accept the trial terms and select Start my free trial.
details: The promotion includes $300 in credits valid for up to 30 days.
stages.success_signal: Oracle begins provisioning cloud services.
stages.failure_or_wait: Console access is unavailable until provisioning finishes.

stages.action: Wait for tenancy provisioning and the redirect to the OCI Console, then retain the welcome email.
details: Oracle does not state a provisioning duration on the signup page.
stages.output: Provisioned OCI tenancy and authenticated Console session.
stages.success_signal: The OCI Console opens and the welcome email confirms the account.
stages.failure_or_wait: The resource tutorial cannot begin until provisioning completes.

stages.action: Confirm that the local macOS, Linux, or Windows computer has an `ssh` client.
details: The tutorial states that current versions of these operating systems include OpenSSH by default.
stages.success_signal: The `ssh` command is available locally.
stages.failure_or_wait: Without an SSH client, the candidate cannot reach its terminal signal.

stages.action: Open Identity & Security, select Compartments, create a compartment with a name, required description, and parent compartment, then select it.
details: The tutorial uses one dedicated compartment for its network, instance, and optional storage resources.
stages.success_signal: The new compartment appears in the list and can be selected.
stages.failure_or_wait: Missing IAM permission or invalid compartment details prevent resource organization and creation.

stages.action: Open Networking, select Virtual cloud networks, choose Start VCN Wizard, and select Create VCN with Internet Connectivity.
details: The wizard creates a VCN, internet gateway, public and private subnets, route tables, and security lists.
stages.success_signal: The VCN configuration form opens for the selected compartment.
stages.failure_or_wait: Missing networking permission or wrong compartment prevents VCN setup.

stages.action: Name the VCN, keep the tutorial defaults for DNS and public and private subnet address settings, review the selections, and select Create.
stages.input: VCN name, compartment, and documented default network settings.
stages.success_signal: OCI begins creating the VCN and its related resources.
stages.failure_or_wait: Invalid CIDR, quota, permission, name, or service errors stop network creation.

stages.action: Wait for VCN creation to complete and select View VCN.
details: The tutorial says network creation might take a moment.
stages.output: Available internet-connected VCN with public subnet.
stages.success_signal: The VCN details page shows the created network resources.
stages.failure_or_wait: Failed or incomplete related resources prevent the instance from receiving internet connectivity.

stages.action: Open Compute, select Instances and Create Instance, then enter the instance name and select the tutorial compartment.
stages.output: Create compute instance page scoped to the compartment.
stages.success_signal: The instance configuration form accepts the name and compartment.
stages.failure_or_wait: Missing compute permission or invalid compartment selection prevents configuration.

stages.action: Keep the tutorial placement and security defaults, select the Oracle Linux image and an Always Free eligible virtual-machine shape, then select the created VCN and its public subnet with a public IPv4 address.
details: The tutorial illustrates Oracle Linux 8 and an Ampere VM.Standard.A1.Flex shape while noting that actual values can differ.
stages.input: Available image, eligible shape, VCN, public subnet, and capacity.
stages.output: Configured Oracle Linux VM with public networking.
stages.success_signal: The form shows the selected image, shape, VCN, public subnet, and public IPv4 assignment.
stages.failure_or_wait: Region capacity, shape eligibility, image, networking, quota, or permission problems block creation.

stages.action: Use Generate a key pair for me, save both private and public SSH keys in a secure location, keep the documented boot-volume and no-block-volume settings, and select Create.
details: OCI-generated private keys cannot be retrieved again after instance creation.
stages.input: Completed instance configuration and local secure key location.
stages.output: Saved SSH key pair and submitted instance creation request.
stages.success_signal: The instance details page opens in Provisioning state.
stages.failure_or_wait: Losing the private key, failing to save it, or an instance validation error prevents later connection.

stages.action: Wait without refreshing until the instance reaches Running, then allow several additional minutes for Oracle Linux to boot and obtain its public IP address.
details: The tutorial says provisioning takes several minutes and the operating system needs another few minutes before connection.
stages.output: Running Oracle Linux instance and public IP address.
stages.success_signal: The lifecycle state is Running and the public IP is visible.
stages.failure_or_wait: Provisioning, capacity, network, or boot failure prevents SSH access.

stages.action: Restrict the private key permissions, then run `ssh -i <private_key_file> opc@<public-ip-address>` and accept the host-key fingerprint on first connection.
details: The Unix-style path uses `chmod 400 <private_key_file>` before SSH; Oracle Linux uses the default username `opc`.
stages.input: Saved private key, public IP, local SSH client, and reachable port 22.
stages.output: Authenticated SSH connection to the instance.
stages.success_signal: SSH accepts the key and opens the remote session.
stages.failure_or_wait: Wrong key permissions, username, key, public IP, network path, boot state, or host-key response blocks connection.

stages.action: Verify that the default Oracle Linux shell prompt is visible on the remote instance.
details: The tutorial's optional block-volume and cleanup tasks occur after this first connected-instance result.
stages.success_signal: The developer is connected to the default shell for the instance.
stages.failure_or_wait: No remote shell means the documented first Linux instance journey is incomplete.

branches.condition: A Linux compute instance is selected.
branches.path: Follow OCI's console tutorial to choose a compartment and image, configure networking and keys, create the instance, wait for its lifecycle state, and connect as documented.
branches.effect_on_first_success: Success becomes a launched and accessible compute instance.

branches.condition: The CLI walkthrough is selected.
branches.path: Meet CLI requirements, choose or create a compartment, obtain tenancy and compartment identifiers, create a VCN, and issue the documented instance-launch commands until the returned resource state can be verified.
branches.effect_on_first_success: The path adds local CLI installation, credential, identifier, networking, and command-line gates.

frictionGates.description: Email verification is mandatory and its signup link expires after 30 minutes.
Home Region must be selected during signup and cannot be changed later.
Most users must provide a payment card, and a temporary verification hold may appear.
Tenancy services must be provisioned before the OCI Console redirect and welcome email.
OCI requires a resource and interface choice among several equally presented first-resource paths.

uncertainties.question: Which OCI resource and interface should represent the platform's first mile?
uncertainties.impact: The full steps, credentials, cost exposure, wait states, and success signal differ by resource.
uncertainties.reason_unresolved: OCI officially presents several first-resource tutorials without a single default.

developerGoal: Run the official Ory Kratos Docker quickstart locally, register a user, verify the address through MailSlurper, and inspect the active session.

entryPoint.developer_intent: Run the official Ory Kratos Docker quickstart locally, register a user, verify the address through MailSlurper, and inspect the active session.
assumed_prior_state: No Ory account or cloud project is assumed.
Git, Docker, Docker Compose, a browser, and the documented local ports are available.

selectedSurface.name: Ory Kratos local Docker quickstart through a verified first user
selectedSurface.selection_basis: Under the workshop policy, the official Ory Kratos Docker quickstart is primary: it is a local, no-account, OSS route and the guide says Git, Docker, and Docker Compose are the only dependencies. The guide chooses Node.js because it is broadly understood and documents the full registration, local email verification, welcome page, and active-session terminal.

alternatives_considered.name: Ory Network
alternatives_considered.reason_not_primary: The portal presents Network, Enterprise License, and Open Source as deployment choices rather than a universal default.

alternatives_considered.name: Next.js App Router Quickstart
alternatives_considered.reason_not_primary: Next.js is one of several framework peers and its final registration screen actions are not enumerated in the public guide.

alternatives_considered.name: Express.js Quickstart
alternatives_considered.reason_not_primary: This route requires an Ory Network account and project and is not selected over the other framework routes.

firstMeaningfulSuccess.official_milestone: Perform registration and verify the first local Ory Kratos user.
firstMeaningfulSuccess.normalized_outcome: A local Ory Kratos identity is registered, its email is verified through MailSlurper, and an active browser session reaches the welcome dashboard.
firstMeaningfulSuccess.observable_completion_signal: `/welcome` opens after verification and `/sessions` reports an active identity with `verified: true` and a `verified_at` timestamp.

boundary_evidence.official_label_or_terminal_state: After local registration and MailSlurper verification, `/welcome` opens and `/sessions` shows an active identity with the email address marked verified.

firstMeaningfulSuccess.why_this_is_the_boundary: This is the earliest observable terminal in the selected official quickstart. The workshop policy permits treating signup as one opaque step and then reconstructing the publicly documented post-login path.

prerequisites.requirement: Git, Docker, and Docker Compose installed.
Local ports 4455, 4433, 4434, and 4436 are free.
Docker has sufficient disk space for the quickstart images and volumes.

stages.action: Open the official Ory Kratos quickstart.
stages.output: The local Docker Compose registration route is visible.
stages.success_signal: Clone and Docker commands are shown.
stages.failure_or_wait: None documented.

stages.action: Install or confirm Git, Docker, and Docker Compose, and ensure ports 4455, 4433, 4434, and 4436 are free.
stages.success_signal: Docker Compose is available and required ports are free.
stages.failure_or_wait: Insufficient Docker disk space or occupied ports block startup.

stages.action: Clone `https://github.com/ory/kratos.git`, enter the repository, and check out the desired documented version.
stages.output: The Ory Kratos source and quickstart files are local.
stages.success_signal: `quickstart.yml` and `quickstart-standalone.yml` are present.
stages.failure_or_wait: Use a valid released version.

stages.action: Run `docker compose -f quickstart.yml -f quickstart-standalone.yml up --force-recreate`.
stages.output: Kratos, the Node.js self-service UI, and MailSlurper start.
stages.success_signal: Logs show admin HTTP on 4434 and public HTTP on 4433.
stages.failure_or_wait: Wait one or two minutes; resolve network or disk errors if health logs do not appear.

stages.action: Open `http://127.0.0.1:4455/welcome`, choose Sign In, then Register new account.
stages.success_signal: A registration flow ID appears in the URL.
stages.failure_or_wait: Use 127.0.0.1, not localhost, so cookies work.

stages.action: Complete the generated registration form with an email, name, and policy-compliant password, then submit.
stages.output: Ory creates the identity and sends a verification message to MailSlurper.
stages.success_signal: The browser asks for email verification.
stages.failure_or_wait: A weak password is rejected by the configured policy.

stages.action: Open `http://127.0.0.1:4436`, open the verification message, and follow its link.
stages.output: The address is verified and the browser redirects to `/welcome`.
stages.success_signal: The welcome dashboard opens.
stages.failure_or_wait: Refresh MailSlurper if the message is not initially visible.

stages.action: Open `http://127.0.0.1:4455/sessions` and inspect the session.
stages.output: The first Ory user and authenticated session are confirmed.
stages.success_signal: The session is active and the address has `verified: true` and a `verified_at` value.
stages.failure_or_wait: An inactive or unverified session is not success.

branches.condition: The developer wants managed SaaS.
branches.path: Choose Ory Network, then choose GitHub, Google, or work email on the public account form before creating a free developer project.
branches.effect_on_first_success: Adds an unresolved account-method and project transition before a Network-backed integration.

branches.condition: The developer chooses Next.js App Router.
branches.path: Choose a package manager, create a Next.js app, install `@ory/elements-react` and `@ory/nextjs`, add configuration, middleware, and self-service pages, run the app, and open `/auth/registration`.
branches.effect_on_first_success: Reaches the public registration page, but the guide does not document the final form-to-user transition.

frictionGates.description: Deployment, product, framework, and package-manager choices change the required journey and are not globally defaulted.
The intended Next.js first-user terminal requires an undocumented generated registration-form transition.
The Network route presents GitHub, Google, and work-email account methods, whose post-selection convergence is not documented on the public form.

excludedAfterSuccess: Login after the verified registration session, recovery, profile settings, social login, production deployment, external databases, and Ory Network occur after the first verified local user.
doesNotProve: Platform records reconstruct official documentation. They do not contain product telemetry, conversion rates, observed completion times, or proven causes of abandonment.
Friction gates are documented requirements or transitions. They are not confirmed drop-off points.
Blocker taxonomy entries are candidate hypotheses. Individual reasons remain not diagnosis eligible unless separate case evidence supports them.
A documented journey analysis is not a diagnosis of a real developer attempt.

developerGoal: Create an OVHcloud account and active Public Cloud project, launch a first public Linux instance, and obtain an authenticated remote shell.

entryPoint.developer_intent: Create an OVHcloud account and active Public Cloud project, launch a first public Linux instance, and obtain an authenticated remote shell.
assumed_prior_state: The developer has a valid and accessible email address.
The developer can provide accurate individual, company, or non-profit account details and accept the Public Cloud contract terms.
The developer can register and validate a payment method supported by the applicable OVHcloud regional entity.
The local device has a current OpenSSH client and can securely retain a private SSH key and optional passphrase.
The developer is authorized to start hourly or monthly billable compute and any default backup or networking resources included in the order summary.

selectedSurface.name: Create a public hourly Linux instance in the OVHcloud Control Panel and complete first SSH login
selectedSurface.selection_basis: Workshop selection policy (2026-07-19): when official docs present peer routes, commit to the fastest and most commonly used documented route. Prefer the vendor's first-listed or recommended quickstart, dashboard/console over CLI when both are peers, and the route with an explicit vendor time claim or fewest documented steps when tied. Selected route: Create a public hourly Linux instance in the OVHcloud Control Panel and complete first SSH login. The current getting-started guide presents this as its complete browser-first procedure and provides exact required SSH-key, wizard, status, username, address, and login steps. Prior surface before policy commit: Create an OVHcloud Public Cloud project, launch a Linux instance, and log in over SSH.

alternatives_considered.name: OVHcloud API instance creation
alternatives_considered.reason_not_primary: The API is a peer tab but requires OVHcloud API access plus separate flavor, image, region, and SSH-key identifiers before the create call.

alternatives_considered.name: OVHcloud CLI instance creation
alternatives_considered.reason_not_primary: The CLI is a peer tab that adds local installation, account credential configuration, project ID, region, and command parameters.

alternatives_considered.name: OpenStack CLI or Horizon
alternatives_considered.reason_not_primary: These interfaces add OpenStack environment or Horizon access. Horizon can create an instance without a key, but the official docs warn that connection then requires rescue mode.

alternatives_considered.name: Terraform
alternatives_considered.reason_not_primary: Terraform adds provider credentials and configuration, and the first Public Cloud project must still be created manually to accept terms and validate payment.

alternatives_considered.name: Windows instance and RDP
alternatives_considered.reason_not_primary: Windows is a fully documented branch but adds VNC-based first-boot locale and Administrator password setup before RDP. The Linux SSH path is the general key-based first connection.

firstMeaningfulSuccess.official_milestone: First login on an instance with a GNU/Linux OS installed
firstMeaningfulSuccess.normalized_outcome: OVHcloud reports the first Linux Public Cloud instance Enabled, and the developer has logged in through SSH as the image's preconfigured sudo user.
firstMeaningfulSuccess.observable_completion_signal: The instance table shows `Enabled`, and `ssh username@IPv4_instance` opens the remote command line using the private key corresponding to the public key selected at creation.

boundary_evidence.official_label_or_terminal_state: First login on an instance with a GNU/Linux OS installed

firstMeaningfulSuccess.why_this_is_the_boundary: The official guide names the connection section `First login`, states that the instance is accessible immediately after creation, and moves password setup, user management, and additional SSH keys into Step 6 after the login. Application installation is also outside the getting-started boundary.

prerequisites.requirement: A valid accessible email address, country of tax residence, currency, management language, unique strong password, and accurate account-type details.
Acceptance of the Public Cloud contract terms during first-project creation.
A valid registered and validated payment method is required to activate a discovery-mode project and create resources.
An active Public Cloud project in the OVHcloud account. Discovery mode permits browsing but not instance creation.
An RSA, ECDSA, or Ed25519 SSH key pair, with the public key available for instance creation and the private key retained locally.
A local OpenSSH-compatible client for the primary GNU/Linux connection route.

stages.action: Open Creating an OVHcloud account, follow its account-creation link, and select Create an account.
stages.output: The official regional account setup opens.
stages.success_signal: Country of tax residence, currency, and management-language fields are visible.
stages.failure_or_wait: An existing OVHcloud account holder can log in and skip to first-project creation.

stages.action: Select country of tax residence, currency, and management language, then confirm.
stages.input: The legal and billing locale for the account.
stages.output: The account is associated with an OVHcloud regional context and currency.
stages.success_signal: Email and password fields become available.
stages.failure_or_wait: The guide requires these three selections before it presents the email and password fields; it does not document a bypass.

stages.action: Enter a valid accessible email address and create a unique, sufficiently complex password, then submit the form.
stages.input: Email address and new OVHcloud account password.
stages.output: A one-time email validation code is sent.
stages.success_signal: The interface displays boxes for the code.
stages.failure_or_wait: An invalid or inaccessible email prevents validation. OVHcloud advises against using an address on a domain that this same account will manage.

stages.action: Retrieve the one-time email code and enter it in the account form.
stages.input: The code from the OVHcloud validation email.
stages.success_signal: The account-type selection appears.
stages.failure_or_wait: If the message is missing, check spam, request another code, or change the email address.

stages.action: Choose Non-profit, Company, or Private individual and complete the requested account details.
stages.input: Accurate identity or organization information for the selected account type.
stages.output: The OVHcloud account is created and receives a unique NIC handle.
stages.success_signal: The developer is automatically logged in to the account dashboard.
stages.failure_or_wait: OVHcloud defines who should use each account type and tells the user to make sure the entered details are correct.

stages.action: Open Public Cloud, read the Public Cloud contracts, tick the acceptance box, and select Discover the Public Cloud universe.
stages.input: An authenticated OVHcloud account and contract acceptance.
stages.output: The first Public Cloud project-creation process begins.
stages.success_signal: The project is created in discovery mode when no payment method is registered, or project naming and payment selection appear when one already exists.
stages.failure_or_wait: Failure to accept the contracts prevents the first project.

stages.action: For the new-account path without a registered payment method, allow the first project to be created in discovery mode and open its homepage.
stages.output: A browsable but non-deployable Public Cloud project exists.
stages.success_signal: The project is visible in the Public Cloud selector and presents Activate project.
stages.failure_or_wait: Discovery mode cannot create resources such as instances, so project presence alone is not deployable success.

stages.action: Create an Ed25519 SSH key pair, or use the documented RSA alternative, and protect the private key with a passphrase when practical.
stages.input: For example `ssh-keygen -t ed25519 -a 100`, an optional custom filename, and an optional recommended passphrase.
stages.output: A public key file and matching private key exist in the local `.ssh` directory.
stages.success_signal: The key generator prints the saved file paths and fingerprint.
stages.failure_or_wait: Losing or exposing the private key prevents or compromises future access. Existing key holders can skip generation.

stages.action: Display and copy the public-key string.
stages.input: For example `cat ~/.ssh/id_ed25519.pub` or the `.pub` path chosen in step 9.
stages.output: The shareable public key is on the clipboard or otherwise ready to paste.
stages.success_signal: The displayed line begins with a supported key type and includes the public-key material.
stages.failure_or_wait: Only the `.pub` content should be copied. The private key must not be uploaded or shared.

stages.action: Open the active project's Home page and select Create an instance.
stages.input: An active project with validated payment and an available SSH public key.
stages.success_signal: Name, location, model, image, SSH key, backup, network, billing, and advanced-setting controls are visible.
stages.failure_or_wait: Discovery mode cannot create resources, and the broader Public Cloud guide says regional quotas can limit the resources a project can start.

stages.action: Review the default automated backup and choose its 7- or 14-day rotation, or change the backup selection deliberately.
stages.input: The default-enabled backup configuration and a 7- or 14-day rotation.
stages.output: Backup behavior is included in the instance order.
stages.success_signal: The selected backup state and rotation appear in the summary.
stages.failure_or_wait: Because automated backups are enabled by default, overlooking this section can preserve a backup and rotation choice the developer did not intend.

stages.action: Use Public Mode for the direct first SSH route, leave private-network selection empty, enable public connectivity, and choose Basic Public IP.
stages.input: No private network, public-connectivity toggle enabled, and the free Basic Public IP tied to the instance lifetime.
stages.output: The instance will be directly reachable over public IPv4 or IPv6.
stages.success_signal: Basic Public IP is present in the network summary without a gateway dependency.
stages.failure_or_wait: Private Mode requires a gateway or Load Balancer plus Floating IP. A reusable Floating IP is a separate configuration branch.

stages.action: Choose hourly billing for a flexible first evaluation and leave advanced settings unchanged unless a flexible instance or post-installation script is required.
stages.input: Hourly rather than monthly billing, one instance, and default advanced settings.
stages.output: The instance can later convert to monthly billing and will accrue hourly charges until deletion.
stages.success_signal: Hourly billing and the quantity are visible in the order summary.
stages.failure_or_wait: Monthly billing is lower-cost but non-reversible. Advanced settings can switch to a flexible instance with one 50 GB disk or add a post-installation script.

stages.action: Review the full order summary and select Launch my instance.
stages.input: Name, location, model, Linux image, SSH key, backup, public network, billing mode, quantity, and any advanced settings.
stages.success_signal: The instance appears in the Instances table with a provisioning status.
stages.failure_or_wait: Regional quota limits can prevent the requested resources from starting. The current instance guide says delivery may take a few minutes.

stages.action: Provision and boot the instance until the Instances table reports `Enabled`.
stages.output: A running Linux instance with public IP and the selected public key installed.
stages.success_signal: Status is `Enabled`; if another status remains after recent creation, the developer can select Refresh.
stages.failure_or_wait: Delivery may take a few minutes. A separate 2024 overview uses billing-specific status terminology: billing starts at `ACTIVE`, displayed as `Activated`, rather than during `BUILD`; the current readiness guide uses `Enabled` as the connection-ready status.

stages.action: Open the instance Dashboard, verify the image-derived preconfigured username in Networks, and retrieve the public IP address shown for the instance or through the documented OVHcloud CLI list command.
stages.output: The exact login username and network address are available.
stages.success_signal: The Dashboard shows the image-specific user, such as `ubuntu`, `debian`, or `fedora`, and the instance's public IP is available for the SSH command.
stages.failure_or_wait: Assuming `root` or another username can cause authentication failure; the guide directs the user to verify the Dashboard value.

stages.action: Run `ssh username@IPv4_instance`, specifying the private-key path when needed and entering its passphrase if configured.
stages.input: The Dashboard username, public IPv4, matching local private key, and optional key passphrase.
stages.output: An authenticated command-line session on the first OVHcloud instance.
stages.success_signal: The remote shell prompt opens, completing the guide's First login milestone.
stages.failure_or_wait: For key errors, verify the local private key and selected public key. A lost or omitted key requires the documented rescue-mode key-replacement route. VNC on a new Linux instance also requires first setting a password after SSH login.

stages.action: Open Creating an OVHcloud account, follow its account-creation link, and select Create an account.
stages.output: The official regional account setup opens.
stages.success_signal: Country of tax residence, currency, and management-language fields are visible.
stages.failure_or_wait: An existing OVHcloud account holder can log in and skip to first-project creation.

stages.action: Select country of tax residence, currency, and management language, then confirm.
stages.input: The legal and billing locale for the account.
stages.output: The account is associated with an OVHcloud regional context and currency.
stages.success_signal: Email and password fields become available.
stages.failure_or_wait: The guide requires these three selections before it presents the email and password fields; it does not document a bypass.

stages.action: Enter a valid accessible email address and create a unique, sufficiently complex password, then submit the form.
stages.input: Email address and new OVHcloud account password.
stages.output: A one-time email validation code is sent.
stages.success_signal: The interface displays boxes for the code.
stages.failure_or_wait: An invalid or inaccessible email prevents validation. OVHcloud advises against using an address on a domain that this same account will manage.

stages.action: Retrieve the one-time email code and enter it in the account form.
stages.input: The code from the OVHcloud validation email.
stages.success_signal: The account-type selection appears.
stages.failure_or_wait: If the message is missing, check spam, request another code, or change the email address.

stages.action: Choose Non-profit, Company, or Private individual and complete the requested account details.
stages.input: Accurate identity or organization information for the selected account type.
stages.output: The OVHcloud account is created and receives a unique NIC handle.
stages.success_signal: The developer is automatically logged in to the account dashboard.
stages.failure_or_wait: OVHcloud defines who should use each account type and tells the user to make sure the entered details are correct.

stages.action: Open Public Cloud, read the Public Cloud contracts, tick the acceptance box, and select Discover the Public Cloud universe.
stages.input: An authenticated OVHcloud account and contract acceptance.
stages.output: The first Public Cloud project-creation process begins.
stages.success_signal: The project is created in discovery mode when no payment method is registered, or project naming and payment selection appear when one already exists.
stages.failure_or_wait: Failure to accept the contracts prevents the first project.

stages.action: For the new-account path without a registered payment method, allow the first project to be created in discovery mode and open its homepage.
stages.output: A browsable but non-deployable Public Cloud project exists.
stages.success_signal: The project is visible in the Public Cloud selector and presents Activate project.
stages.failure_or_wait: Discovery mode cannot create resources such as instances, so project presence alone is not deployable success.

stages.action: Select Activate project, register a payment method, and name the project if prompted.
stages.input: Project name and a payment method, plus any billing, contact, terms, privacy, and payment confirmation fields shown for the applicable regional order path.
stages.output: The Public Cloud project is active and can create resources.
stages.success_signal: The project exits discovery mode and the product creation controls are enabled.
stages.failure_or_wait: Without a registered payment method, the project remains in discovery mode and cannot create resources. OVHcloud's US guide separately documents a $0.99 first-project anti-fraud charge credited to the first bill.

stages.action: Create an Ed25519 SSH key pair, or use the documented RSA alternative, and protect the private key with a passphrase when practical.
stages.input: For example `ssh-keygen -t ed25519 -a 100`, an optional custom filename, and an optional recommended passphrase.
stages.output: A public key file and matching private key exist in the local `.ssh` directory.
stages.success_signal: The key generator prints the saved file paths and fingerprint.
stages.failure_or_wait: Losing or exposing the private key prevents or compromises future access. Existing key holders can skip generation.

stages.action: Display and copy the public-key string.
stages.input: For example `cat ~/.ssh/id_ed25519.pub` or the `.pub` path chosen in step 9.
stages.output: The shareable public key is on the clipboard or otherwise ready to paste.
stages.success_signal: The displayed line begins with a supported key type and includes the public-key material.
stages.failure_or_wait: Only the `.pub` content should be copied. The private key must not be uploaded or shared.

stages.action: Within the active Public Cloud project, open SSH Keys under Settings, select Add an SSH key, name it, paste the public-key string, and select Add.
stages.input: A key name and RSA, ECDSA, or Ed25519 public key.
stages.output: The SSH public key is stored for all Public Cloud regions and selectable during instance creation.
stages.success_signal: The key appears in the project's SSH Keys list.
stages.failure_or_wait: Import is optional because a key can be pasted in the instance wizard, but a public key is mandatory for Control Panel Linux instance creation.

stages.action: Open the active project's Home page and select Create an instance.
stages.input: An active project with validated payment and an available SSH public key.
stages.success_signal: Name, location, model, image, SSH key, backup, network, billing, and advanced-setting controls are visible.
stages.failure_or_wait: Discovery mode cannot create resources, and the broader Public Cloud guide says regional quotas can limit the resources a project can start.

stages.action: Enter the instance name and select a location close to the intended users.
stages.input: A full instance name and an available 3-AZ, 1-AZ, or Local Zone location.
stages.output: The resource identity and deployment locality are set.
stages.success_signal: The wizard displays the location-compatible models and images.
stages.failure_or_wait: For Local Zones, private connectivity is limited to instances in the same Local Zone and is incompatible with vRack.

stages.action: Select an Instance Model suited to the first evaluation.
stages.input: A displayed Best Sellers, General Purpose, Compute Optimized, Memory Optimized, GPU, Discovery, Storage Optimized, or Metal flavor.
stages.output: Processor, memory, storage, and model capabilities are assigned.
stages.success_signal: The selected model appears in the order summary.
stages.failure_or_wait: Initial project quotas can limit model availability. Regular instances can be upgraded but not downgraded; a flexible instance is the documented resize exception.

stages.action: Select a GNU/Linux Distribution Type and Image Version, then select the stored SSH key.
stages.input: A location- and model-compatible Linux image plus the public key imported in step 11.
stages.output: OVHcloud will install the image and its preconfigured sudo user with the selected public key.
stages.success_signal: The image and SSH key appear in the summary.
stages.failure_or_wait: The preconfigured username depends on the image, such as `ubuntu`, `debian`, or `fedora`. Omitting the key is not permitted in the Control Panel Linux path.

stages.action: Review the default automated backup and choose its 7- or 14-day rotation, or change the backup selection deliberately.
stages.input: The default-enabled backup configuration and a 7- or 14-day rotation.
stages.output: Backup behavior is included in the instance order.
stages.success_signal: The selected backup state and rotation appear in the summary.
stages.failure_or_wait: Because automated backups are enabled by default, overlooking this section can preserve a backup and rotation choice the developer did not intend.

stages.action: Use Public Mode for the direct first SSH route, leave private-network selection empty, enable public connectivity, and choose Basic Public IP.
stages.input: No private network, public-connectivity toggle enabled, and the free Basic Public IP tied to the instance lifetime.
stages.output: The instance will be directly reachable over public IPv4 or IPv6.
stages.success_signal: Basic Public IP is present in the network summary without a gateway dependency.
stages.failure_or_wait: Private Mode requires a gateway or Load Balancer plus Floating IP. A reusable Floating IP is a separate configuration branch.

stages.output: The instance can later convert to monthly billing and will accrue hourly charges until deletion.

stages.success_signal: The instance appears in the Instances table with a provisioning status.
stages.failure_or_wait: Regional quota limits can prevent the requested resources from starting. The current instance guide says delivery may take a few minutes.

stages.output: A running Linux instance with public IP and the selected public key installed.
stages.success_signal: Status is `Enabled`; if another status remains after recent creation, the developer can select Refresh.
stages.failure_or_wait: Delivery may take a few minutes. A separate 2024 overview uses billing-specific status terminology: billing starts at `ACTIVE`, displayed as `Activated`, rather than during `BUILD`; the current readiness guide uses `Enabled` as the connection-ready status.

stages.output: The exact login username and network address are available.
stages.success_signal: The Dashboard shows the image-specific user, such as `ubuntu`, `debian`, or `fedora`, and the instance's public IP is available for the SSH command.
stages.failure_or_wait: Assuming `root` or another username can cause authentication failure; the guide directs the user to verify the Dashboard value.
