developerGoal: Create a free managed Redis database and connect to it using the official new-user path.

entryPoint.developer_intent: Create a free managed Redis database and connect to it using the official new-user path.
assumed_prior_state: Discovery is complete and the developer has selected Redis.
The developer does not yet have a Redis Cloud account or database.

selectedSurface.name: Redis Cloud quick start
selectedSurface.selection_basis: Redis calls Redis Cloud the fastest deployment option, tells new users to start with the Redis Cloud quick start, and says the quick start gets a new user up and running by creating a free database and connecting to it.

alternatives_considered.name: Redis Open Source installation routes
alternatives_considered.reason_not_primary: The general page presents multiple operating-system and container installation routes, while explicitly calling Redis Cloud the fastest deployment option and marking its quick start as the place to start if new.

alternatives_considered.name: Redis Enterprise Software and Kubernetes quick starts
alternatives_considered.reason_not_primary: These are separate self-managed deployment surfaces; the official general page recommends the Redis Cloud quick start to a new user.

alternatives_considered.name: Use-case quick starts
alternatives_considered.reason_not_primary: Data structure, document, vector, agent, and RAG guides begin after the deployment and connection milestone selected by the general documentation.

firstMeaningfulSuccess.official_milestone: Connect to your database
firstMeaningfulSuccess.normalized_outcome: A free Redis Cloud database is active and has been opened through one of the quick start's documented connection methods.
firstMeaningfulSuccess.observable_completion_signal: On the first fully specified route for the free Essentials database, selecting Launch Redis Insight web opens Redis Insight in a new browser tab for that database.

boundary_evidence.official_label_or_terminal_state: The quick start's stated second learning outcome is Connect to your database, and its browser route ends when Redis Insight opens in a new tab.

firstMeaningfulSuccess.why_this_is_the_boundary: It is the last stated learning outcome of the selected quick start. Loading sample data, using Insights, executing use-case tutorials, importing data, and database operations appear as optional or later material.

prerequisites.requirement: An email address, or a Google or GitHub identity, is required to create and activate a Redis Cloud account.
No local software is required for the browser Redis Insight route. The desktop, redis-cli, and client-library branches add their own software prerequisites.
The selected path creates a Free 30 MB Essentials database; the quick start does not document a payment-card requirement for this path.

stages.action: Open the Redis getting-started documentation and follow Redis Cloud quick start.
details: Redis Cloud is described as the fastest way to deploy Redis.
The general page marks this quick start with Start here if you're new.
stages.success_signal: The page lists Create an account and a free database, then Connect to your database.
stages.failure_or_wait: No wait is documented.

stages.action: Open the Redis Cloud Sign up page.
stages.success_signal: The form and social signup options are visible.
stages.failure_or_wait: No wait is documented before form submission.

stages.action: Create the account with the form or sign up with Google or GitHub.
details: For the form route, enter the requested information and select Get Started.
stages.success_signal: Redis sends an activation email.
stages.failure_or_wait: The account remains unavailable until the activation email arrives and is used.

stages.action: Open the Redis activation email and select Activate account.
stages.output: Activated Redis Cloud account and Create your database page
stages.success_signal: The Redis Cloud console opens the database-creation page.
stages.failure_or_wait: Missing or delayed email blocks activation; the quick start does not state a delivery time or retry process.

stages.action: Keep the Free plan selected on the Create database page.
details: The guide routes existing account holders to an Essentials database guide instead.
Flex, other Essentials, and Pro databases are separate branches.
stages.success_signal: Free is selected.
stages.failure_or_wait: Selecting another database type changes the documented path.

stages.action: Accept the generated database name or change the Database name field.
stages.success_signal: A database name is present.
stages.failure_or_wait: No failure is documented.

stages.action: Select the Database version.
stages.success_signal: A database version is selected.
stages.failure_or_wait: The quick start does not prescribe one version.

stages.action: Choose a Cloud vendor and Region.
details: The cloud-vendor choices are AWS, Google Cloud, and Microsoft Azure.
The guide links a supported-regions reference.
stages.success_signal: Both fields are selected.
stages.failure_or_wait: Available regions depend on the cloud vendor.

stages.action: Select Create database.
stages.success_signal: The Database details page appears.
stages.failure_or_wait: Redis documents a brief processing pause before the details page appears.

stages.action: Wait for the database status to change from pending to active.
details: An orange clock means pending.
A green circle containing a checkmark means active.
stages.success_signal: The status indicator becomes a green circle with a checkmark.
stages.failure_or_wait: The guide states only that the pause is brief and supplies no retry action.

stages.action: In Configuration details, go to Security and reveal the Default user password.
details: The default username is default.
Select Default user, then Configure, then the eye icon to view the masked password.
stages.success_signal: The default username and password are available for connection.
stages.failure_or_wait: Without the credentials, non-browser connection methods cannot authenticate.

stages.action: Select Connect to open the connection wizard.
details: The wizard offers Redis Insight, redis-cli, and Redis client methods.
stages.success_signal: The connection methods are displayed.
stages.failure_or_wait: No wait is documented.

stages.action: Under Redis Insight, select Launch Redis Insight web.
details: The browser route is available only for Essentials databases, which includes the free database created by this path.
The same launch action is also available from the database page under View and manage data with Redis Insight.
stages.input: Connection wizard for the free Essentials database
stages.success_signal: Redis Insight opens in a new tab for the database.
stages.failure_or_wait: Other database types require installing Redis Insight on a computer.

branches.condition: The developer uses the form instead of Google or GitHub.
branches.path: Enter the requested information and select Get Started; then continue through the required email activation.
branches.effect_on_first_success: Adds manual form entry but reaches the same database-creation page.

branches.condition: The developer already has an account, already has a database, or wants Flex, paid Essentials, or Pro.
branches.path: Use the linked Essentials, database-management, Flex, or Pro guide instead of the new-account Free plan flow.
branches.effect_on_first_success: Changes provisioning, billing, and possibly browser Redis Insight availability, so it is outside the selected new-user path.

branches.condition: The developer uses Redis Insight desktop.
branches.path: In the connection wizard select Download, install Redis Insight for Windows, macOS, or Linux, select Open in desktop, then approve the browser pop-up by selecting Open Redis Insight. The application connects to the database.
branches.effect_on_first_success: Adds a local download, operating-system installation, link-handler prompt, and desktop application before the same connection milestone.

branches.condition: The developer uses a Redis client library.
branches.path: Choose one of the connection-wizard snippets for .NET with StackExchange.Redis or NRedisStack, Node.js with node-redis, Python with redis-py, Java with Jedis or Lettuce, Go with go-redis, or PHP with Predis. Follow that client's own installation and syntax documentation, then use the generated connection information.
branches.effect_on_first_success: Adds a language and client choice plus client-specific installation and execution. The quick start does not demonstrate one universal terminal output for these clients.

branches.condition: The developer uses redis-cli.
branches.path: Install Redis, build redis-cli from source, or run redis-cli in Docker; use the connection details from Redis Cloud. The CLI documentation shows host and port flags or a redis or rediss URI, recommends REDISCLI_AUTH for passwords, and demonstrates PING returning PONG as a connected command result.
branches.effect_on_first_success: Adds installation and command-line credential handling before a PONG connection signal.

frictionGates.description: A new account requires form or social signup followed by activation through email.
The developer must choose a database version, cloud vendor, and supported region; no defaults are prescribed by the quick start.
Database creation has a processing pause and pending state before the active checkmark appears.
Database connection credentials are protected behind Default user, Configure, and password-reveal controls.
The connection wizard offers GUI, CLI, and multiple language-client routes with different installation and execution requirements.

uncertainties.question: Does Redis intend one connection method as the canonical default?
uncertainties.impact: The browser, desktop, CLI, and client routes have different developer actions and friction.
uncertainties.reason_unresolved: The quick start lists methods and fully specifies the eligible browser route first, but does not label any method as recommended or default.

sources.title: Open Source: Get started with Redis Open Source
Redis Cloud quick start
evidence_supported: Complete ordered account and free-database path

sources.title: Redis CLI
evidence_supported: Remote host, port, password, URI, and TLS connection options

excludedAfterSuccess: Loading sample data in Redis Insight
Executing data-structure, document, vector, RAG, or AI use-case guides
Managing, securing, backing up, or monitoring the database
doesNotProve: Platform records reconstruct official documentation. They do not contain product telemetry, conversion rates, observed completion times, or proven causes of abandonment.
Friction gates are documented requirements or transitions. They are not confirmed drop-off points.
Blocker taxonomy entries are candidate hypotheses. Individual reasons remain not diagnosis eligible unless separate case evidence supports them.
A documented journey analysis is not a diagnosis of a real developer attempt.

developerGoal: Sign up for Render, connect a Git provider, deploy a server-side Web Service from a repository, and open the Live onrender.com URL.

entryPoint.developer_intent: Sign up for Render, connect a Git provider, deploy a server-side Web Service from a repository, and open the Live onrender.com URL.
assumed_prior_state: A GitHub, GitLab, or Bitbucket repository already contains the web app to deploy.
No Render account or Live Web Service is assumed.
No Render CLI install is assumed for the Dashboard path.

selectedSurface.name: Your First Render Deploy, Dashboard Web Service
selectedSurface.selection_basis: Render's Your First Render Deploy tutorial is the official first-app path. It documents free resources with no payment required, and the Dashboard tab is the first deployment method shown for creating a Web Service from a connected Git repo and opening the Live onrender.com URL.

alternatives_considered.name: Static Site on the same tutorial
alternatives_considered.reason_not_primary: Peer service type for fully static apps; this reconstruction uses the Web Service path for server-side apps.

alternatives_considered.name: Render CLI deploy tab
alternatives_considered.reason_not_primary: Peer deployment method on the same page; requires installing the CLI before first deploy.

alternatives_considered.name: Language/framework quickstarts
alternatives_considered.reason_not_primary: Example-app variants after the general first-deploy tutorial.

firstMeaningfulSuccess.official_milestone: Your first deploy is Live and the app opens at its onrender.com URL
firstMeaningfulSuccess.normalized_outcome: The developer's Web Service deploy is Live and serves its root content from the assigned onrender.com URL.
firstMeaningfulSuccess.observable_completion_signal: The service shows Live status and opening the onrender.com URL returns the deployed app's root-path content.

boundary_evidence.official_label_or_terminal_state: Your First Render Deploy walks signup, Git connection, Web Service creation, Deploy, then opening the deployed app; free resources require no payment.

firstMeaningfulSuccess.why_this_is_the_boundary: The tutorial's documented terminal state is a successful first deploy that the developer can open. Signup, Git authorization, or form submission alone are not first success. Service-creation form fields are one configuration step with required details, not separate path steps.

prerequisites.requirement: A GitHub, GitLab, or Bitbucket account with a repository containing the web app; this route uses GitHub.
Permission to authorize Render to access the selected repositories.
A server-side web app that listens for HTTP requests and binds its public HTTP server to 0.0.0.0 on the port Render provides.
A payment method is not required for the tutorial Free instance path.

stages.action: Open Your First Render Deploy and follow the Dashboard deployment path for a Web Service.
details: The tutorial states it uses free Render resources and no payment is required.
stages.success_signal: Signup, Git connection, and Deploy sections are visible.
stages.failure_or_wait: No navigation recovery is documented.

stages.action: Sign up for a free Render account.
stages.success_signal: The Dashboard and Account Settings are reachable.
stages.failure_or_wait: Follow the displayed account recovery path.

stages.action: Connect a Git provider under Account Settings → Git Deployment Credentials.
details: Add credential and authorize GitHub (or GitLab/Bitbucket).
Grant access to the repositories needed for deploy.
stages.success_signal: The provider appears under Git Deployment Credentials.
stages.failure_or_wait: Re-authorize if repository access is missing.

stages.action: Create a Web Service from the connected Git repository.
details: In the Dashboard, click New and select Web Service.
Select the repository and click Connect.
Complete the service form: Name, Region, Branch, Language/runtime, Build Command, Start Command, Free instance type, and any required environment variables.
stages.input: Connected Git provider and app repository
stages.output: Configured Web Service ready to deploy
stages.success_signal: The Deploy action is available on the completed form.
stages.failure_or_wait: Correct runtime, build, and start commands if validation blocks submit.

stages.action: Click Deploy to create the Web Service and start the first build.
stages.success_signal: Deploy logs are visible for the new service.
stages.failure_or_wait: Inspect build logs if the deploy fails.

stages.action: Wait until the deploy is Live, then open the service's onrender.com URL and confirm the app's root content.
details: Free web services can spin down after inactivity and spin back up on the next request.
stages.output: Verified Live Web Service at its public URL
stages.success_signal: The service is Live and the onrender.com URL serves the root-path content.
stages.failure_or_wait: Wait for build/start completion; fix build or start command failures from logs.

branches.condition: The app is fully static HTML/CSS/JS.
branches.path: Create a Static Site instead of a Web Service and set Build Command plus Publish Directory.
branches.effect_on_first_success: Same Live URL terminal with static-site fields.

branches.condition: The developer prefers the CLI tab.
branches.path: Install the Render CLI, run render login, then render services create with the documented flags including --plan free.
branches.effect_on_first_success: Adds CLI installation before the same Live deploy boundary.

frictionGates.description: A Render account is required before Dashboard deploy actions.
The developer must authorize Render to access the selected Git repositories.
The tutorial requires choosing Web Service or Static Site, then completing service-specific form fields.
Build command, start command, runtime, and any required environment variables must match the application.
The first build and deploy must finish before the service is Live.
Free web services spin down after 15 minutes of inactivity and spin back up on the next request.

uncertainties.question: Exact interactive signup fields on dashboard.render.com/register.
uncertainties.impact: Could add undocumented form fields before the first Dashboard session.
uncertainties.reason_unresolved: The tutorial says signup is fast and free without publishing the interactive field contract.

excludedAfterSuccess: Custom domains and TLS beyond the default onrender.com URL
Background Workers, Cron Jobs, and other service types
doesNotProve: Platform records reconstruct official documentation. They do not contain product telemetry, conversion rates, observed completion times, or proven causes of abandonment.
Friction gates are documented requirements or transitions. They are not confirmed drop-off points.
Blocker taxonomy entries are candidate hypotheses. Individual reasons remain not diagnosis eligible unless separate case evidence supports them.
A documented journey analysis is not a diagnosis of a real developer attempt.

developerGoal: Create a Replicate account, purchase prepaid credit, create an API token, and receive a successful prediction JSON result over HTTPS.

entryPoint.developer_intent: Create a Replicate account, purchase prepaid credit, create an API token, and receive a successful prediction JSON result over HTTPS.
assumed_prior_state: No Replicate account, API token, prepaid credit, or prediction is assumed.
A terminal with cURL is available; no language SDK is assumed.

selectedSurface.name: HTTP predictions API via cURL (API token + prepaid credit)
selectedSurface.selection_basis: Replicate's Create a prediction guide documents cURL examples with Prefer: wait against https://api.replicate.com/v1/predictions and Authorization: Bearer $REPLICATE_API_TOKEN. Prepaid credit docs state you must first purchase credit upfront before usage. An SDK install is not required.

alternatives_considered.name: Node.js get-started scaffold
alternatives_considered.reason_not_primary: Local Node.js package and project setup; the HTTP API documents the same predictions call with cURL.

alternatives_considered.name: Python get-started
alternatives_considered.reason_not_primary: Peer SDK route; not required when official HTTP docs show cURL.

alternatives_considered.name: Google Colab get-started
alternatives_considered.reason_not_primary: Notebook path with additional Google Colab prerequisites.

firstMeaningfulSuccess.official_milestone: Sync-mode prediction with populated output
firstMeaningfulSuccess.normalized_outcome: An authenticated cURL POST to https://api.replicate.com/v1/predictions with Prefer: wait returns a prediction whose status is successful and whose output field is populated.
firstMeaningfulSuccess.observable_completion_signal: The prediction JSON includes status successful (or equivalent terminal success) and a non-null output for the requested model input.

boundary_evidence.official_label_or_terminal_state: Create a prediction documents Prefer: wait cURL to /v1/predictions and shows a response with output populated and status successful for replicate/hello-world.

firstMeaningfulSuccess.why_this_is_the_boundary: The first meaningful hosted model success is a completed prediction with output. cURL is the documented HTTP path. Prepaid credit must be purchased upfront per billing docs before usage can proceed.

prerequisites.requirement: A Replicate account that can open billing settings and API tokens.
Prepaid credit purchased with a payment method. Prepaid credit docs state you must first purchase credit upfront; usage deducts from the balance.
An API token from the account API tokens page, used as Authorization: Bearer.
A terminal with cURL (or any HTTP client). No language SDK is required.

stages.action: Create or sign in to a Replicate account.
details: API tokens and billing require an authenticated session (GitHub sign-in is documented on the tokens page).
stages.input: Intent to run models on Replicate
stages.success_signal: Billing settings and API tokens are reachable.
stages.failure_or_wait: Account recovery follows the sign-in flow.

stages.action: Add a payment method and purchase prepaid credit.
details: Prepaid credit docs: add a payment method in billing settings, then Add credit.
Purchased credit is valid for 1 year and is not refundable.
When the balance hits zero, new work is prevented.
stages.success_signal: Billing shows a usable credit balance.
stages.failure_or_wait: Without prepaid credit, predictions cannot start under the prepaid model.

stages.action: Create or copy an API token and store it securely.
details: Export as REPLICATE_API_TOKEN for the documented cURL Authorization header.
stages.success_signal: The token value is available locally.
stages.failure_or_wait: No manual approval wait is documented for token creation.

stages.action: Export REPLICATE_API_TOKEN in the terminal.
stages.output: Token available to HTTP clients in the shell
stages.success_signal: The environment variable is set for the current shell.
stages.failure_or_wait: Shell persistence depends on the OS.

stages.action: POST a sync prediction with cURL to https://api.replicate.com/v1/predictions.
details: Include Prefer: wait, Authorization: Bearer $REPLICATE_API_TOKEN, and Content-Type: application/json.
Send version and input as in the Create a prediction cURL example (for example replicate/hello-world).
stages.success_signal: The response includes a prediction object with populated output.
stages.failure_or_wait: Zero credit, invalid token, or long-running models may return incomplete status requiring poll via urls.get.

stages.action: Confirm the prediction JSON shows successful status and non-null output.
stages.success_signal: output is populated and status is successful.
stages.failure_or_wait: error status or null output means the first call did not succeed.

branches.condition: The developer prefers an official client SDK.
branches.path: Use the JavaScript or Python client to create the same prediction; token and prepaid credit requirements stay the same.
branches.effect_on_first_success: Adds optional package installation without changing the prediction boundary.

branches.condition: Sync wait times out before completion.
branches.path: Poll GET urls.get until status is successful or failed.
branches.effect_on_first_success: Adds polling wait without changing credentials or billing gates.

frictionGates.description: Prepaid credit must be purchased with a payment method before usage under the prepaid model.
A Bearer API token is required on every predictions request.

uncertainties.question: Whether any remaining free-limit or legacy arrears accounts can complete a first prediction without prepaid purchase.
uncertainties.impact: Billing step ordering could differ for rare account states.
uncertainties.reason_unresolved: Billing still mentions free limits and arrears accounts while prepaid credit says purchase is required to use Replicate.

sources.title: Prepaid credit
sections_used: What happens when you run out of credit

sources.title: Billing
evidence_supported: Select models free briefly then billing setup

doesNotProve: Platform records reconstruct official documentation. They do not contain product telemetry, conversion rates, observed completion times, or proven causes of abandonment.
Friction gates are documented requirements or transitions. They are not confirmed drop-off points.
Blocker taxonomy entries are candidate hypotheses. Individual reasons remain not diagnosis eligible unless separate case evidence supports them.
A documented journey analysis is not a diagnosis of a real developer attempt.

developerGoal: Set up Resend and send the first transactional email after selecting the platform.

entryPoint.developer_intent: Set up Resend and send the first transactional email after selecting the platform.
assumed_prior_state: Discovery is complete and the developer has selected Resend.
The developer has not selected a language, framework, CLI, or AI-tool route.
The developer does not yet have a Resend account, key, or verified sending domain.

selectedSurface.name: Node.js SDK first transactional email
selectedSurface.selection_basis: Workshop selection policy (2026-07-19): when official docs present peer routes, commit to the fastest and most commonly used documented route. Prefer the vendor's first-listed or recommended quickstart, dashboard/console over CLI when both are peers, and the route with an explicit vendor time claim or fewest documented steps when tied. Selected route: Node.js SDK first transactional email. The Node.js quickstart is a complete officially documented implementation of Resend's named first-email milestone. It is a representative candidate, not a default over the other thirteen language, framework, and CLI quickstarts. Prior surface before policy commit: Resend Get started setup plus quickstart selector.

alternatives_considered.name: Node.js Quickstart
alternatives_considered.reason_not_primary: It is the first listed implementation and is fully demonstrated, but Resend does not label it the default over Next.js, Express, PHP, Laravel, Python, Ruby, Rails, Go, Rust, Elixir, Java, .NET, or CLI.

alternatives_considered.name: CLI first email
alternatives_considered.reason_not_primary: The CLI is a separate top-level quickstart and AI Onboarding demonstrates a send command, but it is not selected over SDK routes.

alternatives_considered.name: AI Onboarding
alternatives_considered.reason_not_primary: It provides MCP, CLI, agent documentation, skills, and AI-builder routes after a human creates the account and key, adding a second route-selection surface.

alternatives_considered.name: Marketing Broadcast
alternatives_considered.reason_not_primary: It is a separate one-to-many marketing outcome; quickstart tutorials explicitly target the first transactional email.

firstMeaningfulSuccess.official_milestone: Send your first transactional email
firstMeaningfulSuccess.normalized_outcome: After common key and domain setup, the developer uses a selected Resend quickstart to send a transactional email and receives that route's success response.
firstMeaningfulSuccess.observable_completion_signal: The fully inspected Node.js route logs the data returned by resend.emails.send after sending Hello World from onboarding@resend.dev to delivered@resend.dev; an error is logged instead if the send fails.

boundary_evidence.official_label_or_terminal_state: The Resend SDK returns and logs data for the Hello World send to delivered@resend.dev.

firstMeaningfulSuccess.why_this_is_the_boundary: Terminal taken from the committed official getting-started route under workshop fastest/most-used policy.

prerequisites.requirement: A human-created Resend account is required before creating an API key or verifying a domain.
The developer must own a domain and be able to update its DNS; Resend requires an associated verified domain before sending or receiving email.
At least one confidential Resend API key is required for code, API, CLI, or AI-tool access.
Software varies by selected quickstart. The inspected Node.js route requires a Node project and npm, yarn, pnpm, or bun; CLI and other SDK routes have different prerequisites.
The sending overview says developers can start with Resend's free plan; no payment-card step is documented in the common setup path.

stages.action: Open the Resend Introduction and follow the Get started sequence.
details: The page requires a verified owned domain and API key before sending.
It distinguishes transactional email from marketing Broadcasts.
stages.input: Intent to start sending with Resend
stages.output: Common account, key, and domain setup
stages.success_signal: Create an API key and Add a domain are visible before the quickstart list.
stages.failure_or_wait: No wait occurs at documentation arrival.

stages.action: Create or sign in to a Resend account as a human.
details: Resend's AI onboarding explicitly says a human must create the account.
stages.success_signal: API keys and Domains pages are accessible.
stages.failure_or_wait: The public docs do not enumerate signup fields, identity-provider options, verification, or team onboarding.

stages.action: Open the Dashboard API keys page and select Create API Key.
details: API, CLI, and MCP key-creation methods also exist, but the guide documents Dashboard actions.
stages.success_signal: Key configuration fields are visible.
stages.failure_or_wait: No wait is documented.

stages.action: Enter a descriptive API-key name of no more than 50 characters.
stages.success_signal: The name is accepted.
stages.failure_or_wait: Names over 50 characters violate the documented limit.

stages.action: Select Sending access unless full API access is required.
details: Sending access limits the key to sending email.
Full access permits creating, deleting, getting, and updating any API resource; permission can be updated later.
stages.success_signal: A permission is selected.
stages.failure_or_wait: An insufficient scope can block later non-sending actions, while full access is broader than required for the first send.

stages.action: Optionally restrict a Sending access key to a specific domain, then create it.
details: Domain restriction is available only with Sending access.
stages.success_signal: The re_ key value is displayed.
stages.failure_or_wait: The secret can only be viewed once.

stages.action: Copy the one-time API key into secure storage.
details: Do not commit it to version control; project .env files may need to be added to .gitignore.
stages.success_signal: The key is available for the selected quickstart.
stages.failure_or_wait: Leaving without saving loses the displayed secret.

stages.action: Enter an owned domain, preferably a purpose-specific subdomain.
details: Resend strongly recommends a subdomain such as notifications.example.com rather than the root domain.
Each subdomain must be configured and verified separately.
stages.input: Owned root domain and sending-purpose subdomain
stages.success_signal: The domain is accepted.
stages.failure_or_wait: A domain the developer does not control cannot be verified.

stages.action: Choose the sending region closest to most recipients.
stages.success_signal: A region is selected.
stages.failure_or_wait: No default region is prescribed.

stages.action: Optionally set a custom Return-Path subdomain; otherwise keep the send.example.com default.
stages.success_signal: The domain can be added with default or custom Return-Path.
stages.failure_or_wait: No failure is documented.

stages.action: Copy the generated DKIM and SPF TXT and MX records into the DNS provider.
details: Use the Records tab to obtain the exact values.
Copy and paste them exactly to avoid configuration errors.
Provider-specific DNS guides are available.
stages.input: Resend-generated DNS records and DNS-provider access
stages.success_signal: The records are publicly visible, which can be checked with dns.email.
stages.failure_or_wait: Incorrect names, types, or values prevent verification.

stages.action: Wait for Resend to verify the domain.
details: Verification often completes within 15 minutes.
DNS propagation can take up to 72 hours globally.
stages.success_signal: The domain becomes verified in the Dashboard.
stages.failure_or_wait: If still unverified after 72 hours, select Restart verification to trigger a new check.

stages.action: In the Node.js project, install the resend package with npm install resend.
details: The guide also offers yarn, pnpm, and bun commands; npm is selected for this candidate.
stages.success_signal: resend is present in the project dependencies.
stages.failure_or_wait: Package, network, lockfile, or permission errors must be corrected before the SDK can be imported.

stages.action: Create the project's .env file and set RESEND_API_KEY to the one-time re_ key saved in the shared path.
details: Ensure the runtime loads the environment file and do not commit it to version control.
stages.success_signal: process.env.RESEND_API_KEY resolves to the intended active key.
stages.failure_or_wait: A missing, truncated, revoked, wrong-scope, domain-restricted, or unloaded key causes authentication or sending failure.

stages.action: Create server.ts, import Resend, and initialize new Resend(process.env.RESEND_API_KEY).
stages.success_signal: server.ts can construct the client without a missing environment value.
stages.failure_or_wait: TypeScript, module, or environment-loading errors must be corrected before execution.

stages.action: In server.ts call resend.emails.send with from Acme , to delivered@resend.dev, subject Hello World, and HTML It works!.
details: Destructure data and error; log error and return on failure, otherwise log data.
stages.input: Initialized Resend client and official test message
stages.success_signal: The message fields and success-or-error handling match the quickstart.
stages.failure_or_wait: Syntax or message-field errors must be corrected before the request can be submitted.

stages.action: Execute server.ts using the Node.js or TypeScript runner configured for the project.
details: The guide supplies the script but does not prescribe one universal TypeScript runner command for every Node project.
stages.success_signal: The program completes the resend.emails.send call.
stages.failure_or_wait: Runtime, authentication, key-scope, sender-domain, quota, network, or API errors are logged through the documented error branch; correct the reported problem and rerun.

stages.action: Verify that the program logs the returned data object rather than error.
stages.success_signal: The Resend SDK returns and logs data for the Hello World send to delivered@resend.dev.
stages.failure_or_wait: If error is logged, no first-send success is recorded; use the returned error to correct the request, credential, domain, or account state and retry.

branches.condition: API, CLI, or MCP is used to create the key.
branches.path: Use the linked Resend API operation, Resend CLI command, or Resend MCP server instead of the Dashboard modal; still apply a name, permission, optional domain restriction, one-time secret handling, and environment configuration appropriate to that route.
branches.effect_on_first_success: Changes authentication and tool setup before the same required key exists.

branches.condition: API, CLI, or MCP is used to create the domain.
branches.path: Create the domain through the selected tool, capture the returned DNS records, add them at the DNS provider, then invoke or wait for domain verification. Resend notes the Dashboard may be easier.
branches.effect_on_first_success: Changes domain-creation controls but not the external DNS and propagation gates.

branches.condition: Node.js is selected.
branches.path: In the project choose npm, yarn, pnpm, or bun and install resend; create .env with RESEND_API_KEY; ensure the runtime loads it and pass process.env.RESEND_API_KEY to new Resend; create server.ts; call resend.emails.send with from Acme , to delivered@resend.dev, subject Hello World, and HTML It works!; log error and stop on failure, otherwise log data; execute the script and verify data is logged.
branches.effect_on_first_success: Produces the inspected first transactional email through the Node.js SDK.

branches.condition: Next.js, Express, PHP, Laravel, Python, Ruby, Rails, Go, Rust, Elixir, Java, .NET, or CLI is selected.
branches.path: Follow that named official quickstart's package or CLI installation, key configuration, message construction, execution, error handling, and route-specific returned result. Each route ends at its first transactional email, but commands and project structure differ.
branches.effect_on_first_success: Changes the complete implementation path and observable program output while retaining the email-send milestone.

branches.condition: AI Onboarding is selected.
branches.path: After human account, key, and domain setup, choose hosted MCP with browser OAuth or Bearer token, locally run MCP, Resend CLI login and send, agent docs, skills, Chat SDK, or an AI-builder guide. For hosted MCP in Claude Code, add https://mcp.resend.com/mcp and complete browser OAuth. For CLI, run resend login then resend emails send with from, to, subject, and text.
branches.effect_on_first_success: Adds an AI-client and OAuth or agent-tool route instead of a language SDK.

branches.condition: DNS verification is still incomplete after 72 hours.
branches.path: Check public records with dns.email, correct any mismatch, then select Restart verification in the Resend Dashboard to trigger a fresh check.
branches.effect_on_first_success: Adds troubleshooting and another verification wait before any send.

frictionGates.description: A human must create the Resend account; the public docs do not expose the exact signup sequence.
The secret is displayed once and requires permission and optional domain-scope decisions.
The developer must own a domain and have permission to change its DNS.
Generated DKIM and SPF TXT and MX records must be copied exactly into an external DNS provider.
Verification often takes 15 minutes but can take 72 hours, with manual restart only after the long wait.
Fourteen code or CLI quickstarts plus multiple AI-tool routes are available without a universal default.

uncertainties.question: Which Resend quickstart should define the canonical workshop journey?
uncertainties.impact: The answer changes packages, project structure, environment loading, authentication, execution, and response verification.
uncertainties.reason_unresolved: The Introduction offers fourteen quickstarts and AI Onboarding adds MCP, CLI, skills, and builder routes without naming one universal default.

uncertainties.question: What exact signup and account-verification screens precede Dashboard access?
uncertainties.impact: These can add identity entry, social login, email verification, team setup, or waiting.
uncertainties.reason_unresolved: Official docs only state that a human must create the account.

developerGoal: Create a Rootly account, connect a standard Slack workspace, create a first test incident, and observe its dedicated incident channel and initial system message.

entryPoint.developer_intent: Create a Rootly account, connect a standard Slack workspace, create a first test incident, and observe its dedicated incident channel and initial system message.
assumed_prior_state: No Rootly account, organization membership, Slack integration, connected Slack user, team configuration, or incident is assumed.
A Slack Free, Pro, or Business+ workspace and Slack Workspace Admin or Owner access are available for the otherwise documented downstream route.
A work email and password would be used for the documented signup route; no SSO-enforced domain or prior organization invitation is assumed.
No account, agreement, Slack authorization, integration, channel, or incident was created during research.

selectedSurface.name: Rootly quick start from signup through a first Slack test incident
selectedSurface.selection_basis: Under the workshop policy, Rootly's own Quick Start statement that Dashboard > Create Test Incident is the fastest first workflow makes the web route primary. It avoids the optional Slack installation and its admin permissions; signup is one opaque step at the official URL.

alternatives_considered.name: Dashboard Create Test Incident
alternatives_considered.reason_not_primary: The quick start calls this the fastest dashboard route, but its detailed setup spine still includes connecting Slack and does not document the dashboard modal field sequence on that page.

alternatives_considered.reason_not_primary: The quick-start navigation presents Microsoft Teams as a peer communication surface with different installation, permission, and channel actions.

alternatives_considered.name: Google Chat integration
alternatives_considered.reason_not_primary: The quick-start navigation presents Google Chat as another peer communication surface rather than selecting Slack for every organization.

alternatives_considered.name: Rootly web or API incident creation

prerequisites.requirement: Choose Google, Slack, SSO, email and password, or an organization invitation for Rootly identity; the candidate uses email and password.

The standard Slack installation requires a Rootly account with Admin or Owner permissions and a Slack Workspace Admin or Owner account.
Choose the Slack Free, Pro, or Business+ workspace install or the Enterprise Grid organization install; the candidate uses a standard workspace install.
Slack must allow the Rootly app, including the scopes required for slash commands, messages, and public or private incident-channel creation.

frictionGates.description: Google, Slack, SSO, email and password, and invitation routes change identity and organization-entry actions.
The signup guide does not establish that a fresh self-service account has the Rootly Admin permission required by the Slack installation guide.
Slack setup requires Rootly Admin or Owner and Slack Workspace Admin or Owner permissions; Enterprise Grid requires an Org Owner or Admin.
Standard Slack, Enterprise Grid, Microsoft Teams, Google Chat, dashboard, web, and API routes have different setup and incident-creation actions.
Slack channel-creation restrictions can require additional admin scopes before Rootly can create incident channels.
The incident modal can enforce customized required, conditional, and lifecycle fields before Create is allowed.

developerGoal: Use Salesforce's REST API Quick Start to authenticate a free Developer Edition org, inspect REST resources and Account metadata, query Accounts, update one Account's BillingCity, and verify the update.

entryPoint.developer_intent: Use Salesforce's REST API Quick Start to authenticate a free Developer Edition org, inspect REST resources and Account metadata, query Accounts, update one Account's BillingCity, and verify the update.
assumed_prior_state: Discovery is complete and the developer has selected Salesforce REST API.
The developer does not yet have a Developer Edition org, Salesforce CLI, authenticated org, access token, or instance URL.
The developer can install Salesforce CLI and use cURL.

selectedSurface.name: Salesforce REST API Quick Start using Developer Edition, Salesforce CLI, and cURL
selectedSurface.selection_basis: Workshop selection policy (2026-07-19): when official docs present peer routes, commit to the fastest and most commonly used documented route. Prefer the vendor's first-listed or recommended quickstart, dashboard/console over CLI when both are peers, and the route with an explicit vendor time claim or fewest documented steps when tied. Committed to already-reconstructed surface `Salesforce REST API Quick Start using Developer Edition, Salesforce CLI, and cURL`. Prior basis: Salesforce's REST API guide labels this the Quick Start, says it sets up a basic environment and updates a record, and explicitly chooses the free Developer Edition, Salesforce CLI authentication, and cURL. Salesforce exposes many co-equal products and development surfaces, so this REST API path is representative rather than organization-wide canonical.

alternatives_considered.name: Development sandbox
alternatives_considered.reason_not_primary: The Quick Start permits a development sandbox, but Developer Edition is its free self-service example.

alternatives_considered.name: Custom connected app OAuth
alternatives_considered.reason_not_primary: The guide says a custom connected app is valid, but uses Salesforce CLI because its connected app requires no configuration.

alternatives_considered.name: Salesforce CLI request tools
alternatives_considered.reason_not_primary: The guide names Salesforce CLI as another API exercise tool, while the selected sample sequence is written in cURL.

alternatives_considered.name: Postman
alternatives_considered.reason_not_primary: Postman is an official documented third-party alternative but changes installation, environment, and request actions.

alternatives_considered.name: Other Salesforce APIs and platform surfaces
alternatives_considered.reason_not_primary: Apex, Lightning, GraphQL, Bulk, Composite, Marketing, Commerce, Data Cloud, and other products have different first successes.

firstMeaningfulSuccess.official_milestone: Quick Start record update completed
firstMeaningfulSuccess.normalized_outcome: The developer authenticates a free Salesforce Developer Edition org with Salesforce CLI, walks through the documented REST discovery and Account queries, patches one Account's BillingCity to Fremont, receives HTTP 204, and sees the changed value in Salesforce.
firstMeaningfulSuccess.observable_completion_signal: The PATCH request returns HTTP/1.1 204 No Content, and refreshing the Account page shows BillingCity changed to Fremont.

boundary_evidence.official_label_or_terminal_state: The Quick Start's final Update a Field on a Record section demonstrates HTTP 204 No Content and directs the developer to refresh the Account page to see Fremont.

firstMeaningfulSuccess.why_this_is_the_boundary: The Quick Start introduction defines its outcome as updating a record. The final PATCH plus visible Account refresh proves signup, permission, CLI authentication, token retrieval, REST discovery, query, request body, write permission, and the update itself.

prerequisites.requirement: A free Salesforce Developer Edition org or an existing development sandbox is required; new-org activation requires an email inbox the developer can access.
The Salesforce user profile must have API Enabled permission and enough Account access to query and update the selected record.
The latest Salesforce CLI for the operating system and cURL or an equivalent REST client are required for the representative route.

A globally unique Salesforce username in email-address format, an accessible account email, and the password set during activation are needed for browser login; CLI then provides the bearer access token and instance URL.
The representative Quick Start uses the free Developer Edition; no payment card is documented.
Developer Edition has a 5 MB data-storage maximum, which Salesforce says does not prevent completing the examples.

stages.action: Open Salesforce's Developer Edition signup page from the REST Quick Start.
details: The current page presents Developer Edition as a free Salesforce Platform environment.
A development sandbox remains an alternative to new-org signup.
stages.success_signal: The Sign up for your Developer Edition form is visible.
stages.failure_or_wait: A page-load, consent-manager, region, or client-side form error must be resolved before submission.

stages.action: Complete the signup prompts and select Sign me up.
details: The current official form requires First name, Last name, Job title, Company, Country or region, and Work email; State or province can be conditionally requested by country.
Use an email inbox you can access because the activation link is delivered there.

Where the signup flow requests Username, Salesforce Help requires a globally unique value in email-address format; it does not need to be the same as the accessible email or even a functioning mailbox.
Accept the required Main Services Agreement for Developer Services, Salesforce Program Agreement, and API Terms of Service.
stages.input: Identity, job title, company, geography, accessible work email, unique username when prompted, and required agreements
stages.success_signal: A signup confirmation message appears.
stages.failure_or_wait: Missing required fields or agreement, invalid email, duplicate username, region-dependent field error, or service error blocks enrollment and requires correction and resubmission.

stages.action: Wait for the Salesforce Developer Edition activation or welcome email to arrive in the accessible inbox.
details: Salesforce says delivery can take a few minutes; no exact REST Quick Start timing guarantee is stated.
stages.success_signal: The Salesforce activation message with its account-verification link is present.
stages.failure_or_wait: Delivery delay, spam filtering, inaccessible email, rejected enrollment, or duplicate-username enrollment failure prevents activation until resolved.

stages.action: Open the activation email and select its Verify Account or verification link.
stages.success_signal: Salesforce prompts the developer to finish registration.
stages.failure_or_wait: An expired, already-used, or wrong-account link requires a new activation or password-reset path.

stages.action: Set the Developer Edition password, select and answer the security challenge question, and complete the login process.
details: Preserve the unique username, password, and login URL securely for CLI authentication and later sign-in.
stages.input: Activation link, new password, and security question answer
stages.output: Activated Developer Edition credentials and authenticated session
stages.success_signal: The new Developer Edition org opens while logged in.
stages.failure_or_wait: A password-policy error, unanswered challenge question, expired activation, or incomplete login requires correction or account recovery.

stages.action: Confirm the first Developer Edition login succeeds and verify the user profile has API Enabled permission.
details: The Quick Start says Developer Edition includes API Enabled and that its 5 MB data-storage maximum does not block the examples.
stages.success_signal: The org is accessible and API Enabled is present for the user.
stages.failure_or_wait: Missing API Enabled or a login failure blocks CLI and REST authentication and requires profile or account correction.

stages.action: Install or update the latest Salesforce CLI and verify the installation.
stages.success_signal: Salesforce CLI verification succeeds.
stages.failure_or_wait: Installation, PATH, or version problems must be corrected before authentication.

stages.action: Run sf org login web, sign in to the Developer Edition org in the opened browser, and select Allow.
details: For a sandbox, append --instance-url with the sandbox My Domain URL.
Salesforce CLI acts as the connected app, so no custom connected-app configuration is required.
stages.input: Org credentials and optional sandbox URL
stages.success_signal: The CLI prints Successfully authorized with the username and org ID.
stages.failure_or_wait: Wrong credentials, sandbox URL, denied consent, or browser callback failure prevents authorization and requires retrying the login.

stages.action: Run sf org display --target-org with the username and copy the Access Token and Instance Url.
details: An alias can optionally shorten the username.
If the token expires, repeat org display to get a new token.
stages.output: Bearer access token and instance URL
stages.success_signal: Org Description shows Status Active plus Access Token and Instance Url.
stages.failure_or_wait: An unknown target org, disconnected status, or expired authorization requires listing orgs or logging in again.

stages.action: Prepare cURL requests with the instance My Domain, straight quotes, Authorization: Bearer token, JSON content type where needed, and local request-body files for writes.
details: On macOS or Linux, escape the exclamation mark in a double-quoted token or use single quotes.
Attach JSON with --data-binary or -d.
stages.input: Instance URL, access token, cURL, and local filesystem
stages.output: Correct cURL authentication and body format
stages.success_signal: The shell preserves the full bearer token and can read the body file.
stages.failure_or_wait: Curly quotes, an unescaped exclamation mark, a truncated token, or missing body file causes shell or API failure.

stages.action: GET /services/data/ on the org's My Domain to list available Salesforce API versions.
details: This Versions request does not require authentication.
stages.success_signal: The response contains label, url, and version entries.
stages.failure_or_wait: Wrong domain or network failure prevents version discovery.

stages.action: GET /services/data/v67.0/ with the bearer token to list resources for the documented version.
stages.success_signal: The response includes sobjects, search, query, and recent resource URLs.
stages.failure_or_wait: Invalid or expired token, API permission, domain, or version errors require correcting authentication or using an available version.

stages.action: GET /services/data/v67.0/sobjects/ to run Describe Global and confirm Account is available.
stages.success_signal: The response includes the Account object's metadata and URLs.
stages.failure_or_wait: Object permissions or an incorrect version can omit or block Account.

stages.action: GET /services/data/v67.0/sobjects/Account/ to retrieve basic Account metadata and recent items.
stages.success_signal: The response includes Account name, label, key prefix, capabilities, and recent Account IDs if present.
stages.failure_or_wait: No recent accounts can make the later example ID unavailable; use a queried Account ID.

stages.action: GET /services/data/v67.0/sobjects/Account/describe/ to retrieve detailed Account fields and relationships.
stages.success_signal: The response includes fields such as Id and updateable, childRelationships, and Account URLs.
stages.failure_or_wait: Missing permission or wrong object path blocks metadata retrieval.

stages.action: Execute the documented SOQL query SELECT name from Account and choose an Account ID from the response.
details: URL-encode the query as shown in the guide.
stages.success_signal: The response has done true, totalSize, and Account records with IDs.
stages.failure_or_wait: SOQL syntax, URL encoding, object permission, or an empty result requires correction or creating sample data.

stages.action: Create patchaccount.json with BillingCity set to Fremont and PATCH /sobjects/Account/{ID} using --data-binary.
details: Send Content-Type application/json and the bearer token.
Use the selected Account ID.
stages.success_signal: The API returns HTTP/1.1 204 No Content.
stages.failure_or_wait: Invalid JSON, wrong ID, non-updateable record or field, insufficient permission, expired token, or missing body prevents the 204 response.

stages.action: Open or refresh the Account page in Salesforce and verify BillingCity is Fremont.
stages.success_signal: The Account billing address shows Fremont.
stages.failure_or_wait: A different Account, stale page, failed PATCH, or automation overwriting the value prevents visual confirmation.

branches.condition: A development sandbox is used.
branches.path: Use the existing sandbox user with API Enabled; install CLI; run sf org login web with --instance-url set to the sandbox My Domain URL; authorize; display the sandbox token and instance URL; then run the same request sequence against that sandbox.
branches.effect_on_first_success: Skips Developer Edition signup but adds sandbox access and the instance-url login flag.

branches.condition: Enrollment reports that the email address is already in use.
branches.path: Check the Username rather than abandoning the accessible account email. Salesforce's troubleshooting article says this message can actually mean the globally unique username is already taken; choose a new username in email-address format, keep the same accessible email if desired, and resubmit.
branches.effect_on_first_success: Adds a corrected signup attempt while preserving activation delivery to the same inbox.

branches.condition: The activation email does not arrive.
branches.path: Confirm the submitted email inbox is accessible, check spam or filtering, verify the signup produced confirmation rather than an enrollment error, correct any duplicate-username failure, and repeat or recover the signup according to the page shown.
branches.effect_on_first_success: Adds email diagnosis, possible resubmission, and another activation wait.

branches.condition: A custom connected app is required.
branches.path: Create and configure the connected app, select OAuth scopes, set callback and policy, implement a supported OAuth flow, exchange for an access token, capture instance_url, and then enter the cURL sequence.
branches.effect_on_first_success: Adds app configuration and OAuth implementation that the Quick Start avoids by using Salesforce CLI.

branches.condition: The access token expires.
branches.path: Run sf org display --target-org again to obtain the refreshed token; if the org is no longer connected, repeat sf org login web and Allow; replace the token in later requests and retry.
branches.effect_on_first_success: Adds token renewal or login before the failed request can succeed.

branches.condition: The token's exclamation mark triggers shell history expansion.
branches.path: Enclose the Authorization header in single straight quotes or escape the exclamation mark with a backslash inside double straight quotes, then resend.
branches.effect_on_first_success: Fixes local shell parsing without changing Salesforce authorization.

branches.condition: cURL is not desired.
branches.path: Use Salesforce CLI or Postman to send equivalent requests with the same instance URL, API version, bearer token, paths, query, Account ID, PATCH body, and final verification.
branches.effect_on_first_success: Changes tool installation and interface while preserving the request sequence and boundary.

branches.condition: API version 67.0 is not in the Versions response.
branches.path: Select a current version returned by /services/data/, replace v67.0 consistently in every versioned request, and continue with that version.
branches.effect_on_first_success: Changes request URLs and potentially response schema while preserving the operations.

branches.condition: No Account record is available to update.
branches.path: Create an Account using an authorized sObject POST or add sample data in the org, query again to obtain its ID, then continue with the PATCH.
branches.effect_on_first_success: Adds a record-creation step before the documented update.

branches.condition: The PATCH does not return 204.
branches.path: Inspect the Salesforce error body; verify token, API Enabled, Account and field permissions, API version, Account ID, Content-Type, straight quotes, and patchaccount.json; correct the cause and retry the PATCH, then refresh the Account.
branches.effect_on_first_success: Adds diagnosis and a repeated write before terminal verification.

frictionGates.description: Signup requires identity, job title, company, geography, accessible work email, a globally unique username where prompted, and submission of the live form.
The current form requires agreement to the Developer Services, Salesforce Program, and API terms.
The developer must wait for an activation email sent to an accessible inbox.
The activation-email verification link must be opened before password setup and first login.
Activation requires a new password and security challenge question; username, password, and login URL must be preserved.
The representative route requires installing and verifying the latest Salesforce CLI plus cURL.
Browser login and an Allow consent action are required before CLI authorization completes.
The bearer token and instance URL must be copied from CLI output and the token can expire.
cURL quoting, token exclamation marks, JSON files, headers, instance domain, and API version create several local failure points.
A queryable and updateable Account record and its ID are required for the terminal PATCH.
The authenticated user needs object, record, and BillingCity update access to receive 204.

uncertainties.question: Which Salesforce product or development surface should represent the workshop's organization-wide first mile?
uncertainties.impact: REST API, Apex, Lightning, GraphQL, Bulk, Marketing, Commerce, Data Cloud, and other surfaces have different accounts, tools, and terminal outcomes.
uncertainties.reason_unresolved: The REST guide is canonical only within REST API and does not claim to represent all Salesforce development.

uncertainties.question: Will the live Developer Edition form request a separate Username or CAPTCHA for this participant?
uncertainties.impact: A Username prompt adds the global-uniqueness choice and duplicate retry; a conditional CAPTCHA would add an anti-bot gate before submission.
uncertainties.reason_unresolved: Current Salesforce Help still instructs developers to supply a unique username, while the current public form schema reviewed on the research date lists identity, company, geography, work email, and agreements but does not expose a Username or CAPTCHA field. Static official sources cannot establish conditionally rendered runtime controls.

uncertainties.question: Will the new Developer Edition org contain an Account record suitable for the documented update?
uncertainties.impact: An empty org adds account creation before the terminal PATCH.
uncertainties.reason_unresolved: The sample responses assume Account records but the signup guide does not guarantee a specific data set.
