selectedSurface.name: Square Get Started, browser-based Sandbox payment path
selectedSurface.selection_basis: Square's official platform Get Started page defines one numbered four-part sequence, create an account and application, make the first API call, view the API logs, and verify the payment. The browser tools prefill the Sandbox token and CreatePayment request, so no language choice is required.

alternatives_considered.name: Square SDK quickstarts
alternatives_considered.reason_not_primary: These add language-specific installation and code, while the platform Get Started sequence explicitly uses Developer Console and API Explorer.

alternatives_considered.name: API Explorer generic GET and POST tutorial
alternatives_considered.reason_not_primary: This is a tool tutorial; the platform Get Started path prepopulates CreatePayment and continues through logs and seller verification.

firstMeaningfulSuccess.official_milestone: Verify the Payment
firstMeaningfulSuccess.normalized_outcome: A Square developer application creates a completed Sandbox payment through API Explorer, exposes its request and response in API Logs, and shows the transaction in the Sandbox Square Dashboard.
firstMeaningfulSuccess.observable_completion_signal: The CreatePayment response contains a Payment object whose status is `COMPLETED`; the matching request and response are visible in API Logs, and the payment appears under Transactions in the default Sandbox test account.

boundary_evidence.official_label_or_terminal_state: Square's platform Get Started page makes `Verify the Payment` step 4 and the final item in its sequence.

firstMeaningfulSuccess.why_this_is_the_boundary: It is the final, seller-visible verification explicitly required by the official four-step platform journey.

prerequisites.requirement: A Square account is required. New registration collects name, email, password, country, agreement to terms and policies, and business name.
The first exercise uses Square's Sandbox, default test account, Developer Console, and API Explorer; no production payments activation is required.

stages.action: Create a Square account or sign in, then open the Developer Console.
details: New registration asks for name, email, password, country, agreements, and business name.
An existing Square account can sign in and skip registration.
stages.input: Square account identity and required registration details.
stages.success_signal: The Developer Console opens.
stages.failure_or_wait: A different country setting affects account context; terms and policy acceptance are required for registration.

stages.action: Create a developer application and supply its name and optional preferences.
details: The account workflow can create the application during onboarding.
Existing developers can select an existing application and skip creation.
stages.input: Application name and any selected preferences.
stages.output: Square developer application with Sandbox context.
stages.success_signal: The application page appears in Developer Console.
stages.failure_or_wait: The first-call banner may be absent; Square provides a direct prepopulated API Explorer link as fallback.

stages.action: Open API Explorer from the application's first-test-payment banner and select Run Request on the prefilled CreatePayment request.
details: API Explorer prepopulates the required fields and Sandbox access token.
The source ID is a Sandbox payment token representing a card.
stages.input: Developer application, default Sandbox test account, and prefilled request.
stages.success_signal: The response contains a Payment object with `status` equal to `COMPLETED`.
stages.failure_or_wait: If the request fails, the Response pane returns an error instead of the Payment object.

stages.action: From the API Explorer response, choose View Logs, select the API call, and inspect its Request and Response tabs.
details: The API Logs page belongs to the selected application.
Square redacts sensitive data in logs.
stages.output: Selected API log entry with request and response details.
stages.success_signal: The matching call and both HTTP payload views are visible.
stages.failure_or_wait: The log must be selected from the results list; log entries are retained for a limited period.

stages.action: Return to Developer Console, open Sandbox test accounts, and choose Square Dashboard for the Default Test Account.
details: This opens the Sandbox Square Dashboard, not the production seller dashboard.
stages.output: Sandbox Square Dashboard for that account.
stages.success_signal: The seller-style Sandbox dashboard opens.
stages.failure_or_wait: Opening a different test account would show a different transaction set.

stages.action: Open Transactions and select the payment created in API Explorer.
details: If it is not visible, expand the date range to include the payment.
stages.success_signal: The matching payment appears and opens in Transactions.
stages.failure_or_wait: A date filter can hide the payment until the range is adjusted.

branches.condition: A Square account already exists.
branches.path: Sign in and proceed to the Developer Console without creating another account.
branches.effect_on_first_success: The registration pages are skipped.

branches.condition: A developer application already exists.
branches.path: Select the existing application instead of creating a new one.
branches.effect_on_first_success: Application creation is skipped, while the Sandbox call and verification remain the same.

branches.condition: The first-test-payment banner is not shown.
branches.path: Use the direct API Explorer link provided on the Make your First API Call page to open the same prepopulated CreatePayment view.
branches.effect_on_first_success: The path reaches the same CreatePayment request without the banner.

branches.condition: The payment is hidden by the current date range.
branches.path: Select a date range that includes when the API Explorer payment was created.
branches.effect_on_first_success: The matching transaction becomes available for final verification.

frictionGates.description: A Square account and required registration fields and agreements precede Developer Console access.
A developer application is required even though API Explorer prefills the Sandbox token and request.
The first API call succeeds only if CreatePayment returns a Payment object with `COMPLETED` status.
Final confirmation occurs in a separate seller-facing Sandbox dashboard and can be obscured by its date filter.

uncertainties.question: Does Square's `in minutes` claim include brand-new account registration and every final verification page?
uncertainties.impact: The phrase may not describe total elapsed time for a developer beginning without an account.
uncertainties.reason_unresolved: The platform page associates the phrase with creating an account and first call but does not define the measurement boundary.

sources.title: Make your First API Call
evidence_supported: Prefilled Sandbox access token and request

excludedAfterSuccess: Language-specific SDK installation and source code
Production access tokens and payments activation
Web Payments SDK or In-App Payments SDK tokenization
Orders, inventory, customers, and other Square APIs
doesNotProve: Platform records reconstruct official documentation. They do not contain product telemetry, conversion rates, observed completion times, or proven causes of abandonment.
Friction gates are documented requirements or transitions. They are not confirmed drop-off points.
Blocker taxonomy entries are candidate hypotheses. Individual reasons remain not diagnosis eligible unless separate case evidence supports them.
A documented journey analysis is not a diagnosis of a real developer attempt.

developerGoal: Create a Stability AI Developer Platform account with free credits and an API key, then run the Stable Image Core Python sample until `lighthouse.webp` is saved.

entryPoint.developer_intent: Create a Stability AI Developer Platform account with free credits and an API key, then run the Stable Image Core Python sample until `lighthouse.webp` is saved.
assumed_prior_state: No Stability AI membership account, API key, credits, payment method, Python dependency, or generated image is assumed.
The developer has a Google account for the documented free-credit route, is at least 18 and of legal age, and is authorized to accept the platform terms.
No account, agreement, OAuth authorization, credential, credit purchase, API request, or image generation was performed during research.

selectedSurface.name: Stable Image Core REST v2beta through the first saved lighthouse image
selectedSurface.selection_basis: Under the workshop policy, Stable Image Core is primary because official docs call it the flagship and primary fast text-to-image service and publish a Python sample with a saved-image terminal. Google signup is one opaque step because it is the documented route that grants 25 free credits.

alternatives_considered.name: Stable Image Ultra
alternatives_considered.reason_not_primary: Ultra is the highest-quality option and costs more credits, while the Stable Image overview calls Core the flagship service and the reference calls Core the primary fast service.

alternatives_considered.name: Stable Diffusion 3.5 API
alternatives_considered.reason_not_primary: This requires choosing among model variants, while Core is presented as the primary service without prompt engineering.

alternatives_considered.name: Username and password account
alternatives_considered.reason_not_primary: The guide documents this account option but explicitly ties 25 free credits to Google social login; credits are required for the first generation.

alternatives_considered.name: Legacy gRPC SDK
alternatives_considered.reason_not_primary: The Stable Image overview says REST v2beta is the newer API and places gRPC and REST v1 in legacy maintenance.

firstMeaningfulSuccess.official_milestone: Generate an image with Stable Image Core
firstMeaningfulSuccess.normalized_outcome: The Stable Image Core sample returns HTTP 200 and saves the generated lighthouse image as `lighthouse.webp`.
firstMeaningfulSuccess.observable_completion_signal: A non-empty `lighthouse.webp` file is written locally after the API returns HTTP 200.

boundary_evidence.official_label_or_terminal_state: On HTTP 200, write `response.content` to `./lighthouse.webp`.

firstMeaningfulSuccess.why_this_is_the_boundary: The saved image is the first durable proof that account credits, API-key authentication, generation, and response handling work end to end.

prerequisites.requirement: A Stability AI Developer Platform account created through Google social login for the explicitly documented 25 free credits.
Be at least 18 and of legal age, be authorized to accept the Terms, and accept the required Terms and Privacy Policy checkbox in the post-login Welcome modal.
Generate an API key from the account API Keys view and keep it secret.
Have at least 3 credits for a successful Stable Image Core generation; the documented new-account grant is 25 credits.
A Python environment with the `requests` package used by the published sample.

stages.action: Open Getting Started and select Login from the Developer Platform documentation.
stages.output: The Stability AI Developer Platform account application opens.
stages.success_signal: The official platform.stability.ai account surface is reached.
stages.failure_or_wait: No navigation failure or recovery behavior is documented.

stages.action: Complete Google signup through the official Developer Platform.
details: Treat signup as one opaque step; this route grants the documented 25 free credits.
stages.output: An authenticated Developer Platform account with free credits is available.
stages.success_signal: The account dashboard opens and shows credits.
stages.failure_or_wait: Complete Google authorization and any required legal acceptance.

stages.action: Open API Keys and generate a key with the plus control.
details: Copy and store the key securely.
stages.output: A Stability API key is available.
stages.success_signal: The new key is copied for use.
stages.failure_or_wait: Create a replacement if the key display is lost.

stages.action: Create a Python environment, install `requests`, and expose the API key securely.
stages.success_signal: Python imports `requests` and reads the key.
stages.failure_or_wait: Correct the environment or key variable.

stages.action: Run the official Stable Image Core Python sample with the lighthouse prompt and WebP response.
details: The request uses the v2beta Stable Image Core endpoint and bearer API key.
stages.input: At least 3 credits and the API key
stages.output: The API returns generated image bytes.
stages.success_signal: The response status is HTTP 200.
stages.failure_or_wait: Handle documented authentication, moderation, credit, rate-limit, or server errors.

stages.action: Verify that the sample wrote `lighthouse.webp` and open it.
stages.output: A generated lighthouse image is visible locally.
stages.success_signal: `lighthouse.webp` exists and renders as an image.
stages.failure_or_wait: Inspect the response body and file-write permissions.

branches.condition: Use username and password
branches.path: Create a Developer Platform account with a username and password instead of Google social login.
branches.effect_on_first_success: Avoids Google authorization, but Getting Started does not explicitly attach the 25-credit grant to this route even though the API-key help article describes new accounts as receiving it.

branches.condition: Free credits are depleted
branches.path: Purchase additional credits in Billing at $1 per 100 credits.
branches.effect_on_first_success: Adds a payment action before the generation request.

branches.condition: Use JSON response
branches.path: Set `accept` to `application/json` and decode the base64-encoded output instead of accepting direct image bytes.
branches.effect_on_first_success: Adds JSON decoding before a local image artifact exists.

branches.condition: Use Ultra or Stable Diffusion 3.5
branches.path: Select another generate endpoint and pay its documented per-success credit cost.
branches.effect_on_first_success: Changes service, cost, and possibly output resolution while preserving a generated-image terminal.

frictionGates.description: The selected Google social-login route continues through a client application whose provider consent and account-convergence sequence are not public in the official guide.

After login, the public client defines a required checkbox agreeing to the Terms, acknowledging the Privacy Policy, and confirming age 18; Accept submits it, Decline prevents API use, and marketing opt-in is optional.
Stability AI directs developers to store API keys in a secret manager or environment variable and never expose them in client-side code or public repositories.
Stable Image Core charges 3 credits only for a successful generation; failed generations are not charged.
A `403` response means the request was flagged by content moderation.
The API is limited to 150 requests per 10 seconds; exceeding the limit returns `429`.

sources.title: Where can I find my API Key?
sections_used: Where can I find my API Key?

sources.title: Stability AI - Developer Platform
sections_used: Welcome to Stability AI API legal modal
evidence_supported: Client-rendered account and API-key application without a public static provider contract
Required Terms, Privacy Policy, and age-18 checkbox
Accept, Decline, and optional marketing controls

sources.title: STABILITY AI API Terms of Service

excludedAfterSuccess: No complete first-success boundary is claimed while Google account authorization and convergence remain incompletely documented.
Other generate services, upscaling, editing, controls, audio, 3D, paid credit purchases, multiple keys, integrations, and production operation are outside the blocked selected path.
doesNotProve: Platform records reconstruct official documentation. They do not contain product telemetry, conversion rates, observed completion times, or proven causes of abandonment.
Friction gates are documented requirements or transitions. They are not confirmed drop-off points.
Blocker taxonomy entries are candidate hypotheses. Individual reasons remain not diagnosis eligible unless separate case evidence supports them.
A documented journey analysis is not a diagnosis of a real developer attempt.

developerGoal: Reach Stream's first useful documented developer success after selecting Stream as the platform.

entryPoint.developer_intent: Reach Stream's first useful documented developer success after selecting Stream as the platform.
assumed_prior_state: Discovery is complete and the developer has selected Stream.
The developer has not selected Chat, Video & Audio, Activity Feeds, AI Moderation, or Vision Agents.
The developer has not selected a client platform, server language, UI kit, REST route, sample app, or agent-assisted route.

selectedSurface.name: React Chat connected client with tutorial credentials
selectedSurface.selection_basis: Workshop selection policy (2026-07-19): when official docs present peer routes, commit to the fastest and most commonly used documented route. Prefer the vendor's first-listed or recommended quickstart, dashboard/console over CLI when both are peers, and the route with an explicit vendor time claim or fewest documented steps when tied. Selected route: React Chat connected client with tutorial credentials. The current React Chat tutorial provides generated credentials and an explicit earliest connection checkpoint before channel and message UI work. It is a complete low-dependency candidate, not a platform-wide default over Stream's other products or SDKs. Prior surface before policy commit: Stream product and SDK documentation selector.

alternatives_considered.name: Chat Messaging
alternatives_considered.reason_not_primary: It has UI-component, client, server, REST, tutorial, sample-app, and agent-skill routes across many platforms, but is not selected over Stream's other products.

alternatives_considered.name: Video & Audio
alternatives_considered.reason_not_primary: It offers seven client SDKs, Platform API, Vision Agents, example apps, several use cases, and prebuilt versus custom React paths without being the platform default.

alternatives_considered.name: Activity Feeds
alternatives_considered.reason_not_primary: It offers client and server SDKs in both v3 and v2 and explicitly separates client-side from server-side responsibilities.

alternatives_considered.name: AI Moderation
alternatives_considered.reason_not_primary: It requires an app and policy before a check, offers seven server SDKs, and is a separate outcome from chat, video, or feeds.

alternatives_considered.name: Vision Agents
alternatives_considered.reason_not_primary: It is a separate Python and Gemini quickstart that needs both Stream and Google credentials and ends with a browser voice-agent interaction.

firstMeaningfulSuccess.official_milestone: The page displays Chat with client is ready! and the Network panel shows the secure WebSocket connection.
firstMeaningfulSuccess.normalized_outcome: The developer first chooses a Stream product and implementation route, then completes that route's distinct observable success.
firstMeaningfulSuccess.observable_completion_signal: Signals differ by route: a secure chat WebSocket and rendered ready state, a joined call with rendered participants, a created feed plus added Hello Stream Feeds activity, a keep/flag/remove moderation result, or a voice agent that greets the developer in the browser.

boundary_evidence.official_label_or_terminal_state: The page displays Chat with client is ready! and the Network panel shows the secure WebSocket connection.

firstMeaningfulSuccess.why_this_is_the_boundary: A product and route decision changes prerequisites, credentials, installation, code, external services, hardware permissions, and verification. Selecting any route as canonical would require human workshop judgment.

prerequisites.requirement: A Stream account and app are required for own-application routes. A single account provides access to Chat, Feeds, Video, Moderation, and Vision Agents; signup accepts email and password or Google or GitHub. Stream's React Chat tutorial can instead provide generated tutorial credentials.

Credential shape is route-specific: Chat uses API key plus user token, Video uses API key plus token or token provider, Feeds uses API key plus user token, Moderation uses app credentials, and Vision Agents uses Stream key and secret plus a Google AI Studio key.

Software depends on the selected SDK. Representative routes require a React toolchain and npm or yarn, a supported Feeds SDK, a server SDK for moderation, or uv with Python 3.10 through 3.13 for Vision Agents.

Video, Audio, and Vision Agent routes can require camera, microphone, browser permissions, and a user interaction before media playback; chat, feeds, and text moderation do not share this prerequisite.

Free entitlements are product-specific and must not be merged: Chat Build is free with no card and publishes 1,000 MAU plus 100 concurrent connections; every Video account receives $100 per month in free Audio/Video API usage with no card; Moderation provides $100 in free monthly credits for messages, images, and video with no card.

stages.action: Open Stream's developer documentation and inspect the product-level starting choices.
details: The common navigation lists Chat Messaging, Video & Audio, Activity Feeds, AI Moderation, and Vision Agents.
Chat, Video, and Feeds pages each advertise free coding without a credit card.
stages.success_signal: The developer can see that Stream does not expose a single product-neutral quickstart.
stages.failure_or_wait: No execution wait occurs at documentation arrival.

stages.action: Choose Chat Messaging, Video & Audio, Activity Feeds, AI Moderation, or Vision Agents, then choose that product's implementation route.
details: Chat offers React, iOS/Swift, Android, React Native, Flutter, Unreal, Unity, Angular, and JavaScript client or UI routes, plus NodeJS, Python, Ruby, PHP, Java, .NET, Go, and REST backend routes.
Video offers React, iOS/Swift, Android, React Native, Flutter, JavaScript, Unity, Platform API, and Vision Agents, with use-case choices including calls, audio rooms, livestreaming, voice, and AI.
Feeds v3 offers JavaScript, iOS, Android, React, React Native, Flutter clients and Node, Go, .NET, PHP, Java, Python servers, while v2 remains another documented route.
Moderation offers Node, Python, Go, Java, PHP, Ruby, and .NET server examples; Vision Agents uses its Python quickstart.
stages.input: Product outcome, target platform, language, UI-control preference, and authentication model
stages.output: A human-selected product and first-success route
stages.success_signal: One documented branch is selected for end-to-end execution.
stages.failure_or_wait: The docs do not establish a universal default, so a canonical journey cannot continue without a human choice.

stages.action: For an own-application route, create or sign in to the common Stream account and choose Dashboard or Stream CLI onboarding.
details: Email and password, Google, and GitHub are documented account methods.
The signup flow asks for the main product to personalize onboarding, but the account can use multiple Stream products.
The React Chat tutorial's generated-credential route can bypass this own-account step.
stages.input: Account identity, selected main product, and Dashboard-versus-CLI preference
stages.output: Authenticated Stream account or documented generated-credential bypass
stages.success_signal: The Dashboard is open or getstream init is ready to authenticate.
stages.failure_or_wait: The current official article does not enumerate email-verification or anti-abuse screens.

stages.action: Create, clone, pick, or temporarily provision the Stream application through the selected Dashboard or CLI path.
details: Dashboard creation asks for an application name and a server region for Chat and Feeds; the region is locked after creation.
Choose development or production mode. Stream recommends separate development and hardened production apps, and app settings can switch between those modes later.
An existing application can be cloned instead of creating a new one.

getstream init authenticates in a browser, selects an organization and existing or newly created app, and writes local credentials. getstream login --guest can create a temporary guest account backed by a throwaway app.
stages.input: Application name, immutable server-region choice where applicable, development or production mode, and optional source app to clone
stages.output: Stream application with API key and secret
stages.success_signal: The application is created or selected and its credentials are available to the Dashboard or local project.
stages.failure_or_wait: A wrong server region cannot be changed after the Chat or Feeds app is created; production mode restricts destructive Dashboard actions.

stages.action: Open the new application's overview and verify its common setup output before product-specific code begins.
details: Dashboard creation routes to the app overview screen.
For Chat, the overview exposes API key and secret plus usage and configuration areas.
The CLI path writes .stream/creds.yaml and adds it to .gitignore automatically.
stages.success_signal: The overview is visible or local credential file exists, and the selected product quickstart can use the app.
stages.failure_or_wait: Missing application credentials blocks own-app product routes.

stages.action: Confirm a React 19-compatible local toolchain and npm are available for the versions tested by the tutorial.
details: The tutorial states it was tested with React ^19 and Stream Chat SDK ^14; a configured CodeSandbox is an alternative.
stages.success_signal: The Vite React TypeScript scaffold can run locally.
stages.failure_or_wait: Use the linked sandbox or correct the local Node, npm, or React environment before continuing.

stages.action: Run npm create vite chat-example -- --template react-ts, then cd chat-example.
stages.success_signal: The shell is inside the generated chat-example project.
stages.failure_or_wait: Package, network, template, or filesystem errors must be corrected and the scaffold rerun.

stages.action: Run npm i stream-chat stream-chat-react.
stages.output: Installed Stream Chat client and React component packages
stages.success_signal: Both packages are installed in chat-example.
stages.failure_or_wait: npm, network, lockfile, or permissions errors must be corrected before imports resolve.

stages.action: Use the tutorial-generated apiKey, userId, userToken, and optional userName values shown with the code sample.
details: The API key identifies the generated Stream application; userId and userToken authorize the chat user.
Using an own Stream app and JWT generator is a separate branch.
stages.output: Temporary Chat application and user credentials
stages.success_signal: All required placeholder values can be replaced in App.tsx.
stages.failure_or_wait: Expired, missing, copied incorrectly, or mismatched generated credentials prevent the client connection; refresh the tutorial values or use an own app and valid JWT.

stages.action: Replace src/App.tsx with the tutorial's initial Client Setup code and substitute the generated credentials.
details: The code imports Chat and useCreateChatClient, calls the hook once with apiKey, tokenOrProvider, and userData, renders Setting up client & connection... until ready, then renders Chat with the text Chat with client is ready!.
The tutorial warns that the client must establish a connection before it is passed to Chat.
stages.output: React app that creates and renders a connected Stream Chat client
stages.success_signal: App.tsx has no remaining credential placeholders and matches the initial setup.
stages.failure_or_wait: TypeScript, imports, placeholder, or credential errors must be corrected before the app can connect.

stages.action: Run npm run dev.
stages.output: Vite development server and Stream connection attempt
stages.success_signal: Vite prints a local site URL and the application loads without a terminal build error.
stages.failure_or_wait: Build, dependency, port, or runtime errors must be corrected and the server restarted.

stages.action: Open the Vite site and verify both the ready text and a secure WebSocket connection to Stream in browser developer tools.
details: Stop at this first connected-client checkpoint; channels, message UI, styling, attachments, and channel lists are later tutorial sections.
stages.success_signal: The page displays Chat with client is ready! and the Network panel shows the secure WebSocket connection.
stages.failure_or_wait: If the page remains on Setting up client & connection... or no secure WebSocket appears, inspect browser errors, network access, package versions, and all generated credential values, then retry.

branches.condition: Chat Messaging with the current React tutorial is selected.
branches.path: Choose npm or yarn; scaffold the Vite React TypeScript chat-example project; install stream-chat and stream-chat-react; use the tutorial-generated apiKey, userId, userToken, and optional userName or supply an own Stream app and generated JWT; replace src/App.tsx with the useCreateChatClient setup; pass the connected client to Chat; run npm run dev or yarn dev; open the generated site; verify the ready text and a secure WebSocket in browser developer tools. Creating channels and the full chat UI comes after that first connected-client signal.
branches.effect_on_first_success: Ends at the tutorial's first observable Stream connection before later channel-list, message, styling, and attachment work.

branches.condition: Another Chat route is selected.
branches.path: Choose UI components or client SDK for React, iOS/Swift, Android, React Native, Flutter, Angular, Unity, Unreal, or JavaScript, or choose NodeJS, Python, Ruby, PHP, Java, .NET, Go, or REST for the backend. Follow that route's install, app-key, user-token, client initialization, connection, and route-specific rendered or API verification. Stream also offers complete iOS, Android, React Native, and Flutter tutorials, a sample-app install, and an agent-skill route.
branches.effect_on_first_success: Changes every implementation action and observable signal while retaining a chat connection as the product outcome.

branches.condition: Video & Audio with React custom integration is selected.
branches.path: Choose npm or yarn; install @stream-io/video-react-sdk; obtain an API key, user identity, and token or tokenProvider; create StreamVideoClient once at the app root; create call type default with ID my-first-call; join it with create:true and handle errors; pass it through StreamVideo and StreamCall; render participants with useParticipants and ParticipantView; add a user action before join for autoplay policy; grant camera and microphone permission when required; verify the call reaches JOINED state and participant media renders; leave and disconnect during cleanup.
branches.effect_on_first_success: Ends at a joined and rendered call, with device-permission, autoplay, and connection-error branches absent from non-media routes.

branches.condition: Video & Audio prebuilt, another SDK, Platform API, example app, or use-case tutorial is selected.
branches.path: Choose prebuilt components for a drop-in call lifecycle or custom integration for full control, then select React, iOS/Swift, Android, React Native, Flutter, JavaScript, Unity, Platform API, Vision Agents, or an example app. Within the product choose audio room, live shopping, livestreaming, video calling, voice AI, or voice calling. Complete that route's credential, install, client, call creation or lookup, join, media-permission, render, and route-specific verification steps.
branches.effect_on_first_success: Can reduce UI setup through prebuilt components or replace the client journey entirely with a server/API or sample-app path.

branches.condition: Activity Feeds v3 is selected.
branches.path: Choose client-side or server-side responsibility and an official v3 SDK. For a client example, install the matching package, initialize FeedsClient with the API key, connect user john with a user token, reference the user/john feed, call getOrCreate with watch enabled where supported, add an activity with type post and text Hello, Stream Feeds!, then verify the returned activity or state update. Server-side code is additionally used for feed-group or view configuration, user sync, token return, and writing to another user's feed. If v2 is selected, follow its separate legacy SDK path instead.
branches.effect_on_first_success: Ends at a created feed with its first activity; client/server and v2/v3 choices materially change credentials and package setup.

branches.condition: AI Moderation custom-content checking is selected.
branches.path: Create a Stream app; choose Node, Python, Go, Java, PHP, Ruby, or .NET server SDK; create a moderation policy with rules and actions; initialize the server moderation client with Stream app credentials; submit entity type, entity ID, creator ID, text and/or image payload, policy config key, and optional synchronous-check setting to the moderation Check API; verify the response exposes a recommended keep, flag, remove, or shadow-block action and process that action. Dashboard monitoring, review queue work, and action notifications follow the first recommendation.
branches.effect_on_first_success: Ends at an immediate policy-based recommendation rather than a user-facing communication or feed UI.

branches.condition: Vision Agents quickstart is selected.
branches.path: Install uv with Python 3.10 through 3.13; run uvx vision-agents init my-agent and enter the directory; let the scaffold create agent.py, pyproject.toml, .env.example, tests, Dockerfile, and dependencies; create a free Stream account for STREAM_API_KEY and STREAM_API_SECRET; create a Google AI Studio GOOGLE_API_KEY; copy .env.example to .env and fill all three secrets; inspect or customize the generated agent.py; run uv run agent.py run; open the printed browser join link; verify the agent greets the developer after joining the call.
branches.effect_on_first_success: Adds an external Google account and key, Python and uv tooling, three secrets, and a live browser audio interaction. This route alone claims completion in under five minutes.

branches.condition: Stream Agent Skills is selected instead of direct documentation.
branches.path: Install the Stream skill with npx skills add GetStream/agent-skills --skill stream, then invoke the product-specific command such as /stream-react. The router installs a specialist pack when first needed. The generated work must still satisfy the chosen product's credentials, runtime, execution, and observable verification boundary.
branches.effect_on_first_success: Delegates scaffolding to a coding agent but does not remove the product-choice, credential, or runtime gates.

branches.condition: Dashboard onboarding is selected for an own Stream app.
branches.path: Create the account with email and password, Google, or GitHub; select the main product during signup; in the Dashboard select Create application; enter the app name; for Chat or Feeds select the server region and treat it as locked after creation; select development or production mode; submit creation; wait for navigation to the app overview; verify the API key, secret, and product settings are visible.
branches.effect_on_first_success: Exposes every common UI action and makes the irreversible region selection visible before product-specific code.

branches.condition: Stream CLI onboarding is selected on macOS or Linux.
branches.path: Run curl -fsSL https://getstream.io/cli.sh | bash; verify installation with getstream --version; from the project directory run getstream init; complete browser authentication; select the organization; pick an existing app or create one; allow the CLI to write .stream/creds.yaml with app key and secret and add it to .gitignore; choose whether to install Stream skills or select None; verify the local project is linked.
branches.effect_on_first_success: Moves account authentication and app selection or creation into the terminal while still using a browser login and producing local secret material.

branches.condition: The CLI user does not yet want a durable Stream account.
branches.path: Run getstream login --guest before getstream init to create a temporary guest account backed by a throwaway app, then initialize and link the project. Replace the guest state before relying on the app durably.
branches.effect_on_first_success: Removes immediate durable-account creation but makes the first app intentionally temporary.

branches.condition: An existing Stream application should be reused as the starting point.
branches.path: Choose Clone application in the Dashboard rather than creating an empty app, select the source application, supply the new app's required name and region or mode choices, complete cloning, and open the cloned app overview.
branches.effect_on_first_success: Carries configuration from an existing app into the first-mile state and changes what counts as newly configured work.

branches.condition: Chat Messaging is the selected product and the Build free entitlement applies.
branches.path: Start without a credit card under the Chat Build tier. The current product pricing publishes a 1,000 monthly-active-user allowance, 100 concurrent connections, up to 1 million API calls, up to 100,000 Query Channel calls, and up to 10 GB API bandwidth, with additional Build limits applying to storage and channels.
branches.effect_on_first_success: Removes a card gate for the Chat first connection while retaining Chat-specific usage ceilings that do not apply to Video or Moderation.

branches.condition: Video & Audio is the selected product and the Build free entitlement applies.
branches.path: Start without a credit card and use the account's $100 per month in free Audio/Video API usage. After those credits are consumed, Stream calculates Video usage monthly according to the product's participant-minute, quality, and optional-feature pricing.
branches.effect_on_first_success: Provides a Video-specific monthly credit rather than Chat MAU limits or Moderation content credits.

branches.condition: AI Moderation is the selected product and the Start for Free entitlement applies.
branches.path: Start without a credit card and use $100 in free credits each month across moderation of messages, images, and video, with a Dashboard allowance for three moderators. Beyond the free credit, Pay-As-You-Go rates differ by content type.
branches.effect_on_first_success: Provides a Moderation-specific content credit and moderator allowance, separate from Chat and Video entitlements.

frictionGates.description: Five top-level products lead to incompatible outcomes, with no platform-wide default.
Each product adds SDK, UI-versus-client, client-versus-server, version, use-case, sample-app, REST, or agent-route choices.
Credential shape changes by route and can include API key, API secret, user JWT, token provider, and external Google AI key.
Every code route requires language and package tooling appropriate to the selected SDK.
Video and voice routes can require camera and microphone permission plus a user gesture for browser autoplay policy.
Own-app routes require a Stream account and app, while Vision Agents also requires Google AI Studio; the React Chat tutorial can bypass Stream signup using generated credentials.
Common own-app onboarding branches between Dashboard and Stream CLI, with an additional temporary guest-account path in the CLI.

Dashboard app creation requires a name and, for Chat and Feeds, a server region that is locked after creation; the developer also chooses development or production mode and can optionally clone an existing app.

Chat, Video, and Moderation each offer no-card first use, but their entitlements differ and are captured separately: Chat usage limits, Video monthly API credit, and Moderation monthly content credit.

uncertainties.question: Which Stream product defines the workshop's canonical first mile?
uncertainties.impact: The answer changes the success outcome itself, not merely the implementation language.
uncertainties.reason_unresolved: Official navigation treats Chat, Video & Audio, Activity Feeds, AI Moderation, and Vision Agents as separate peer products.

uncertainties.question: Within the chosen product, which SDK, UI, server, version, use-case, sample-app, REST, or agent route should be canonical?
uncertainties.impact: The choice changes prerequisites, signup bypass possibilities, credentials, install commands, code, failures, and completion signal.
uncertainties.reason_unresolved: The product landing pages list multiple routes without one universal default.

uncertainties.question: What exact dynamic signup fields, email-verification screens, organization controls, and anti-abuse checks appear in the current Stream Dashboard?
uncertainties.impact: They may add account or verification actions before the now-documented Dashboard or CLI app-creation sequence.
uncertainties.reason_unresolved: The official getting-started article establishes email/password or social signup, main-product selection, Dashboard and CLI choices, app creation or cloning, immutable region, mode, and overview, but does not enumerate every live account screen.

developerGoal: A cURL-created Checkout Session opens on Stripe's hosted page and completes a test-mode payment.

The journey starts at account creation and stops at the first documented successful outcome.

selectedSurface.name: Stripe-hosted Checkout via cURL Checkout Session
selectedSurface.selection_basis: The official documentation presents this as the hosted path from account setup to Complete a Stripe-hosted Checkout payment. It reaches the requested first-success boundary without extending into post-success production work.

boundary_evidence.official_label_or_terminal_state: The official documentation leads the developer to: Complete a Stripe-hosted Checkout payment.

firstMeaningfulSuccess.why_this_is_the_boundary: This is the first observable happy-path result on the selected Stripe-hosted Checkout via cURL Checkout Session surface; later configuration and production hardening occur after success.

stages.success_signal: Stripe accepts the test payment and completes Checkout.

developerGoal: Create a Stytch account and project, select an official authentication implementation, and test it until the guide's login or signup flow produces an authenticated session.

entryPoint.developer_intent: Create a Stytch account and project, select an official authentication implementation, and test it until the guide's login or signup flow produces an authenticated session.
assumed_prior_state: No Stytch account, workspace, project, environment, API key, public token, redirect URL, authorized domain, SDK, application, or end-user session is assumed.
The developer has a local environment appropriate to the selected framework and can receive a test authentication email if the selected route uses email magic links.
No account signup, dashboard configuration, API-key retrieval, email send, authentication, or session creation was performed during research.

selectedSurface.name: Authentication Quickstart through a working login or signup flow
selectedSurface.selection_basis: Under the workshop policy, the first-listed prebuilt Stytch UI route with React and Consumer Authentication is primary because it minimizes custom authentication code and is the quickest documented path to a working login/signup flow. Dashboard signup is one opaque step.

alternatives_considered.name: Stytch UI
alternatives_considered.reason_not_primary: The Quickstart presents Stytch UI as a peer implementation type alongside Headless and Backend only, and it still requires a framework and Consumer versus Multi-tenant choice.

alternatives_considered.name: Headless frontend SDK
alternatives_considered.reason_not_primary: This is a peer route for developers who want their own UI; the general Quickstart does not rank it above the other implementation types.

alternatives_considered.name: Backend-only Node.js Consumer Authentication
alternatives_considered.reason_not_primary: This route is fully documented after credentials and is preserved in the downstream gap, but it is not selected as Stytch's universal route.

alternatives_considered.name: Multi-tenant Authentication
alternatives_considered.reason_not_primary: Stytch presents Multi-tenant Authentication for organization/member applications and Consumer Authentication for individual users as peer project types.

firstMeaningfulSuccess.official_milestone: Add login or signup with Stytch UI
firstMeaningfulSuccess.normalized_outcome: The local React app renders Stytch UI and completes a Consumer Authentication login or signup, producing session and user data.
firstMeaningfulSuccess.observable_completion_signal: After the user completes the Stytch UI flow, the application receives an authenticated session and user object.

boundary_evidence.official_label_or_terminal_state: Handle session and user data after login or signup

firstMeaningfulSuccess.why_this_is_the_boundary: An authenticated session is the first proof that project configuration, frontend SDK, Stytch UI, and the selected auth method work end to end.

prerequisites.requirement: A Stytch account created through dashboard signup.
A Consumer Authentication or Multi-tenant Authentication project and an environment with the credentials required by the selected route.
Use the environment's public_token for frontend SDKs or project_id and secret for backend API or SDK calls; each project has distinct Live and Test environment credentials.
A framework environment selected from the options Stytch exposes for the chosen implementation type.

stages.action: Open Stytch's general Quickstart.
stages.success_signal: Implementation type, framework, and authentication project-type controls are displayed.
stages.failure_or_wait: No navigation recovery behavior is documented.

stages.action: Select Stytch UI, React, and Consumer Authentication.
details: This is the first-listed prebuilt UI route.
stages.output: The Add login or signup guide opens.
stages.success_signal: The React Stytch UI instructions are visible.

stages.action: Sign up through the official Stytch dashboard.
details: Treat signup, workspace creation, and initial project provisioning as one opaque step.
stages.output: An authenticated Consumer Authentication project is available.
stages.success_signal: The dashboard exposes the Test environment public token.
stages.failure_or_wait: Complete any account verification requested by Stytch.

stages.action: Enable Frontend SDKs, authorize the local development domain, and copy the Test public token.
stages.output: The project permits the local frontend.
stages.success_signal: The public token and authorized domain are configured.
stages.failure_or_wait: Correct the domain or environment selection.

stages.action: Install the Stytch React SDK, initialize the provider with the public token, and render the documented Stytch UI login/signup component.
stages.input: A React app and Test public token
stages.output: The local app renders Stytch UI.
stages.success_signal: The login/signup form appears.
stages.failure_or_wait: Check package installation, provider setup, token, and domain authorization.

stages.action: Run the app and complete a login or signup through the rendered Stytch UI.
stages.success_signal: The auth flow completes and returns to the app.
stages.failure_or_wait: Follow the selected method's verification prompt.

stages.action: Verify the React app receives and displays authenticated session or user data.
stages.success_signal: Session and user hooks return authenticated data.
stages.failure_or_wait: Inspect the SDK error and project settings.

branches.condition: Choose Stytch UI
branches.path: Select React, Next.js, or Vanilla JS and follow the relevant prebuilt login/signup component guide using the environment public_token, enabled Frontend SDKs, and an authorized domain.
branches.effect_on_first_success: Uses a prebuilt UI and frontend session hooks instead of backend routes and secret-key authentication.

branches.condition: Choose Headless
branches.path: Select a supported web or mobile framework and build the UI using the headless frontend SDK route.
branches.effect_on_first_success: Adds developer-owned UI state and changes SDK, configuration, and testing steps.

branches.condition: Choose Backend only
branches.path: Select Node, Python, Go, Ruby, or Java and implement server-side login, callback, and protected-session handling with project_id and secret.
branches.effect_on_first_success: Uses server credentials and backend routes; exact installation and code differ by language.

branches.condition: Choose Multi-tenant Auth
branches.path: Follow the organization/member authentication route rather than Consumer Authentication.
branches.effect_on_first_success: Changes core entities, API surface, login discovery behavior, and terminal data.

frictionGates.description: The developer must select implementation type, framework, and Consumer versus Multi-tenant Authentication before Stytch exposes a concrete guide.
A Stytch account is required before API keys or public tokens are available.
The client-rendered dashboard hides the public account, workspace, and initial-project transitions.
Each selected guide requires environment-specific credentials and project configuration before code execution.

developerGoal: Create a Sumo Logic trial account and reach the preloaded Kickstart sample dashboard that shows key performance metrics without configuring ingestion.

entryPoint.developer_intent: Create a Sumo Logic trial account and reach the preloaded Kickstart sample dashboard that shows key performance metrics without configuring ingestion.
assumed_prior_state: No Sumo Logic account, organization, region selection, identity-provider configuration, collector, source, or ingested data is assumed.
The email candidate assumes access to a business email inbox and accurate contact and organization details.
No signup, agreement acceptance, email activation, region selection, login, collector installation, or data ingestion was performed during research.

selectedSurface.name: Business email activation to Kickstart sample dashboard
selectedSurface.selection_basis: Workshop selection policy (2026-07-19): when official docs present peer routes, commit to the fastest and most commonly used documented route. Prefer the vendor's first-listed or recommended quickstart, dashboard/console over CLI when both are peers, and the route with an explicit vendor time claim or fewest documented steps when tied. Selected route: Business email activation to Kickstart sample dashboard. The current official signup page and account guide jointly expose the business-email fields, email activation, account-completion fields, region choice, password requirements, and the first-login Kickstart state. Prior surface before policy commit: Sumo Logic free-trial activation and Kickstart Data dashboard.

alternatives_considered.name: Google SSO signup
alternatives_considered.reason_not_primary: This is a two-step documented signup branch, but it delegates identity selection and authentication to Google.

alternatives_considered.name: AWS Marketplace signup
alternatives_considered.reason_not_primary: This creates a new Sumo Logic organization through a paid marketplace relationship and can convert to paid billing after the 30-day trial.

alternatives_considered.name: Ingest the developer's own data
alternatives_considered.reason_not_primary: This requires selecting AWS, Kubernetes, Linux, Windows, or macOS and then a collector or source path, while Kickstart Data is preloaded on first login without setup.

firstMeaningfulSuccess.official_milestone: This dashboard will give you an instant view of key performance metrics using Kickstart sample data.
firstMeaningfulSuccess.normalized_outcome: A newly activated trial user sees preloaded Kickstart sample data and an application, business, or security dashboard without configuring a collector or ingesting real data.
firstMeaningfulSuccess.observable_completion_signal: The Get Started experience displays a sample dashboard with preloaded log data and visible key performance metrics.

boundary_evidence.official_label_or_terminal_state: When you first log in, you'll see sample data preloaded into Sumo Logic, along with log searches and sample dashboards.

firstMeaningfulSuccess.why_this_is_the_boundary: The quickstart presents the sample dashboard before its onboarding checklist and before Step 1 for the developer's own data. It explicitly says no setup, data ingestion, or firewall configuration is required, so later searches, alerts, app installation, team invitations, and real-data collection are after the earliest visible value state.

prerequisites.requirement: Choose Google SSO, business email, or AWS Marketplace as the account route; the reconstructed candidate uses business email.
The direct email form requires agreement to the Sumo Logic Service License Agreement; region-specific personal-information choices can also appear during account completion.
Select the AWS region where Sumo Logic will store the account's data, based on data-residency needs.
The email route requires opening an activation email within three days.

stages.action: Open the official Get Started home and follow Sign Up and Activate Your Account, then Quickstart.
stages.output: The official account-first journey and Kickstart terminal are identified.
stages.success_signal: The page links signup and activation immediately before the quickstart.
stages.failure_or_wait: No navigation recovery is documented.

stages.action: Open the Sumo Logic Free Trial page and select Continue with email.
stages.success_signal: Business email and contact fields are visible.
stages.failure_or_wait: A mobile visitor is advised that Sumo Logic is intended for desktop.

stages.action: Enter business email, first name, last name, company, and phone, accept the Service License Agreement, choose whether to receive communications, and select Start free trial.
stages.input: Business identity and contact details plus required agreement
stages.output: The page instructs the developer to check the inbox for an activation link.
stages.success_signal: Please check your inbox and the submitted email address are displayed.
stages.failure_or_wait: The page advises checking the spam folder if the message is missing; no other resend flow is documented on the page.

stages.action: Open the Sumo Logic activation email and select Activate Now.
stages.input: Access to the submitted business-email inbox
stages.output: The Complete your account form opens.
stages.success_signal: Region, organization, country, and password controls are visible.
stages.failure_or_wait: The activation link expires after three days; after expiry, the signup process must be completed again. The guide also says to check spam and trash if the email is missing.

stages.action: Select the AWS region for data storage, confirm first name, last name, organization, and country, supply state or province when required, and create a password.
details: The displayed password rules require 8 to 100 characters, a lowercase letter, an uppercase letter, a number, and a special character.
The region list includes North America, Montreal, Dublin, Frankfurt, Zurich, Seoul, Sydney, and Tokyo options.
stages.input: Data-residency decision, organization details, country-dependent address detail, and compliant password
stages.output: The account-completion form is ready for activation.
stages.success_signal: All required controls pass their displayed validation requirements.
stages.failure_or_wait: The official sources do not document recovery for unavailable regions or rejected account details.

stages.action: Accept the required Service License Agreement and any applicable regional personal-information choices, then select the account-completion control.
details: The guide calls this control Activate, while the current public form labels it Get started.
stages.input: Completed account form and required agreements
stages.output: The trial account is activated and enters the personalized onboarding experience.
stages.success_signal: The official signup guide states that activated accounts are guided through personalized onboarding.
stages.failure_or_wait: The current label difference is preserved; no failed-activation behavior is documented.

stages.action: If the activation flow does not preserve an authenticated session, sign in with the registered email address and password.
stages.success_signal: The developer reaches the authenticated Get Started experience.
stages.failure_or_wait: The guide documents email and password as a sign-in method but does not state whether a fresh email activation requires this extra login.

stages.action: Display the Get Started page with preloaded Kickstart sample data and sample dashboards on first login.
details: Documented dashboard groups are Application reliability, Business KPIs, and Security.
stages.output: A sample dashboard shows key performance metrics without collector setup or real-data ingestion.
stages.success_signal: Preloaded sample metrics and log-derived dashboard content are visible.
stages.failure_or_wait: Kickstart Data remains available for 20 days or until real-data ingestion starts, whichever comes first, and can be skipped at any time.

branches.condition: The developer selects Google SSO
branches.path: Select Continue with Google, choose a Google account, complete the account form, and select Activate.
branches.effect_on_first_success: Replaces business-email verification and local-password creation with Google identity selection.

branches.condition: The developer selects AWS Marketplace
branches.path: Choose the pay-as-you-go 30-day trial in AWS Marketplace, configure and subscribe to the contract, wait for processing, set up a new Sumo Logic organization with a new business email, select a region, accept the Service License Agreement, sign up, and activate through email.
branches.effect_on_first_success: Adds AWS identity, contract, processing, new-email, and automatic post-trial billing behavior.

branches.condition: The developer wants to use real data instead of Kickstart sample data
branches.path: Select AWS, Kubernetes, Linux, Windows, or macOS, then follow the provided CloudFormation, CLI, Terraform, Terminal, or PowerShell collector setup and select Start using Sumo.
branches.effect_on_first_success: Adds platform, collector, source, installation, permissions, and an expected couple-of-minutes data-flow wait before dashboards or searches.

frictionGates.description: Google SSO, business email, and AWS Marketplace change identity, verification, agreement, and billing actions.
The business-email path requires work contact and organization data across an initial and a completion form.
Email activation must be completed within three days or signup must be repeated.
The developer must select the data-storage region based on residency requirements.
The direct signup path requires acceptance of the Service License Agreement and can show region-specific personal-information choices.

uncertainties.question: Which identity and commercial route should represent Sumo Logic's general first mile?
uncertainties.impact: Google, email, and AWS Marketplace produce different screen counts, credentials, agreements, billing effects, and external dependencies.
uncertainties.reason_unresolved: The official signup guide presents all three without selecting a universal default.

uncertainties.question: Does email activation automatically authenticate the new account, or is a separate first login required?
uncertainties.impact: The candidate path may contain one conditional authentication step before the Get Started page.
uncertainties.reason_unresolved: The signup guide states the post-activation onboarding and supported sign-in methods but does not specify session continuity after the current Get started control.

uncertainties.question: Should sample-data visibility or the developer's own data define workshop first success?
uncertainties.impact: Choosing own data adds a platform-specific collector, source, installation path, and wait; choosing Kickstart stops on first login.
uncertainties.reason_unresolved: The quickstart deliberately offers immediate sample data before the real-data collection journey.

developerGoal: Create a private tailnet and enroll two devices into it.

entryPoint.developer_intent: Create a private tailnet and enroll two devices into it.

selectedSurface.name: Tailscale browser-led quickstart
selectedSurface.selection_basis: Tailscale's install documentation directly routes new users to the Tailscale quickstart, which explicitly creates a tailnet, installs the client on two devices, displays both devices, and redirects to the admin console.

alternatives_considered.name: Install client first
alternatives_considered.reason_not_primary: The quickstart permits this order, but the browser-led Get Started path includes account and plan setup in one sequence.

alternatives_considered.name: Server auth-key onboarding

firstMeaningfulSuccess.official_milestone: Create a tailnet
firstMeaningfulSuccess.normalized_outcome: A new tailnet contains two authenticated devices, both are visible in onboarding, and the developer reaches the Tailscale admin console.
firstMeaningfulSuccess.observable_completion_signal: After the second device authenticates, both devices display; selecting Take me home opens the admin console.

boundary_evidence.official_label_or_terminal_state: The official Create a tailnet sequence ends after both devices display and the user is redirected to the admin console.

selectedSurface.name: Teleport Community Edition local Docker welcome screen
selectedSurface.selection_basis: Under the workshop policy, the official Community Edition local Docker route is primary because it is public, open source, no-account, and avoids the gated Enterprise Cloud signup. The guide provides exact Docker, TLS, install, configure, start, and welcome-screen checkpoint steps.

firstMeaningfulSuccess.official_milestone: Checkpoint: Verify Teleport is running
firstMeaningfulSuccess.normalized_outcome: A local Docker container runs Teleport Community Edition and serves the Teleport Web UI over HTTPS.
firstMeaningfulSuccess.observable_completion_signal: Opening `https://localhost:3080` displays the Teleport welcome screen.

boundary_evidence.official_label_or_terminal_state: Confirm that you can access the Teleport Web UI and see the welcome screen.

prerequisites.requirement: Docker is installed and can run containers.
mkcert is installed so a local certificate authority and localhost certificate can be created.
Local port 3080 is available to map to the container HTTPS listener.

stages.action: Open the Community Edition deployment guide and select Local Docker container.
stages.output: The local route and checkpoint are visible.

stages.action: Install mkcert, create `teleport-tls`, install the local CA, generate a localhost certificate, and copy `rootCA.pem` into the directory.

stages.failure_or_wait: Correct mkcert installation or certificate paths.

stages.failure_or_wait: Resolve Docker mount or port conflicts.

stages.action: Install curl, trust the copied mkcert CA, and run the official Teleport install script inside the container.
stages.success_signal: The `teleport` command is available.
stages.failure_or_wait: Check network access and CA file paths.

stages.action: Run `teleport configure` with cluster name localhost, public address localhost:443, and the mounted certificate and key.
stages.success_signal: Configuration completes without error.
stages.failure_or_wait: Correct certificate filenames and mount location.

stages.action: Run `teleport start --config="/etc/teleport.yaml"`.
stages.output: Teleport Auth, Proxy, and SSH services start.
stages.success_signal: The process remains running and listens through mapped port 3080.
stages.failure_or_wait: Review terminal logs for certificate or port errors.

stages.action: Open `https://localhost:3080`, accept the Community Edition terms, and verify the welcome screen.
stages.output: The Teleport Web UI welcome screen is visible.
stages.success_signal: The guide's Verify Teleport is running checkpoint passes.
stages.failure_or_wait: Check container status, port 3080, and Teleport logs.

developerGoal: Use Temporal's official Quickstart to set up a local development service and run the documented Hello World Workflow.

entryPoint.developer_intent: Use Temporal's official Quickstart to set up a local development service and run the documented Hello World Workflow.
assumed_prior_state: A supported workstation, terminal, editor, and outbound package-download access are available.
No Temporal account, Cloud Namespace, SDK, CLI, local service, Workflow, Activity, Worker, or language selection is assumed.

selectedSurface.name: Go SDK on macOS with local Hello World
selectedSurface.selection_basis: Workshop selection policy (2026-07-19): when official docs present peer routes, commit to the fastest and most commonly used documented route. Prefer the vendor's first-listed or recommended quickstart, dashboard/console over CLI when both are peers, and the route with an explicit vendor time claim or fewest documented steps when tied. Selected route: Go SDK on macOS with local Hello World. Go is the first language listed by the Quickstarts index, and the Go page provides a complete local setup, application, execution, and verification path. macOS Homebrew is one fully specified CLI installation branch. Prior surface before policy commit: Temporal local Hello World Quickstarts, with a Go and macOS candidate.

alternatives_considered.name: Java Hello World Quickstart
alternatives_considered.reason_not_primary: Java is a peer SDK with different runtime, build, Worker, and starter commands.

alternatives_considered.name: Python Hello World Quickstart
alternatives_considered.reason_not_primary: Python is a peer SDK and Temporal does not rank it below or above Go.

alternatives_considered.name: Temporal Cloud
alternatives_considered.reason_not_primary: The Quickstarts index explicitly separates local language quickstarts from the account, Namespace, and authentication path for Temporal Cloud.

firstMeaningfulSuccess.official_milestone: Run a Hello World Workflow locally.
firstMeaningfulSuccess.normalized_outcome: In the Go candidate, the local Worker processes the Workflow and Activity, the starter prints `Workflow result: Hello Temporal`, and the execution is visible in the local Web UI.
firstMeaningfulSuccess.observable_completion_signal: The terminal prints `Workflow result: Hello Temporal`, while Worker processing and Workflow details are observable in the Worker terminal and Web UI.

boundary_evidence.official_label_or_terminal_state: Run Hello World: Test Your Installation

firstMeaningfulSuccess.why_this_is_the_boundary: The documentation home names a local Hello World Workflow as the Quickstart outcome, and the Go page has a Verify Success section that defines the exact terminal output and UI evidence before later tutorials.

prerequisites.requirement: Choose one supported Temporal SDK language; the Quickstarts index does not select a universal language.
For the retained candidate, install Go and confirm it with `go version`; the tutorial was produced with Go 1.18.
Install Temporal CLI for the workstation operating system; the retained candidate uses Homebrew on macOS.
Keep the local Temporal development server and Worker running in separate terminal windows while executing the Workflow starter.

stages.action: Open Temporal Docs and follow Quickstart to the local language index.
stages.output: The local Hello World objective and eight SDK choices are visible.
stages.success_signal: Quickstarts says to choose a language to get started locally.
stages.failure_or_wait: Temporal Cloud is a separate path.

stages.action: Run `go version` and confirm Go is installed.
details: The tutorial was produced with Go 1.18 and shows `go1.18.1` as example output.
stages.success_signal: The command returns a Go version.
stages.failure_or_wait: Install Go before continuing if the command is unavailable.

stages.action: Run `mkdir goproject`, `cd goproject`, `go mod init my-org/greeting`, `go get go.temporal.io/sdk`, `go get go.temporal.io/sdk/client`, and `go mod tidy`.
stages.output: A Go module with the Temporal SDK and client dependencies
stages.success_signal: The module resolves and tidies its dependencies.
stages.failure_or_wait: No package-install recovery is documented on the page.

stages.action: Run `brew install temporal` and ensure `temporal` is on PATH.
stages.success_signal: The `temporal` command is available in a new terminal.
stages.failure_or_wait: Windows and Linux use separate archive-download and PATH branches.

stages.action: Open a new terminal and run `temporal server start-dev`, leaving it running.
stages.output: A local in-memory Temporal Service, default Namespace, and Web UI
stages.success_signal: The service listens on `localhost:7233` and the Web UI on `http://localhost:8233`.
stages.failure_or_wait: Pressing CTRL+C stops the service; the page documents `--ui-port` when a different Web UI port is needed.

stages.action: Create `activity.go` in package `greeting` with the published `Greet` Activity that returns `Hello <name>`.
stages.success_signal: The file defines `Greet(ctx, name)` and returns the formatted greeting.
stages.failure_or_wait: None documented for file creation.

stages.action: Create `workflow.go` with `SayHelloWorkflow`, a 10-second Activity StartToClose timeout, and `ExecuteActivity(...).Get(...)` returning the Activity result.
stages.success_signal: The Workflow returns the Activity result or its error.
stages.failure_or_wait: The Workflow returns an error if the Activity result cannot be obtained.

stages.action: Create `worker/main.go` with the published client, Worker on `my-task-queue`, Workflow and Activity registrations, and `w.Run(worker.InterruptCh())`.
stages.success_signal: The Worker connects and registers both handlers.
stages.failure_or_wait: The program exits with `Unable to create client` or `Unable to start worker` when those documented operations fail.

stages.action: In another terminal, run `go run worker/main.go` and leave the Worker polling.
stages.input: Running local service and Worker code
stages.success_signal: The Worker remains running and ready to process Workflow and Activity tasks.
stages.failure_or_wait: Client or Worker startup errors use the messages defined in the published code.

stages.action: Create `start/main.go` with the published client, Workflow ID `greeting-workflow`, task queue `my-task-queue`, `ExecuteWorkflow`, result wait, and result log.
stages.success_signal: The code logs IDs after start and waits for the Workflow result.
stages.failure_or_wait: The program exits with the documented client, execution, or result error when those operations fail.

stages.action: Run `go run start/main.go Temporal`.
stages.input: Running service, running Worker, starter code, and name argument `Temporal`
stages.output: A completed Hello World Workflow Execution
stages.success_signal: The starter prints `Workflow result: Hello Temporal`, the Worker processes the Workflow and Activity, and details appear in the local Web UI.
stages.failure_or_wait: The published starter logs fatal errors if it cannot create the client, execute the Workflow, or obtain the result.

branches.condition: Choose Java, PHP, Python, Ruby, TypeScript, .NET, or Rust
branches.path: Use that SDK's language-specific local Quickstart instead of the Go candidate.
branches.effect_on_first_success: Changes runtime, package manager, application structure, Worker, starter, and terminal output formatting while retaining a local Hello World Workflow.

branches.condition: Use Windows
branches.path: Download the matching amd64 or arm64 Temporal CLI archive, extract it, and add `temporal.exe` to PATH.
branches.effect_on_first_success: Replaces Homebrew installation with the Windows archive route.

branches.condition: Use Linux
branches.path: Download the matching Temporal CLI archive, extract it, and move the binary into PATH, such as `/usr/local/bin`.
branches.effect_on_first_success: Replaces Homebrew installation with the Linux archive route and may require elevated permission for the move.

branches.condition: Use Temporal Cloud
branches.path: Follow the separate Cloud getting-started path with an account, Namespace, endpoint, and authentication instead of the local development server.
branches.effect_on_first_success: Adds managed-account and Cloud resource setup and changes client connection details.

frictionGates.description: Eight SDK language routes are presented as peers and no universal SDK is selected.
Temporal CLI installation differs across macOS, Windows, and Linux.
The local Temporal Service must remain running while the Worker and starter execute.
The Worker must poll the same `my-task-queue` used by the starter before the Workflow can complete.
