excludedAfterSuccess: Completing all five sections of the in-Editor tutorial to create a simple playable game; this is the next learning milestone after the first project opens.
The optional 45-minute Unity Learn exercise completion and optional Unity Play or Learn-platform sharing, submission metadata, visibility, preview, CAPTCHA, and publishing.
Unity Essentials topics covering 2D and 3D fundamentals, programming, audio, cameras, visual effects, animation, lighting, UI, iteration, certification, and publishing.
Build-target modules, player builds, code signing, app stores, Unity Gaming Services, Cloud, Analytics, Ads, Multiplayer, source control, CI, deployment, and production operations.
doesNotProve: Platform records reconstruct official documentation. They do not contain product telemetry, conversion rates, observed completion times, or proven causes of abandonment.
Friction gates are documented requirements or transitions. They are not confirmed drop-off points.
Blocker taxonomy entries are candidate hypotheses. Individual reasons remain not diagnosis eligible unless separate case evidence supports them.
A documented journey analysis is not a diagnosis of a real developer attempt.

platform.name: Upsun
platform.organization: Platform.sh SAS doing business as Upsun

developerGoal: Create and deploy a first application to a live production environment on Upsun.

entryPoint.developer_intent: Create and deploy a first application to a live production environment on Upsun.
assumed_prior_state: The developer has or can create an Upsun account and first organization.
The developer has Git installed and basic command-line familiarity.
Despite the route's Start from zero label, the linked Setup Guide requires a local project that already runs in a supported runtime and is initialized as a Git repository.
The application can be represented by generated `.upsun/config.yaml` settings and has a command or framework behavior that serves HTTP traffic on the configured route.
The developer is authorized to provision a trial organization and resources.

selectedSurface.name: Create a project and deploy local code with the Upsun CLI
selectedSurface.selection_basis: Workshop selection policy (2026-07-19): when official docs present peer routes, commit to the fastest and most commonly used documented route. Prefer the vendor's first-listed or recommended quickstart, dashboard/console over CLI when both are peers, and the route with an explicit vendor time claim or fewest documented steps when tied. Selected route: Create a project and deploy local code with the Upsun CLI. The current Get Started page explicitly says its ordered steps follow Start from zero and lead to a live deployment as fast as possible. Prior surface before policy commit: Get Started with Upsun, Start from zero CLI path.

alternatives_considered.name: Bring your project
alternatives_considered.reason_not_primary: The quickstart presents this as a peer situation for an existing app, while its detailed ordered steps explicitly follow Start from zero.

alternatives_considered.name: Connect your repository
alternatives_considered.reason_not_primary: The quickstart presents GitHub, GitLab, and Bitbucket as a peer route and makes source integration optional after direct Upsun push.

alternatives_considered.name: Console project creation
alternatives_considered.reason_not_primary: The full guide labels Console recommended, but the concise quickstart gives the CLI command and the subsequent path is CLI-centered. This unresolved tension is preserved.

alternatives_considered.name: Upsun Fixed
alternatives_considered.reason_not_primary: Upsun Fixed is a distinct current offer and documentation surface. The roster names broad Upsun, whose current main developer documentation defaults to Upsun Flex-style resource configuration and the upsun CLI.

firstMeaningfulSuccess.official_milestone: Now that you have a live deployment
firstMeaningfulSuccess.normalized_outcome: Upsun has accepted the committed configuration, built and deployed the application to the default production environment, and allows requests to the application's primary environment URL.
firstMeaningfulSuccess.observable_completion_signal: The deployment activity succeeds, the production environment is Active, and `upsun environment:url --primary` opens or returns the environment's primary live URL.

boundary_evidence.official_label_or_terminal_state: Now that you have a live deployment

firstMeaningfulSuccess.why_this_is_the_boundary: The Get Started page labels step 5 Push and go live, then begins What's next with the statement that a live deployment now exists. Local development, source integration, scaling, domains, and monitoring follow the boundary.

prerequisites.requirement: An Upsun account, created through the linked free signup or already existing. The public docs do not reconstruct the current signup form and verification sequence.
A first organization, whose creation automatically activates a 15-day free trial with no credit card required.
Git installed locally and a shell capable of the operating-system-specific Upsun CLI installation.
A local project that runs with Node.js, PHP, Python, Go, Java, Ruby, or .NET. The detailed Setup Guide requires code even though the quickstart card says Start from zero.
A local Git repository with an initial commit, a main default branch, and dependency directories excluded from version control.

stages.action: Open Get Started with Upsun and select the Start from zero path.
stages.output: The ordered quickstart for a new project is visible.
stages.success_signal: The page shows Install CLI, Create project, Add config, Set resources, and Push and go live.
stages.failure_or_wait: Developers with an existing project, external repository, or AI agent can select a peer route instead.

stages.action: Initialize the local application as a Git repository if needed, stage the source, and create the initial commit.
stages.output: The local application has a committed Git history.
stages.success_signal: The initial commit exists.
stages.failure_or_wait: Git identity or repository errors are outside the Upsun guide.

stages.action: Add the application's dependency directory to `.gitignore`, then commit the ignore-file update.
stages.input: Use the runtime-specific documented example: `echo "node_modules" >> .gitignore` for JavaScript or Node.js, `echo "vendor" >> .gitignore` for PHP, or `echo "env" >> .gitignore` for Python; then run `git add .gitignore && git commit -m "Update .gitignore to ignore deps."`.
stages.output: The selected dependency directory is excluded from Git and that rule is committed.
stages.success_signal: A second commit contains the runtime-specific dependency-directory entry in `.gitignore`.
stages.failure_or_wait: The setup page places this instruction after the initial `git add .` and commit even while saying dependencies should never be committed. If dependencies were already present, the documented sequence does not explain how to remove them from the first commit. Other runtimes also require the developer to identify the appropriate dependency directory.

stages.action: Ensure the local default branch is main.
stages.input: `git branch -M main` when the current default differs.
stages.output: The local repository uses main as the branch expected by the guide.
stages.success_signal: The current branch is main.
stages.failure_or_wait: A different default such as master must also be selected during project creation if retained.

stages.action: Install the Upsun CLI for the local operating system.
stages.input: macOS: `brew install upsun/tap/upsun-cli`; Linux or WSL: `curl -fsSL https://raw.githubusercontent.com/upsun/cli/main/installer.sh | bash`; Windows: add the Upsun Scoop bucket and run `scoop install upsun`.
stages.success_signal: The CLI can be invoked from the shell.
stages.failure_or_wait: Installation method and PATH behavior vary by operating system; the concise quickstart links to the full setup guide.

stages.action: Run `upsun login`.
stages.output: The CLI begins browser-based Upsun authentication.
stages.success_signal: The CLI session becomes authenticated to the account.
stages.failure_or_wait: A new developer must complete the linked account signup first. The current public registration page requires JavaScript but does not expose signup fields, identity alternatives, verification, or consent ordering to the research client.

stages.action: If prompted during first project creation, create the first Upsun organization.
stages.input: Organization information required by the current Console or CLI prompt.
stages.output: An organization exists and its 15-day trial starts.
stages.success_signal: The organization can own one active trial project.
stages.failure_or_wait: The full project guide documents this first-organization prompt on the recommended Console route, not inside the CLI route. The separate organization guide lists label, organization URL, company name, country, and security contact for creating an organization, but the current first-organization field order remains undocumented.

stages.action: Run `upsun project:create` from the local project and follow the prompts.
stages.input: `upsun project:create`, then follow the prompts. The CLI guide specifically documents the default production branch and the later local-remote question; it does not enumerate the other current CLI fields or their order.
stages.output: A new Upsun project and production environment are created in the organization.
stages.success_signal: The project ID is available and the CLI asks whether to set the project as the repository remote.
stages.failure_or_wait: The precise current CLI prompt order is not reproduced in the docs. Project name and region are documented for the recommended Console branch, not as confirmed CLI prompts.

stages.action: Answer Yes when asked to set the new project as the remote for the local repository.
stages.input: `y` to `Set the new project <projectName> as the remote for this repository?`
stages.output: Upsun writes `.upsun/local/project.yaml` and adds the `upsun` Git remote.
stages.success_signal: The local source is linked to the new project.
stages.failure_or_wait: If the developer does not set the remote during creation, use `upsun project:set-remote <PROJECT_ID>` later.

stages.action: Run the initialization command from the project root.
stages.input: `upsun init` in the concise quickstart; the full configuration guide currently spells this `upsun project:init`.
stages.output: The CLI detects the framework or asks for a runtime, asks about services, and generates `.upsun/config.yaml` plus `.environment` when services require it.
stages.success_signal: The generated configuration defines applications, services, and routes.
stages.failure_or_wait: The generated starting configuration can require manual changes for the package manager, variables, build, deploy, workers, cron, or other app-specific requirements.

stages.action: Review and adjust `.upsun/config.yaml` so the selected application runtime, build hook, services, route, and upstream match the local app.
stages.input: The generated YAML and the local application's actual requirements.
stages.output: A valid Upsun configuration for the application.
stages.success_signal: Applications and route configuration name a matching app and a route for `https://{default}/`.
stages.failure_or_wait: Framework detection is not a guarantee of complete configuration. A missing `.upsun` directory or invalid YAML aborts the build on first push.

stages.action: Set CPU, RAM, and disk resources for the app.
stages.input: `upsun resources:set --size myapp:1`, replacing `myapp` if the generated app name differs.
stages.output: An explicit resource size is assigned to the application.
stages.success_signal: The resource update is accepted for the project.
stages.failure_or_wait: The full configuration guide also says default resources are assigned automatically at first deployment, creating tension over whether this concise step is strictly required.

stages.action: Stage and commit the generated Upsun configuration.
stages.output: The deployment configuration is part of the repository history.
stages.success_signal: A commit containing `.upsun/config.yaml` exists on main.
stages.failure_or_wait: If `.environment` or other generated required files are present, the full guide uses `git add .` so they are committed too.

stages.action: Run `upsun push`.
stages.input: `upsun push` from the linked local project.
stages.output: The committed source is sent to the Upsun remote and a deployment activity begins.
stages.success_signal: Upsun validates the configuration and begins building the configured application.
stages.failure_or_wait: Missing or invalid configuration aborts the build. Add or correct the required configuration files, then push again.

stages.action: Validate configuration, build container images, allocate application and service resources, run deployment hooks, start containers, and enable request traffic.
stages.input: The Git commit, `.upsun/config.yaml`, resource settings, and any generated environment configuration.
stages.output: A live production environment able to serve requests.
stages.success_signal: The deployment completes and the environment becomes Active, meaning it is deployed with services and data.
stages.failure_or_wait: A failed deployment does not establish the live-deployment milestone. Build cannot access services; deploy hooks can. No typical build duration is stated beyond the broad end-to-end quickstart claim.

stages.action: Open or retrieve the environment's primary URL.
stages.output: The primary URL for the production environment is returned or opened.
stages.success_signal: The deployed application responds at the primary URL and the quickstart's live-deployment boundary is observable.
stages.failure_or_wait: The general Get Started page declares the live-deployment milestone but does not include this verification command; the official Express tutorial documents the command. Application-specific recovery when the URL does not serve the intended app is not reconstructed by the selected general route.

stages.action: Open Get Started with Upsun and select the Start from zero path.
stages.output: The ordered quickstart for a new project is visible.
stages.success_signal: The page shows Install CLI, Create project, Add config, Set resources, and Push and go live.
stages.failure_or_wait: Developers with an existing project, external repository, or AI agent can select a peer route instead.

stages.action: Initialize the local application as a Git repository if needed, stage the source, and create the initial commit.
stages.output: The local application has a committed Git history.
stages.success_signal: The initial commit exists.
stages.failure_or_wait: Git identity or repository errors are outside the Upsun guide.

stages.action: Add the application's dependency directory to `.gitignore`, then commit the ignore-file update.
stages.input: Use the runtime-specific documented example: `echo "node_modules" >> .gitignore` for JavaScript or Node.js, `echo "vendor" >> .gitignore` for PHP, or `echo "env" >> .gitignore` for Python; then run `git add .gitignore && git commit -m "Update .gitignore to ignore deps."`.
stages.output: The selected dependency directory is excluded from Git and that rule is committed.
stages.success_signal: A second commit contains the runtime-specific dependency-directory entry in `.gitignore`.
stages.failure_or_wait: The setup page places this instruction after the initial `git add .` and commit even while saying dependencies should never be committed. If dependencies were already present, the documented sequence does not explain how to remove them from the first commit. Other runtimes also require the developer to identify the appropriate dependency directory.

stages.action: Ensure the local default branch is main.
stages.input: `git branch -M main` when the current default differs.
stages.output: The local repository uses main as the branch expected by the guide.
stages.success_signal: The current branch is main.
stages.failure_or_wait: A different default such as master must also be selected during project creation if retained.

stages.action: Install the Upsun CLI for the local operating system.
stages.input: macOS: `brew install upsun/tap/upsun-cli`; Linux or WSL: `curl -fsSL https://raw.githubusercontent.com/upsun/cli/main/installer.sh | bash`; Windows: add the Upsun Scoop bucket and run `scoop install upsun`.

stages.failure_or_wait: Installation method and PATH behavior vary by operating system; the concise quickstart links to the full setup guide.

stages.failure_or_wait: A new developer must complete the linked account signup first. The current public registration page requires JavaScript but does not expose signup fields, identity alternatives, verification, or consent ordering to the research client.

stages.failure_or_wait: The full project guide documents this first-organization prompt on the recommended Console route, not inside the CLI route. branches.condition: The developer already has an external Git repository
branches.path: Choose Connect your repo and link GitHub, GitLab, or Bitbucket so pushes and pull requests trigger deployments and live previews.
branches.effect_on_first_success: Replaces direct Upsun Git remote setup with external-provider authorization and integration configuration.

branches.condition: Operating system
branches.path: Use Homebrew on macOS, the installer script on Linux or WSL, or Scoop on Windows.
branches.effect_on_first_success: All supported branches converge on the `upsun` CLI.

branches.condition: The developer follows the full guide's recommended Console path
branches.path: Open the Upsun Console, create the first organization if prompted, choose Create project and Start from scratch under Deploy with Git, enter project name, local default branch, and region, then run `upsun project:set-remote <PROJECT_ID>` locally.
branches.effect_on_first_success: Replaces interactive CLI project creation but rejoins before configuration initialization.

branches.condition: Framework detection does not identify the application
branches.path: Select the runtime language when prompted, decide whether to add services, then review the generated YAML manually.
branches.effect_on_first_success: Adds required configuration choices before a valid first push.

branches.condition: The project environment uses manual deployment rather than the default automatic deployment
branches.path: The push is staged; explicitly trigger deployment through the Console Deploy button, CLI environment deploy command, or deployment API.
branches.effect_on_first_success: Adds an approval action between push and live deployment.

frictionGates.description: An Upsun account is required, but the linked public signup page's current atomic sequence is not documented.
A first organization must be created and this starts the time-limited trial.
The quickstart uses CLI creation while the full guide labels Console creation recommended.
Framework or runtime and optional services can require interactive choices during initialization.
Generated YAML is only a starting configuration and can require app-specific correction before deployment.
Resources allocated to the app and services determine usage charges after the no-card trial and are limited during trial.
A missing `.upsun` directory or invalid configuration aborts the first build. Add or correct the required files and push again.
Upsun validates, builds, allocates, deploys, starts, and routes traffic before the live URL is ready.
An Active environment can still return an application error. Use official 502 and development troubleshooting if the primary URL does not serve the intended app.

uncertainties.question: What local application does the general Start from zero quickstart expect a developer with no project to deploy?
uncertainties.impact: Application creation, framework choice, local dependencies, start command, and rendered terminal can add many undocumented steps.
uncertainties.reason_unresolved: The quickstart says no project yet, but its detailed setup requires local working code and only links multiple framework examples without selecting one.

uncertainties.question: What is the current account signup, identity, verification, and first-organization field order?
uncertainties.impact: The pre-CLI journey cannot be counted atomically from public documentation.
uncertainties.reason_unresolved: The official docs link the signup page and establish the first-organization and trial effects, but the live registration gate requires JavaScript and does not expose the authenticated continuation to the research client.

uncertainties.question: Is `upsun init` currently an alias for `upsun project:init`, and is explicit resource setting mandatory on the current trial?
uncertainties.impact: The exact command and number of required configuration actions may differ.
uncertainties.reason_unresolved: Current official concise and full guides use different command forms and differ on explicit versus default resource initialization.

developerGoal: Create a Vercel account, import a Git repository, and open the first hosted deployment.

entryPoint.developer_intent: Create a Vercel account, import a Git repository, and open the first hosted deployment.
assumed_prior_state: The developer begins before account creation with no platform resources or credentials.
A supported browser and internet connection are available.

selectedSurface.name: Dashboard Git deployment
selectedSurface.selection_basis: The dashboard imports and deploys a Git repository without requiring the Vercel CLI or a local deployment command.

alternatives_considered.name: Vercel CLI deployment
alternatives_considered.reason_not_primary: It requires local CLI installation and authentication; the dashboard Git path is fully hosted.

firstMeaningfulSuccess.official_milestone: Deploy a Git repository to Vercel
firstMeaningfulSuccess.normalized_outcome: Vercel builds the imported repository and serves a production deployment URL.
firstMeaningfulSuccess.observable_completion_signal: The deployment shows Ready and its generated URL loads the application.

boundary_evidence.official_label_or_terminal_state: The deployment shows Ready and its generated URL loads the application.

firstMeaningfulSuccess.why_this_is_the_boundary: This is the first observable end-to-end result on the selected official surface; later production configuration and expansion follow it.

prerequisites.requirement: A Vercel account.
A supported Git provider account with a deployable repository.
Permission for Vercel to access the selected repository.

stages.action: Create or sign in to Vercel.
stages.success_signal: The Vercel dashboard is available.
stages.failure_or_wait: Correct the documented configuration or prerequisite before continuing.

stages.action: Authorize the Git provider and choose Add New Project.
stages.success_signal: Vercel can list accessible repositories.
stages.failure_or_wait: Correct the documented configuration or prerequisite before continuing.

stages.action: Import the repository and review the detected framework and project settings.
stages.output: The project is ready to deploy.
stages.success_signal: The project is ready to deploy.
stages.failure_or_wait: Correct the documented configuration or prerequisite before continuing.

stages.action: Select Deploy.
stages.output: Vercel starts the build and deployment.
stages.success_signal: Vercel starts the build and deployment.
stages.failure_or_wait: Correct the documented configuration or prerequisite before continuing.

stages.action: Wait for the deployment to reach Ready.
stages.output: A production deployment URL is available.
stages.success_signal: A production deployment URL is available.
stages.failure_or_wait: Correct the documented configuration or prerequisite before continuing.

stages.action: Open the generated deployment URL.
stages.success_signal: The deployed application loads.
stages.failure_or_wait: Correct the documented configuration or prerequisite before continuing.

frictionGates.description: The Git provider must authorize Vercel to read the repository.
The build and deployment must complete before the URL is ready.

uncertainties.question: Git-provider authorization screens and build configuration vary by repository and framework.
uncertainties.impact: The exact number or ordering of required onboarding actions may vary for a fresh account.
uncertainties.reason_unresolved: Public official documentation does not fully expose every authenticated or account-dependent state.

developerGoal: Make a first Vonage API interaction without a preselected communications channel or API.

entryPoint.developer_intent: Make a first Vonage API interaction without a preselected communications channel or API.
assumed_prior_state: The developer has arrived after discovery.
No SMS, Voice, Video, Verify, Number Insight, or other API is selected.

selectedSurface.name: Send an SMS request with the Messages API
selectedSurface.selection_basis: Workshop selection policy (2026-07-19): when official docs present peer routes, commit to the fastest and most commonly used documented route. Prefer the vendor's first-listed or recommended quickstart, dashboard/console over CLI when both are peers, and the route with an explicit vendor time claim or fewest documented steps when tied. Selected route: Send an SMS request with the Messages API. The platform guide does not select an API, but the official Messages API SMS getting-started page and API reference define the setup and an explicit `Accepted` response with a message UUID. Prior surface before policy commit: Vonage API and channel not selected.

alternatives_considered.name: Messages API SMS
alternatives_considered.reason_not_primary: This requires Messages API account settings, a Vonage Application, JWT or Basic authentication, webhooks, and an SMS-specific sender and recipient.

alternatives_considered.name: Direct API sample requests
alternatives_considered.reason_not_primary: The platform guide recommends curl or Postman for experimenting but does not name one endpoint or response as the platform outcome.

firstMeaningfulSuccess.official_milestone: The selected Messages API route demonstrates first success when the response is `Accepted` and includes `message_uuid`.
firstMeaningfulSuccess.normalized_outcome: The response is `Accepted` and includes `message_uuid`.
firstMeaningfulSuccess.observable_completion_signal: The response is `Accepted` and includes `message_uuid`.

boundary_evidence.official_label_or_terminal_state: The response is `Accepted` and includes `message_uuid`.

firstMeaningfulSuccess.why_this_is_the_boundary: Selected under workshop fastest/most-used policy; terminal taken from the committed official candidate route.

prerequisites.requirement: A Vonage account provides an API key and secret; requests are charged per request, with initial free credit described.
A communications API, channel, and operation must be selected.

stages.action: Open the Vonage platform getting-started overview and review the common account, authentication, tool, webhook, and phone-number concepts.
details: The overview applies across a suite of APIs.
stages.output: Account setup plus API and tool choices.
stages.success_signal: No API result is produced at platform level.
stages.failure_or_wait: No wait is documented.

stages.action: Select the target API, operation, authentication mode, and curl, Postman, or SDK surface.
details: Some APIs require a Vonage Application and JWT; Basic auth is easier but does not support all features.
Phone-number operations use E.164-format digits without a leading plus.
stages.success_signal: No common terminal signal is documented.
stages.failure_or_wait: The platform guide cannot continue as one path without this choice.

stages.action: Sign up for a Vonage API account and open the Developer Dashboard.
details: Signup creates an API key and secret and provides initial free credit; API requests are charged per request.
stages.input: Developer identity and account signup details.
stages.output: Vonage account with API key, secret, and initial balance.
stages.success_signal: The Dashboard shows the account credentials and balance.
stages.failure_or_wait: Account access or insufficient balance blocks authenticated paid requests.

stages.action: Open Dashboard Settings and set SMS settings to `Messages API`.
details: This makes SMS webhooks use the format expected by the selected API.
stages.output: Account configured for Messages API SMS.
stages.success_signal: The SMS setting is saved as Messages API.
stages.failure_or_wait: Leaving the account in another SMS mode can produce webhook-format mismatch.

stages.action: Create a Vonage Application for Messages API credentials and configuration.
details: The application is the container for security and Messages API settings.
stages.success_signal: The application appears in the Developer Dashboard.
stages.failure_or_wait: Missing account permission or invalid application configuration prevents creation.

stages.action: Configure the application's message-status webhook and inbound-message webhook when those callbacks are required.
details: Webhook endpoints must be publicly reachable; Vonage recommends ngrok for development exposure.
stages.input: Public callback URLs or a deliberate no-webhook first request.
stages.success_signal: The application contains the intended callback URLs.
stages.failure_or_wait: A private or unreachable URL prevents later status or inbound callbacks, though the accepted API response remains a separate signal.

stages.action: Generate a JWT for the Vonage Application and prepare the `Bearer` Authorization header.
details: The Messages API supports JWT or Basic authentication, but not both; JWT supports webhook-enabled application features.
stages.input: Application credentials and a JWT generation tool or CLI.
stages.success_signal: A non-expired JWT is available for the Authorization header.
stages.failure_or_wait: Invalid application credentials, token generation, or an expired token causes authentication failure.

stages.action: Build the JSON request with `channel: sms`, `message_type: text`, message `text`, `from`, and `to`.
details: Sender and recipient use E.164 digits without a leading plus or international-access prefix.
Country-specific regulatory fields or an eligible sender can be additional gates.
stages.input: Message text, valid sender, recipient, and any required jurisdictional values.
stages.output: Valid Messages API SMS request body.
stages.success_signal: All required fields satisfy the API schema.
stages.failure_or_wait: Invalid number format, sender, balance, required field, or country regulation causes rejection.

stages.action: POST the JSON body to `https://{api-region}.nexmo.com/v1/messages` with the JWT and JSON content type.
details: Select the API region appropriate to the account and request.
stages.input: Endpoint, JWT, and valid SMS body.
stages.success_signal: The API accepts the request.
stages.failure_or_wait: Authentication, schema, balance, policy, sender, recipient, or region errors return a non-accepted response that must be corrected before retrying.

stages.action: Inspect the accepted response and capture its `message_uuid`.
details: The API reference labels the successful send response `Accepted` and returns the message UUID; later delivery status is a webhook-stage signal.
stages.success_signal: The response is `Accepted` and includes `message_uuid`.
stages.failure_or_wait: No UUID or a non-accepted response means the candidate has not reached its documented API terminal.

branches.condition: Messages API SMS is selected.
branches.path: Set account SMS mode to Messages API, create a Vonage Application, configure status and inbound webhooks as needed, choose JWT or Basic authentication, provide SMS channel, message type, text, sender, and E.164 recipient, then POST to the Messages API and inspect the API response and later delivery status.
branches.effect_on_first_success: Success becomes an accepted or delivered SMS, with application, credential, number, balance, and regulatory gates.

branches.condition: Another Vonage API is selected.
branches.path: Follow that API's official documentation from account credentials through its first documented operation and response.
branches.effect_on_first_success: Authentication, prerequisites, cost, and observable completion signal change.

frictionGates.description: Account signup creates the API key and secret and exposes balance management.
The API, channel, operation, tool, and authentication method must be chosen.
API access is charged per request, with initial free credit and top-up described.
Messages API SMS additionally requires account mode, application, and webhook configuration.

uncertainties.question: Which Vonage API and terminal outcome should represent the platform?
uncertainties.impact: The complete steps range from a simple authenticated lookup to application, webhook, number, policy, and delivery flows.
uncertainties.reason_unresolved: The general official guide explicitly requires the developer to choose an API.

developerGoal: Create a Vultr account, provision a first general-purpose Linux Cloud Compute instance, and verify interactive shell access.

entryPoint.developer_intent: Create a Vultr account, provision a first general-purpose Linux Cloud Compute instance, and verify interactive shell access.
assumed_prior_state: The developer has a working email address, or a GitHub or Google account for the documented OAuth signup branch.
The developer can provide an accepted payment method and accept Vultr's Terms of Service and Privacy Policy.
The local machine has a terminal and OpenSSH client for the primary connection path.
The developer is authorized to create a billable Cloud Compute resource or use an applicable promotional credit.

selectedSurface.name: Deploy a Linux Cloud Compute instance in the Vultr Console and verify it over OpenSSH
selectedSurface.selection_basis: Workshop selection policy (2026-07-19): when official docs present peer routes, commit to the fastest and most commonly used documented route. Prefer the vendor's first-listed or recommended quickstart, dashboard/console over CLI when both are peers, and the route with an explicit vendor time claim or fewest documented steps when tied. Selected route: Deploy a Linux Cloud Compute instance in the Vultr Console and verify it over OpenSSH. The account guide directs a new user to a first server, the current provisioning guide supplies the general Console workflow, and the OpenSSH guide supplies exact default-credential and `whoami` verification steps. Prior surface before policy commit: Create a Vultr account, deploy a Cloud Compute instance, and connect with OpenSSH.

alternatives_considered.name: Sign up with GitHub or Google
alternatives_considered.reason_not_primary: This is a valid shorter identity branch, but it still converges on profile completion and billing before the first deployment.

alternatives_considered.name: Deploy through the Vultr API
alternatives_considered.reason_not_primary: The provisioning guide documents API as a peer interface, but it adds API-key handling and request construction before a first instance.

alternatives_considered.name: Deploy through Vultr CLI
alternatives_considered.reason_not_primary: The CLI is a peer deployment interface and requires local CLI installation and API credentials, while the Console path is fully documented after signup.

alternatives_considered.name: Deploy through Terraform
alternatives_considered.reason_not_primary: Terraform is appropriate for infrastructure as code but adds provider, credential, and configuration work before the first instance.

alternatives_considered.name: Vultr Console connection
alternatives_considered.reason_not_primary: The built-in noVNC console is a documented alternative and recovery route. OpenSSH is the primary remote-access procedure in the connection documentation and ends with an explicit `whoami` verification command.

firstMeaningfulSuccess.official_milestone: Verify the active SSH identity with `whoami`
firstMeaningfulSuccess.normalized_outcome: Vultr has provisioned the developer's first Cloud Compute instance, and the developer can open its Linux shell and verify the logged-in user.
firstMeaningfulSuccess.observable_completion_signal: The SSH session accepts the instance credentials and `whoami` prints the current remote username.

boundary_evidence.official_label_or_terminal_state: Verify the active SSH identity with `whoami`

firstMeaningfulSuccess.why_this_is_the_boundary: The account guide says the user can deploy a first server after billing setup, the provisioning guide ends with Deploy, and the OpenSSH guide ends its default-credentials procedure by running `whoami`. Installing an application or configuring a production workload occurs after this first usable compute success.

prerequisites.requirement: A deliverable email address and a strong password, or a GitHub or Google identity for the documented provider-signup branch.
Acceptance of the Terms of Service and Privacy Policy during registration and the applicable terms presented during billing setup.
An accepted payment method for account verification. The credit-card route can link a card with a $0.00 deposit, while other documented methods require a small deposit.
A terminal with OpenSSH installed for the primary remote connection path.
The username and password shown on the instance Overview page, or a private key matching a public key selected during deployment.

stages.action: Open How to Create a Vultr Account and follow its registration path.
stages.output: The official account creation and billing sequence is visible.
stages.success_signal: The page lists registration, email verification, billing, and the transition to deploying a first server.
stages.failure_or_wait: An existing account holder can sign in and skip to the compute deployment step.

stages.action: Enter an email address and strong password, review the Terms of Service and Privacy Policy, and select Create free account.
stages.input: Email, password, and acceptance of the linked legal terms.
stages.output: A pending Vultr account registration exists and a verification message is sent.
stages.success_signal: The interface instructs the developer to verify the supplied email address.
stages.failure_or_wait: Invalid credentials, unmet password requirements, unavailable email, or failure to accept the terms prevents progression. GitHub or Google signup is a separate branch.

stages.action: Open the Vultr verification message and select Verify Your E-mail.
stages.input: Access to the mailbox used for registration.
stages.output: The email address is verified and the flow opens Billing.
stages.success_signal: The developer lands on the Billing page.
stages.failure_or_wait: The guide does not document delivery time or recovery behavior when the verification message is missing.

stages.action: Optionally enter a valid promotional coupon, review the billing terms, and submit the payment-linking or deposit action.
stages.input: Optional coupon, required agreement acknowledgement, and the selected payment action.
stages.output: Billing setup and account verification complete.
stages.success_signal: The account can access Vultr services and deploy a first server.
stages.failure_or_wait: An invalid or ineligible coupon does not establish universal credit. Billing verification must still complete.

stages.action: Open Products, choose Compute, and select Deploy.
stages.input: An active Vultr account with completed billing verification.
stages.success_signal: The Shared CPU product option and instance configuration controls are visible.
stages.failure_or_wait: The account guide does not advance to service use until payment details have been added.

stages.action: Choose Shared CPU for the general first Cloud Compute instance.
stages.input: Shared CPU from the available compute products.
stages.output: The deployment form is scoped to shared virtual CPU plans.
stages.success_signal: Location, plan, and software configuration become selectable.
stages.failure_or_wait: The provisioning guide selects Shared CPU for this Console path; it does not name a universal first workload or plan.

stages.action: Leave the optional SSH Keys, Startup Script, and Firewall Group settings unselected for the default-credentials route used by this primary path.
stages.input: No optional SSH key, startup script, or firewall group for this primary path.
stages.output: The instance remains on the default-credentials branch documented by the OpenSSH guide.
stages.success_signal: No SSH key, startup script, or firewall group is selected before continuing.
stages.failure_or_wait: If the SSH-key branch is chosen instead, the key must be added during deployment. The OpenSSH guide warns that adding one through the Console after deployment wipes the instance to install it.

stages.action: Set the server hostname and label.
stages.input: A hostname and an account-facing server label.
stages.output: The instance has its system and Console identities.
stages.success_signal: Both required fields contain the entered values.
stages.failure_or_wait: The provisioning guide does not document hostname or label validation rules.

stages.action: Review additional options and retain a public IP for the primary direct-SSH path.
stages.input: Instance(s) with Public IP with the default Public IPv4 setting, plus any deliberately selected optional VPC, backup, DDoS Protection, Limited User Login, or Cloud-Init settings.
stages.output: Networking and optional service behavior are set for the instance.
stages.success_signal: Instance(s) with Public IP is selected and Public IPv4 remains enabled.
stages.failure_or_wait: A private-only instance requires a different network path. Limited User Login changes the documented login user to `linuxuser`.

stages.action: Review the configuration and select Deploy.
stages.input: Location, plan, image, authentication, hostname, label, network, and any optional settings.
stages.output: Vultr begins provisioning the billable Cloud Compute instance.
stages.success_signal: Once the instance is available, its management page exposes the Overview information and default credentials used by the connection guide.
stages.failure_or_wait: The general provisioning and connection guides do not publish a universal provisioning duration or named readiness status. The FAQ confirms that a created instance remains billable even when stopped.

stages.action: Open the instance management page and copy the username, server IP address, and password shown in Overview.
stages.output: The exact default login values for this operating system are available locally.
stages.success_signal: The developer has the username, IP address, and password required by the OpenSSH guide.
stages.failure_or_wait: The username can vary by image and Limited User Login selection, so it should be copied rather than assumed. An SSH-key deployment uses the matching private key instead.

stages.action: Run `ssh username@SERVER-IP` in a terminal and accept the host key by entering `yes` when first prompted.
stages.input: The copied username and server IP, plus the host-key confirmation.
stages.output: OpenSSH attempts an encrypted connection to the instance.
stages.success_signal: The remote host presents the documented authentication prompt.
stages.failure_or_wait: The connection guide does not enumerate OpenSSH failure causes or a readiness wait. The built-in Vultr Console is the documented alternate access route.

stages.action: Enter the copied password, then run `whoami` in the remote shell.
stages.input: The Overview password and the `whoami` command.
stages.output: An authenticated interactive shell on the first instance.
stages.success_signal: `whoami` prints the active remote username, completing the documented first-use boundary.
stages.failure_or_wait: If this instance was deployed with an SSH key instead, use the documented `ssh -i /path/to/private/key username@SERVER-IP` branch. The built-in Vultr Console is the other documented access route.

branches.condition: Signup identity
branches.path: Select Sign Up with GitHub or Sign Up with Google, authenticate with the provider, and complete the requested user profile instead of creating email-password credentials directly.
branches.effect_on_first_success: The identity step changes, but the path still converges on Billing before resource creation.

branches.condition: Payment method
branches.path: Link a credit card with a $0.00 deposit, or use another available payment method that requires a small deposit.
branches.effect_on_first_success: The verification charge or deposit differs, but billing setup remains a required pre-deployment gate.

branches.condition: Workload characteristics
branches.path: Choose Cloud Compute, High Frequency, or High Performance and then a suitable displayed plan.
branches.effect_on_first_success: The documented categories differ in CPU and storage characteristics. Cloud Compute is the primary balance-of-price-and-performance selection.

branches.condition: Image source
branches.path: Use Marketplace, ISO or iPXE, ISO Library, Backup, or Snapshot instead of a standard Operating System image.
branches.effect_on_first_success: Adds artifact-specific prerequisites and can change credentials, boot behavior, and the meaning of first success.

branches.condition: Authentication preference
branches.path: Select a stored SSH public key during deployment and later connect with `ssh -i /path/to/private/key username@SERVER-IP` instead of the generated password.
branches.effect_on_first_success: Removes password entry after key setup, but adds prior key generation and upload. Adding the key after deployment through Console is destructive to the instance.

branches.condition: Login privilege
branches.path: Enable Limited User Login and use `linuxuser` instead of the image's default privileged username.
branches.effect_on_first_success: Changes the connection username while preserving the same SSH success signal.

branches.condition: Network exposure
branches.path: Deploy without a public IP behind private NAT and connect through an appropriate private-network access route.
branches.effect_on_first_success: Direct internet SSH no longer works and the path requires additional networking that the primary quick path excludes.

branches.condition: Automation interface
branches.path: Provision the equivalent instance through the Vultr API, Vultr CLI, or Terraform using the guide's documented alternatives.
branches.effect_on_first_success: Replaces Console configuration with credentials, local tooling, or request/configuration code, then converges on instance connection.

branches.condition: OpenSSH is unavailable or fails
branches.path: Enable browser popups, open View Console on the instance management page, enter the Overview username, paste the password using Send Clipboard, and press Enter.
branches.effect_on_first_success: Reaches the instance through the built-in noVNC terminal instead of its public SSH service.

frictionGates.description: Email verification is mandatory for the direct registration path before billing setup opens.
A payment method is required before the account can deploy services. The card route can use a $0.00 deposit but can trigger a temporary authorization hold; other methods require a small deposit.
Promotional credit is conditional. Coupon eligibility and value cannot be treated as universal account behavior.
The first compute order requires location, plan-category, and plan decisions before the user has workload measurements.
Multiple image sources and operating-system versions add choice, although the standard Operating System branch is sufficient for first shell access.
SSH key, startup script, and firewall group are optional deployment choices. The docs warn that adding an SSH key in Console after deployment wipes the instance and its data to install the key.
The built-in Vultr Console provides documented browser terminal access without SSH.
Stopping an instance does not stop normal charges. The FAQ says charges stop when the instance is destroyed.

uncertainties.question: How long does a generic Cloud Compute instance take to become ready for its first connection?
uncertainties.impact: The wait between Deploy and usable Overview credentials cannot be included in a verified total time-to-success estimate.
uncertainties.reason_unresolved: The current general provisioning and connection guides document the actions and readiness-dependent connection, but do not publish a universal duration or generic status transition.

uncertainties.question: Which promotional credit, if any, applies to every signup entry point and account eligibility profile?
uncertainties.impact: The effective first-use cost and cleanup urgency vary between users.
uncertainties.reason_unresolved: The generic account guide describes optional coupons and credits for up to 30 days, while the GitHub or Google guide presents a specific $250 over 30 days offer without establishing universal eligibility.

uncertainties.question: Which plan, Linux image, and location should be treated as the universal tutorial default?
uncertainties.impact: The exact choices, price, capacity, username, and provisioning behavior differ across valid first instances.
uncertainties.reason_unresolved: The provisioning guide requires the developer to choose among current regional options and does not name one canonical combination.

excludedAfterSuccess: Installing or deploying an application on the Cloud Compute instance.
Creating production users, hardening SSH, or rotating the generated credentials.
Configuring DNS, domains, TLS, load balancing, object storage, block storage, backups, monitoring, or private networking.
Automating future deployments with API, CLI, Terraform, startup scripts, or Cloud-Init.
Destroying the evaluation instance to stop billing after the workshop measurement.
doesNotProve: Platform records reconstruct official documentation. They do not contain product telemetry, conversion rates, observed completion times, or proven causes of abandonment.
Friction gates are documented requirements or transitions. They are not confirmed drop-off points.
Blocker taxonomy entries are candidate hypotheses. Individual reasons remain not diagnosis eligible unless separate case evidence supports them.
A documented journey analysis is not a diagnosis of a real developer attempt.

platform.name: Weaviate Cloud
platform.organization: Weaviate LLC for United States registration; Weaviate B.V. otherwise

developerGoal: Start Weaviate and Ollama locally with Docker Compose, then create and populate the Movie collection with Python.

entryPoint.developer_intent: Start Weaviate and Ollama locally with Docker Compose, then create and populate the Movie collection with Python.
assumed_prior_state: No Weaviate Cloud account, organization, cluster, API key, endpoint, collection, billing instrument, local client, or data is assumed.
The developer has an email address and can create a password, but the complete current signup inputs and validations are not assumed.
The developer is authorized to accept the self-serve terms for the selected registration country and organization.
Python and pip are available locally for the selected language tab; installation itself was not executed during research.
No account, agreement, cluster, credential, package installation, API request, or data import was performed during research.

selectedSurface.name: Locally hosted Weaviate with Docker Compose and Python
selectedSurface.selection_basis: Under the workshop policy, the official local Docker quickstart takes priority over Weaviate Cloud signup. It requires no account, starts Weaviate and Ollama with Docker Compose, and preserves the same explicit three-object Movie import terminal.

alternatives_considered.name: Weaviate Cloud Jeopardy quickstart
alternatives_considered.reason_not_primary: This route is longer, downloads a separate dataset, and has no positive Python import message, while the current database quickstart has an explicit three-object import terminal.

alternatives_considered.name: Cluster readiness check
alternatives_considered.reason_not_primary: A True value or HTTP 200 proves connectivity but the quickstart itself says more meaningful operations follow.

alternatives_considered.name: Built-in Query tool
alternatives_considered.reason_not_primary: The selected quickstart explicitly uses an official client to create and populate a collection.

alternatives_considered.name: Weaviate Cloud free cluster
alternatives_considered.reason_not_primary: The managed route requires account, cluster, and API-key creation; policy prioritizes the complete no-account local Docker quickstart.

alternatives_considered.name: Shared Cloud cluster
alternatives_considered.reason_not_primary: Shared Cloud is paid and requires billing, while the free cluster requires no card.

alternatives_considered.name: Pre-computed vectors
alternatives_considered.reason_not_primary: The quickstart labels vectorization during import as recommended; pre-computed vectors add a model and vector-generation prerequisite.

firstMeaningfulSuccess.official_milestone: Step 1: Create a collection and import data.
firstMeaningfulSuccess.normalized_outcome: A local Movie collection contains three objects vectorized by the local Ollama embedding model.
firstMeaningfulSuccess.observable_completion_signal: The Python program prints `Imported & vectorized 3 objects into the Movie collection`.

boundary_evidence.official_label_or_terminal_state: Imported & vectorized 3 objects into the Movie collection

firstMeaningfulSuccess.why_this_is_the_boundary: This is the first explicit successful data operation in the local quickstart. Semantic search and RAG follow as later steps.

prerequisites.requirement: Install Docker with Docker Compose.
Use a modern computer with at least 8 GB RAM; 16 GB or more is preferred.
Have Python and pip available.

stages.action: Open the locally hosted Docker quickstart.
stages.output: The Docker Compose and Python route is visible.
stages.success_signal: Start Weaviate and Ollama with Docker Compose is reached.

stages.action: Save the published Compose definition as `docker-compose.yml`.
details: It runs Weaviate 1.38.1 and Ollama 0.12.9, enables anonymous local access, and configures the Ollama vectorizer and generator modules.
stages.output: A local Compose project is configured.
stages.success_signal: The file defines `weaviate` and `ollama` services.

stages.action: Start both services and pull the two documented Ollama models.
stages.input: docker-compose up -d; docker compose exec ollama ollama pull nomic-embed-text; docker compose exec ollama ollama pull llama3.2
stages.output: Weaviate and Ollama run locally with the embedding and generative models.
stages.success_signal: Both model pulls complete successfully.
stages.failure_or_wait: Wait for containers to start and model downloads to finish.

stages.action: Install the official Python client.
stages.success_signal: pip completes successfully.

stages.action: Run the published Python example that connects locally, creates `Movie`, and imports the three sample objects with `text2vec-ollama`.
details: The objects are The Matrix, Spirited Away, and The Lord of the Rings: The Fellowship of the Ring.
stages.output: Three objects are stored and vectorized.
stages.success_signal: The program prints `Imported & vectorized 3 objects into the Movie collection`.
stages.failure_or_wait: Confirm Weaviate and Ollama are reachable and the embedding model is present.

branches.condition: Registration country is the United States
branches.path: Accept the self-serve terms with Weaviate LLC as the contracting entity.
branches.effect_on_first_success: Changes the contracting entity without changing the documented post-account technical sequence.

branches.condition: Registration country is outside the United States or another supplied mechanism applies
branches.path: Accept the self-serve terms with Weaviate B.V. as the contracting entity.
branches.effect_on_first_success: Changes the contracting entity without changing the documented post-account technical sequence.

branches.condition: Select a cloud provider other than the GCP default
branches.path: Choose the available provider in the free-cluster form before creation.
branches.effect_on_first_success: Adds a provider decision but does not change the published client import sequence.

branches.condition: Change advanced configuration
branches.path: Configure auto schema generation or allow all CORS origins before cluster creation.
branches.effect_on_first_success: Adds optional policy choices that are not required by the selected quickstart.

branches.condition: Choose JavaScript/TypeScript, Go, Java, or C#
branches.path: Install the corresponding official client and use that language tab's Movie collection and import sample.
branches.effect_on_first_success: Changes local tooling and the terminal output shape; Python is selected because its success line is explicit.

branches.condition: Import pre-computed vectors
branches.path: Choose the alternative import path and provide objects with externally generated vectors.
branches.effect_on_first_success: Adds an embedding-generation prerequisite and bypasses the recommended Weaviate Embeddings path.

branches.condition: Use readiness as an earlier technical checkpoint
branches.path: Run the published client readiness check for True or curl the ready endpoint for HTTP 200.
branches.effect_on_first_success: Proves the cluster is reachable but stops before the meaningful collection-and-data operation selected here.

branches.condition: Choose Shared Cloud
branches.path: Configure billing and create a paid production-ready cluster.
branches.effect_on_first_success: Adds billing and paid-resource decisions that the free route avoids.

frictionGates.description: The account guide delegates registration to an interactive frame, and the public signup response exposes only a client-rendered shell, so the complete registration contract is not public in prose or static response.
The registration country or equivalent mechanism determines whether the agreement is with Weaviate LLC or Weaviate B.V.
Free-cluster provisioning typically takes 1 to 3 minutes; readiness is shown by a checkmark next to the cluster name.
Weaviate Cloud may append a random suffix to the chosen cluster name to ensure uniqueness.
A newly created API key is displayed only once; the documented recovery is to copy or download and store it securely immediately.
Forgot Password sends a reset email, and its link remains valid for only five minutes.
The documentation says snippets target the latest client and database versions and directs failures or feedback to a GitHub issue; installing the latest version is part of the selected route.
After seven days of inactivity the free cluster is suspended with data preserved; the documented recovery is reactivation from the console.

developerGoal: Obtain the required Workday developer and tenant access, choose an API or app surface, and define one first authenticated business-object or application result.

entryPoint.developer_intent: Obtain the required Workday developer and tenant access, choose an API or app surface, and define one first authenticated business-object or application result.

No API family, functional service, endpoint, user or integration identity, OAuth flow, or first data result is supplied.

selectedSurface.name: Invite an Enterprise Developer Site user and connect App Builder to a Workday Development tenant
selectedSurface.selection_basis: Workshop selection policy (2026-07-19): when official docs present peer routes, commit to the fastest and most commonly used documented route. Prefer the vendor's first-listed or recommended quickstart, dashboard/console over CLI when both are peers, and the route with an explicit vendor time claim or fewest documented steps when tied. Selected route: Invite an Enterprise Developer Site user and connect App Builder to a Workday Development tenant. The official Enterprise user-management and App Builder connection guides form a bounded access-gated route with a green connection indicator as the documented terminal result. Prior surface before policy commit: Unresolved at developer program access, product surface, tenant, API family, endpoint, and OAuth flow.

alternatives_considered.name: REST API Explorer
alternatives_considered.reason_not_primary: It requires selecting one of many functional services and versions, a tenant, endpoint, OAuth scope, and user security.

alternatives_considered.name: Graph API Explorer

alternatives_considered.name: SOAP API
alternatives_considered.reason_not_primary: It uses XML, service/version selection, different integration contexts, and possibly Integration System User credentials.

prerequisites.requirement: Access to the Workday Developer Site through a Personal or Enterprise developer arrangement or company invitation.
Access to a Workday development or customer tenant containing the data and features required by the selected route.
The endpoint's OAuth scope plus the Workday domain or business-process security permissions for the acting user or Integration System User.

stages.action: Open Workday's API overview and Developer Site resources.
details: The overview presents REST, SOAP, and Graph APIs for different transaction and data-exchange styles.
The Developer Site also supports building Extend and Integration apps.
stages.success_signal: REST, SOAP, Graph, Extend, and Integration paths can be compared.
stages.failure_or_wait: None documented.

stages.action: Choose direct API use, an Extend app, or an Integration app.
details: REST is positioned for small, user-initiated self-service transactions with JSON.
SOAP is positioned for high-volume scheduled system-to-system work.
Graph returns selected data in one query.
Extend builds apps that run on Workday.
Orchestrate builds event-driven integrations.
stages.input: Business workflow, data volume, interaction model, UI needs, and system boundaries.
stages.success_signal: The surface matches the use case.
stages.failure_or_wait: The roster provides no use case, so this choice is unresolved.

stages.action: Obtain Developer Site and tenant access for the selected surface.
details: Workday Extend customers receive Workday Cloud Platform Development tenants that host apps and API explorers.
Enterprise companies add Developer Site users by company administrator invitation and email setup link.
Orchestrate access can require subscription agreement checks, innovation-services opt-in, company creation, and designated administrator.
stages.input: Eligible Workday customer, partner, Personal Account, enterprise company, or administrator invitation.
stages.output: Authenticated Developer Site account and accessible development or customer tenant.
stages.success_signal: The developer can open the relevant console, App Builder, API Explorer, or tenant.
stages.failure_or_wait: Enrollment, licensing, subscription, invitation, MFA, administrator, and tenant-provisioning steps depend on organization and are not represented by one public self-service flow.

stages.action: Choose the API service, version, endpoint or app tutorial, and acting identity.
details: The REST explorer lists many finance, HR, platform, student, analytics, and miscellaneous services with separate versions.
The selected endpoint determines OAuth scopes and Workday security domains.
Interactive user and Integration System User routes return different authorized data.
stages.input: Functional area, desired business object or workflow, tenant data, and user or system actor.
stages.output: One endpoint or executable app tutorial with required scopes and policies.
stages.success_signal: Service, version, operation, identity, and expected response are known.
stages.failure_or_wait: No generic Workday endpoint represents every tenant or product.

stages.action: Follow the selected guide's request, build, deploy, or orchestration steps and verify its terminal result.
details: A direct API path sends the chosen request and inspects tenant data.
An Extend path builds through App Builder/App Hub and deploys to a development tenant for preview.
An Integration app path builds and executes an Orchestrate workflow.
stages.input: Resolved tenant, permission, authentication, tooling, and concrete operation.
stages.success_signal: The endpoint returns authorized data or the selected app/integration reaches its documented final state.
stages.failure_or_wait: Research stops here rather than fabricating tenant data, a business endpoint, or enterprise access.

stages.action: Confirm that the company has eligible Enterprise Developer Site access, a provisioned Workday Cloud Platform Development tenant, and a company administrator who can invite the developer.
details: Workday documents the Development tenant as an entitlement for eligible Extend customers, not a public self-service resource.
stages.input: The required Workday commercial arrangement, company, administrator, and Development tenant.
stages.success_signal: The administrator can open company user management and the Development tenant is available for assignment.
stages.failure_or_wait: Contract, enrollment, administrator designation, or tenant provisioning can require organizational coordination and waiting before this public workflow begins.

stages.action: Open the Developer Site Console, go to Users, and select Add a User.
details: Only a company administrator performs this Enterprise user invitation.
stages.success_signal: The Add a User fields are visible.
stages.failure_or_wait: A non-administrator cannot create the company user.

stages.action: Enter the developer's first name, last name, company email, and company role, then select Add User.
details: The email domain must match the company's domain.
stages.input: Developer identity, matching company email, and appropriate role.
stages.success_signal: The new user is added and Workday initiates the setup email.
stages.failure_or_wait: An email outside the company domain or an invalid role blocks correct provisioning.

stages.action: Deliver the Workday Developer Site account-setup email to the invited developer.
details: The email contains the setup link required to activate access.
stages.success_signal: The invited developer receives the setup link.
stages.failure_or_wait: Email delivery is an explicit wait; without the invitation message the developer cannot activate the account.

stages.action: Open the setup link and complete the Developer Site access setup described by the invitation.
details: The Manage Users guide documents this link as setting up Developer Site access; its separate MFA reset action applies to an existing user and is not part of the invitation setup sequence.
stages.input: The account-setup email and its setup link.
stages.output: Developer Site access for the invited enterprise user.
stages.success_signal: The setup link completes the user's Developer Site access setup.
stages.failure_or_wait: If the setup link cannot be used, access remains incomplete; the guide does not decompose a recovery flow.

stages.action: Sign in to the Workday Developer Site and open App Builder.
details: App Builder is the documented surface used to connect to the Development tenant.
stages.input: The enterprise user with completed Developer Site access setup.
stages.success_signal: The App Builder workspace opens.
stages.failure_or_wait: Account, entitlement, or session errors prevent the connection controls from loading.

stages.action: Open the tenant connection control represented by the cylinder and lightning icon.
details: The same connection workflow can be initiated from API Explorers or App Builder.
stages.success_signal: API Explorers and App Builder connection targets are visible.
stages.failure_or_wait: A missing connection option indicates that the expected tenant entitlement or assignment is unavailable.

stages.action: Select App Builder as the connection target.
details: The official sequence requires choosing App Builder after opening the cylinder and lightning connection control.
stages.output: The App Builder Development tenant selection dialog.
stages.success_signal: The dialog exposes the available Development tenant choice.
stages.failure_or_wait: Selecting API Explorers leaves this App Builder candidate route.

stages.action: Select the Development tenant, choose Bypass SSO only if required, and confirm with OK.
details: Bypass SSO is an optional documented selection, not a universal requirement.
stages.input: The assigned Development tenant and the applicable Bypass SSO choice.
stages.output: A submitted App Builder tenant connection.
stages.success_signal: The connection dialog closes after OK and App Builder evaluates the connection.
stages.failure_or_wait: A wrong tenant or Bypass SSO choice can leave the documented connection inactive or needing attention.

stages.action: Verify that App Builder shows the Development tenant connection with a green status dot.
details: Workday documents green as connected, orange as a session needing attention, and inactive as not connected.
stages.output: A confirmed active Development tenant connection.
stages.success_signal: The connection indicator is green.
stages.failure_or_wait: An orange or inactive indicator means the documented terminal state has not been reached and the session or authentication must be repaired.

branches.condition: The selected route is REST API exploration.
branches.path: Open the REST API Explorer, choose a functional service and highest appropriate production version, select an endpoint whose data exists in the tenant, inspect its description for OAuth scope and security domain, connect or sign in to the development tenant, obtain or copy an access token through the supported flow, fill tenant, path, and query parameters, send the request, and verify the JSON data array, total, object fields, or endpoint-specific response.
branches.effect_on_first_success: Defines success as one tenant-specific REST response and requires actual endpoint and data choices.

branches.condition: An Enterprise company administrator invites the developer.
branches.path: In Developer Site Console open Users, click Add a User, enter company email, first name, last name, and company role, click Add User, wait for the email, follow its setup link, complete account and MFA setup, then sign in. The email domain must match the company domain.
branches.effect_on_first_success: Adds an administrator handoff and invitation wait before the developer can reach tools.

branches.condition: The selected external Extend app uses client credentials.
branches.path: Create an API client in Console with name, secure redirect URI if applicable, CORS domains, and endpoint scopes; save the client ID and secret; in Workday create an Integration System User; create a Client Credentials Mapping from the client ID to that user; configure domain security; create a POST to the token endpoint; Base64-encode Client ID:Client Secret; set Authorization to ID plus the encoded value; set an x-www-form-urlencoded body with grant_type=client_credentials and tenant_alias; send; and verify the returned access token.
branches.effect_on_first_success: Defines success as an OAuth access token for service-to-service Extend REST access, not business data.

branches.condition: The developer connects App Builder to a WCP Development tenant.
branches.path: Open App Builder, click the cylinder/lightning icon, choose API Explorers or App Builder, select a Development tenant, optionally select Bypass single sign-on, click OK, and verify the connection dot is green.
branches.effect_on_first_success: Defines an intermediate success as active tenant connectivity; the session later becomes orange near expiration and then inactive.

branches.condition: The selected path is Orchestrate for Integrations and the company is not enabled.
branches.path: Determine MSA versus UMSA through Workday Community; if MSA, in the production tenant open Innovation Services and Data Selection Opt-In, select WCP and Workday Orchestrate for Integration, click Next, Next, and OK; run Maintain Workday Build Company for Orchestrate for Integration; designate administrator name and email; then let that administrator create Developer Site users before starting the Orchestrate tutorial.
branches.effect_on_first_success: Adds subscription, security, production-tenant administrator, opt-in, company, and invitation gates.

branches.condition: The selected route is SOAP.
branches.path: Choose service and version in SOAP API Explorer, determine Extend versus Integration app versus traditional integration authentication, obtain the correct bearer token or integration identity, create POST to the API Gateway SOAP URL, set Content-Type text/xml, add the SOAP envelope, add Authorization Bearer, send, and inspect the XML response.
branches.effect_on_first_success: Changes protocol, payload, endpoint, and authentication context from REST or Graph.

frictionGates.description: REST, SOAP, Graph, Extend apps, and Integration apps are materially different starting surfaces.
Developer Site and Workday tenant access are required for owned authenticated data or app paths.
Company administrator invitation, Workday customer entitlement, subscription agreement, or innovation-services opt-in may be required.
Enterprise user onboarding uses an emailed setup link and companies manage MFA resets.
Tenant, Developer Site, App Builder, API Explorer, or Orchestrate access depends on company and security roles.
The developer must select a functional service, version, endpoint, tenant, and data-bearing identity.
OAuth scope alone is insufficient; Workday domain and business-process security policies also govern access.
API client ID, secret, redirect/CORS settings, user or ISU identity, and access token vary by OAuth flow.
Authorization code, client credentials, initiating-user token, ISU, or other documented flows must match the app context.
A selected tenant must contain business objects visible to the acting user for a meaningful response.
