developerGoal: Reach the first documented WorkOS success after selecting WorkOS as the platform.

entryPoint.developer_intent: Reach the first documented WorkOS success after selecting WorkOS as the platform.
assumed_prior_state: Discovery is complete and the developer has selected WorkOS.
The developer has not chosen a WorkOS product or first-success outcome.
The developer has not chosen the AuthKit AI Installer, a manual framework guide, or an additional-product quickstart.

selectedSurface.name: AuthKit AI Installer on a supported existing project
selectedSurface.selection_basis: Workshop selection policy (2026-07-19): when official docs present peer routes, commit to the fastest and most commonly used documented route. Prefer the vendor's first-listed or recommended quickstart, dashboard/console over CLI when both are peers, and the route with an explicit vendor time claim or fewest documented steps when tied. Selected route: AuthKit AI Installer on a supported existing project. The AuthKit AI Installer is prominently offered on the WorkOS docs home and documents a complete automated path from framework detection through build validation and the AuthKit is ready terminal. It avoids the manual Node.js guide's unresolved CSRF_SECRET generation gap, but remains a representative AuthKit route rather than a platform-wide default over eleven peer products. Prior surface before policy commit: WorkOS product documentation and AuthKit route selector.

alternatives_considered.name: AuthKit AI Installer
alternatives_considered.reason_not_primary: It is a one-command AuthKit route and claims about two minutes, but requires an existing supported project and is not the default for non-AuthKit products.

alternatives_considered.name: AuthKit manual Node.js
alternatives_considered.reason_not_primary: It is a complete hosted-authentication journey with explicit validation, but WorkOS also offers five other manual start guides and fifteen installer-supported frameworks.

alternatives_considered.name: Single Sign-On
alternatives_considered.reason_not_primary: It integrates an existing authentication stack and ends at an enterprise SSO flow, not a new AuthKit user.

alternatives_considered.name: Directory Sync
alternatives_considered.reason_not_primary: It has a directory, webhook, and user-provisioning outcome rather than interactive sign-in.

alternatives_considered.name: Additional WorkOS products
alternatives_considered.reason_not_primary: Admin Portal, Audit Logs, RBAC, FGA, Vault, Pipes, Feature Flags, Widgets, and Domain Verification each expose separate setup and success boundaries.

firstMeaningfulSuccess.official_milestone: The AuthKit is ready message and npm run dev instruction appear, and the developer understands the files, routes, middleware, and environment changes.
firstMeaningfulSuccess.normalized_outcome: The developer chooses a WorkOS product and route, configures the staging environment and credentials required by that route, executes its first workflow, and verifies the product-specific result.
firstMeaningfulSuccess.observable_completion_signal: For the fully inspected AuthKit Node.js route, the local app redirects through hosted AuthKit, creates and authenticates a user, returns to the app with a sealed session and welcome email, and lists that user in the Dashboard Users section.

boundary_evidence.official_label_or_terminal_state: The AuthKit is ready message and npm run dev instruction appear, and the developer understands the files, routes, middleware, and environment changes.

firstMeaningfulSuccess.why_this_is_the_boundary: Selecting a product changes the required resources, credentials, external systems, API calls, waits, and terminal verification. AuthKit is visually prominent, but treating it as the universal WorkOS journey would omit eleven peer products.

prerequisites.requirement: A WorkOS Dashboard account and team workspace are required; each account belongs to exactly one team, and each team starts with one project.
The developer needs a Dashboard role allowed to configure staging resources and API keys. Admin and Developer have full access; Sandbox Developer can manage staging but is read-only in production.
Most server and API routes require the environment-scoped WorkOS API key; AuthKit also requires the application Client ID. Staging supplies a pre-generated, repeatedly viewable API key.
Runtime and SDK depend on the selected route. AuthKit's AI Installer requires Node.js 20+ and a supported project; the manual Node.js route requires an Express-capable Node project and npm.
The staging environment is free and permits localhost HTTP. Production requires billing information, but production is outside the first staging success.

The manual Node.js AuthKit logout example reads a CSRF_SECRET environment variable through csrf-csrf. The official guide requires that configuration in code but does not prescribe its value, length, or generation procedure.

stages.action: Open the WorkOS documentation landing page and inspect the product-level routes.
details: AuthKit is foregrounded with a Quick Start and npx command.
The same page separately lists SSO, Directory Sync, Admin Portal, Audit Logs, RBAC, FGA, Vault, Pipes, Feature Flags, Widgets, and Domain Verification.
stages.success_signal: The product inventory and separate outcomes are visible.
stages.failure_or_wait: No runtime wait occurs at documentation arrival.

stages.action: Choose the WorkOS product and its documented starting route.
details: For AuthKit, choose the AI Installer and CLI or a manual Next.js, Remix, Node.js, Ruby, Python, or React guide.

The installer itself supports Next.js, React, React Router, TanStack Start, SvelteKit, Node.js/Express, Vanilla JS, Python/Django, Ruby/Rails, Go, PHP, Laravel, .NET/ASP.NET Core, Kotlin, and Elixir/Phoenix.
stages.input: Product outcome, existing stack, framework, and preference for automatic or manual integration
stages.success_signal: One product, implementation route, and terminal outcome are selected.
stages.failure_or_wait: WorkOS does not identify one platform-wide default, so the canonical journey cannot proceed without this human decision.

stages.action: Open an existing project supported by the AuthKit installer and verify Node.js 20 or later.
details: Supported integrations include Next.js, React, React Router, TanStack Start, SvelteKit, Node.js/Express, Vanilla JS, Python/Django, Ruby/Rails, Go, PHP, Laravel, .NET/ASP.NET Core, Kotlin, and Elixir/Phoenix.
stages.success_signal: The shell is in the project and Node.js 20+ is available.
stages.failure_or_wait: If framework detection is unsupported or ambiguous, use a manual guide or rerun the installer with --integration and an explicit supported integration.

stages.action: Run npx workos@latest install from the project root.
stages.success_signal: The CLI begins framework detection and opens or requests WorkOS authentication.
stages.failure_or_wait: Package download, Node version, network, or filesystem errors must be corrected and the command rerun; use --debug for more detail.

stages.action: Complete the browser handoff to create or sign in to the WorkOS account and authorize the CLI for the intended staging project.
details: Staging is free and supports localhost HTTP; production billing is outside this candidate.
stages.success_signal: The browser returns control to the CLI and it can read the WorkOS project configuration.
stages.failure_or_wait: Account, team-role, browser callback, or connectivity problems must be resolved before installation can continue; workos doctor is the documented diagnostic route.

stages.action: Allow the installer to detect the project's framework and integration shape.
stages.success_signal: The CLI identifies a supported framework and presents the planned setup.
stages.failure_or_wait: If detection fails or chooses incorrectly, stop and rerun with --integration to select the correct route explicitly.

stages.action: Review and confirm the installer prompts for redirect URI, CORS, and homepage URL for the local application.
stages.input: Actual local application origin and callback path
stages.success_signal: The chosen URLs match the application's local runtime.
stages.failure_or_wait: Incorrect callback, CORS, or homepage values cause login return, browser-origin, or post-login navigation failure and must be corrected.

stages.action: Allow the installer to install the matching WorkOS SDK.
stages.success_signal: The dependency is added to the project's package or dependency manifest.
stages.failure_or_wait: Package-manager, lockfile, network, or compatibility errors stop installation and must be corrected.

stages.action: Allow the installer to analyze the project and create or update the callback routes, authentication middleware or provider wrappers, and environment configuration appropriate to the framework.
stages.input: Project structure and WorkOS staging application
stages.output: Generated AuthKit integration code and secrets configuration
stages.success_signal: The CLI reports the expected files and configuration changes.
stages.failure_or_wait: Unsupported project patterns, conflicting existing auth code, or unwritable files require manual review, correction, or a framework-specific guide.

stages.action: Let the installer run its build validation.
stages.success_signal: The project passes the installer's build validation.
stages.failure_or_wait: Fix pre-existing or integration-related build errors, rerun with --debug if needed, and repeat until validation passes.

stages.action: Confirm the CLI displays AuthKit is ready and Run npm run dev to get started, then inspect git diff before accepting the generated integration.
details: The documented terminal establishes installer completion, not successful signup or sign-in.
stages.output: Reviewed AuthKit integration at the documented installer terminal
stages.success_signal: The AuthKit is ready message and npm run dev instruction appear, and the developer understands the files, routes, middleware, and environment changes.
stages.failure_or_wait: Unexpected or unsafe changes must be corrected or reverted before running the app; the installer result alone does not prove user authentication.

branches.condition: AuthKit manual Node.js is selected.
branches.path: Create or sign in to the WorkOS account and use the staging environment; retrieve the staging API key and application Client ID; prepare an Express Node project; install @workos-inc/node, csrf-csrf, and cookie-parser; in Dashboard Redirects set http://localhost:3000/callback as the default redirect URI, configure a sign-out redirect, and register the application's /login sign-in endpoint; store WORKOS_API_KEY and WORKOS_CLIENT_ID as managed environment secrets; create index.html with sign-in and POST sign-out controls plus CSRF token fetching; create the Express server and WorkOS client; add /login to generate an AuthKit authorization URL with provider authkit, callback, and client ID, then redirect to hosted sign-in; add /callback to reject a missing code and exchange the returned code, which is valid for 10 minutes, for a User; generate a 32-character cookie password with openssl rand -base64 32 or another secure generator and store WORKOS_COOKIE_PASSWORD; update callback authentication to seal the session and set an HTTP-only, secure, sameSite lax wos-session cookie, retrying /login on failure; load and authenticate the sealed session on / and render Welcome plus the user email; add protected-route middleware that redirects a missing session to login, refreshes an invalid session, updates the cookie and retries the original URL, or clears the cookie and returns to login on refresh failure; configure CSRF_SECRET because the documented doubleCsrf setup reads process.env.CSRF_SECRET, while preserving the unresolved fact that the guide supplies no value, length, or generation instruction for it; add /csrf-token and implement POST /logout with double-CSRF protection; clear the cookie and redirect through WorkOS logout, noting missing sign-out redirect causes an error; run node server.js; open localhost:3000; sign up; sign in; verify the welcome state and the new user in Dashboard Users.
branches.effect_on_first_success: Produces the inspected hosted-authentication success and includes every guide heading through validation.

branches.condition: AuthKit AI Installer is selected.
branches.path: Use an existing supported project with Node.js 20+; run npx workos@latest install; let the CLI detect the framework, open a browser for WorkOS authentication, configure redirect URI, CORS, and homepage URL, install the matching SDK, analyze the project, create callback routes and auth middleware or provider wrappers, write environment configuration, and run a build validation; review the displayed AuthKit is ready state and inspect git diff. If detection fails, rerun with --integration; if build validation fails, fix pre-existing build errors and use --debug; use workos doctor for unresolved configuration or connectivity problems.
branches.effect_on_first_success: Automates dashboard and code changes and claims about two minutes to the documented AuthKit is ready installer terminal. Running the application and proving user authentication remain unverified after that terminal.

branches.condition: AuthKit manual Next.js, Remix, Ruby, Python, or React is selected.
branches.path: Follow that framework-specific guide's account, staging key and Client ID, package installation, redirect, sign-in endpoint, secret configuration, authorization URL, callback exchange, session, protected route, sign-out, local execution, signup, sign-in, and Dashboard user-verification actions. Framework file structure, SDK, session mechanism, and development command differ from Node.js.
branches.effect_on_first_success: Retains the AuthKit user outcome while replacing every implementation-specific action.

branches.condition: Single Sign-On is selected.
branches.path: Use the staging environment and Test Organization, install and initialize the chosen WorkOS SDK with API key and Client ID, create an SSO authorization endpoint and callback that exchanges the code for a normalized profile, configure the callback in the application's Redirects tab, then use Dashboard Test SSO to exercise the flow end-to-end and verify the returned user profile. The exact SAML or OIDC customer connection and Admin Portal onboarding are later production work.
branches.effect_on_first_success: Integrates an existing authentication stack and ends at a test enterprise SSO profile rather than an AuthKit-created user.

branches.condition: Directory Sync is selected.
branches.path: Choose Directory Sync, use staging and its test directory or provider setup, create or select an organization and directory, configure an event or webhook receiver, connect the directory, ingest directory user and group events, and verify synced records in the Dashboard or API. Provider admin setup and propagation can introduce external approval and wait gates.
branches.effect_on_first_success: Ends at provisioned directory data, not login.

branches.condition: Admin Portal is selected.
branches.path: Create or select a WorkOS organization in staging, configure portal intent and return behavior, generate an Admin Portal link with the server API or SDK, open the link as the organization's IT administrator, complete the chosen self-service setup, and verify the resulting resource in the WorkOS Dashboard.
branches.effect_on_first_success: Adds an organization and customer-admin browser journey before a connection exists.

branches.condition: Audit Logs is selected.
branches.path: Choose Audit Logs, create an organization, initialize the server SDK with the staging key, construct and send the first audit event with actor, action, targets, context, and occurred-at data, then verify it through the WorkOS Dashboard or events API before configuring exports or streams.
branches.effect_on_first_success: Ends at an ingested audit event rather than authentication.

branches.condition: RBAC or Fine-Grained Authorization is selected.
branches.path: Choose the authorization model, define the first permission and role or FGA resource and relationship, assign it to a test subject, call the relevant authorization check from the application with staging credentials, and verify the documented allow result plus a denied control case. Modeling choices precede code and materially change the journey.
branches.effect_on_first_success: Ends at an authorization decision and requires policy modeling rather than user authentication.

branches.condition: Vault is selected.
branches.path: Choose Vault, obtain staging credentials, create the first secret with its access context through the Dashboard, SDK, or API, retrieve it through an authorized server-side call, and verify that unauthorized access is rejected before adding rotation or production policy.
branches.effect_on_first_success: Ends at encrypted secret storage and retrieval.

branches.condition: Pipes is selected.
branches.path: Choose Pipes, create the required organization and provider connection configuration, generate or open the customer authorization experience, authorize a third-party account, handle WorkOS events or returned connection data, and verify the connected account can be used by the application.
branches.effect_on_first_success: Adds an external provider OAuth journey and ends at a connected third-party account.

branches.condition: Feature Flags is selected.
branches.path: Choose Feature Flags, create the first flag in the project, define its initial targeting or rollout, initialize the documented client or server evaluation path, evaluate for a test context, and verify the expected enabled or disabled value before production rollout.
branches.effect_on_first_success: Ends at a deterministic flag evaluation rather than an identity event.

branches.condition: Widgets is selected.
branches.path: Choose the relevant enterprise workflow widget, create the prerequisite user, organization, roles, or API-key configuration, generate a short-lived client token server-side, embed the widget with that token, and verify its first rendered and completed workflow.
branches.effect_on_first_success: Adds server token issuance and a client UI component tied to an existing WorkOS resource model.

branches.condition: Domain Verification is selected.
branches.path: Choose Domain Verification, create or select an organization and domain, generate the verification challenge, publish the required DNS record at the external DNS provider, trigger or wait for verification, and confirm the domain becomes verified. Incorrect DNS records or propagation add retry and wait paths.
branches.effect_on_first_success: Ends at a verified customer-domain claim and requires external DNS control.

branches.condition: A raw WorkOS API or Postman route is selected.
branches.path: Use the pre-generated staging API key and Client ID, keep the sk_ key server-side, choose an endpoint with all required resource IDs, send it over HTTPS with Bearer authentication, and verify its JSON response. Missing or incorrect authentication returns 401, insufficient permissions return 403, and plain HTTP fails. For Postman, fork or import the official collection and environment, populate api_key, client_id, base_url, and request-specific IDs, select the environment, and send the request.
branches.effect_on_first_success: Bypasses a product UI quickstart but still requires a product-specific resource and endpoint outcome.

frictionGates.description: Twelve top-level products define different first-success outcomes, and AuthKit adds automatic versus manual and framework choices.
A WorkOS account belongs to exactly one team and is required to access the staging project, Dashboard, and personalized credentials.
Dashboard role controls whether the developer can manage keys and environment resources; Sandbox Developer is intentionally staging-only.
The staging API key is environment-scoped and powerful; AuthKit also needs a Client ID, while the manual session path adds cookie and CSRF secrets.
Auth and SSO routes require exact redirect, sign-in, sign-out, CORS, and homepage values; missing sign-out redirect is a documented logout error.
Every SDK path requires the selected framework runtime, package manager, and code changes; the AI Installer additionally analyzes and writes the existing project.
Staging is free, but production remains locked until billing information is added and is excluded from first staging success.

uncertainties.question: Which WorkOS product defines the canonical workshop journey?
uncertainties.impact: The choice changes the success outcome itself, from authentication to provisioning, logs, policy, secrets, connection, rollout, widget, or DNS verification.
uncertainties.reason_unresolved: The official docs expose twelve product families without one platform-wide default.

uncertainties.question: If AuthKit is chosen, should the workshop use the AI Installer or a manual framework guide?
uncertainties.impact: The installer analyzes and edits the project and configures Dashboard settings automatically; manual guides expose each action and friction point for participant scoring.
uncertainties.reason_unresolved: WorkOS offers both as co-equal starting paths and supports many frameworks.

uncertainties.question: What exact signup and initial team-creation screens appear for a new WorkOS account?
uncertainties.impact: They may add identity fields, email verification, workspace naming, invitations, or anti-abuse checks before staging access.
uncertainties.reason_unresolved: Official docs require an account and describe the resulting team and project model but do not enumerate the dynamic signup screens.

uncertainties.question: What value and generation procedure should the manual Node.js AuthKit path use for CSRF_SECRET?
uncertainties.impact: The documented logout implementation reads this environment value, so omitting it can break CSRF-token generation or logout protection.
uncertainties.reason_unresolved: The official Node.js guide references process.env.CSRF_SECRET in doubleCsrf configuration but does not state a value, required length, or generation command. The separate documented openssl command applies only to WORKOS_COOKIE_PASSWORD and must not be reused by inference.

excludedAfterSuccess: Selecting a canonical WorkOS product without workshop-owner direction
Running the generated application, completing signup or sign-in, and confirming a user in Dashboard Users after the documented AuthKit is ready installer terminal
Production unlock, billing, HTTPS domains, one-time production keys, and live customer traffic
AuthKit branding, MFA, passkeys, social login, magic auth, organizations, invitations, roles, and production session hardening after first login
SSO customer connection onboarding and production launch after Test SSO
Directory provider-specific production onboarding and full lifecycle handling after first synced record
Advanced authorization modeling, secret rotation, audit exports, connection refresh, rollout analytics, and widget customization
Additional projects, environments, workspace security, team invitations, and enterprise contracting
doesNotProve: Platform records reconstruct official documentation. They do not contain product telemetry, conversion rates, observed completion times, or proven causes of abandonment.
Friction gates are documented requirements or transitions. They are not confirmed drop-off points.
Blocker taxonomy entries are candidate hypotheses. Individual reasons remain not diagnosis eligible unless separate case evidence supports them.
A documented journey analysis is not a diagnosis of a real developer attempt.

developerGoal: Create a Zendesk Support development trial, enter Agent Workspace as an admin or agent, execute the promoted browser-console Ticketing API quickstart, and verify the three generated tickets.

entryPoint.developer_intent: Create a Zendesk Support development trial, enter Agent Workspace as an admin or agent, execute the promoted browser-console Ticketing API quickstart, and verify the three generated tickets.
assumed_prior_state: The developer has no Zendesk Support account or subdomain.
The developer has a work email, company context, phone number, and a supported browser but no CLI, SDK, OAuth client, API token, or application code.

The developer does not have an administrator who can prepare an existing organization account; the new trial owner is expected to receive admin access, but the account form does not document the provisioning transition.

selectedSurface.name: Sunshine Platform: 10-minute Ticketing API quick start
selectedSurface.selection_basis: Workshop selection policy (2026-07-19): when official docs present peer routes, commit to the fastest and most commonly used documented route. Prefer the vendor's first-listed or recommended quickstart, dashboard/console over CLI when both are peers, and the route with an explicit vendor time claim or fewest documented steps when tied. Committed to already-reconstructed surface `Sunshine Platform: 10-minute Ticketing API quick start`. Prior basis: The current Zendesk Developer Documentation home presents two consecutive first-mile calls to action: create a free 14-day developer trial and Create your first ticket through a 10-minute API quickstart. The linked quickstart uses only the signed-in Agent Workspace and the browser JavaScript console, then verifies three created tickets in the console and Zendesk view.

alternatives_considered.name: Using the Ticketing API
alternatives_considered.reason_not_primary: This longer Python and OAuth tutorial reads groups, updates a ticket, and creates a ticket; the documentation home explicitly promotes the 10-minute browser-console route as Create your first ticket.

alternatives_considered.name: Zendesk Apps Framework quickstart
alternatives_considered.reason_not_primary: The documentation home presents this as a separate 15-minute app-framework path after the general developer-account and first-ticket route.

alternatives_considered.name: Messaging SDK, Help Center, Voice, Custom Data, Sales CRM, webhooks, and integration paths
alternatives_considered.reason_not_primary: These are equally real product surfaces, but the documentation home explicitly labels the Ticketing API route Create your first ticket and places it directly after developer-account creation.

firstMeaningfulSuccess.official_milestone: Create your first ticket
firstMeaningfulSuccess.normalized_outcome: Use the JavaScript console inside a signed-in Zendesk Agent Workspace to execute the official loop of three Create Ticket API POST requests and verify the resulting Test ticket 1, Test ticket 2, and Test ticket 3 records.
firstMeaningfulSuccess.observable_completion_signal: The browser console lists three returned ticket objects; after reloading Agent Workspace and opening Views > Recently updated tickets, the three new test tickets appear.

boundary_evidence.official_label_or_terminal_state: The developer-documentation home labels the route Create your first ticket and calls it a 10-minute API quickstart. The quickstart's successful terminal checks are returned JavaScript ticket objects and the tickets visible under Recently updated tickets, before troubleshooting, cleanup, and next steps.

firstMeaningfulSuccess.why_this_is_the_boundary: The first created ticket is the homepage's named platform milestone, while the quickstart deliberately creates three in one snippet and provides two verification surfaces. Deleting test data and exploring other APIs occur after the demonstrated success.

prerequisites.requirement: A Zendesk Support account with agent or administrator access; Zendesk offers a free 14-day trial for testing.
For the current nine-step trial form: work email, first name, last name, phone number, company country, company name, job title, company employee-count band, chosen Zendesk subdomain, and password.
Administrator authority is required to activate first-time API access settings; the quickstart itself permits an admin or agent role for the browser-console request.

The quickstart says API password access must be enabled, but current Zendesk help says team-member password API access is deprecated and unsupported for new accounts; this is a documented contradiction rather than a reliably satisfiable new-trial prerequisite.
Chrome, Firefox, Safari, or Microsoft Edge with a built-in JavaScript console and scripts not blocked by browser settings or plugins.
Network access to the Zendesk trial, the assigned .zendesk.com subdomain, Agent Workspace, and the same-origin Ticketing API.

stages.failure_or_wait: None documented.

stages.action: Choose a Zendesk subdomain.
details: The chosen value forms the account's <subdomain>.zendesk.com address used for Agent Workspace and same-origin API calls.
stages.success_signal: The form accepts the subdomain and advances to password creation.
stages.failure_or_wait: An unavailable or invalid subdomain requires another choice; exact rules are not displayed in the crawled form.

stages.action: Create a password and select Create your Zendesk account.
details: This is the last of the form's nine labeled steps.
stages.input: Completed registration fields and new password.
stages.success_signal: Zendesk accepts the registration submission.
stages.failure_or_wait: Password policy, anti-abuse, email verification, terms, and provisioning transitions are not fully exposed in the static form or developer account guide.

stages.action: Provision the free 14-day Zendesk Support trial and any required identity confirmation.
details: The developer docs establish the trial as the testing account but do not document the post-submit sequence.
stages.output: Reachable Support instance with trial owner access.
stages.success_signal: The developer can sign in at the chosen Zendesk subdomain as an administrator or agent.
stages.failure_or_wait: Provisioning duration, email confirmation, automated screening, and possible sales or setup interstitials are not documented.

stages.action: Sign in to the Zendesk Support instance as the trial admin or an agent and enter Agent Workspace.
details: Run the quickstart from the Agent Workspace page itself so its relative /api/v2/tickets.json requests use the Zendesk origin and signed-in browser session.
stages.success_signal: Agent Workspace is loaded in the browser.
stages.failure_or_wait: A 404 from the quickstart script can indicate that the developer is not signed in.

stages.action: As administrator, open Admin Center > Apps and integrations > APIs > API configuration for first-time API activation.
details: Current help says first-time API access requires accepting the Zendesk Terms of Service and Application Developer and API Agreement.
stages.success_signal: The terms checkbox and Get started action are available if API access is not already active.
stages.failure_or_wait: Agents without admin authority need an administrator to perform this setup.

stages.action: Read and accept the Zendesk terms and Application Developer and API Agreement, then select Get started.
details: This activates the account's API configuration surface.
stages.input: Authority to accept agreements for the trial account.
stages.success_signal: API access settings become available.
stages.failure_or_wait: The developer may lack organizational authority to accept the agreements.

stages.action: Evaluate the quickstart's stated API password access prerequisite against the current account.
details: The quickstart says API password access must be enabled.
Current help says team-member password API access is deprecated and unsupported in new accounts, while its remaining password control is for end users.
The quickstart code provides no email, password, token, or OAuth header and instead runs inside the signed-in Agent Workspace.
stages.input: Current trial API settings and current documentation.
stages.success_signal: No authoritative current page explains whether the same-origin browser-session request needs any API access toggle beyond initial terms activation.
stages.failure_or_wait: Following the quickstart literally may be impossible on a new account because the named team-member password setting is unavailable.

stages.action: Open the browser's JavaScript console from Agent Workspace.
details: Chrome: View > Developer > JavaScript Console.
Firefox: Tools > Browser Tools > Web Developer Tools, then Console.
Safari: Develop > Show JavaScript Console.
Microsoft Edge: Tools > Developer > JavaScript Console.
stages.input: Signed-in Agent Workspace in a supported browser.
stages.output: JavaScript console attached to Agent Workspace.
stages.success_signal: The console prompt is available on the Zendesk page.
stages.failure_or_wait: Browser policies may disable developer tools.

stages.action: Paste the official three-iteration ticket-creation script into the JavaScript console.
details: The loop assigns Test ticket 1 through Test ticket 3 subjects and corresponding bodies.
Each $.ajax request posts JSON to /api/v2/tickets.json with contentType application/json and logs data.ticket when complete.
stages.input: Official quickstart snippet and Agent Workspace console.
stages.success_signal: The complete loop appears at the console prompt.
stages.failure_or_wait: Pasting altered syntax or running outside Agent Workspace can cause JavaScript or ajax errors.

stages.action: Press Enter to run the script.
details: The loop issues three HTTP POST requests to the Create Ticket endpoint.
stages.input: Prepared script and authenticated same-origin session.
stages.success_signal: Zendesk accepts each request and creates a ticket.
stages.failure_or_wait: 404 means confirm sign-in; TypeError $.ajax not a function means run from Agent Workspace; $.ajax undefined means check browser settings or plugins that block scripts.

stages.action: Verify the console lists the returned tickets as JavaScript objects.
details: Expand each object to inspect its ticket properties.
stages.output: Direct API-response proof for three created tickets.
stages.success_signal: Three ticket objects appear in the JavaScript console.
stages.failure_or_wait: If any object is absent, inspect console or network errors and apply the documented troubleshooting before rerunning; rerunning can create duplicates.

stages.action: Reload the Agent Workspace browser tab and open Views > Recently updated tickets.
details: This checks the created resources in the Zendesk product rather than only the API response.
stages.success_signal: Test ticket 1, Test ticket 2, and Test ticket 3 are visible in Agent Workspace.
stages.failure_or_wait: View filters, asynchronous refresh, permissions, or failed requests can prevent the expected tickets from appearing.

branches.condition: The developer already has a Zendesk Support account or sandbox with agent or admin access.
branches.path: Skip the 14-day registration and sign into that instance, subject to its administrator's API configuration and permission policies.
branches.effect_on_first_success: Eliminates the nine-step trial form and uncertain provisioning transition.

branches.condition: The developer is creating a Zendesk Marketplace application and needs a durable sponsored test instance.
branches.path: Create a Support trial, rename its subdomain with the d3v- prefix, submit the Sponsored Account Request Form, and wait for Zendesk to confirm conversion; sponsored Support permits up to five agents and cannot be used for real support.
branches.effect_on_first_success: Adds subdomain change, partner eligibility, external form, manual approval, and an undocumented wait, so it is not the fastest first-ticket route.

branches.condition: The user has only a non-agent end-user role.
branches.path: Obtain agent or administrator access from the account owner before using the selected Ticketing API quickstart.
branches.effect_on_first_success: Adds a role-assignment and possible administrator wait gate.

branches.condition: The developer switches to an externally authenticated curl, Postman, SDK, or application request rather than the same-origin browser-session quickstart.
branches.path: Use OAuth as the durable current route, or temporarily enable and generate API tokens where still supported; Zendesk says password authentication was removed in January 2026 and API tokens begin removal July 28, 2026 with final shutdown April 30, 2027.
branches.effect_on_first_success: Adds OAuth client and grant implementation or administrator token enablement, generation, one-time secure copy, credentials, and migration risk.

branches.condition: The browser is Chrome, Firefox, Safari, or Edge.
branches.path: Use the browser-specific developer-tools menu documented in the quickstart.
branches.effect_on_first_success: Changes only the route to the JavaScript console.

branches.condition: The script returns a documented error.
branches.path: For 404, sign in; for $.ajax not a function, run from Agent Workspace; for $.ajax undefined, disable or adjust browser settings and plugins that block scripts, then retry carefully.
branches.effect_on_first_success: Adds diagnosis and may create duplicate tickets if some asynchronous requests succeeded before retry.

frictionGates.description: The documentation-home path requires a work-email-based 14-day Zendesk Support trial when no suitable instance exists.
The trial form collects personal and company data across nine steps and submits under Zendesk's Privacy Notice; marketing consent is separately presented.
The developer must select an available Zendesk subdomain that becomes the instance origin.
The account must be provisioned and usable, but the developer docs and static form do not document the email, verification, screening, or post-submit transition.
The quickstart requires an agent or administrator role and a signed-in Agent Workspace session.
First-time API activation requires acceptance of Zendesk Terms of Service and the Application Developer and API Agreement.

The quickstart's named password-access prerequisite conflicts with current API settings for new accounts, and no current official page resolves whether the browser-session script needs a substitute toggle.
Browser or organizational policy must allow developer tools, console paste, page scripts, and same-origin API POSTs.

uncertainties.question: What replaces the quickstart's API password access prerequisite for a new July 2026 trial?
uncertainties.impact: The stated prerequisite is unavailable to new team-member accounts, so a literal reader cannot establish whether to activate API terms only, enable API tokens, create OAuth, enable end-user password access, or do nothing beyond the signed-in browser session.
uncertainties.reason_unresolved: The quickstart still requires and links password access, while current help says team-member password access is deprecated and unsupported for new accounts. The script supplies no explicit credentials and runs same-origin inside Agent Workspace.

uncertainties.question: What exact post-submit actions occur before a new trial owner reaches Agent Workspace?
uncertainties.impact: Email confirmation, anti-abuse review, launch setup, product selection, data-region choice, or guided onboarding could add pages and waits before the quickstart.
uncertainties.reason_unresolved: The registration page exposes nine input stages but the developer account guide only links to the live form and does not document provisioning or the first signed-in transition.

uncertainties.question: Does the advertised 10-minute duration include any account or API-access preparation?
uncertainties.impact: A new developer's actual time to first ticket can be much longer than the homepage label.
uncertainties.reason_unresolved: The quickstart label is 10 minutes, but its What you'll need section treats account, role, API access, and browser as pre-existing prerequisites.

uncertainties.question: Is creating three test tickets in a real Support instance acceptable to the developer's organization?
uncertainties.impact: The quickstart mutates ticket data and can trigger business rules, notifications, routing, reporting, or compliance workflows in a configured account.
uncertainties.reason_unresolved: The docs recommend a trial or sponsored test account and later deletion but do not inventory instance-specific automation side effects.

developerGoal: Install Milvus Lite locally, create a file-backed collection, and prepare the three documented entities.

entryPoint.developer_intent: Install Milvus Lite locally, create a file-backed collection, and prepare the three documented entities.
assumed_prior_state: No Zilliz Cloud account, organization, project, cluster, credential, endpoint, billing instrument, SDK, collection, or data is assumed.
The developer has a work email address, access to its inbox, and can create a policy-compliant password.
The developer is authorized to accept the Terms of Service and Privacy Policy.
The developer can select GCP us-west1 during onboarding so the automatic Default Project can host the documented free cluster example.
Python and pip are available locally; no installation or service action was executed during research.
No account, agreement, onboarding response, organization, project, cluster, credential, package, collection, or record was created during research.

selectedSurface.name: Milvus Lite local Python quickstart
selectedSurface.selection_basis: Under the workshop policy, the official local OSS quickstart takes priority over Zilliz Cloud signup. The current Milvus guide presents Milvus Lite first, requires only Python and pip, stores data in a local file, and demonstrates an explicit three-entity preparation result before later search steps.

alternatives_considered.name: Quickstart to Serving Cluster
alternatives_considered.reason_not_primary: This production-oriented route adds a database, custom schema, indexes, load operation, external object storage, bulk import job, and a later vector search.

alternatives_considered.name: Basic Vector Search on docs home
alternatives_considered.reason_not_primary: The snippet assumes an existing collection and data; the AutoID guide includes collection creation and an explicit first-insert output.

alternatives_considered.name: Serverless or Dedicated free trial
alternatives_considered.reason_not_primary: This uses expiring credits and later organization freeze, while the free cluster provides basic features at no cost without payment information.

alternatives_considered.name: Zilliz CLI quickstart
alternatives_considered.reason_not_primary: This adds CLI installation and browser or device-code authentication, then offers a menu of operations rather than the explicit insert result used here.

alternatives_considered.name: Google registration
alternatives_considered.reason_not_primary: This adds a Google login and the guide tells users to disable Google-managed MFA before linking.

alternatives_considered.name: GitHub registration
alternatives_considered.reason_not_primary: This requires a public GitHub email, GitHub login, and the guide tells users to disable GitHub-managed MFA before linking.

alternatives_considered.name: Manual primary keys

firstMeaningfulSuccess.official_milestone: Set up a local Milvus vector database and prepare data.
firstMeaningfulSuccess.normalized_outcome: Milvus Lite opens `milvus_demo.db`, creates `demo_collection`, and prepares three 768-dimensional entities for insertion.
firstMeaningfulSuccess.observable_completion_signal: The program prints `Data has 3 entities` and `Vector dim: 768`.

boundary_evidence.official_label_or_terminal_state: Data has 3 entities; Vector dim: 768

firstMeaningfulSuccess.why_this_is_the_boundary: This is the first explicit successful local setup and data-preparation output in the official quickstart. Insertion and vector search continue afterward.

prerequisites.requirement: Have Python 3.8 or newer and pip available locally.
Have package-index access and, for the recommended embedding route, access to download the approximately 50 MB default model.

stages.action: Open the Milvus quickstart and use its first-listed Milvus Lite route.
details: Milvus Lite is embedded in the Python client and needs no account or server process.

stages.success_signal: pip completes successfully.
stages.failure_or_wait: Use Python 3.8 or newer.

stages.output: A local file-backed Milvus database and collection exist.
stages.success_signal: `demo_collection` is created without error.
stages.failure_or_wait: The example drops an existing collection of the same name before recreating it.

stages.action: Install the model extras used by the recommended embedding path.
stages.success_signal: pip completes successfully.
stages.failure_or_wait: The package includes larger ML dependencies such as PyTorch and can take time to download.

stages.failure_or_wait: If the embedding model cannot be downloaded, the guide provides a random-vector fallback that still completes the example.

branches.condition: Google registration
branches.path: Select Google, accept the Zilliz terms and privacy policy, submit, authenticate at Google, and return to a success notification.
branches.effect_on_first_success: Replaces work-email password and confirmation code with provider authentication, but the first-login onboarding gap remains.

branches.condition: GitHub registration
branches.path: Make the GitHub email public, select GitHub, accept terms and privacy policy, authenticate at GitHub, and return to a success notification.
branches.effect_on_first_success: Adds GitHub profile changes and provider authentication, while the first-login onboarding gap remains.

branches.condition: Confirmation code not received
branches.path: Select Resend Code and wait for a replacement message.
branches.effect_on_first_success: Adds an email retry before the same onboarding gate.

branches.condition: Choose Serverless or Dedicated trial
branches.path: Use the $100 work-email trial credit for an eligible cluster instead of the free cluster.
branches.effect_on_first_success: Adds deployment choices and a 30-day credit expiry, while the free cluster avoids payment and credit dependence.

branches.condition: Use Google Cloud region other than us-west1
branches.path: Select another currently supported GCP onboarding region and create the free cluster there.
branches.effect_on_first_success: Changes the Default Project region and endpoint; free clusters remain GCP-only.

branches.condition: Use an API key instead of cluster credentials
branches.path: Create an API key with required privileges and use it as the connection token.
branches.effect_on_first_success: Adds control-plane credential creation; the one-time cluster username and password already provide the selected route.

branches.condition: Manual primary IDs
branches.path: Disable AutoID, create the published manual-ID schema, include `PROD-001` and `PROD-002` in the two inserts, and print those IDs.
branches.effect_on_first_success: Adds responsibility for unique identifiers, while AutoID is recommended for simpler ingestion when external IDs are not needed.

branches.condition: Continue to vector search
branches.path: Use the stored data in a loaded collection and run the documented similarity-search example.
branches.effect_on_first_success: Occurs after the first insert already returns generated IDs.

frictionGates.description: The developer must keep using the same email, Google, or GitHub method for login unless the method is changed later in account management.
The work-email password needs eight or more characters and three of four published character categories.
If the confirmation code does not arrive, select Resend Code; no delivery time or code-expiration behavior is published.
The required first-login onboarding requests additional information without a complete public list of fields, validation states, or submit transition.
If registration fails because the email may already be registered, try logging in directly; if the issue persists, create a support ticket.
The automatic Default Project is created in the onboarding region, and a project region cannot be changed after creation; create a new project to use another region.
The generated cluster username and password are shown only once, so save them while the cluster is being created.
For connection failures, check `Running` status, IP allowlist, endpoint port, TLS, endpoint and token, then test the port; submit a support request if the issue persists.
A free cluster is automatically suspended with notice after seven days of inactivity and can be resumed when needed.
If free-cluster insert or upsert returns the documented disk-quota-exceeded error, upgrade to Serverless or Dedicated and migrate the data.

uncertainties.impact: The required onboarding step cannot be counted or reconstructed, so no complete candidate path can be claimed.

uncertainties.question: How long does free-cluster provisioning take?
uncertainties.impact: The wait before endpoint use is unbounded in the documented path.
uncertainties.reason_unresolved: The page defines `Running` as success but gives no duration or timeout recovery.

uncertainties.question: What exact generated integer IDs will the AutoID insert return?
uncertainties.impact: The values vary, but success remains two IDs in the published output shape.
uncertainties.reason_unresolved: The guide labels its IDs as an output example rather than fixed values.

developerGoal: Create a Zoom General App, complete OAuth authorization, obtain an access token, and receive a successful Zoom REST API response.

entryPoint.developer_intent: Create a Zoom General App, complete OAuth authorization, obtain an access token, and receive a successful Zoom REST API response.
assumed_prior_state: Discovery is complete and the developer has chosen Zoom.
The developer has not yet created the General App being researched.
No Zoom account, developer role, product feature, or management type is silently assumed; documented branches cover those conditions.

selectedSurface.name: Zoom General App creation build flow
selectedSurface.selection_basis: The Zoom Developer Platform home presents Start building as its primary action and links it to the unified General App creation build flow. That flow creates the OAuth client, collects redirect URLs and scopes, and uses Local Test Add App Now to obtain a user authorization code. Zoom's OAuth 2.0 documentation continues that same General App path: exchange the authorization code for an access token, then call Zoom REST APIs with the bearer token. First success is the first authenticated Zoom API response, not app creation alone.

alternatives_considered.name: Server-to-Server OAuth app
alternatives_considered.reason_not_primary: Server-to-Server OAuth is a separate app type with account_credentials tokens and no user consent. The platform home Start building path creates a General App, so the selected route continues that General App OAuth code exchange into an API call.

alternatives_considered.name: Zoom Video SDK for web
alternatives_considered.reason_not_primary: This is a product and runtime-specific route with its own credential and toolkit branches rather than the vendor's unified General App start.

alternatives_considered.name: Zoom Meeting SDK for web
alternatives_considered.reason_not_primary: This is a product and runtime-specific installation path rather than the vendor's unified General App start.

alternatives_considered.name: Zoom Apps in-client surface
alternatives_considered.reason_not_primary: In-client Zoom Apps are one feature path inside General App creation; the shared first-success boundary for API access remains OAuth token exchange plus an authenticated REST call.

firstMeaningfulSuccess.official_milestone: Exchange the General App authorization code for an access token, then call GET /users/me on the Zoom REST API.
firstMeaningfulSuccess.normalized_outcome: An authenticated Zoom REST API response returns the current user's profile JSON from GET https://api.zoom.us/v2/users/me.
firstMeaningfulSuccess.observable_completion_signal: The Zoom API returns HTTP 200 with a JSON body that includes the authenticated user's id and email or display identity fields.

boundary_evidence.official_label_or_terminal_state: Zoom OAuth 2.0 documents that Add App Now / Allow yields an authorization code, that POST https://zoom.us/oauth/token exchanges that code for an access_token, and that the access token authenticates Zoom API requests. The Users API documents GET /users/{userId} with userId=me as the authenticated current-user read.

firstMeaningfulSuccess.why_this_is_the_boundary: App creation and Local Test authorize the OAuth client but do not prove API access. Zoom's own OAuth guide treats token exchange and API use as the continuation of the General App authorization path. GET /users/me is the shortest authenticated product API call after that token is issued and does not require creating meetings or other product resources.

prerequisites.requirement: A Zoom account.
The account owner or an account admin role, or an assigned Zoom for developers role with View and Edit permissions.
A development OAuth redirect URL or endpoint and at least one OAuth allow-list URL.
A local Zoom client is needed to observe the documented My apps completion signal for a user-managed app.
Access to the email address used for signup is required if the developer must create a Zoom account, because Zoom sends a verification code.

stages.action: Open the Zoom Developer Platform home and select Start building.
details: The primary home-page action links to the Zoom App creation build flow.
stages.success_signal: The build-flow page opens.
stages.failure_or_wait: No wait is documented.

stages.action: Review the build-flow overview and its What, Where, and Which questions.
details: The flow asks what kind of app is being created, where users will use it, and which Zoom products it will work with.
Use the Development view for the local-test path.
stages.input: Intended app and integration use case
stages.output: Inputs needed for the unified build flow
stages.success_signal: The developer can proceed to the Quick start guide.
stages.failure_or_wait: The documentation does not choose these use-case answers for the developer.

stages.action: Read Key concepts as required by the build-flow prerequisites.
details: Review app distribution labels, adding versus installing apps, and admin-managed versus user-managed apps.
stages.success_signal: The stated knowledge prerequisite is completed.
stages.failure_or_wait: No wait is documented.

stages.action: Go to the Zoom App Marketplace and sign in with a Zoom account.
details: If no account exists, follow the documented account-creation branch before returning to sign in.
stages.success_signal: The Marketplace loads for the signed-in account.
stages.failure_or_wait: Account creation requires email verification when the developer does not already have an account.

stages.action: Select Developer in the lower-left Marketplace navigation.
details: Zoom displays the Developer link only when the account has proper developer permissions.
stages.success_signal: The Developer link is visible and opens Created apps.
stages.failure_or_wait: If the link is absent, an account admin must assign the required role permissions.

stages.action: On Created apps, select Develop and then Build an app.
stages.success_signal: The app creation screen is shown.
stages.failure_or_wait: No wait is documented.

stages.action: Select General app and then Create.
stages.output: New General App in the build flow
stages.success_signal: The Basic Info page for the new app is available.
stages.failure_or_wait: No wait is documented.

stages.action: Generate a generic app name and development app credentials.
details: The build flow automatically generates a client ID and client secret.
Development credentials differ from production credentials.
stages.output: Generic name, development client ID, and development client secret
stages.success_signal: The generated values appear on Basic Info.
stages.failure_or_wait: No wait is documented.

stages.action: Optionally replace the generated app name and save by clicking outside the name field.
details: Select the edit icon to change the name.
stages.success_signal: The new name is displayed.
stages.failure_or_wait: The generated name remains if the developer does not change it.

stages.action: Confirm whether the app is admin-managed or user-managed.
details: The management choice changes who can add the app and which products, features, scopes, and permissions are available.
Changing management type later requires reviewing and reconfirming features and scopes.
stages.input: Intended app manager and data-access level
stages.success_signal: Basic Info displays the chosen management type.
stages.failure_or_wait: The documentation provides no universal default because the choice depends on the app's intended users and access.

stages.action: Enter the required development OAuth Redirect URL or endpoint.
details: After a user approves the app, Zoom redirects an authorization code or token to this registered URL.
Loopback and development endpoints can be used in development but should not be used for production redirects.
stages.success_signal: The redirect URL is present in OAuth Information.
stages.failure_or_wait: An unregistered or invalid redirect prevents the intended OAuth return path.

stages.action: Add the required OAuth allow-list URL or URLs.
details: Zoom accepts either a complete URL or a base URL without path or query parameters.
stages.input: Approved redirect domain or complete URL
stages.success_signal: The required allow-list entry is present.
stages.failure_or_wait: Redirects outside the allow list are not accepted.

stages.action: Optionally enable Strict Mode URL or Subdomain check.
details: Strict mode permits only exact registered redirect matches.
Subdomain check restricts redirects to registered subdomains.
stages.success_signal: The selected restriction is reflected in OAuth Information.
stages.failure_or_wait: Exact or subdomain mismatches are rejected when the corresponding restriction is enabled.

stages.action: On Features, select and configure the Zoom product and feature path required by the intended General App.
details: Access supports tokens and event subscriptions.
Surface supports in-client Zoom products and features.
Embed provides Meeting, Contact Center, and Phone SDKs.
Connect configures third-party API connectors.
stages.input: The app's What, Where, and Which answers
stages.success_signal: The intended feature configuration is represented in the app.
stages.failure_or_wait: Required fields vary by the feature path and are later checked by the Local Test wizard.

stages.action: On Scopes, select Add Scopes.
stages.success_signal: Zoom product and scope choices are available.
stages.failure_or_wait: Available scopes depend on the app management type and selected features.

stages.action: Select the Zoom product, check the desired scopes, and select Done.
details: Scopes define which Zoom API methods the app is allowed to call.
stages.success_signal: The selected scopes appear on the app.
stages.failure_or_wait: Scopes unnecessary for the app may be rejected by Zoom Security Review.

stages.action: Explain in Scope Description why each requested scope is necessary.
details: Zoom states that adding a scope submits a request to its Security Review team and that unnecessary scope requests may be rejected.
stages.success_signal: The scope justification is present.
stages.failure_or_wait: The documentation does not state a review duration for this first-mile path.

stages.action: Open Local Test in the Development view.
details: Local Test is available only in Development.
stages.success_signal: The Local Test wizard is visible.
stages.failure_or_wait: The page is not available in the Production view.

stages.action: Verify the app against all local-test requirements and display any incomplete fields.
stages.output: Requirement checklist or list of incomplete fields
stages.success_signal: No required field remains incomplete.
stages.failure_or_wait: The developer must return to and complete any fields listed by the wizard before adding the app.

stages.action: Optionally select Preview Your App Listing Page.
details: The preview shows a summary of app details and the Marketplace listing page.
stages.success_signal: The preview is displayed.
stages.failure_or_wait: Preview is not required to take the documented Add App path.

stages.action: Select Add App Now for the developer's own account.
stages.input: App that passes Local Test requirements
stages.success_signal: The Allow action is presented.
stages.failure_or_wait: Incomplete required fields block progression and are listed by the wizard.

stages.action: Review the authorization request and select Allow.
stages.input: Consent to add the development app
stages.success_signal: Zoom accepts the authorization and adds the app.
stages.failure_or_wait: Declining authorization prevents the app from being added.

stages.action: Redirect to the configured OAuth redirect URI with an authorization code after Allow.
details: Zoom OAuth 2.0 states that after the user authorizes the app, Zoom redirects the browser to the redirect_uri and includes an authorization code such as https://example.com/callback?code=AUTH_CODE.
Add App Now on Local Test is the documented way to start that user authorization for the developer's own account.
stages.output: Authorization code on the redirect URI
stages.success_signal: The redirect URI receives a code query parameter.
stages.failure_or_wait: Declining authorization prevents issuance of an authorization code.

stages.action: Capture the authorization code from the OAuth redirect request.
details: The application endpoint that owns the registered redirect URI reads the code query parameter.
stages.success_signal: The code value is available to the token exchange request.
stages.failure_or_wait: Missing or expired codes cannot be exchanged for tokens.

stages.action: Exchange the authorization code for an access token at Zoom's OAuth token endpoint.
details: Send POST https://zoom.us/oauth/token with grant_type=authorization_code, the authorization code, and the same redirect_uri used during authorization.
Authenticate the token request with HTTP Basic credentials using the development client ID and client secret.
stages.input: Authorization code, redirect_uri, development client ID, and client secret
stages.success_signal: Zoom returns JSON containing access_token, token_type bearer, expires_in, and scope.
stages.failure_or_wait: Invalid code, redirect_uri mismatch, or bad client credentials cause token exchange to fail.

stages.action: Call GET https://api.zoom.us/v2/users/me with the access token.
details: Include Authorization: Bearer <access_token>.
userId=me selects the authenticated user on the Users API Get a user operation.
The app must have requested a user read scope such as user:read:user during scope configuration.
stages.input: Bearer access token from the authorization_code exchange
stages.success_signal: Zoom returns HTTP 200 with JSON for the authenticated user.
stages.failure_or_wait: Missing scopes, expired tokens, or invalid bearer credentials return API errors instead of the user profile.

stages.action: Confirm the Users API JSON identifies the authenticated Zoom user.
details: Inspect fields such as id and email or display name in the 200 response body.
stages.success_signal: The response body identifies the authorized Zoom user.
stages.failure_or_wait: An empty or error body means API access has not succeeded.

branches.condition: The developer has no Zoom account.
branches.path: Open Zoom's signup page, enter a birth year, enter an email address, select Continue, wait for the verification email, enter the code, select Verify, enter full name and password, and select Continue. Zoom then creates a Basic account; return to Marketplace sign-in.
branches.effect_on_first_success: Adds identity data entry and an email-verification wait before the Marketplace path can begin.

branches.condition: The Developer link is absent because the account lacks app-development permissions.
branches.path: An administrator signs into the Zoom web portal, opens User Management, Roles, All, edits the relevant role, opens Role Settings, Advanced features, and enables both View and Edit for Zoom for developers. The developer then returns to the Marketplace.
branches.effect_on_first_success: Introduces an administrator dependency before app creation can continue.

branches.condition: The intended app is admin-managed.
branches.path: Select Admin-managed. Account admins add and manage the app for users on their account. At the terminal local-test state the app is managed through the web portal and is hidden from the Zoom client.
branches.effect_on_first_success: Admin-managed apps change who can authorize the app and which scopes are available before the OAuth code exchange and API call.

branches.condition: The intended app is user-managed.
branches.path: Select User-managed. Individual users add and manage the app, and each user must authenticate. At the terminal local-test state the app is displayed in My apps in the local Zoom client.
branches.effect_on_first_success: The final completion signal is directly visible in the Zoom client.

branches.condition: The app is an SDK app that does not need end-user authorization.
branches.path: Use the company's home domain in the required OAuth Redirect URL field, as directed by the OAuth Information page.
branches.effect_on_first_success: Replaces an authorization callback endpoint with the company home domain for this SDK-specific condition.

branches.condition: The app needs API access or event notifications.
branches.path: Use Access to manage the app token and, if needed, configure Event Subscription with an event endpoint. Corresponding event scopes are selected automatically.
branches.effect_on_first_success: Adds credential handling or event endpoint configuration before Local Test authorization, token exchange, and the API call.

branches.condition: Users will open the app inside Zoom products.
branches.path: Use Surface to select the Zoom products, enter the Home URL domain, add accepted URLs to the Domain Allow List, select applicable in-client features, and choose supported Zoom clients. The Home URL must return the required OWASP response headers.
branches.effect_on_first_success: Adds hosted-application, domain, security-header, product, and client-support configuration before Local Test authorization, token exchange, and the API call.

branches.condition: The app embeds Zoom capabilities in a third-party application.
branches.path: Use Embed and select or download the applicable Meeting, Contact Center, or Phone SDK.
branches.effect_on_first_success: Adds an SDK choice and SDK-specific work beyond the generic local-add milestone.

branches.condition: The app connects third-party APIs into Zoom.
branches.path: Use Connect, upload an OpenAPI 3.0.0 through 3.1.0 JSON or YAML file or enter the base URL and authentication parameters, then configure and test GET, POST, DELETE, PATCH, or PUT endpoints.
branches.effect_on_first_success: Adds an API description, authentication, endpoint configuration, and endpoint test before Local Test authorization, token exchange, and the API call.

frictionGates.description: A Zoom account is required to sign in to the App Marketplace.
New account creation requires an email verification code after age and email entry.
The Developer link is exposed only to an owner, admin, or role with Zoom for developers permissions.
The developer must choose admin-managed or user-managed, which changes products, features, scopes, permissions, and the final verification surface.
A development OAuth redirect URL or endpoint is required.
At least one OAuth allow-list URL is required.
The developer must map the use case to applicable Zoom products and the Access, Surface, Embed, or Connect feature path.
Requested scopes determine allowed API methods and are subject to Zoom's stated minimal-access review principle.
The Local Test wizard blocks progression by listing incomplete required fields that must be resolved.
The developer must select Allow on Zoom's authorization screen to add the app.
Development client ID and client secret are required to exchange the authorization code for an access token.
A user read scope such as user:read:user must have been granted for GET /users/me to succeed.

uncertainties.question: Which minimum user-read scope string is currently required on a fresh General App for GET /users/me?
uncertainties.reason_unresolved: The live Marketplace scope picker labels and Users API scope list need a dated fresh-account confirmation for the current granular scope names.
uncertainties.impact: Scope labels have migrated toward granular scopes; an outdated scope selection can block the API call after a successful token exchange.

uncertainties.question: Which management type is universal for a General App?
uncertainties.impact: The final verification surface differs: My apps for user-managed apps versus web-portal management for admin-managed apps.
uncertainties.reason_unresolved: Zoom's official quickstart requires the developer to confirm the choice based on who will add and manage the app; it does not specify a universal default.

uncertainties.question: Which Features configuration is required for every General App?
uncertainties.impact: The number and type of configuration steps can expand substantially before Local Test.
uncertainties.reason_unresolved: The official build flow is driven by the app's What, Where, and Which answers and presents Access, Surface, Embed, and Connect as conditional paths rather than one universal configuration.

uncertainties.question: Does Zoom Security Review add a wait before this local-test completion state?
uncertainties.impact: A required review could lengthen the path between scope selection and app addition.
uncertainties.reason_unresolved: The quickstart says that adding scopes submits a request and that unnecessary requests may be rejected, but it does not state whether or when that review blocks the documented Add App Now flow or provide a duration.

excludedAfterSuccess: Executing a Zoom REST API request or validating a returned resource
Implementing or running a Video SDK, Meeting SDK, Zoom Apps SDK, Contact Center SDK, or Phone SDK feature
Receiving and verifying a webhook or WebSocket event
Generating and sharing an Authorization URL with internal users
Marketplace review, unlisted or public distribution, and publishing
doesNotProve: Platform records reconstruct official documentation. They do not contain product telemetry, conversion rates, observed completion times, or proven causes of abandonment.
Friction gates are documented requirements or transitions. They are not confirmed drop-off points.
Blocker taxonomy entries are candidate hypotheses. Individual reasons remain not diagnosis eligible unless separate case evidence supports them.
A documented journey analysis is not a diagnosis of a real developer attempt.
